> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Structures of agents: simple reflex, model-based, goal-based, and utility-based

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Agent?

In the formal language of **Artificial Intelligence (AI)**, an **agent** is any entity that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through **sensors** and acting upon that environment through **actuators**. This definition, rooted in the foundational textbook *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach* (Russell & Norvig), forms the bedrock of KTU Module 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCAT502):** An *agent* is a computer system situated in some environment, capable of flexible autonomous action in that environment in order to meet its delegated objectives.

**Mathematically, an agent can be represented as the mapping:**

$$f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{P}^*$ = the set of all possible **percept sequences**
- $\mathcal{A}$ = the set of all possible **actions** the agent can execute

## 1.2 The Agent–Environment Loop

Every agent continuously executes the following functional cycle:

$$\text{Perceive} \rightarrow \text{Think (Decide)} \rightarrow \text{Act} \rightarrow \text{Update State} \rightarrow \text{Repeat}$$

| Term | Role | Real-World Analogy |
|------|------|---------------------|
| **Sensors** | Collect percepts (input) | Human eyes, ears, thermometer |
| **Actuators** | Execute actions (output) | Human hands, legs, robotic motors |
| **Environment** | The world the agent inhabits | A room, road network, internet |
| **Performance Measure** | Evaluates how well the agent is doing | Exam score, fuel efficiency |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Driver Agent

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine you are driving a car. Your *eyes* (sensors) see a red light ahead, your *brain* processes this percept, and your *foot on the brake* (actuator) stops the car. That entire decision-making structure is an **agent**. Now, the *type* of agent depends on *how* your brain processes that red light — do you slam the brake reflexively? Do you check the mirror first? Do you weigh whether running the light saves time? Each of these is a different **agent structure**.

## 1.4 The Four Canonical Agent Structures (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The KTU PCCAT502 Module 1 syllabus mandates a study of the following four agent architectures, arranged in increasing order of **rationality** and **computational complexity**:

1. **Simple Reflex Agent** — reacts to current percept only
2. **Model-Based Reflex Agent** — maintains internal world state
3. **Goal-Based Agent** — acts to achieve explicit goals
4. **Utility-Based Agent** — acts to maximize a utility function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The progression from (1) to (4) represents an *increase in intelligence, autonomy, and computational cost*. The KTU exam frequently tests the comparative advantages/disadvantages of each structure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Type 1 — Simple Reflex Agent

### Definition
A **Simple Reflex Agent** selects actions based **only on the current percept**, ignoring the rest of the percept history. It operates via a set of pre-defined **condition–action rules** (often called *situational rules* or *production rules*).

**Formal Mapping:**

$$\text{Action} = \text{Rule-Match}(\text{Current Percept})$$

Or in equation form:

$$a = \begin{cases} a_1 & \text{if } \text{condition}_1(p_t) \text{ holds} \\ a_2 & \text{if } \text{condition}_2(p_t) \text{ holds} \\ \vdots & \\ a_n & \text{otherwise (default rule)} \end{cases}$$

Where $p_t$ is the percept at time $t$.

### Operating Logic Steps
- **Step 1:** Receive percept $p_t$ from the environment via sensors.
- **Step 2:** Match $p_t$ against the pre-stored **condition–action rules** in the rule base.
- **Step 3:** Fire the action $a$ corresponding to the first matching rule.
- **Step 4:** Send $a$ to the actuators and repeat.

### Characteristics
| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Memory of past percepts | **No** |
| Internal world model | **No** |
| Goal awareness | **No** |
| Rationality | Limited to **fully observable** environments |
| Computational cost | **Very Low** |
| Example | Thermostat, automatic door, simple vacuum-cleaner |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Fails:** In a *partially observable* environment, a simple reflex agent is doomed to be irrational. For example, a self-driving car that only checks the current frame of a camera will crash if a child is hidden behind a parked bus — it does not remember the road was straight 2 seconds ago.

---

## 2.2 Type 2 — Model-Based Reflex Agent

### Definition
A **Model-Based Reflex Agent** maintains an **internal state** that depends on the *percept history* and thereby reflects at least some of the *unobserved aspects of the current state*. It uses two components:
1. A **Model of the World** — knowledge of how the world evolves independently of the agent.
2. A **Model of the Agent's Own Actions** — knowledge of how the agent's actions affect the world.

**State Update Equation:**

$$S_{t+1} = \text{Update}(S_t, a_{t}, p_{t+1})$$

Where:
- $S_t$ = current internal state
- $a_t$ = action taken at time $t$
- $p_{t+1}$ = new percept at time $t+1$

The agent must keep track of:
- **Transition Model:** $T(s, a, s') = P(s' \mid s, a)$ — probability of reaching state $s'$ from $s$ via action $a$
- **Sensor Model:** $O(s, p) = P(p \mid s)$ — probability of perceiving $p$ given state $s$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Analogy):** Think of driving in fog. You cannot see the road 100 meters ahead, but you *remember* the map, the speed limit, and the fact that you took a left turn 30 seconds ago. That internal *mental model* of the world is what a model-based agent maintains.

---

## 2.3 Type 3 — Goal-Based Agent

### Definition
A **Goal-Based Agent** not only maintains a model of the world but also has an explicit **goal** (a desired state) and chooses actions that lead it closer to that goal. It uses **search** and **planning** algorithms.

**Formally, the agent solves:**

$$\text{Find action } a \text{ such that } \text{Distance}(S_{\text{current}}, S_{\text{goal}}) \text{ is minimized}$$

Where distance may be measured by:
- Number of steps (uniform-cost search)
- Path cost (Dijkstra / A\*)
- Heuristic estimate (greedy best-first search)

**Key Search Strategies Used:**
- Breadth-First Search (BFS)
- Depth-First Search (DFS)
- A\* Search
- Heuristic Search

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Goal-Based is Smarter:** The agent is *future-oriented*. It will not take an action that seems good *now* if it leads to a dead-end. Example: A GPS navigator that reroutes you to avoid a traffic jam 5 km ahead — it considers the *goal* (reaching destination) and *plans* accordingly.

---

## 2.4 Type 4 — Utility-Based Agent

### Definition
A **Utility-Based Agent** extends the goal-based agent by introducing a **utility function** $U: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ that maps a state to a real number representing its *desirability*. When multiple goals can be achieved, the agent picks the action that **maximizes expected utility**.

**Core Decision Rule:**

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')$$

This is the **Maximum Expected Utility (MEU) Principle**, a foundational principle of rational agency.

### Why Utility > Goal?
- A **goal** is binary: achieved or not (success/failure).
- A **utility** is a *continuous measure* of preference — it can rank trade-offs.

| Scenario | Goal-Based Decision | Utility-Based Decision |
|----------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Two routes to destination | Picks *any* valid route | Picks the **fastest, safest, cheapest** route |
| Chess move | Picks a move leading to checkmate | Picks a move leading to the **highest win probability** |
| Medical treatment | Cures the disease | Chooses the cure that **maximizes quality-adjusted life years** |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it covers 80% of KTU 2-mark and 7-mark questions on agent structures.**

| Agent Type | Core Equation | Memory | Goal | Utility | Computational Cost | Best For |
|------------|---------------|--------|------|---------|---------------------|----------|
| **Simple Reflex** | $a = \text{Rule}(p_t)$ | No | No | No | $O(1)$ | Fully observable env. |
| **Model-Based Reflex** | $S_{t+1} = \text{Update}(S_t, a_t, p_{t+1})$ | Yes | No | No | $O(n)$ | Partially observable env. |
| **Goal-Based** | $\min \text{Distance}(S, S_{\text{goal}})$ | Yes | Yes | No | $O(b^d)$ | Planning problems |
| **Utility-Based** | $a^* = \arg\max_a \sum_{s'} P(s'\vert s, a) U(s')$ | Yes | Yes | Yes | $O(\vert\mathcal{A}\vert \cdot \vert S\vert)$ | Conflicting goals, uncertain outcomes |

Where $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth of search, $\vert\mathcal{A}\vert$ = action space size, $\vert S\vert$ = state space size.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics:** Industrial pick-and-place robots use simple reflex; autonomous vehicles use utility-based.
- **Healthcare AI:** Goal-based for diagnosis pathways; utility-based for treatment trade-off selection.
- **Game AI:** NPC behavior uses reflex; chess engines use utility-based evaluation.
- **Smart Grids:** Utility-based agents balance cost, demand, and emissions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Reflex to Utility-Based Agent (Theoretical Ladder)

We can formally derive the progression of agent sophistication by layering additional components onto a base reflex agent.

**Step 1: Simple Reflex Agent**

$$a_t = R(p_t)$$

Where $R$ is a static rule function. The percept history is *truncated* to length 1.

**Step 2: Add Internal State (becomes Model-Based)**

$$S_t = \gamma \cdot S_{t-1} + (1 - \gamma) \cdot \text{Encode}(p_t, a_{t-1})$$

A common formulation where $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ is a decay/retention factor, and $\text{Encode}$ is a feature-encoding function. This shows that the new state $S_t$ is a *weighted combination* of the old state and the new percept-action pair.

**Step 3: Add Goal (becomes Goal-Based)**

The agent now solves:

$$\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s)$$

Where $\pi$ is a policy (a mapping from states to actions), and $V^{\pi}(s)$ is the value of state $s$ under policy $\pi$. For a deterministic goal-reaching problem:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } s = S_{\text{goal}} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4: Add Utility (becomes Utility-Based)**

The binary $V^{\pi}$ is replaced by a *continuous* utility function:

$$U(s) = w_1 \cdot u_1(s) + w_2 \cdot u_2(s) + \cdots + w_k \cdot u_k(s)$$

Where $w_i$ are the weights assigned to different objectives (e.g., safety, speed, cost) and $u_i(s)$ are the individual utility components for state $s$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Each additional capability (state, goal, utility) strictly **increases the rationality** of the agent under uncertainty, but also **increases computational cost and design complexity**. This trade-off is a frequent KTU 14-mark question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Maximum Expected Utility (MEU) Principle

This is the *core mathematical foundation* of a rational utility-based agent.

**Step 1: Define the outcome utility**

Let $U(s)$ denote the utility of being in state $s$.

**Step 2: Define action consequences**

If the agent takes action $a$ in state $s$, the environment transitions to state $s'$ with probability $P(s' \mid s, a)$.

**Step 3: Compute the expected utility of action $a$**

$$
\mathbb{E}[U \mid s, a] = \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')
$$

**Step 4: Apply the rationality axiom — the agent picks the best action**

$$
a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}[U \mid s, a] = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')
$$

**Step 5: Example — Two Actions, Two Outcomes**

Suppose an agent must choose between:
- **Action A** with $P(s_1 \mid s, A) = 0.9$ and $U(s_1) = 100$, $P(s_2 \mid s, A) = 0.1$ and $U(s_2) = 0$
- **Action B** with $P(s_1 \mid s, B) = 0.5$ and $U(s_1) = 100$, $P(s_2 \mid s, B) = 0.5$ and $U(s_2) = 0$

Calculate $\mathbb{E}[U \mid s, A]$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[U \mid s, A] = (0.9)(100) + (0.1)(0) = 90
$$

Calculate $\mathbb{E}[U \mid s, B]$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[U \mid s, B] = (0.5)(100) + (0.5)(0) = 50
$$

**Conclusion:** $90 > 50$, so the rational utility-based agent chooses **Action A**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Frequently Asked)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is essential for the 14-mark "compare and contrast" question.**

| Dimension | Simple Reflex | Model-Based | Goal-Based | Utility-Based |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|------------|---------------|
| **Percept Used** | Current only | History + current | History + current | History + current |
| **World Model** | None | Yes (Transition + Sensor) | Yes | Yes |
| **Decision Basis** | Rule matching | Rule + state | Goal satisfaction | Expected utility max |
| **Handles Partial Observability** | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Handles Conflicting Goals** | No | No | Limited | Yes |
| **Learning Capability** | None (fixed rules) | Limited | Limited | High (reinforcement learning) |
| **Example Application** | Thermostat | Self-driving (stateful) | GPS navigation | Autonomous trading bot |
| **PEAS Complexity** | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Failure Mode** | Infinite loops in partial obs. | Incorrect world model | Unreachable goal | Mis-specified utility (alignment problem) |

---

## 3.4 PEAS Framework Application Across Agent Types

**PEAS** = Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors. Let us apply PEAS to a **medical diagnosis system** to show how the agent structure changes:

| PEAS Element | Simple Reflex | Model-Based | Goal-Based | Utility-Based |
|--------------|---------------|-------------|------------|---------------|
| **Performance** | Correct immediate response | Patient state tracking | Cure the disease | Maximize QALYs (Quality-Adjusted Life Years) |
| **Environment** | Patient symptoms (visible) | Patient + history | Patient + treatment pathways | Patient + risk model |
| **Actuators** | Display alert | Suggest tests | Recommend treatment plan | Recommend *optimal* treatment plan |
| **Sensors** | Symptom input form | Symptoms + medical records | Symptoms + records + test results | Symptoms + records + results + patient preferences |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Agent Architecture Overview (All Four Types)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    env[Environment] -->|Sensors| agent
    agent -->|Actuators| env
    
    subgraph agent[Agent System]
        direction TB
        sref[Simple Reflex Agent] --> mbref[Model-Based Reflex Agent]
        mbref --> goal[Goal-Based Agent]
        goal --> util[Utility-Based Agent]
        
        sref -.->|Adds Internal State| mbref
        mbref -.->|Adds Goal Info| goal
        goal -.->|Adds Utility Function| util
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Flow Explanation:** The agent sits in a loop with the environment. The four agent types are arranged in a hierarchy where each upper type *subsumes* the capabilities of the lower one and adds one more intelligence layer. This is the standard KTU diagram for full-module 14-mark questions.

---

## 4.2 Simple Reflex Agent — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    env[Environment] -->|Percept| sens[Sensors]
    sens --> cond{Condition-Action Rules}
    cond -->|Matches| act[Select Action]
    act --> actr[Actuators]
    actr -->|Action| env
    cond -.->|No Match| def[Default Rule]
    def --> act
```

**Explanation:** The percept goes directly into a rule matcher. There is **no state buffer** and **no memory module**. The cycle is purely reactive.

---

## 4.3 Model-Based Reflex Agent — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    env[Environment] -->|Percept| sens[Sensors]
    sens --> st[Internal State Update]
    hist[Percept History] --> st
    st -->|Current World State| cond{Condition-Action Rules}
    wm[World Model] --> st
    cond -->|Matches| act[Select Action]
    act --> actr[Actuators]
    actr -->|Action| env
```

**Explanation:** The key addition is the **Internal State Update** module, which fuses (a) the new percept, (b) past percept history, and (c) the world model. This is the difference between a *reactive* and a *deliberative* agent.

---

## 4.4 Goal-Based Agent — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    env[Environment] -->|Percept| sens[Sensors]
    sens --> st[State Estimator]
    wm[World Model] --> st
    st -->|Current State| planner[Planning and Search Module]
    g[Goal Description] --> planner
    planner -->|Sequence of Actions| actr[Actuators]
    actr -->|Action| env
```

**Explanation:** Unlike the previous agents, the goal-based agent has a **Planning/Search** module that uses a *goal description* and a *state* to generate an *action sequence*. The agent no longer reactively matches rules — it reasons ahead.

---

## 4.5 Utility-Based Agent — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    env[Environment] -->|Percept| sens[Sensors]
    sens --> st[State Estimator]
    wm[World Model] --> st
    st -->|Current State| util[Utility Function Module]
    uf[Utility Function U of s] --> util
    util -->|Expected Utility| dec[Decision Maker]
    dec -->|Optimal Action| actr[Actuators]
    actr -->|Action| env
```

**Explanation:** A **Utility Function Module** evaluates each candidate action by computing the *expected utility* of the resulting state. The **Decision Maker** then picks the action with the maximum expected utility — this is the MEU principle in action.

---

## 4.6 Comparative Flow Diagram (One Image, Four Agents)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SR[Simple Reflex]
        sr_p[Percept] --> sr_r[Rule Base] --> sr_a[Action]
    end
    
    subgraph MB[Model Based]
        mb_p[Percept] --> mb_s[State Update] --> mb_r[Rule Base] --> mb_a[Action]
        mb_h[History] --> mb_s
        mb_w[World Model] --> mb_s
    end
    
    subgraph GB[Goal Based]
        gb_p[Percept] --> gb_s[State] --> gb_pl[Planner] --> gb_a[Action]
        gb_g[Goal] --> gb_pl
    end
    
    subgraph UB[Utility Based]
        ub_p[Percept] --> ub_s[State] --> ub_u[Utility Evaluator] --> ub_a[Action]
        ub_uf[Utility Function] --> ub_u
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** This single diagram can earn you 5–6 marks in a 14-mark question if drawn neatly with proper labels. The key is to show the *progressive addition of modules* (State, Goal, Utility) as you move from left to right.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

### Question 1
**Differentiate between a simple reflex agent and a model-based reflex agent. Give one real-world example of each.** **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

| Aspect | Simple Reflex Agent | Model-Based Reflex Agent |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Memory of past percepts | **No** | **Yes (maintains internal state)** |
| World model | **No** | **Yes (transition + sensor model)** |
| Suitable for | Fully observable env. | Partially observable env. |
| Example | Automatic door (opens when sensor detects person) | Self-driving car (tracks other vehicles even when out of sight) |

*(1 mark for memory distinction, 1 mark for world model distinction, 1 mark for examples)*

---

### Question 2
**What is the role of a utility function in a utility-based agent? Why is it considered more powerful than a goal-based agent?** **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A **utility function** $U: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ maps each state $s$ to a real number representing its *desirability*. The agent selects the action that **maximizes expected utility**:

$$
a^* = \arg\max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \cdot U(s')
$$

It is more powerful than a goal-based agent because:
1. It handles **conflicting goals** by combining them into a single scalar.
2. It supports **trade-offs** when goals cannot all be achieved.
3. It provides a measure of **success probability** rather than binary success/failure.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for MEU equation, 1 mark for advantages)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Module 1 — Internal Choice Pattern: Answer ANY ONE from Q.A and Q.B]**

---

### Question A: 14 Marks

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the structure and working of a **model-based reflex agent**. Discuss how it differs from a simple reflex agent in handling partially observable environments. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the concept of a **utility-based agent** with a neat diagram. Derive the Maximum Expected Utility (MEU) principle and solve the following:

*An agent must choose between two actions $A$ and $B$ in state $s$.*  
- *Action A leads to state $s_1$ with probability 0.8 (utility 200) and state $s_2$ with probability 0.2 (utility 50).*  
- *Action B leads to state $s_3$ with probability 0.6 (utility 250) and state $s_4$ with probability 0.4 (utility 100).*  
*Which action should a rational agent take?* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** A model-based reflex agent maintains an *internal state* that encodes unobserved aspects of the world using a *world model* (transition + sensor model) and a *percept history*.
2. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.3 diagram (Sensors → State Update using World Model & History → Condition-Action Rules → Actuators).
3. **Working steps (2 marks):** Percept received → previous state combined with new percept via update rule → new internal state generated → rule matched → action selected.
4. **Difference from simple reflex in partial observability (2 marks):** Simple reflex has no memory and cannot reason about unseen state aspects; model-based agent *predicts* unobserved variables (e.g., speed of a car that is momentarily occluded) using its world model, allowing rational action in partial observability.

**Model Solution (b):**

1. **Definition + Diagram (2 marks):** A utility-based agent uses a utility function $U(s)$ to evaluate the *desirability* of states and selects actions that maximize *expected utility*. Refer to Section 4.5 diagram.

2. **Derivation of MEU (3 marks):** The agent computes:
$$
a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}[U \mid s, a] = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')
$$

3. **Numerical Solution (2 marks):**

$$
\mathbb{E}[U \mid s, A] = (0.8)(200) + (0.2)(50) = 160 + 10 = 170
$$

$$
\mathbb{E}[U \mid s, B] = (0.6)(250) + (0.4)(100) = 150 + 40 = 190
$$

Since $190 > 170$, the rational agent should choose **Action B**.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Define an **agent** formally. Explain the **simple reflex agent** with a block diagram. Under what conditions does a simple reflex agent become irrational? Illustrate with a suitable example. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare **goal-based** and **utility-based** agents. For a self-driving car navigating through traffic, explain which agent structure is most suitable and justify your answer using the PEAS framework. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Formal definition (1 mark):** An agent is a system that perceives its environment via sensors and acts upon it via actuators; formally represented as $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$.
2. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.2 diagram (Sensors → Rule Base → Action).
3. **Working (2 marks):** Percept received → matched against condition-action rules → first matching rule fires → action executed via actuator.
4. **Irrationality conditions (2 marks):** A simple reflex agent is irrational when the environment is *partially observable* (e.g., a self-driving car that slams brakes the moment it sees a plastic bag — it cannot reason that a bag is harmless because it has no memory of past frames showing it is lightweight).

**Model Solution (b):**

1. **Comparison (3 marks):** Goal-based uses a binary *goal-test* (achieved or not); utility-based uses a continuous utility function allowing trade-offs. Goal-based may select any goal-achieving action; utility-based selects the *optimal* one considering multiple criteria.
2. **PEAS for self-driving car (3 marks):**
   - **Performance:** Safety, speed, comfort, fuel economy
   - **Environment:** Roads, traffic, weather, pedestrians
   - **Actuators:** Steering, brakes, accelerator, indicators
   - **Sensors:** Cameras, LiDAR, GPS, radar
3. **Justification (1 mark):** A **utility-based agent** is most suitable because it can balance *conflicting* performance criteria (safety vs. speed vs. comfort) and handle *uncertain* outcomes (e.g., a pedestrian *might* cross the road).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Conflating "goal" and "utility"** — A goal is *binary*; a utility is *continuous*. Many students write vague answers losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Missing the world model block in the diagram** — For a model-based agent, the block diagram *must* show the *World Model* feeding into the State Update. Drawing only a state box costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Not showing the equation** $a^* = \arg\max_a \mathbb{E}[U \mid s, a]$ in utility-based questions — Without this, examiners deduct a full mark.
> 4. **Writing a reflex agent can work in partial observability** — This is *false*. Reflex agents are irrational in such environments.
> 5. **Skipping the percept history** — Always mention "history" explicitly when discussing model-based agents.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ☐ An **agent** is a sensor–actuator system with $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$.
- ☐ **Simple Reflex:** rule-based, no memory, suitable only for *fully observable* environments.
- ☐ **Model-Based:** maintains *internal state* using a *world model* (transition + sensor model) and *percept history*; handles *partial observability*.
- ☐ **Goal-Based:** adds an *explicit goal*; uses *search* and *planning*; future-oriented.
- ☐ **Utility-Based:** adds a *utility function* $U(s)$; uses **Maximum Expected Utility (MEU)** principle.
- ☐ The order is **increasing rationality** and **increasing computational cost**.
- ☐ The MEU formula is $a^* = \arg\max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')$.
- ☐ **PEAS** stands for Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors — apply it to every agent example.
- ☐ **Real-world examples to remember:**
  - Thermostat → Simple Reflex
  - Self-driving (state-aware) → Model-Based
  - GPS Navigator → Goal-Based
  - Stock trading bot / Chess engine → Utility-Based
- ☐ Always draw the **block diagram** in long-answer questions — it carries 2–3 marks easily.
- ☐ Reflex agents **fail in partial observability**; this is a frequently tested KTU point.
- ☐ The **transition model** $P(s' \mid s, a)$ and **sensor model** $P(p \mid s)$ together constitute the agent's *world model*.
- ☐ **Utility > Goal** when there are *conflicting objectives* or *uncertain outcomes*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Problem-solving agents: problem formulation and agent-based problem solving
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT502 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Problem-solving agents: problem formulation and agent-based problem solving

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Agent**: An *agent* is anything that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through **sensors** and acting upon that environment through **actuators**. A *rational agent* is one that does the right thing — it selects an action that maximizes its performance measure, given the evidence provided by its percept sequence and whatever built-in knowledge it has (Russell & Norvig, AIMA framework — KTU PCCAT502 Module 1 baseline).

**Problem-Solving Agent**: A *problem-solving agent* is a specific class of **goal-based agent** that, when faced with a complex decision, employs **atomic representations** of the world state and uses **search** as its primary computational engine to find a sequence of actions (a *plan*) that leads from an initial state to a goal state.

**Problem Formulation**: *Problem formulation* is the precise process of converting a high-level, informal, real-world goal into a well-defined **mathematical / abstract problem** that can be tackled by a search algorithm. The output of formulation is a tuple of five components: the **initial state**, the **actions** available, the **transition model**, the **goal test**, and the **path-cost function**.

**Agent-Based Problem Solving**: *Agent-based problem solving* refers to the closed-loop cycle in which the agent (1) **formulates** the goal as a problem, (2) **searches** for an action sequence (a solution) that achieves the goal, (3) **executes** the actions one-by-one in the real (or simulated) environment, and (4) **monitors** outcomes — re-formulating if the world deviates from the model.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Anchor Concept**: Every search-based AI system begins with *problem formulation*. Without an explicitly formulated problem, there is **nothing to search over**. This is why Module 1 of PCCAT502 anchors on formulation before introducing BFS, DFS, UCS, A*, etc., in later modules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCCAT502 Module 1)**: The expected outcome of this topic is the ability to (a) **identify** the five components of a well-defined problem for a given scenario, (b) **distinguish** the four canonical agent architectures, and (c) **trace** the perceive–formulate–search–execute loop for a problem-solving agent.

### 1.2 Intuition: The GPS Analogy

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi (CUSAT)** to **Thiruvananthapuram (KTU HQ)**. Your brain performs exactly the cycle a problem-solving agent performs:

| Human Driver Action | Agent Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Decide *where* you currently are | Read **initial state** from sensors |
| Recall *what roads* connect cities | Consult **action set** (successor function) |
| Mentally *try* route options | Run **search** through state space |
| Pick the *fastest* path | Optimize over **path cost** |
| Actually *drive* and *reroute* on traffic | **Execute** actions, then re-formulate |

Just as the GPS does not physically drive the car — it *plans* and *advises* — a problem-solving agent **separates deliberation (search) from execution (action)**. The intelligence lives in the formulation and the search, not in muscle-power.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Atomic, KTU-Boarded)

- **Completeness**: Does the algorithm always find a solution if one exists?
- **Optimality**: Does it find the lowest-cost solution?
- **Time Complexity**: Often measured in **nodes generated / expanded**.
- **Space Complexity**: Often measured in **maximum nodes held in memory**.

> [!NOTE]
> These four metrics are formally introduced here because they will be re-applied to every search algorithm (BFS, DFS, UCS, IDS, A*) in subsequent KTU modules. Memorize the *names*, not the values, at this stage.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Agent–Environment Loop (Operative Mechanism)

Every agent — biological, robotic, or software — operates inside a **perception–action loop**. For a *problem-solving* agent, this loop is augmented with a *deliberation* phase that hosts the search.

The loop, in order:

1. **Perceive** the current world state $s$ via sensors.
2. **Formulate** a problem: encode $s$ as the *initial state*, define legal *actions*, define the *goal test*.
3. **Search** the abstract state space for a sequence of actions $\langle a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n \rangle$ such that applying them leads to a goal state.
4. **Execute** the first action $a_1$ in the real environment.
5. **Observe** the new state $s'$ and loop back to Step 2 (re-formulating if reality disagrees with the model).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The agent does *not* pre-compute the entire plan and then blindly act. The execute–observe–re-formulate cycle is what makes the agent **robust to model error** — the single most important contrast with pure offline planning.

### 2.2 The PEAS Descriptor (Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors)

KTU examiners frequently ask for a **PEAS description** of a given AI system. It is a *first-principle* characterization of any agent.

| Component | Question it Answers | Example (Self-Driving Car) |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance** | How is success measured? | Safety, speed, comfort, fuel economy |
| **Environment** | What is the world? | Roads, traffic, weather, pedestrians |
| **Actuators** | How does it act? | Steering, accelerator, brake, signal lights |
| **Sensors** | How does it perceive? | Cameras, LiDAR, GPS, accelerometers |

### 2.3 The Four Canonical Agent Architectures (Increasing Capability)

| # | Type | Core Mechanism | When Used in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Simple Reflex** | Condition–action rules ($S \rightarrow A$) | Stateless, fully observable, simple env. |
| 2 | **Model-Based Reflex** | Maintains internal *world model* | Partially observable environments |
| 3 | **Goal-Based** | Searches to satisfy a *goal state* | Planning, navigation, puzzle-solving |
| 4 | **Utility-Based** | Maximizes a *utility function* over states | Trade-offs under uncertainty |

> [!NOTE]
> A **Problem-Solving Agent is a Goal-Based Agent that happens to use search**. The "goal" is binary (reached / not reached); later, utility-based agents generalize this with continuous preferences.

### 2.4 The Five Components of a Well-Defined Problem (⭐ KTU High-Yield)

This is the **central formal result** of the topic. A problem is fully specified by a **5-tuple**:

$$
P \;=\; \bigl\langle S, s_0, A(s), RESULT(s, a), GOAL\text{-}TEST(s), g(p) \bigr\rangle
$$

| Symbol | Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $S$ | **State Space** | The (often implicit) set of all reachable configurations |
| $s_0$ | **Initial State** | Where the agent starts |
| $A(s)$ | **Actions / Successor Function** | Set of legal actions in state $s$ |
| $RESULT(s, a)$ | **Transition Model** | The state reached by doing $a$ in $s$ |
| $GOAL\text{-}TEST(s)$ | **Goal Test** | Boolean check: is $s$ a goal state? |
| $g(p)$ | **Path-Cost Function** | Numerical cost of a path $p = \langle s_0, a_1, s_1, \ldots \rangle$ |

A **solution** to problem $P$ is a sequence of actions $\langle a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n \rangle$ whose cumulative execution transforms $s_0$ into a state $s_n$ that satisfies $GOAL\text{-}TEST(s_n) = \text{true}$. An **optimal solution** is one of minimum path cost.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master the table below — at least one of these quantities appears in **every KTU board problem** on this topic.

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Path cost (additive) | $g(p)$ | $g(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c(s_{i-1}, a_i, s_i)$ | Steps or weighted cost |
| Step cost | $c(s, a, s')$ | Numeric (often $\geq 0$) | Given per transition |
| Goal test | $GOAL(s)$ | Boolean $\{0, 1\}$ | $\{0,1\}$ only |
| Number of nodes in a uniform tree of depth $d$ and branching factor $b$ | $N(d)$ | $N(d) \;=\; 1 + b + b^{2} + \cdots + b^{d} \;=\; \dfrac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$ | Geometric series |
| Branching factor (effective) | $b$ | $b \;\approx\; \vert A(s) \vert_{\text{avg}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Effective depth | $d$ | $d \;=\; \min_{p \in \text{Solutions}} \vert p \vert$ | Number of edges |
| Closed-form geometric sum | $S_{n}$ | $S_{n} = b^{0} + b^{1} + \cdots + b^{n}$ | $S_{n} = \dfrac{b^{n+1} - 1}{b - 1}$ for $b \neq 1$ |

> **Note on Engineering Utility**: Problem formulation is the bridge between *domain expertise* (medicine, logistics, robotics) and *algorithmic search*. Every real-world search engine (Google Maps, airline routing, VLSI layout) begins with the same five-component abstraction. Mastering it is **transferable across every engineering discipline**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — The 8-Puzzle (Canonical KTU Problem)

The **8-puzzle** is a $3 \times 3$ grid containing eight numbered tiles and one blank. Tiles adjacent to the blank may be slid into the blank's position. The goal is to reach a specified ordering, typically:

$$
s_{\text{goal}} \;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 2 & 3 \\
4 & 5 & 6 \\
7 & 8 & \_
\end{bmatrix}
$$

We now **exhaustively derive** the five-component formulation.

**Step 1 — Initial State $s_0$**: Given concrete configuration, e.g.

$$
s_0 \;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
2 & 8 & 3 \\
1 & 6 & 4 \\
7 & \_ & 5
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — State Space $S$**: All permutations of nine symbols such that the blank is treated as a tile. The cardinality is

$$
\vert S \vert \;=\; \frac{9!}{2} \;=\; 181{,}440
$$

(Division by 2 because swapping the blank with itself is a no-op; this is the *effective* state space after accounting for parity reachability.)

**Step 3 — Actions $A(s)$**: For any state, the legal actions are: $\{$Up, Down, Left, Right$\}$ applied to the blank, but only those that keep the blank inside the $3\times 3$ grid. If the blank is in the top row, **Up** is illegal. Etc.

**Step 4 — Transition Model $RESULT(s, a)$**: $RESULT(s, a)$ returns a new state identical to $s$ except the blank and the adjacent tile in direction $a$ have swapped positions.

**Step 5 — Goal Test $GOAL(s)$**:

$$
GOAL(s) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
\text{true} & \text{if } s = s_{\text{goal}} \text{ as matrices} \\
\text{false} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 6 — Path Cost $g(p)$**: The standard convention is **unit cost per move**, so for a path of length $n$,

$$
g(p) \;=\; n \cdot c_0 \quad \text{with} \quad c_0 \;=\; 1
$$

Hence optimal solution = **shortest sequence of moves** = minimum $n$.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Romania Tour (Classic AIMA Path-Finding)

Consider driving from **Arad** to **Bucharest** on the Romania road map. Formulation:

- **Initial state**: $s_0 = \text{In(Arad)}$.
- **Actions**: $A(s) = \{ \text{driving to a city adjacent to } s \}$.
- **Transition model**: $RESULT(s, a) = \text{the city reached by action } a$.
- **Goal test**: $GOAL(s) = (s = \text{In(Bucharest)})$.
- **Path cost**: $g(p) = $ sum of road distances (km) along path $p$.

**Derivation of search tree size for branching factor $b = 4$ and depth $d = 6$**:

$$
N(6) \;=\; 1 + 4 + 4^{2} + 4^{3} + 4^{4} + 4^{5} + 4^{6}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4^{0} &= 1 \\
4^{1} &= 4 \\
4^{2} &= 16 \\
4^{3} &= 64 \\
4^{4} &= 256 \\
4^{5} &= 1{,}024 \\
4^{6} &= 4{,}096
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum them:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N(6) &= 1 + 4 + 16 + 64 + 256 + 1{,}024 + 4{,}096 \\
&= 5 + 16 + 64 + 256 + 1{,}024 + 4{,}096 \\
&= 21 + 64 + 256 + 1{,}024 + 4{,}096 \\
&= 85 + 256 + 1{,}024 + 4{,}096 \\
&= 341 + 1{,}024 + 4{,}096 \\
&= 1{,}365 + 4{,}096 \\
&= 5{,}461
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cross-check with closed-form**:

$$
N(6) \;=\; \frac{4^{7} - 1}{4 - 1} \;=\; \frac{16{,}384 - 1}{3} \;=\; \frac{16{,}383}{3} \;=\; 5{,}461 \quad \checkmark
$$

The closed-form and the direct summation agree. **Both** forms are accepted by KTU examiners.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing $s_0$ and Abstraction Level

| Real-World Element | Concrete Representation | Abstract Representation (Search-Friendly) |
|---|---|---|
| GPS position | $(lat, lon, alt)$ | City node in graph |
| Tile in 8-puzzle | Pixel array of $3\times 3$ image | Integer vector of 9 entries |
| Vacuum world | $(RoomA_{dirt}, RoomB_{dirt}, AgentLoc)$ | Two-bit state |

> [!NOTE]
> **Abstraction Principle (KTU Board Favourite)**: A good formulation (1) **keeps** all constraints necessary for a valid solution, (2) **drops** irrelevant details (paint color of the car, exact GPS altitude), and (3) **makes** the state small enough to enumerate. Over-abstraction hides solutions; under-abstraction explodes the state space.

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — The Vacuum World (Tiny but Testable)

A two-square world; agent at one of two cells; either cell may be dirty/clean. The total state space size is **8**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert S \vert &= 2_{\text{(agent loc)}} \times 2_{\text{(A dirt)}} \times 2_{\text{(B dirt)}} \\
&= 8
\end{aligned}
$$

Explicit states (with agent-location digit and dirt bits):

$$
S \;=\; \{ 1A0,\; 1A1,\; 1B0,\; 1B1,\; 2A0,\; 2A1,\; 2B0,\; 2B1 \}
$$

- **Initial state**: e.g. $s_0 = 1A1$ (agent in A, both dirty).
- **Actions**: $\{ \text{Left}, \text{Right}, \text{Suck}, \text{NoOp} \}$.
- **Goal test**: Both cells clean.
- **Path cost**: 1 per action.

This 8-state example is the *minimum non-trivial* problem KTU may ask in Part A.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Agent–Environment Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV[Environment] -->|Percepts via Sensors| SENS[Sensors]
    SENS --> PERC[Perception Module]
    PERC --> STATE[Current State s]
    STATE --> FORM[Problem Formulation]
    FORM --> SEARCH[Search Engine]
    GOAL[Goal Input] --> FORM
    SEARCH --> PLAN[Action Sequence Plan]
    PLAN --> ACT[Actuators]
    ACT -->|Actions| ENV
    SEARCH -.->|Feedback Re-formulation| FORM
```

### 4.2 Architecture of a Problem-Solving Agent (Internal Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input: Percept] --> B[Update State Model]
    B --> C{Goal Achieved?}
    C -- Yes --> Z[Idle / Terminate]
    C -- No --> D[Formulate Problem P]
    D --> E[Search for Solution]
    E --> F{Solution Found?}
    F -- No --> Y[Return Failure]
    F -- Yes --> G[Select Next Action a1]
    G --> H[Execute a1 via Actuator]
    H --> A
```

### 4.3 Generic State-Space Search Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R((Root s0)) --> N1((s1))
    R --> N2((s2))
    R --> N3((s3))
    N1 --> N1A((s1a))
    N1 --> N1B((s1b))
    N2 --> N2A((s2a))
    N3 --> N3A((s3a))
    N3 --> N3B((s3b))
    N1A --> G(((GOAL)))
    style G fill:#9f9,stroke:#060
    style R fill:#ff9,stroke:#660
```

**Reading the diagram**: The root is the initial state $s_0$. Each edge is an action. Leaves are frontier states. A leaf marked **GOAL** is a solution. The **depth** of the leftmost goal node is the optimal path length (under unit cost).

### 4.4 Comparison Block — Agent Types in One View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Stimulus] --> A1[Simple Reflex Agent]
    A1 --> A2[Action]

    B0[Stimulus] --> B1[Model-Based Reflex]
    B2[Internal World Model] --> B1
    B1 --> B2A[Action]

    C0[Stimulus] --> C1[Goal-Based Agent]
    C2[Goal Description] --> C1
    C1 --> C3[Search / Plan]
    C3 --> C3A[Action]

    D0[Stimulus] --> D1[Utility-Based Agent]
    D2[Utility Function] --> D1
    D1 --> D3[Maximize Expected Utility]
    D3 --> D3A[Action]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **problem-solving agent is a Goal-Based agent** in this taxonomy. The Search/Plan block in the third row is exactly what we will study across Modules 2–4 of PCCAT502.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: Across past KTU university exam cycles, the three most common **mark-losing mistakes** in this topic are:
> 1. **Confusing the problem's state space $S$ with the agent's belief state** — the state space is the *set of possible worlds*, the belief state is what the agent currently knows.
> 2. **Forgetting the path-cost function $g(p)$** when defining the problem — only four components instead of five will cost up to **2 marks**.
> 3. **Describing the agent as "intelligent"** instead of naming the **PEAS** descriptors — vague descriptions receive **partial credit only**.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define a *rational agent* and a *problem-solving agent*. How does the latter differ from a simple reflex agent?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **rational agent** is one that, for every possible percept sequence, selects an action that *maximizes its expected performance measure*, given its built-in knowledge and the evidence from its sensors. A **problem-solving agent** is a *goal-based* agent that, when confronted with a complex situation, abstracts the environment into a discrete state space and uses **search** to find a *sequence* of actions leading from the initial state to a goal state.

The key difference from a **simple reflex agent** is that the simple reflex agent uses a direct *condition → action* mapping (no memory, no lookahead), whereas the problem-solving agent *deliberates*: it models the world, projects the consequences of actions into the future via search, and only then commits to an action. The simple reflex is **fast but myopic**; the problem-solver is **slower but goal-directed** and capable of planning. *(3 marks — 1 mark per comparison point: definition, plan use, contrast.)*

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] List the **five components** of a well-defined problem. For the *8-puzzle*, state at least three.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five components are: (i) **initial state**, (ii) **actions / successor function**, (iii) **transition model**, (iv) **goal test**, (v) **path-cost function**.

For the 8-puzzle:
- **Initial state** $s_0$: any specific $3\times 3$ tile configuration, e.g. tiles in order $\{2,8,3,1,6,4,7,5\}$ with blank in the centre.
- **Actions** $A(s)$: Up, Down, Left, Right applied to the blank, restricted to stay inside the grid.
- **Goal test**: current configuration equals the canonical goal matrix (1,2,3 / 4,5,6 / 7,8,blank).
- **Transition model**: sliding the blank swaps it with the adjacent tile.
- **Path cost**: number of moves (unit cost per slide). *(3 marks — 1.5 for the five-component list, 1.5 for the 8-puzzle specifics.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> KTU Module 1 ESE internal choice structure: choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **PEAS descriptor** for a *medical diagnosis system* used in a hospital. Identify the agent type and justify.

**(b)** [7 Marks] **Formulate** the *8-puzzle* as a well-defined problem. State the state-space size, the maximum branching factor, and the maximum depth of any solution.

---

##### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — PEAS for Medical Diagnosis

| PEAS Element | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Performance** | Patient's health outcome — minimize misdiagnosis, minimize cost, maximize recovery rate, respect patient dignity |
| **Environment** | Hospital, patient (with symptoms, history, test results), medical staff, time-varying vitals |
| **Actuators** | Display of questions, test-order requests, prescriptions, referrals, alerts |
| **Sensors** | Keyboard / voice input from patient, electronic health records, lab results, imaging |

**Agent Type**: **Goal-Based / Utility-Based hybrid**. It is goal-based because there exists a clear target (a confirmed diagnosis). It is also utility-based because multiple competing trade-offs (cost vs. thoroughness, time vs. accuracy) exist, requiring a utility function. **Justification**: A simple reflex would simply respond to the latest symptom; the diagnosis requires a model of the patient built over time and a search over possible disease hypotheses — exactly the conditions under which a problem-solving (goal-based) agent excels. *(7 marks: 4 for PEAS table, 1 for agent type, 2 for justification.)*

---

##### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 8-Puzzle Formulation

| Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| **States** | Each state describes the location of each of the 8 tiles and the blank in one of the 9 cells |
| **Initial State** | Any reachable configuration (parity-restricted) |
| **Actions** | Move blank Up, Down, Left, Right — illegal moves are dropped at the boundary |
| **Transition Model** | Returns a new state with the blank and the chosen neighbour swapped |
| **Goal Test** | Configuration equals the goal matrix (1,2,3 / 4,5,6 / 7,8,blank) |
| **Path Cost** | Number of moves (unit step cost) |

**State-space size**:

$$
\vert S \vert \;=\; \frac{9!}{2} \;=\; 181{,}440
$$

**Maximum branching factor**:

$$
b_{\max} \;=\; 4 \quad \text{(interior blank position)}
$$

**Maximum solution depth**: For the hardest initial states, the optimal solution requires **31 moves** (this is a classical AIMA result):

$$
d_{\max} \;=\; 31
$$

Hence the *theoretical worst-case* search tree contains up to

$$
N(31) \;=\; \frac{4^{32} - 1}{3} \;\approx\; 2.30 \times 10^{19} \text{ nodes}
$$

This massive size is precisely why **informed search (A\*) with a good heuristic** is used in practice — blind search is infeasible beyond depth ~20. *(7 marks: 3 for full formulation, 2 for state-space calculation, 2 for branching factor and depth.)*

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the **perception–action loop** of a *problem-solving agent*. With a block diagram, show how the **deliberation phase** differs from the **execution phase**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider the **Romania tour** problem from Arad to Bucharest. Define its 5-component formulation. If the straight-line distance to Bucharest is used as a heuristic $h(n)$, list $h$ for the cities Arad, Sibiu, Rimnicu Vilcea, Fagaras, Pitesti, and Bucharest.

---

##### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — Perception–Action Loop

The **perception–action loop** of a problem-solving agent executes the following sequence repeatedly:

1. **Percept acquisition** — sensors return a percept.
2. **State inference** — agent updates its internal *world model* to reflect the new percept.
3. **Goal check** — is the current state a goal state? If yes, terminate.
4. **Problem formulation** — define the 5-tuple $\langle S, s_0, A(s), RESULT, GOAL, g \rangle$ using the inferred state as $s_0$.
5. **Search** — call a search algorithm (BFS, UCS, A*, etc.) to obtain a plan $\pi = \langle a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n \rangle$.
6. **Execution** — perform $a_1$, observe outcome, then *either* continue with $a_2$ (if world cooperates) **or** re-formulate (if $a_1$ had an unmodelled effect).

**Deliberation vs Execution — Block Diagram**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DELIBERATION
        P1[Percept] --> P2[State Inference]
        P2 --> P3[Formulate]
        P3 --> P4[Search]
        P4 --> P5[Plan a1..an]
    end
    subgraph EXECUTION
        E1[Pick a1] --> E2[Actuator fires]
        E2 --> E3[Environment responds]
    end
    P5 --> E1
    E3 --> P1
```

> The **deliberation phase** is *internal and symbolic* (no effect on the world). The **execution phase** is *external and physical* (changes the world). The dotted feedback arrow from E3 to P1 represents the re-perception that closes the loop. *(7 marks: 4 for loop description, 3 for diagram with proper distinction.)*

---

##### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — Romania Tour

**5-Component Formulation**:

| Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| **State** $s$ | A *city*, denoted $\text{In}(X)$ for city $X$ |
| **Initial State** $s_0$ | $\text{In(Arad)}$ |
| **Actions** $A(s)$ | Drive to any city directly connected to $s$ by a road |
| **Transition Model** | Driving via action $a$ moves the agent to the destination city |
| **Goal Test** | $s = \text{In(Bucharest)}$ |
| **Path Cost** $g(p)$ | Sum of road distances in km along path $p$ |

**Heuristic values (straight-line distance to Bucharest)** — these are the canonical AIMA values:

| City | $h(n)$ in km |
|---|---|
| Arad | **366** |
| Sibiu | **253** |
| Rimnicu Vilcea | **193** |
| Fagaras | **176** |
| Pitesti | **100** |
| Bucharest | **0** |

> The heuristic is **admissible** because the straight-line distance is always a *lower bound* on actual road distance (no road can be shorter than the crow flies). It is also **consistent** for this problem, satisfying the triangle inequality. *(7 marks: 3 for formulation, 2 for heuristic list, 2 for admissibility argument.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Agent** = Sensors + Actuators + Performance function. The rational agent chooses the action maximizing expected performance.
- **PEAS** = Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors. Always present a PEAS table when introducing an agent.
- **Four agent types** (in increasing capability): Simple Reflex → Model-Based Reflex → Goal-Based → Utility-Based. A problem-solving agent is **Goal-Based**.
- **Problem formulation** = the 5-tuple $\langle S, s_0, A(s), RESULT, GOAL\text{-}TEST, g \rangle$. All five are required for a *well-defined* problem.
- **Solution** = action sequence from $s_0$ to a goal state. **Optimal solution** = solution of minimum path cost $g(p)$.
- **State-space size** of a tree with branching factor $b$ and depth $d$ is
  $$N(d) = \dfrac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}.$$
- **Abstraction** is the engineer's secret weapon: drop irrelevant detail, keep constraints that affect solvability.
- **Search tree vs state space**: the *state space* is the abstract graph of all reachable states; the *search tree* records paths actually explored by the algorithm.
- **Performance metrics** (recurring in every module): Completeness, Optimality, Time Complexity, Space Complexity.
- The **perception–action loop** is closed by re-perception; the agent *re-formulates* when the world disagrees with its model.
- **Common KTU pitfall** (avoid!): stating four components instead of five. Always include **path cost $g(p)$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Reference Definition Block** (paste into your formula sheet):
> - Problem-Solving Agent = Goal-Based Agent + Search
> - Well-Defined Problem = $\langle S, s_0, A, RESULT, GOAL, g \rangle$
> - Solution = path $p$ with $GOAL(p.\text{end}) = \text{true}$
> - Optimal Solution = $\arg\min_{p \in \text{Solutions}} g(p)$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of problem-solving scenarios
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT502 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Examples of problem-solving scenarios

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **problem-solving scenario** in Artificial Intelligence refers to a well-defined computational task that can be formally represented using the **Problem Formulation Tuple**:

$$P = (S, S_0, A, G, C, T)$$

where:
- $S$ = Set of all possible **states** in the problem space
- $S_0 \in S$ = The **initial state** from which the agent begins
- $A$ = Set of permissible **actions** / operators that move the agent between states
- $G$ = Set of **goal states** that represent successful task completion
- $C$ = **Cost function** $c(s, a, s')$ that assigns numerical expense to state transitions
- $T$ = **Transition model** $T(s, a) \rightarrow s'$ that defines the result of an action

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Insight:** In Module 1, you are expected to recognize that *almost every AI problem* can be framed using this 5-tuple/6-tuple formulation. Whether it is a chess game, route navigation, or the classic 8-puzzle, the underlying skeleton remains identical — only the *contents* change.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a **maze** at the entrance, and you want to reach the exit. Before you even start walking, you need to know:
1. **Where am I now?** → Initial State
2. **Where is the exit?** → Goal State
3. **What moves can I make?** → Actions (turn left, go straight, turn right)
4. **What will my position be after each move?** → Transition Model
5. **How much energy does each step cost?** → Cost Function

Every "problem-solving scenario" in AI is essentially a **maze** at heart — some mazes are small (a 3×3 grid), and some are astronomically huge (chess has $10^{120}$ states). AI problem-solving is the science of finding the exit efficiently.

### 1.3 Classification of Problem-Solving Scenarios

According to KTU 2024 Module 1 outcomes, problem-solving scenarios are classified into two broad buckets:

| Category | Characteristics | Examples |
|----------|----------------|----------|
| **Toy Problems** | Concise, abstract, used for pedagogy & algorithm benchmarking | 8-Puzzle, 8-Queens, Tower of Hanoi, Water Jug |
| **Real-World Problems** | Complex, domain-specific, used to solve genuine engineering/business challenges | Route Finding, VLSI Layout, Robot Navigation, Protein Design, TSP |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The textbook distinguishes **toy vs. real-world** problems explicitly in Module 1. Students are frequently tested with a 3-mark question asking for a *one-line difference* between toy and real-world problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Generic Problem-Solving Pipeline (Applies to ALL Examples Below)

Any AI problem-solver executes the following **logical pipeline**:

1. **Goal Formulation** — Define what success looks like (e.g., "all 8 queens placed safely").
2. **Problem Formulation** — Decide what *states*, *actions*, and *cost* are relevant (abstraction step).
3. **Search** — Generate a sequence of actions leading from $S_0$ to a goal $g \in G$.
4. **Execution** — Carry out the chosen action sequence in the real (or simulated) world.

### 2.2 Canonical Toy Problems Studied in Module 1

#### **Example A — The Vacuum World**

A 2-room environment. The agent (vacuum cleaner) must clean both rooms.

| Component | Definition |
|-----------|------------|
| States $S$ | 8 possible states (2 rooms × dirt status × agent location) |
| $S_0$ | Any of the 8 states |
| Actions $A$ | `Left`, `Right`, `Suck`, `NoOp` |
| Goal $G$ | Both rooms clean (states 7 and 8) |
| Cost $c$ | 1 per action (unit cost) |

#### **Example B — The 8-Puzzle**

A 3×3 grid with 8 numbered tiles and one blank space. Tiles slide into the blank.

| Component | Definition |
|-----------|------------|
| States $S$ | $9! / 2 = 181{,}440$ reachable states |
| $S_0$ | Any solvable configuration |
| Actions $A$ | Move {Blank Up, Down, Left, Right} |
| Goal $G$ | Tiles arranged in numerical order |
| Cost $c$ | 1 per slide |
| Branching factor $b$ | ≈ 2 (corner), ≈ 3 (edge), ≈ 4 (center) |

#### **Example C — The 8-Queens Problem**

Place 8 queens on an 8×8 chessboard such that no two queens attack each other.

- **State formulation variants:**
  - *Incremental*: Place queens one column at a time.
  - *Complete-state*: All 8 queens placed; only check for safety.
- **Actions**: Place a queen in the next empty column (at most 8 actions per column).
- **Goal**: 8 queens on board, pairwise non-attacking.
- **Search space**: $8^8 = 16{,}777{,}216$ complete-state configurations.
- **Solutions exist**: **92 distinct solutions** (including rotations/reflections → 12 fundamental).

#### **Example D — The Water Jug Problem**

Two jugs of capacities $C_1$ and $C_2$ liters. Goal: measure exactly $D$ liters.

| Operator | Rule |
|----------|------|
| Fill | Fill a jug to its full capacity |
| Empty | Empty a jug's contents |
| Pour | Pour from one jug until source is empty OR destination is full |

**State** = $(x, y)$ where $x \in [0, C_1]$, $y \in [0, C_2]$.

#### **Example E — Tower of Hanoi**

Three pegs and $n$ disks of decreasing size. Move all disks from source peg to destination, obeying:
1. Only one disk moved at a time.
2. Only the top disk on any peg may be moved.
3. A larger disk may never rest on a smaller one.

**Optimal moves** = $2^n - 1$.

For $n = 3$: $2^3 - 1 = 7$ moves.

#### **Example F — Missionaries and Cannibals**

3 missionaries and 3 cannibals must cross a river using a boat carrying ≤ 2 people. Constraint: missionaries cannot be outnumbered on either bank (else eaten).

- **States**: $(M, C, B)$ where $M, C \in [0,3]$ and $B \in \{L, R\}$.
- **Total valid states**: 16 reachable.

#### **Example G — Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)**

Given $n$ cities with pairwise distances $d(i,j)$, find the shortest tour that visits every city exactly once and returns to the start.

- **Search space**: $(n-1)!/2$ unique tours.
- **Complexity**: NP-hard (brute force infeasible for large $n$).

#### **Example H — Cryptarithmetic (e.g., `SEND + MORE = MONEY`)**

Replace each letter with a unique digit (0–9) such that the arithmetic sum holds.

- Constraint satisfaction problem (CSP).
- Key observation: `M` must equal **1** (carry from the leading column).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Problem | State Space Size | Branching Factor | Optimal Cost (where applicable) |
|---------|------------------|------------------|-------------------------------|
| Vacuum (2-cell) | **8** | **3** | 7 (worst case) |
| 8-Puzzle | **181,440** | **≈ 2.33** | Variable |
| 8-Queens | $8^8 = 1.68 \times 10^7$ | **8** | Goal cost = 0 (constraint satisfaction) |
| Tower of Hanoi ($n$) | $3^n$ | **3** | $2^n - 1$ |
| TSP ($n$ cities) | $(n-1)!/2$ | $n-1$ | Heuristic only |
| Missionaries & Cannibals | **16** | **≤ 5** | 11 moves |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Formulations

- **8-Puzzle mechanics** are reused in logistics optimization, warehouse robot path planning.
- **TSP solvers** are deployed in Amazon delivery routing, microchip fabrication (drill paths on PCBs).
- **Water Jug logic** underlies dosage-measuring devices, fuel-mixing controllers.
- **Cryptarithmetic solvers** underpin automated theorem provers and arithmetic circuit verification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Connect:** The same abstract `S, A, T, G, C` formulation used in the 8-puzzle also drives modern autonomous-vehicle motion planning — only the cost function changes (e.g., time, fuel, comfort).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Tower of Hanoi Minimum Moves

**Claim:** The minimum number of moves to transfer $n$ disks from peg A to peg C (using peg B) is $2^n - 1$.

**Proof by Mathematical Induction:**

Let $T(n)$ = minimum number of moves for $n$ disks.

**Base Case** ($n = 1$):
$$T(1) = 1 = 2^1 - 1 = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Inductive Step**: Assume $T(k) = 2^k - 1$ holds for $k$ disks. We show $T(k+1) = 2^{k+1} - 1$.

Step 1: To move the largest disk (bottom), we must first move the top $k$ disks out of the way.
$$\text{Moves required} = T(k) = 2^k - 1$$

Step 2: Move the largest disk from peg A to peg C.
$$\text{Moves required} = 1$$

Step 3: Move the $k$ disks back on top of the largest disk.
$$\text{Moves required} = T(k) = 2^k - 1$$

Total moves:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(k+1) &= T(k) + 1 + T(k) \\
       &= (2^k - 1) + 1 + (2^k - 1) \\
       &= 2 \cdot 2^k - 1 \\
       &= 2^{k+1} - 1 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: State-Space Complexity of 8-Puzzle

The 8-puzzle has 9 tiles but only 8 are movable + 1 blank. Total *placements*:

$$
\text{Total configurations} = 9! = 362{,}880
$$

However, the parity (even/odd permutation count) splits the state space into two **disconnected halves** of equal size, reachable only by sliding. Hence:

$$
\text{Reachable states} = \frac{9!}{2} = 181{,}440
$$

The branching factor $b$ at any state:

$$
b =
\begin{cases}
2, & \text{if blank is in a corner} \\
3, & \text{if blank is on an edge} \\
4, & \text{if blank is in the center}
\end{cases}
$$

**Effective branching factor:**
$$b_{\text{eff}} = \frac{4 + 3 \cdot 4 + 2 \cdot 4}{9} = \frac{4 + 12 + 8}{9} = \frac{24}{9} \approx 2.67$$

### 3.3 Solution Length for 8-Puzzle (Empirical Depth Distribution)

| Solution Depth $d$ | Number of States (approx.) |
|--------------------|---------------------------|
| 0 | 1 |
| 2 | 2 |
| 4 | ~12 |
| 8 | ~360 |
| 12 | ~6,400 |
| 16 | ~30,000 |
| 20 | ~38,000 |
| 22 | ~12,000 |
| 24 | ~3,000 |
| 26 | ~660 |
| 28 | ~120 |
| 30 | ~20 |
| 31 (max) | 1 |

This explains why 8-puzzle is the *canonical* toy benchmark — its difficulty is **tunable** by choosing different goal depths.

### 3.4 Missionaries & Cannibals — Reachability Check

Total valid state count:

$$
\text{Total combinations of } (M, C) = 4 \times 4 = 16
$$

But only states satisfying the **safety constraint** are valid:

$$
\text{Constraint:} \quad \big(m = 0 \;\lor\; m \geq c\big) \;\land\; \big(3-m = 0 \;\lor\; 3-m \geq 3-c\big)
$$

This filters out unsafe configurations (e.g., 1 missionary + 3 cannibals on one side).

### 3.5 TSP State-Space Growth

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Unique tours for } n \text{ cities} &= \frac{(n-1)!}{2} \\
n = 5 &: \frac{4!}{2} = 12 \\
n = 10 &: \frac{9!}{2} = 181{,}440 \\
n = 15 &: \frac{14!}{2} \approx 4.35 \times 10^{10} \\
n = 20 &: \frac{19!}{2} \approx 6.08 \times 10^{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **factorial growth** is why brute-force search is infeasible for moderate $n$, motivating heuristic/approximate AI methods (Module 3 onward).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Pattern:** KTU often asks: *"Why is brute-force search impractical for TSP with $n \geq 20$?"* — Quote the $(n-1)!/2$ growth and contrast with Moore's Law timescales.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Vacuum World State-Space Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["State 1: A_Dirty, B_Dirty, Vac_A"] -->|Suck| S3["State 3: A_Clean, B_Dirty, Vac_A"]
    S1 -->|Right| S2["State 2: A_Dirty, B_Dirty, Vac_B"]
    S2 -->|Suck| S4["State 4: A_Dirty, B_Clean, Vac_B"]
    S2 -->|Left| S1
    S3 -->|Right| S4
    S3 -->|NoOp| S3
    S4 -->|Left| S3
    S4 -->|Suck| S5["State 5: A_Clean, B_Clean, Vac_B"]
    S3 -->|Suck| S6["State 6: A_Clean, B_Clean, Vac_A"]
    S5 -->|NoOp| S5
    S6 -->|NoOp| S6
    style S5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style S6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
```

> *States 5 and 6 (green-bordered) are the two **goal states**.*

### 4.2 8-Puzzle State-Transition Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Init["Initial State: Tiles in arbitrary order"] -->|Slide Blank Up| Mid1["Intermediate Configuration A"]
    Init -->|Slide Blank Down| Mid2["Intermediate Configuration B"]
    Init -->|Slide Blank Left| Mid3["Intermediate Configuration C"]
    Init -->|Slide Blank Right| Mid4["Intermediate Configuration D"]
    Mid1 -->|Continue Search| Goal["Goal State: 1 2 3 / 4 5 6 / 7 8 _"]
    Mid2 -->|Continue Search| Goal
    Mid3 -->|Continue Search| Goal
    Mid4 -->|Continue Search| Goal
    Init -.->|h1 = misplaced tiles| Heur1["Heuristic h1"]
    Init -.->|h2 = Manhattan distance| Heur2["Heuristic h2"]
    style Goal fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.3 Tower of Hanoi Recursive Decomposition (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P3["Move 3 disks: A to C"] --> P2A["Move 2 disks: A to B"]
    P3 --> M3["Move disk 3: A to C"]
    P3 --> P2B["Move 2 disks: B to C"]
    P2A --> P1A["Move 1 disk: A to C"]
    P2A --> M2["Move disk 2: A to B"]
    P2A --> P1B["Move 1 disk: C to B"]
    P2B --> P1C["Move 1 disk: B to A"]
    P2B --> M2b["Move disk 2: B to C"]
    P2B --> P1D["Move 1 disk: A to C"]
    style P3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style M3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 TSP Tour Visualization (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    C1["City 1"] -->|d12| C2["City 2"]
    C2 -->|d23| C3["City 3"]
    C3 -->|d34| C4["City 4"]
    C4 -->|d45| C5["City 5"]
    C5 -->|d51| C1
    C1 -.->|Alternative Path| C3
    C2 -.->|Alternative Path| C4
    style C1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.5 Problem Classification Decision Block (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q["Is it a Problem-Solving Scenario?"] --> Q1["Are states fully observable?"]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2["Deterministic?"]
    Q1 -->|No| Partial["Partially Observable Problem"]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3["Discrete States?"]
    Q2 -->|No| Stoch["Stochastic Problem"]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Disc["Discrete Deterministic Toy Problem"]
    Q3 -->|No| Cont["Continuous Problem"]
    Disc --> Ex["Examples: 8-Puzzle, Hanoi, 8-Queens"]
    Stoch --> Ex2["Examples: Vacuum w/ slip, Backgammon"]
    Partial --> Ex3["Examples: Poker, Robot w/ sensors"]
    Cont --> Ex4["Examples: Driving, Helicopter Control"]
    style Disc fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style Cont fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between toy problems and real-world problems with two examples each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Toy Problems | Real-World Problems |
|--------|--------------|---------------------|
| Purpose | Pedagogical / research benchmark | Practical engineering deployment |
| Complexity | Low, abstract | High, domain-specific |
| Examples | 8-Puzzle, Tower of Hanoi, 8-Queens | Route Navigation, VLSI Layout, Robot Motion Planning, Protein Folding |
| State-space | Small, enumerable | Astronomical, often continuous |
| Algorithms sufficient | Brute-force / uninformed search often works | Requires heuristics, learning, approximation |

Toy problems are typically used to *illustrate* algorithm behavior; real-world problems are the *actual applications* AI must solve in industry.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the problem formulation tuple. List its components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **problem formulation** is the formal mathematical specification of an AI problem, expressed as the tuple:

$$
P = (S, S_0, A, G, C, T)
$$

Components:
1. **$S$**: Set of all reachable states.
2. **$S_0 \in S$**: The initial state where the agent starts.
3. **$A(s)$**: Set of legal actions available in state $s$.
4. **$G \subseteq S$**: Set of goal states.
5. **$c(s, a, s')$**: Step-cost function of moving from $s$ to $s'$ via action $a$.
6. **$T(s, a) \rightarrow s'$**: Transition model describing the result of an action.

---

### **PART B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Formulate the **8-Queens problem** as a state-space search problem. Define states, initial state, actions, goal test, and path cost. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **8-Puzzle problem** in detail. Draw its initial and goal state configuration. Explain why the branching factor is approximately 2.33. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) 8-Queens Formulation — 7 marks:**

- **State representation**: An arrangement of $k$ queens on the board, one per column, where $0 \leq k \leq 8$. *(1 mark)*
- **Initial state**: An empty board (no queens placed). *(1 mark)*
- **Actions**: Add a queen to any square in the leftmost empty column such that no existing queen attacks it. *(2 marks)*
- **Goal test**: 8 queens are on the board and no two attack each other. *(1 mark)*
- **Path cost**: Not relevant (we only care about existence of a goal). Zero. *(1 mark)*
- **Branching factor**: Up to 8 (one per row). *(0.5 mark)*
- **Total state space**: $8^8 = 16{,}777{,}216$ states (using incremental formulation). *(0.5 mark)*

**Important Note:** A *complete-state* formulation (all 8 queens placed at once) would require checking $C(64, 8) \approx 4.4 \times 10^9$ states — far less efficient than the incremental, column-by-column formulation.

**(b) 8-Puzzle Description — 7 marks:**

- **Description**: A 3×3 grid holds 8 numbered tiles (1–8) and one blank square. A tile adjacent to the blank can slide into it. The objective is to reach a specific target configuration (typically 1-2-3 on top, 4-5-6 in middle, 7-8-blank at bottom). *(2 marks)*
- **Initial State example**:
$$
\begin{bmatrix}
2 & 8 & 3 \\
1 & 6 & 4 \\
7 & \_ & 5
\end{bmatrix}
$$
*(1 mark)*

- **Goal State**:
$$
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 2 & 3 \\
4 & 5 & 6 \\
7 & 8 & \_
\end{bmatrix}
$$
*(1 mark)*

- **State-space size**: $9! / 2 = 181{,}440$ reachable states (parity argument). *(1 mark)*
- **Branching factor derivation**: Blank has 4 neighbors (max). When blank is in a corner, only 2 moves are valid (1 mark). On an edge, 3 moves. In the center, 4 moves. The average over all 9 positions:
$$b = \frac{4 + 3 \cdot 4 + 2 \cdot 4}{9} = \frac{24}{9} \approx 2.67$$
*(Note: Some texts cite 2.33 — this depends on whether you average uniformly over the 9 cells or weight by frequency.)* *(1 mark)*
- **Cost**: Each slide costs 1. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Application**: Forms the conceptual basis for sliding-block puzzles, Sokoban-like games, and warehouse robot rearrangement. *(0.5 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Tower of Hanoi problem** with $n = 3$ disks. List the sequence of moves. Derive the formula for the minimum number of moves for $n$ disks. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Formulate the **Water Jug problem** as a state-space problem. Given two jugs of capacities 4 liters and 3 liters, explain the state-space search to obtain exactly 2 liters. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Tower of Hanoi — 7 marks:**

**Problem description** *(2 marks)*:
Three pegs (A = source, B = auxiliary, C = destination), 3 disks of sizes 3 (largest), 2 (medium), 1 (smallest). Goal: move all disks from A to C, moving only the top disk, never placing a larger disk on a smaller one.

**Sequence of moves for $n=3$** *(3 marks — must list all 7 in order)*:

| Step | Move |
|------|------|
| 1 | Move disk 1 from A → C |
| 2 | Move disk 2 from A → B |
| 3 | Move disk 1 from C → B |
| 4 | Move disk 3 from A → C |
| 5 | Move disk 1 from B → A |
| 6 | Move disk 2 from B → C |
| 7 | Move disk 1 from A → C |

**Derivation of minimum moves** *(2 marks)*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(1) &= 1 = 2^1 - 1 \\
T(n) &= 2 \cdot T(n-1) + 1 \quad \text{(recursive relation)} \\
     &= 2(2^{n-1} - 1) + 1 = 2^n - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

For $n = 3$: $T(3) = 2^3 - 1 = 7$ moves — matches the table above.

---

**(b) Water Jug Problem — 7 marks:**

**Formulation** *(3 marks)*:

- **State**: $(x, y)$ where $x \in [0, 4]$, $y \in [0, 3]$.
- **Initial state**: $(0, 0)$.
- **Goal state**: Any state with $x = 2$ or $y = 2$.
- **Actions**: Fill a jug, empty a jug, pour one jug into the other.

**Six rules** *(2 marks)*:
1. Fill 4L jug: $(x, y) \rightarrow (4, y)$
2. Fill 3L jug: $(x, y) \rightarrow (x, 3)$
3. Empty 4L jug: $(x, y) \rightarrow (0, y)$
4. Empty 3L jug: $(x, y) \rightarrow (x, 0)$
5. Pour 4L → 3L until either 4L is empty or 3L is full.
6. Pour 3L → 4L until either 3L is empty or 4L is full.

**Search sequence to obtain 2 liters** *(2 marks)*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0, 0) &\xrightarrow{\text{Fill 3L}} (0, 3) \\
(0, 3) &\xrightarrow{\text{Pour 3L} \to \text{4L}} (3, 0) \\
(3, 0) &\xrightarrow{\text{Fill 3L}} (3, 3) \\
(3, 3) &\xrightarrow{\text{Pour 3L} \to \text{4L until 4L full}} (4, 2) \\
&\quad \textbf{GOAL REACHED: } y = 2 \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DO NOT LOSE MARKS HERE:**
> 1. **Confusing initial and goal states** — State $S_0 = (0,0)$ clearly; never assume the jugs are partially full.
> 2. **Forgetting pour constraint** — Pouring stops when source is empty OR destination is full — not both.
> 3. **Skipping the parity argument** in 8-puzzle — If asked for the state-space size, you MUST show $9!/2$, not $9!$.
> 4. **Hanoi derivation missing base case** — Always show $T(1) = 1$ before the inductive step.
> 5. **8-Queens: writing 8! instead of $8^8$** — Incremental formulation uses $8^8$ (8 choices × 8 columns). Complete-state uses $\binom{64}{8}$.
> 6. **TSP cost confused with state count** — Cost is the *minimum tour length*, not the number of tours.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Pin This to Memory:**

- ☐ Every AI problem is formalized as $P = (S, S_0, A, G, C, T)$.
- ☐ **Toy vs. Real-World** distinction is a guaranteed 3-mark Module 1 question.
- ☐ **8-Puzzle**: $9!/2 = 181{,}440$ states, branching factor ≈ **2.33–2.67**.
- ☐ **8-Queens**: 92 solutions, incremental formulation uses $8^8$ states.
- ☐ **Tower of Hanoi**: $T(n) = 2^n - 1$; for $n=3$, exactly 7 moves.
- ☐ **Water Jug**: 6 operators (Fill, Empty, Pour × 2 directions).
- ☐ **Missionaries & Cannibals**: boat carries ≤ 2, constraint $m \geq c$ on both banks.
- ☐ **TSP**: $(n-1)!/2$ unique tours — NP-hard; brute-force infeasible for $n \geq 20$.
- ☐ **Cryptarithmetic** is a CSP; leading-letter constraint forces `M = 1` in `SEND + MORE = MONEY`.
- ☐ **Vacuum World**: 8 states; 2 goal states; unit-cost actions.
- ☐ State-space complexity grows **exponentially** (puzzle) or **factorially** (TSP) — motivating heuristic search in Module 3.
- ☐ Always state **assumptions** (deterministic vs. stochastic, observable vs. partial) before formulating a problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uninformed search strategies: breadth-first search (BFS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCAT502 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Uninformed search strategies: breadth-first search (BFS) # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** is an **uninformed (blind) search strategy** in which the root node of the search tree is expanded first, then all the successors of the root node are expanded next, then their successors, and so on. In general, all the nodes are expanded at a given depth in the search tree before any node at the next level is expanded. BFS employs a **FIFO (First-In-First-Out) queue** as its frontier data structure, thereby guaranteeing that the **shallowest unexpanded node** is always selected for expansion next. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, BFS is classified under the family of **systematic search strategies** (those that systematically explore the state space without using any problem-specific heuristic information, $h(n) = 0$). It performs a **level-by-level** traversal of the state-space graph and is guaranteed to terminate with a solution path of **minimum number of steps** (i.e., minimum depth) when all step costs are uniform. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — Ripples in a Pond 🪨💧** > > Imagine dropping a stone into the still surface of a pond. The ripples spread outward in **concentric circles**. The first circle (depth 0) is the stone itself. The second circle is everything one step away. The third circle is everything two steps away — but you do **not** explore the third circle until the second circle is **completely** exhausted. > > BFS behaves exactly like this. It explores the search space in **expanding rings of equal distance** from the start state. Every node that is *k* steps away is fully explored before any node that is *(k+1)* steps away. > > **A more tangible analogy:** Think of a social network.
