BFS from your profile will find all your **direct friends** (depth 1) first, then all **friends-of-friends** (depth 2), and so on — never jumping to a friend-of-friend before exhausting every direct friend. ## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchored to KTU Notation | Symbol | Meaning | KTU Standard | |--------|---------|--------------| | $b$ | **Branching factor** — maximum number of successors of any node | Finite, $\geq 2$ typically | | $d$ | **Depth of the shallowest goal node** | Solution depth in tree | | $m$ | **Maximum depth** of the state space | Could be $\infty$ | | $\epsilon$ | **Path cost** of each action (uniform for standard BFS) | $= 1$ per step | | Frontier (Open List) | Set of nodes generated but not yet expanded | Implemented as a **FIFO queue** | | Explored (Closed List) | Set of nodes already expanded | Prevents loops/revisits | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** BFS is the *only* uninformed search that is both **complete** and **optimal** (under uniform step costs). This makes it the canonical reference algorithm for the KTU Module 2 viva and ESE questions. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Algorithmic Operational Logic — Structured Step Breakdown The standard KTU-board algorithm for BFS is presented below. Each step is annotated with the **why** behind it: > [!NOTE] > **BFS Algorithm (Russell & Norvig Style — KTU Reference Form)** 1. **Initialize the frontier** as a FIFO queue containing only the **initial state** $S_0$. - *Why:* Establishes the starting point of the search. 2. **Initialize the explored set** to the empty set $\emptyset$. - *Why:* Tracks visited nodes to prevent infinite loops in graphs with cycles. 3. **Loop Check:** *Is the frontier empty?* - If **YES** → return **failure** (no solution exists in the reachable state space). - *Why:* If we have nothing left to explore, the search is exhausted. 4. **Pop the LEFTMOST node $n$** from the frontier queue (FIFO discipline). - *Why:* This ensures the **shallowest unexpanded node** is always chosen — the defining property of BFS. 5. **Goal Test:** *Is $n$ a goal state?* - If **YES** → return $n$ (and the path reconstructed via parent pointers). - *Why:* Since BFS expands in non-decreasing depth order, the *first* goal encountered is at **minimum depth**. 6. **Add $n$ to the explored set** (mark as visited). - *Why:* Prevents reprocessing of the same node later. 7. **Expand $n$:** For every action $a$ applicable in state $n$: - Generate the successor state $n'$ with path cost $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, a, n')$. - If $n'$ is **neither in frontier nor in explored set**, add $n'$ to the **RIGHT END** of the frontier queue and record its parent as $n$. - *Why:* Appending to the right preserves FIFO order — newly generated nodes are at depth $d(n) + 1$, so they must wait until all depth $d(n)$ nodes are processed. 8. **Go to Step 3.** ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are board-relevant and may appear directly in KTU ESE numerical/model questions.** | Property | Formula / Expression | Condition / Notes | |----------|----------------------|-------------------| | **Completeness** | $\text{Yes}$ (always returns a solution if one exists) | Requires finite branching factor $b$ | | **Optimality** | $\text{Yes}$ (finds the shallowest goal) | Only when all step costs are **identical** ($c \geq \epsilon$) | | **Time Complexity** | $O(b^{d+1})$ or equivalently $O(b^d)$ | $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = solution depth | | **Space Complexity** | $O(b^{d+1})$ or $O(b^d)$ | Frontier + Explored sets both grow as $b^d$ | | **Nodes Generated** | $1 + b + b^2 + b^3 + \dots + b^d = \dfrac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$ | Geometric series expansion | | **Queue Discipline** | **FIFO** (First-In, First-Out) | The defining data-structure property | | **Data Structure** | **Queue** for frontier; Hash-set/array for explored | Standard implementation | | **Heuristic Used** | $h(n) = 0$ for all $n$ | Purely uninformed / blind | | **Order of Expansion** | Increasing depth $d = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ | Level-by-level | | **Backtracking** | **Not required** | Parent pointers + frontier suffice | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility BFS underpins several mission-critical engineering applications: - **Shortest-path routing in unweighted networks** — e.g., the **Autonomous System (AS) topology** discovery in computer networks using traceroute-like protocols. - **Social Network Analysis** — computing the *Erdős number* (degree of separation) in collaboration graphs (LinkedIn "people you may know" feature). - **Garbage Collection in Compilers** — the *mark phase* of the **mark-and-sweep GC** uses BFS over the object-reference graph. - **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Networks** — BFS-based lookup in distributed hash tables (Chord, Kademlia variants). - **Web Crawlers** — early-generation crawlers used BFS to discover pages within a domain. - **AI Game Trees** — although limited by depth, BFS variants underlie **checkers/mancala solvers** for small state spaces. - **GPS Navigation in road networks** where each road segment has uniform cost — BFS gives the **fewest-turns route**. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** In practice, BFS is rarely used on huge state spaces (e.g., $b=10, d=16$ would require $\sim 10^{16}$ memory nodes) due to its **exponential space consumption**. This is precisely the motivation for the **heuristic / informed** searches (A\*, Greedy Best-First) introduced later in KTU Module 2. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Tree-Expansion Example (Romania / Arad Problem Variant) We trace BFS on a simplified search tree rooted at node **S**, with goal **G**. The branching factor is $b = 3$ and we deliberately make G appear at depth 3 to show the *level-by-level* expansion. ### Tree Structure ``` S (depth 0) / | \ A B C (depth 1) /\ /\ /\ D E F G H I (depth 2) | \ J K (depth 3) ← J is GOAL ``` ### Step-by-Step Trace (Queue Evolution Table) | Step | Action Taken | Frontier Queue (left → right) | Explored Set | Notes | |------|--------------|-------------------------------|--------------|-------| | 0 | Initialize with S | `[S]` | `{}` | Start state loaded | | 1 | Pop S, expand → A, B, C | `[A, B, C]` | `{S}` | S not goal | | 2 | Pop A, expand → D, E | `[B, C, D, E]` | `{S, A}` | A not goal | | 3 | Pop B, expand → F, G | `[C, D, E, F, G]` | `{S, A, B}` | B not goal | | 4 | Pop C, expand → H, I | `[D, E, F, G, H, I]` | `{S, A, B, C}` | C not goal | | 5 | Pop D, no children | `[E, F, G, H, I]` | `{S, A, B, C, D}` | Leaf node | | 6 | Pop E, expand → J | `[F, G, H, I, J]` | `{S, A, B, C, D, E}` | J generated | | 7 | Pop F, no children | `[G, H, I, J]` | `{S, A, B, C, D, E, F}` | Leaf | | 8 | Pop G, no children | `[H, I, J]` | `{…, F, G}` | Leaf | | 9 | Pop H, no children | `[I, J]` | `{…, G, H}` | Leaf | | 10 | Pop I, expand → K | `[J, K]` | `{…, H, I}` | K generated | | 11 | Pop J, **GOAL TEST → SUCCESS** ✅ | `[K]` | `{…, I, J}` | **BFS terminates** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Observation:** When J is discovered at **Step 6**, it is *not* returned immediately. BFS continues to expand nodes at the same depth (F, G, H, I) to ensure no shallower goal exists. Only when J itself is **popped** at Step 11 is the goal test performed. This is what guarantees **optimality**. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Time & Space Complexity We derive the total number of nodes generated by BFS up to (and including) the level of the shallowest goal. > [!NOTE] > **Derivation Setup:** > Let $b$ = maximum branching factor, $d$ = depth of the shallowest goal node. The BFS expansion is **level-synchronous** — all nodes at depth $k$ are expanded before any node at depth $k+1$. **Step 1 — Count nodes at each level:** At depth $0$ (root), there is exactly $1$ node. $$ N_0 = 1 $$ At depth $1$, the root can generate at most $b$ successors. $$ N_1 = b $$ At depth $2$, each of the $b$ nodes at depth 1 can generate $b$ successors (worst case), giving $b^2$ total. $$ N_2 = b^2 $$ By induction, the number of nodes at depth $k$ is: $$ N_k = b^k $$ **Step 2 — Sum the geometric series:** The total number of nodes generated up to depth $d$ is the sum: $$ N_{total} = \sum_{k=0}^{d} N_k = \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k $$ **Step 3 — Apply the geometric series formula:** For a geometric series with first term $a = 1$, common ratio $r = b$, and $d+1$ terms: $$ \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} $$ Hence: $$ N_{total} = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} $$ **Step 4 — Asymptotic simplification:** For large $b$ and $d$, the constant numerator $-1$ and the $b-1 \approx b$ denominator yield: $$ N_{total} \approx \frac{b^{d+1}}{b} = b^d $$ Therefore, in **Big-O** notation (which the KTU board expects verbatim): $$ T(n) = O(b^{d+1}) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad O(b^d) $$ **Step 5 — Space complexity justification:** The frontier queue, just before the goal at depth $d$ is dequeued, holds **all leaf nodes at depth $d$** (which are not yet goal-tested) plus the explored set entries from depths $0$ to $d-1$. Both are bounded by the total node count: $$ S(n) = O(b^{d+1}) = O(b^d) $$ **Step 6 — Concrete numerical illustration (KTU-favourite style):** Take $b = 10$, $d = 8$ (representative of small-puzzle problems): $$ N_{total} = \frac{10^{9} - 1}{10 - 1} = \frac{999{,}999{,}999}{9} \approx 1.11 \times 10^{8} $$ This is **111 million nodes** — an intractable main-memory requirement at standard RAM scales, proving the BFS memory bottleneck empirically. ## 3.3 Why BFS is Optimal (Proof Sketch — KTU 14-Mark Favourite) **Claim:** If all step costs are equal ($c(n, n') = 1$), BFS returns an optimal (minimum-step) solution. **Proof Outline:** 1. BFS expands nodes in non-decreasing order of depth. *(By FIFO queue discipline — earlier-inserted = shallower.)* 2. Suppose BFS returns a goal $G'$ at depth $d'$. 3. Assume for contradiction there exists another goal $G^*$ at depth $d^* < d'$. 4. $G^*$ would have been generated (and subsequently dequeued) **before** $G'$, since the frontier at depth $d^*$ is exhausted before depth $d' + 1$ is reached. 5. BFS performs the goal test **at dequeue time**, so $G^*$ would have been detected first. 6. Contradiction. ∎ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility of this Proof:** This same skeleton is reused in the KTU 2024 syllabus when discussing **Uniform-Cost Search (UCS)** optimality — UCS generalises BFS to non-uniform costs by ordering the frontier by path cost $g(n)$ instead of FIFO. BFS is the special case where $c \equiv 1$. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of BFS The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the *control flow* of BFS at a system-architecture level, showing the interaction between the **Frontier Queue**, the **Explored Set**, the **Goal Test Module**, and the **Node Expander**. ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode([Start]) --> initQ[Initialize Frontier Queue with initial state S0] initQ --> initEx[Initialize Explored Set to empty] initEx --> checkEmpty{Frontier Empty?} checkEmpty -- YES --> failNode([Return FAILURE]) checkEmpty -- NO --> popNode[Pop LEFTMOST node n from queue] popNode --> goalTest{n is GOAL?} goalTest -- YES --> successNode([Return n with parent-pointer path]) goalTest -- NO --> addEx[Add n to Explored Set] addEx --> expandNode[For each action a in Actions n : generate successor n prime] expandNode --> checkSeen{n prime already in Frontier or Explored?} checkSeen -- YES --> expandNode checkSeen -- NO --> enqueue[Enqueue n prime at RIGHT END of queue and record parent] enqueue --> checkEmpty ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The blue decision diamonds (`{}`) are the only branching points. Note that the goal test is performed **after** popping a node, not when it is generated — this is a critical KTU viva point that distinguishes BFS from depth-limited variants. ## 4.2 Schematic of the Level-by-Level Expansion Behaviour The next diagram visualizes the **wavefront** of BFS expansion through a search tree. Each level is exhausted before the next is touched. ```mermaid flowchart TB level0["LEVEL 0 : Root S0"] level1["LEVEL 1 : b nodes (A, B, C, ...)"] level2["LEVEL 2 : b^2 nodes (D1, D2, ...)"] level3["LEVEL 3 : b^3 nodes containing goal G"] level0 --> level1 level1 --> level2 level2 --> level3 expandNote["Expansion Order: All level-0 nodes fully expanded
before ANY level-1 node is touched"] level0 -.-> expandNote ``` ```mermaid graph LR state0((Start S0)) -- step1 --> state1((Level 1 nodes)) state1 -- step2 --> state2((Level 2 nodes)) state2 -- step3 --> state3((Level 3 GOAL G)) state3 -- returns path --> result[Optimal Path: S0 to G] classDef goalNode fill:#9bff9b,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px class state3 goalNode ``` ## 4.3 Data-Structure State Diagram The following block diagram clarifies the **dual data-structure architecture** that BFS maintains in memory. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph frontier["FRONTIER (FIFO QUEUE) — Contains nodes to be expanded"] f1[Node : depth 1 - left end - next to pop] f2[Node : depth 1] f3[Node : depth 2] f4[Node : depth 2 - right end - newly added] end subgraph explored["EXPLORED SET (Hash Table / Boolean Array) — Contains nodes already expanded"] e1[Root S0] e2[Child 1] e3[Child 2] end popOp[Pop operation removes leftmost] --> frontier enqOp[Enqueue operation appends to right] --> frontier frontier -- after pop --> goalTest2{Goal?} goalTest2 -- YES --> successEnd([Return solution]) goalTest2 -- NO --> moveToEx[Move to Explored Set] moveToEx --> explored moveToEx --> frontier ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Tip:** When asked in a viva to *state the time and space complexity of BFS and justify*, always draw this dual-buffer diagram. It demonstrates that both the frontier and the explored set must be stored in memory, hence the $O(b^d)$ space bound. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCCAT502 / Module 2]` **Define Breadth-First Search (BFS). Mention any two of its properties.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** BFS is an **uninformed search strategy** that explores the state space *level by level*. It expands all nodes at depth $k$ before any node at depth $k+1$, using a **FIFO queue** to maintain the frontier. **Two Properties (1 mark for both):** 1. **Complete** — BFS is guaranteed to find a solution if one exists, provided the branching factor $b$ is finite. 2. **Optimal** — BFS finds a solution of minimum depth (fewest steps) when all step costs are equal. *Common Mistake to Avoid:* Students often confuse "optimal" with "lowest path cost". BFS is optimal in terms of **number of steps** (i.e., depth) only when step costs are uniform. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCCAT502 / Module 2]` **Why is BFS considered an "uninformed" or "blind" search? Justify in one sentence.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** BFS is classified as an *uninformed* (or *blind*) search because it possesses **no problem-specific heuristic information** about the distance or location of the goal — mathematically, it uses $h(n) = 0$ for every node $n$. The search proceeds based purely on the **structural order of expansion** (level-by-level) without any guidance toward the goal. *Justification line:* Since BFS has no domain knowledge, it cannot distinguish a "promising" node from a "useless" one, which is precisely why it must explore the entire state space up to depth $d$ in the worst case. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, Module 2 questions of 14 marks come with **internal choice** — students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Both questions below carry equal weight and follow the standard (7 + 7) sub-part split. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model, Module 2]` **(a)** Explain the Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm with a suitable example tree. Clearly show the contents of the **frontier queue** and the **explored set** at each step. State the **time and space complexity** of BFS in terms of the branching factor $b$ and the solution depth $d$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A search tree has a **branching factor $b = 5$** and the **shallowest goal is at depth $d = 6$**. Calculate: (i) The **total number of nodes** generated by BFS in the worst case. (ii) The **memory requirement** in megabytes, assuming each node occupies **64 bytes** in RAM. (iii) Comment on the **practical feasibility** of running this BFS on a standard desktop with 16 GB RAM. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question A** **(a) BFS Algorithm with Worked Example (7 Marks):** **Algorithm Outline (2 marks):** 1. Insert the initial node into an empty FIFO queue (frontier). 2. If queue is empty → return failure. 3. Dequeue the leftmost node $n$. 4. Perform the **goal test** on $n$. If true → return $n$. 5. Insert $n$ into the **explored set**. 6. For each successor $n'$ of $n$ that is *not* in frontier or explored, enqueue $n'$ at the right end and record its parent as $n$. 7. Go to step 2. **Worked Example (3 marks):** Consider the following search tree rooted at `S`, with goal `G` at depth 2. ``` S / \ A B / \ \ D E G ← GOAL ``` **Trace Table:** | Step | Operation | Frontier (left → right) | Explored Set | |------|-----------|--------------------------|--------------| | 0 | Initialize | `[S]` | `{}` | | 1 | Pop S, expand | `[A, B]` | `{S}` | | 2 | Pop A, expand | `[B, D, E]` | `{S, A}` | | 3 | Pop B, expand | `[D, E, G]` | `{S, A, B}` | | 4 | Pop D (leaf) | `[E, G]` | `{S, A, B, D}` | | 5 | Pop E (leaf) | `[G]` | `{S, A, B, D, E}` | | 6 | Pop G, **GOAL** | `[]` | `{S, A, B, D, E, G}` ✅ | **Time & Space Complexity (2 marks):** $$ T(n) = O(b^{d+1}) = O(b^d), \qquad S(n) = O(b^{d+1}) = O(b^d) $$ **Justification:** At depth $k$, the number of nodes is $b^k$. Summing the geometric series $1 + b + b^2 + \dots + b^d$ gives $\frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$, which is asymptotically $O(b^d)$. Both the frontier and explored set together occupy this many nodes in memory. --- **(b) Numerical Analysis (7 Marks):** **(i) Total Nodes Generated (3 marks):** $$ N_{total} = \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} $$ Substituting $b = 5$, $d = 6$: $$ N_{total} = \frac{5^{7} - 1}{5 - 1} = \frac{78125 - 1}{4} = \frac{78124}{4} = 19531 $$ **Total nodes generated = 19,531.** **(ii) Memory Requirement (2 marks):** $$ \text{Memory} = 19531 \times 64 \text{ bytes} = 1{,}249{,}984 \text{ bytes} $$ Converting to megabytes (1 MB = $1024 \times 1024 = 1{,}048{,}576$ bytes): $$ \text{Memory} = \frac{1{,}249{,}984}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 1.19 \text{ MB} $$ **Memory required ≈ 1.19 MB.** **(iii) Practical Feasibility (2 marks):** This BFS instance is **highly feasible** on any standard desktop. A requirement of **1.19 MB** is negligible compared to the 16 GB RAM available. The CPU cost of generating 19,531 nodes is also trivial (sub-second on a modern processor). However, the feasibility **decays exponentially**: doubling the depth to $d = 12$ would multiply the node count by $5^6 = 15{,}625$, giving $\sim 305$ million nodes and $\sim 19.5$ GB memory — which would overflow the 16 GB RAM. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model, Module 2]` **(a)** Discuss the **differences** between Breadth-First Search (BFS) and Depth-First Search (DFS) with respect to: (i) Data structure used for the frontier (ii) Order of expansion (iii) Completeness and Optimality (iv) Time and Space complexity **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For the following graph, perform BFS starting from node `A` and write the **order of node visits** (traversal order) and the **path found to node `G`**. Clearly mention the frontier queue and explored set at each step. ``` A ——— B ——— C | | | D ——— E ——— F | G ``` **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question B** **(a) BFS vs DFS Comparison Table (7 Marks):** | # | Property | **BFS** | **DFS** | |---|----------|---------|---------| | (i) | **Frontier Data Structure** | **FIFO Queue** | **LIFO Stack** | | (ii) | **Order of Expansion** | Level-by-level (increasing depth) | Deepest-first (depth-incrementing) | | (iii) | **Completeness** | Complete (finite $b$) | Complete only for finite trees; **not complete** for infinite-depth or graph search with cycles (unless cycle-check is added) | | (iv) | **Optimality** | Optimal for uniform step costs | **Not optimal** — may return a deeper goal first | | (v) | **Time Complexity** | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^m)$ where $m$ is max depth | | (vi) | **Space Complexity** | $O(b^d)$ — memory bottleneck | $O(bm)$ — memory efficient | | (vii) | **Goal Test Placement** | At **dequeue** (uniform cost) | At **dequeue** (latest-encountered goal first) | | (viii) | **Best Use Case** | Shallow goals in small branching-factor spaces | Deep goals with abundant memory but need to backtrack | *(Each correct row attracts proportionate marks; a 4-row table is the minimum KTU expectation.)* --- **(b) BFS on the Given Graph (7 Marks):** **Adjacency List:** $$ A: \{B, D\}, \quad B: \{A, C, E\}, \quad C: \{B, F\} $$ $$ D: \{A, E\}, \quad E: \{B, D, F\}, \quad F: \{C, E, G\}, \quad G: \{F\} $$ **Step-by-Step Trace:** | Step | Pop | Frontier Queue (left → right) | Explored Set | Goal Found? | |------|-----|-------------------------------|--------------|-------------| | 0 | — | `[A]` | `{}` | — | | 1 | A | `[B, D]` | `{A}` | No | | 2 | B | `[D, C, E]` | `{A, B}` | No | | 3 | D | `[C, E]` | `{A, B, D}` | No | | 4 | C | `[E, F]` | `{A, B, D, C}` | No | | 5 | E | `[F]` | `{A, B, D, C, E}` | No | | 6 | F | `[G]` | `{A, B, D, C, E, F}` | No | | 7 | G | `[]` | `{…, F}` | **YES ✅** | **Order of Node Visits:** `A → B → D → C → E → F → G` **Path Found to G:** Reconstructed via parent pointers → `A → B → C → F → G` (one of the valid shortest paths of length 4). **Shortest Path Length:** $4$ edges. *(Allocate 1 mark per correct row of the trace table, 1 mark for the visit order, 1 mark for the path reconstruction, and 1 mark for verifying the path length.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on BFS Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to maintain the Explored Set (4 marks lost):** Many students implement BFS as a pure tree-search, missing cycles. This makes the algorithm **incomplete for graph search**. Always explicitly state and maintain the *Explored* (closed) set in the trace. > > 2. **Goal Test at Generation vs. Dequeue (3 marks lost):** The KTU board distinguishes these carefully. BFS in its **optimal** form tests the goal **at dequeue time**, not at generation. Testing at generation can produce a non-optimal goal. Mention this distinction explicitly. > > 3. **Confusing BFS Optimality Condition (3 marks lost):** BFS is optimal **only when step costs are uniform**. If costs differ, BFS may NOT be optimal, and **Uniform-Cost Search (UCS)** is required. Writing "BFS is always optimal" will be marked wrong. > > 4. **Arithmetic Error in Geometric Series (2 marks lost):** When asked to compute $b^d$, students often compute $b \cdot d$ (multiplication) instead of $b^d$ (exponentiation). Re-read the question carefully. > > 5. **Forgetting the Big-O Constant (2 marks lost):** The total nodes are $\frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b-1}$, not merely $b^d$. Show the full derivation for full marks. > > 6. **Missing Parent-Pointer Reconstruction (1 mark lost):** In the final step, the *path* must be traced back via parent pointers. Just writing "goal found" is incomplete. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — BFS (Module 2)** - ✅ BFS is an **uninformed / blind** search with $h(n) = 0$. - ✅ Uses a **FIFO Queue** for the frontier. - ✅ Explores **level by level** (increasing depth order). - ✅ **Complete** if branching factor $b$ is finite. - ✅ **Optimal** for **uniform step costs** (otherwise prefer UCS). - ✅ **Time Complexity:** $O(b^{d+1}) = O(b^d)$. - ✅ **Space Complexity:** $O(b^{d+1}) = O(b^d)$ — this is the **main drawback**. - ✅ **Goal Test** is performed at **dequeue** time for optimality. - ✅ **Explored (Closed) Set** is essential for graph search to prevent cycles. - ✅ **Total Nodes Generated:** $\frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$ (geometric series). - ✅ BFS is best for **shallow goals** in **moderate-branching** spaces (e.g., shortest path on maps). - ✅ BFS fails on **large state spaces** (e.g., Rubik's cube, 15-puzzle with $b \approx 20$) due to memory. - ✅ Distinguish BFS from **Uniform-Cost Search (UCS)** — UCS uses **priority queue** ordered by $g(n)$. - ✅ Distinguish BFS from **Depth-First Search (DFS)** — DFS uses a **stack** and is **not optimal**. - ✅ Distinguish BFS from **Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS)** — IDDFS combines BFS optimality with DFS memory efficiency (next topic in KTU Module 2). - ✅ **Common exam trick:** "Will BFS work on infinite-depth trees?" — **No, it diverges.** Use IDS / IDDFS. - ✅ **Memory rule of thumb:** If $b = 10$, $d = 8$, expect $\sim 10^8$ nodes and $\sim 10$ GB RAM — typically infeasible.

topics.title: Depth-first search (DFS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT502 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Depth-first search (DFS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Depth-First Search (DFS)** is an **uninformed (blind) search strategy** that always expands the *deepest node* in the current frontier (boundary) of the search tree. It proceeds by exploring as far as possible down a single branch of the tree before *backtracking* to explore alternative branches. Operationally, DFS uses a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** stack data structure (or its equivalent, function-call recursion) to manage the frontier of unexplored nodes.

In the formal KTU taxonomy of search algorithms, DFS belongs to the family of **graph/tree traversal algorithms** that predate heuristic methods and serve as a foundational pillar of *state-space search*. The "frontier" represents the set of nodes that have been generated but not yet expanded.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Maze Explorer:** Imagine you are trapped inside a dark maze with only a single long thread of yarn tied to the entrance. You walk forward, always choosing the leftmost unexplored path at every junction, and continue doing so until you hit a dead-end. Only when you cannot proceed further do you retrace your steps back to the most recent junction that still has an unexplored path. This is exactly how DFS behaves: **go deep first, backtrack only when stuck**.

Another everyday analogy is reading a Wikipedia article: you click the first link in the article, then the first link in *that* article, and so on — diving deeper and deeper. Only when you hit a page with no useful links do you press the "Back" button and try the next link on the previous page.

### 1.3 Core Properties of DFS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics (KTU Board Highlights):**
> - **Search Strategy Class:** Uninformed / Blind Search
> - **Data Structure Used:** **Stack (LIFO)** — *or equivalent recursion call stack*
> - **Completeness:** **Yes**, but only in finite search spaces; **No** in infinite spaces (can get stuck in infinite branches)
> - **Optimality:** **No** — it does not guarantee the lowest-cost path
> - **Time Complexity:** $O(b^m)$ where $b$ = branching factor, $m$ = maximum depth of any node
> - **Space Complexity:** $O(bm)$ — linear in depth, much better than BFS

These four properties (completeness, optimality, time, space) are **the most frequently asked metric** in KTU Part A questions for this module.

### 1.4 Where DFS Fits in the AI Search Hierarchy

```
Uninformed Search
        ├── Breadth-First Search (BFS)   ← uses Queue (FIFO)
        ├── Uniform-Cost Search (UCS)   ← uses Priority Queue
        ├── Depth-First Search (DFS)    ← uses Stack (LIFO)  ◄── THIS TOPIC
        └── Depth-Limited Search (DLS) / Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How DFS Works Step-by-Step

The DFS algorithm follows a strict sequence of operations:

1. **Initialize** an empty stack (or recursive call frame) and push the **root/initial node** onto it. Mark the initial node as visited.
2. **Loop** (or recurse) while the stack is non-empty:
   - **Pop** the top node $n$ from the stack — this is the node to be expanded.
   - **Goal Test:** If $n$ satisfies the goal condition, return the solution path.
   - **Expand $n$:** Generate all successor/child nodes of $n$ that have **not been visited** yet.
   - **Push** these successor nodes onto the stack.
3. **Backtrack** automatically occurs because the algorithm always re-visits the most recently pushed node when a dead-end is reached (the LIFO behavior of the stack).
4. **Termination:** The algorithm returns failure if the stack becomes empty before reaching a goal.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why a Stack?** A stack enforces Last-In-First-Out access. The *most recently generated* node is always the *first to be expanded*. This naturally creates the "deepest-first" behavior.
> - **Why mark visited?** Without a visited-set, DFS can loop forever in graphs with cycles. In trees (acyclic), visited-marking is implicit via path tracking.
> - **Why "backtrack"?** Backtracking is *not* a separate command — it emerges naturally when a node with no unvisited successors is popped, causing the algorithm to return to its parent in the search tree (i.e., the next-deepest node still on the stack).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Reference Table for DFS — Memorize for KTU Board Exams**

| Property | Value / Expression | Symbol Definitions | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Time Complexity** | $O(b^m)$ | $b$ = branching factor, $m$ = max depth | ★★★★★ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(bm)$ | Linear in depth | ★★★★★ |
| **Data Structure** | **Stack (LIFO)** | or recursion | ★★★★★ |
| **Complete?** | No (infinite spaces) | Yes only for finite | ★★★★ |
| **Optimal?** | **No** | Finds *a* path, not *best* | ★★★★★ |
| **Worst-Case Nodes Expanded** | $b + b^2 + b^3 + \cdots + b^m$ | Geometric series | ★★★ |
| **Frontier Order** | LIFO | Last generated → first expanded | ★★★★ |
| **Variant** | Depth-Limited DFS | Cutoff depth $L$ | ★★★ |
| **Variant** | Iterative Deepening DFS | Loops $L=0,1,2,\ldots$ | ★★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU RULE:** In the KTU answer key, examiners expect the exact form $O(b^m)$ for time and $O(bm)$ for space. Writing "$O(b \cdot m)$" instead of "$O(bm)$" is a common pitfall — the space grows **multiplicatively**, not as a product, because only one path's siblings exist in the stack at a time (so $O(bm)$, not $O(b^m)$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of DFS

- **Compiler Design:** Topological sorting of dependency graphs (e.g., resolving `#include` order in C).
- **Operating Systems:** Detecting deadlocks and cycles in resource allocation graphs.
- **Robotics & Pathfinding:** Useful when a single deep path is acceptable and memory is constrained (e.g., maze-solving microcontrollers).
- **Network Analysis:** Detecting strongly connected components (Tarjan's / Kosaraju's algorithms) in social networks and the World Wide Web.
- **AI Game Trees:** Foundation of minimax search in chess engines (combined with alpha-beta pruning).
- **Garbage Collection:** Mark-and-sweep collectors traverse object reference graphs using DFS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Time Complexity $O(b^m)$

Let us rigorously derive the time complexity. In the worst case, DFS will explore **every node** in the search tree before terminating (or finding the goal at the rightmost deepest leaf).

The search tree has:
- Root node: 1 node
- Depth 1: $b$ nodes
- Depth 2: $b^2$ nodes
- Depth 3: $b^3$ nodes
- $\vdots$
- Depth $m$: $b^m$ nodes

**Step 1:** Sum the number of nodes generated across all depths.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &= 1 + b + b^2 + b^3 + \cdots + b^m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Recognize the right-hand side as a **finite geometric series** with first term $a = 1$, common ratio $r = b$, and $m+1$ terms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \sum_{k=0}^{m} b^k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the closed-form formula for a geometric series.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{b^{m+1} - 1}{b - 1} \quad \text{for } b \neq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply **Big-O asymptotic analysis**. For large $m$ and $b > 1$, the term $b^{m+1}$ dominates all others. We discard the constant and lower-order terms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &\approx b^{m+1} = O(b^m)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** In Big-O notation, the time complexity is $O(b^m)$. Each node generation takes constant time, so the total time is $O(b^m)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Space Complexity $O(bm)$

**Step 1:** The DFS stack simultaneously stores nodes along **only one path** from the root to a leaf, plus at most $b$ siblings waiting at each level.

**Step 2:** Maximum depth of any path = $m$. Maximum siblings per level = $b$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximum stack size} &= b \times m = bm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply Big-O notation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Space} &= O(bm)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** DFS has **linear space complexity** $O(bm)$, which is dramatically better than BFS's $O(b^m)$. This is the **single most important practical advantage** of DFS.

---

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — DFS on a Graph

Consider the undirected graph:

$$
V = \{S, A, B, C, D, G\}, \quad E = \{S\!-\!A,\; S\!-\!B,\; A\!-\!C,\; A\!-\!D,\; B\!-\!G,\; C\!-\!G,\; D\!-\!G\}
$$

Goal: Find node **G** starting from **S**. Assume children are pushed onto the stack in **alphabetical order**.

| Step | Action | Stack (top → bottom) | Visited Set | Path |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initialize: push S | `[S]` | `{S}` | — |
| 1 | Pop S, expand → push A, B | `[A, B]` | `{S, A, B}` | S |
| 2 | Pop A (deepest), expand → push C, D | `[C, D, B]` | `{S,A,B,C,D}` | S→A |
| 3 | Pop C, expand → push G | `[G, D, B]` | `{S,A,B,C,D,G}` | S→A→C |
| 4 | Pop G → **GOAL FOUND** | — | — | **S→A→C→G** |

**Note:** Although S→B→G is a shorter path (2 edges), DFS returns S→A→C→G (3 edges). This empirically demonstrates why DFS is **not optimal**.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: DFS vs BFS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Comparison Table — Frequently Asked in KTU Part B (14-mark) Questions**

| Criterion | BFS | DFS | DLS | IDDFS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Structure** | Queue (FIFO) | **Stack (LIFO)** | Stack + depth limit $L$ | Loop $L = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ |
| **Complete?** | Yes | No (infinite) | Yes (if $L \geq$ goal depth) | Yes |
| **Optimal?** | Yes (unit costs) | **No** | No | Yes (unit costs) |
| **Time** | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^L)$ | $O(b^d)$ |
| **Space** | $O(b^d)$ | **$O(bm)$** | $O(bL)$ | $O(bd)$ |
| **Strategy** | Shallow-first | **Deepest-first** | Deep with cutoff | Repeated DLS |

Where $d$ = depth of shallowest goal, $m$ = max depth, $L$ = depth limit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DFS Traversal Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the control flow of an iterative DFS implementation, which is the standard reference used in KTU board evaluations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([START]) --> nodeB[Initialize empty STACK and VISITED set]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Push ROOT node onto STACK]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Is STACK empty?}
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE([RETURN FAILURE])
    nodeD -- No --> nodeF[POP top node n from STACK]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Is n GOAL?}
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH([RETURN SOLUTION PATH to n])
    nodeG -- No --> nodeI{n already in VISITED?}
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeD
    nodeI -- No --> nodeJ[Mark n as VISITED]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Generate all SUCCESSORS of n]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Filter out already-VISITED successors]
    nodeL --> nodeM[PUSH remaining successors onto STACK in order]
    nodeM --> nodeD
```

**Explanation of flow:**
- The **diamond decision blocks** (`{}`) correspond to branching conditions in the algorithm.
- The **rectangular process blocks** (`[]`) correspond to data-structure operations.
- The **rounded terminal blocks** (`([])`) mark the start and end of the algorithm.
- The **backward arrow from nodeM → nodeD** represents the iterative loop that re-checks the stack condition.

---

### 4.2 DFS Search Tree Expansion on a Sample Graph

The following diagram shows how DFS explores a tree of branching factor $b = 2$ and depth $m = 3$, with numeric labels indicating the **order in which nodes are expanded**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1[1: ROOT] --> n2[2: Child A]
    n1 --> n3[8: Child B]
    n2 --> n4[3: Grandchild A1]
    n2 --> n5[6: Grandchild A2]
    n3 --> n6[9: Grandchild B1]
    n3 --> n7[10: Grandchild B2]
    n4 --> n8[4: Great-grandchild A1a]
    n4 --> n9[5: Great-grandchild A1b]
    n5 --> n10[7: Great-grandchild A2a]
```

**Reading the diagram:** DFS expands nodes in the sequence $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5 \to 6 \to 7 \to 8 \to 9 \to 10$, drilling down the leftmost branch to maximum depth (nodes 4, 5) before backtracking to expand node 6, then 7, and finally the entire right subtree (nodes 8, 9, 10). This is the **defining "deepest-first"** behavior.

---

### 4.3 Recursive Call Stack Visualization (Backtracking Mechanism)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rec1[DFS call: ROOT] --> rec2[DFS call: Child A]
    rec2 --> rec3[DFS call: Grandchild A1]
    rec3 --> rec4[DFS call: A1a - dead end]
    rec4 -. return .-> rec3
    rec3 --> rec5[DFS call: A1b - dead end]
    rec5 -. return .-> rec3
    rec3 -. return .-> rec2
    rec2 --> rec6[DFS call: Grandchild A2]
    rec6 -. return .-> rec2
    rec2 -. return .-> rec1
    rec1 --> rec7[DFS call: Child B]
```

The dotted arrows represent **return / backtrack** events. When a recursive call returns, the algorithm implicitly pops that call frame from the system stack, allowing the next sibling to be explored. This is why recursion is a natural fit for DFS.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Define Depth-First Search. What data structure does it use, and is it optimal? Justify briefly.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Depth-First Search is an uninformed search strategy that expands the deepest unexpanded node in the search tree's current frontier, exploring a branch completely before backtracking to explore alternative branches. It uses a **Stack (LIFO)** data structure (or recursion). DFS is **not optimal** because it does not consider path cost — it returns the first goal node it encounters, which may not be on the shortest or lowest-cost path. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for data structure, 1 for optimality justification)

---

> **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** State the time and space complexity of DFS in terms of branching factor $b$ and maximum depth $m$. Why is DFS preferred over BFS in memory-constrained applications?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Time complexity of DFS is $O(b^m)$ and space complexity is $O(bm)$. DFS is preferred over BFS in memory-constrained applications because BFS requires $O(b^d)$ space (it must store the entire frontier at depth $d$), whereas DFS only needs $O(bm)$ space (one path's worth of nodes in the stack). For deep search spaces, $bm \ll b^d$, making DFS far more memory-efficient. (3 marks: 1 each for time, space, and the comparison reasoning)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **Q3(a). `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2]`** *Question A (14 Marks)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Depth-First Search algorithm in detail. State its algorithm steps and discuss its time and space complexity. Comment on its completeness and optimality.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Apply DFS to the following graph to find a path from node **S** to node **G**. Show the stack contents at each step. Assume children are pushed in alphabetical order.

$$
V = \{S, A, B, C, D, E, G\}, \quad E = \{S\!-\!A,\; S\!-\!D,\; A\!-\!B,\; A\!-\!C,\; D\!-\!E,\; B\!-\!G,\; C\!-\!G,\; E\!-\!G\}
$$

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

Depth-First Search is a blind search technique that always expands the deepest node in the current frontier. **Algorithm steps** (1 mark):

1. Push the initial node onto a stack.
2. While stack is non-empty:
   - Pop the top node $n$.
   - If $n$ is the goal, return success.
   - Else, push all unvisited successors of $n$ onto the stack.
3. If stack becomes empty, return failure.

**Complexity analysis** (2 marks):
- Time: $O(b^m)$ — derived from geometric series $\sum_{k=0}^{m} b^k = O(b^m)$.
- Space: $O(bm)$ — only one path's siblings are stored at any time.

**Completeness and Optimality** (2 marks):
- **Complete?** No in infinite spaces (may follow an infinite branch forever). Yes in finite spaces.
- **Optimal?** No — may find a deeper or costlier path before the optimal one.

**Diagram of recursion vs iterative** (2 marks): Mention that recursion uses the call stack implicitly; iterative DFS uses an explicit stack.

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

| Step | Action | Stack (top→bottom) | Visited | Path So Far |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Push S | `[S]` | `{S}` | — |
| 1 | Pop S → push A, D | `[A, D]` | `{S, A, D}` | S |
| 2 | Pop A → push B, C | `[B, C, D]` | `{S,A,D,B,C}` | S→A |
| 3 | Pop B → push G | `[G, C, D]` | `{S,A,D,B,C,G}` | S→A→B |
| 4 | Pop G → **GOAL** | — | — | **S→A→B→G** |

**Path returned:** S → A → B → G (length 3).
**Note:** The shortest path S→D→E→G (length 3 as well) exists, demonstrating DFS's non-optimality in general.
(Full marks: 1 for stack tracking setup, 4 for stepwise tracking, 1 for final path, 1 for comment on optimality)

---

> **Q3(b). `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2]`** *Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast Depth-First Search (DFS) and Breadth-First Search (BFS) with respect to: (i) data structure used, (ii) completeness, (iii) optimality, (iv) time complexity, (v) space complexity, and (vi) typical application scenarios.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Depth-Limited Search (DLS)** and **Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS)**. Why is IDDFS considered the best of both worlds?

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

| Criterion (1 mark each for first 5, 1 for application, 1 for synthesis) | BFS | DFS |
|---|---|---|
| Data structure | Queue (FIFO) | Stack (LIFO) |
| Complete? | Yes (finite, $b$ finite) | No (infinite spaces) |
| Optimal? | Yes (unit step costs) | No |
| Time | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^m)$ |
| Space | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bm)$ |
| Applications | Shortest path in unweighted graphs; level-order traversal | Topological sort, cycle detection, maze solving, game trees |

(1 mark for diagram or synthesis: BFS explores uniformly; DFS dives deep; BFS wastes memory; DFS wastes time on wrong deep paths.)

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**Depth-Limited Search (DLS):** DFS with a predefined depth cutoff $L$ (3 marks). Nodes at depth $L$ are treated as having no successors. Solves the *infinite-path problem* of vanilla DFS. Time $O(b^L)$, Space $O(bL)$, Complete only if $L \geq d$ (goal depth), Not optimal.

**Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS):** Runs DLS repeatedly with $L = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ until goal is found (2 marks). Each iteration starts fresh, so it is complete and optimal (for unit costs) like BFS, but uses only $O(bd)$ space like DFS.

**Why "best of both worlds"** (2 marks): Combines BFS's completeness + optimality with DFS's low memory footprint. Time complexity is still $O(b^d)$ — the overhead of repeated shallow searches is asymptotically negligible because most nodes reside at the deepest level. This is why KTU examiners frequently cite IDDFS as the *preferred* blind search method.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in DFS Questions — KTU Board Patterns:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $O(bm)$ with $O(b \cdot m)$:** Examiners want $O(bm)$ — *multiplicative product written without the dot*. Writing "$O(b \times m)$" is acceptable; writing "$O(b+m)$" is **wrong** and loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Claiming DFS is complete:** It is *NOT* complete in infinite spaces. Saying "DFS is complete" without qualification will lose 1 mark. Correct phrasing: "DFS is complete only in finite search spaces."
> 3. **Confusing LIFO with FIFO:** Stack ≠ Queue. Mixing them up in a comparison table costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the visited set:** In graph-based DFS (not tree-based), failing to mention a `visited` set when the graph has cycles loses 2 marks. Examiners specifically look for this in algorithmic trace questions.
> 5. **Skipping backtracking explanation:** Tracing DFS without explicitly stating *when* and *why* backtracking occurs costs 1 mark in 7-mark algorithmic questions.
> 6. **Unit omission:** Writing "$b$" without defining it as the branching factor costs 0.5 marks. Always define symbols on first use.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DFS Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Board-Exam Ready):**
>
> - ✅ **Definition:** Uninformed search that expands the deepest frontier node first.
> - ✅ **Data Structure:** **Stack (LIFO)** — or recursion.
> - ✅ **Time Complexity:** $O(b^m)$ — derived from geometric series.
> - ✅ **Space Complexity:** $O(bm)$ — *linear in depth*, the key advantage.
> - ✅ **Complete?** Only in **finite** search spaces. **NOT** complete in infinite spaces.
> - ✅ **Optimal?** **NO** — does not guarantee shortest or lowest-cost path.
> - ✅ **Backtracking** is *implicit* — it emerges from the LIFO stack behavior.
> - ✅ **Visited set** is essential for graph-based DFS to avoid infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
> - ✅ **Variants to know for KTU:** Depth-Limited Search (DLS) with cutoff $L$, and Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS) which loops $L = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$
> - ✅ **Engineering applications:** Topological sorting, cycle detection, garbage collection, game-tree search, maze solving.
> - ✅ **Comparison mantra:** BFS = "shallow and wide" (Queue), DFS = "deep and narrow" (Stack).
> - ✅ **Exam tip:** When a 14-mark question asks for "search strategy comparison," always include the four standard metrics (completeness, optimality, time, space) in a **table format** — KTU examiners award easy marks for tabular organization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heuristic search: hill climbing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT502 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heuristic search: hill climbing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heuristic Search** is an informed search strategy that uses a domain-specific **heuristic function** $h(n)$ — an estimate of the cost or distance from any state $n$ to the goal — to guide the search process and prioritize more promising states over uninformed exploration.

**Hill Climbing** is a fundamental **greedy, local search algorithm** belonging to the family of heuristic (informed) search techniques. It is an *iterative improvement algorithm* that continuously moves toward states of *higher value* (lower cost) by evaluating a heuristic evaluation function.

Mathematically, if $f(s)$ is the evaluation function for a state $s$, the algorithm transitions to a successor state $s'$ if and only if:

$$f(s') > f(s)$$

The process halts when **no successor state has a value greater than the current state**, indicating a local optimum has been reached.

### Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Standard)

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Heuristic Function $h(n)$** | An estimated measure of "goodness" or cost-to-goal from state $n$ |
| **Evaluation Function $f(n)$** | The function used to rank states (often equal to $h(n)$) |
| **State Space** | The complete set of all possible configurations of the problem |
| **Local Maxima** | A state better than all its neighbors but **not** the global optimum |
| **Global Maximum** | The best possible state in the entire state space |
| **Plateau** | A flat region in the state space where neighboring states have equal evaluation |
| **Ridge** | A region where directional movement leads to a slope |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Hill Climbing"?** The name derives directly from the analogy of a mountaineer attempting to reach the highest peak in a foggy landscape — they can only see their immediate surroundings and must decide their next step based on local information.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Foggy Mountain Climber

Imagine a **hiker trapped in thick fog on a mountainous terrain** at an unknown location. Their goal is to reach the **highest peak**. They cannot see the entire mountain range; they can only feel the **slope of the ground directly beneath their feet**.

- **Step 1:** The hiker checks all 8 directions (N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW).
- **Step 2:** They take a step toward whichever direction goes **steepest uphill**.
- **Step 3:** They repeat this until — upon checking all 8 directions — **every direction goes downhill or is flat**. They declare they have reached the "summit" and stop.

This summit may be:
- ✅ **The true Mount Everest** (Global Maxima — success!)
- ⚠️ **A small foothill** (Local Maxima — failure, but the algorithm halts thinking it succeeded)

This physical analogy directly maps to how hill climbing works in AI — the *hiker* is the *current state*, the *ground slope* is the *heuristic function value*, and the *terrain* is the *state space*.

---

The course outcome alignment is:

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Variants of Hill Climbing

KTU examiners frequently test the **three classical variants** of hill climbing. Understanding their differences is critical for exam success.

### A. Simple Hill Climbing (Next-Ascent)

- **Logic:** Evaluates successors **one at a time** in a fixed order.
- **Decision Rule:** Move to the *first* successor that is better than the current state.
- **Trade-off:** ⚡ Fast, but **can miss better successors** that appear later in the order.

### B. Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing (Best-First Variant)

- **Logic:** Examines **all** successor states exhaustively before deciding.
- **Decision Rule:** Move to the successor with the **highest evaluation value** among all neighbors.
- **Trade-off:** 🎯 More accurate, but **slower** due to full neighborhood evaluation.

### C. Stochastic Hill Climbing

- **Logic:** Selects a successor **randomly** from the set of "better" successors.
- **Decision Rule:** Pick a random uphill move with probability proportional to steepness.
- **Trade-off:** 🎲 Avoids getting trapped in some local maxima through randomness.

---

## 2.2 The General Hill Climbing Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this algorithm** — it appears in nearly every KTU exam paper either directly or as a precursor to A\* search.

The operational logic can be decomposed into **8 distinct phases**:

1. **Initialization Phase:** Construct the initial state $s_0$ (randomly or heuristically).
2. **Evaluation Phase:** Compute $f(s_0)$ using the heuristic function.
3. **Loop Entry:** If $s_0$ is a goal state → **terminate with success**.
4. **Neighbor Generation:** Generate the set of all successor states $S' = \{s_1', s_2', ..., s_n'\}$.
5. **Successor Evaluation:** Compute $f(s_i')$ for every $s_i' \in S'$.
6. **Selection Step:** Identify $s^* = \arg\max_{s_i' \in S'} f(s_i')$.
7. **Acceptance Test:**
   - If $f(s^*) > f(s_0)$ → set $s_0 = s^*$ and **go to Step 3** (continue climbing).
   - Otherwise → **terminate with failure (local maxima reached)**.
8. **Return Solution:** Output the final state $s_0$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this table** — these equations appear in derivations, numerical problems, and theory questions.

| Concept | Mathematical Formulation | KTU Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal Test** | $f(s) = f_{goal}$ | $f_{goal}$ is the optimum value |
| **Acceptance Criterion** | $f(s_{next}) > f(s_{current})$ | Strict inequality for maximization |
| **Acceptance Criterion (Min)** | $f(s_{next}) < f(s_{current})$ | For minimization problems |
| **8-Queens Heuristic** | $h(n) = \text{number of attacking queen pairs}$ | Range: $0 \leq h(n) \leq 28$ |
| **Max Attacking Pairs** | $h_{max} = \sum_{i=1}^{7} i = 28$ | Sum of 1 to 7 |
| **Probability of Random Move** | $P(s_i) = \frac{e^{f(s_i)/T}}{\sum_j e^{f(s_j)/T}}$ | Boltzmann distribution (extension) |
| **Worst-Case Time Complexity** | $O(\vert S \vert)$ | Where $\vert S \vert$ = state space size |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(1)$ | Only current state is stored |
| **Branching Factor** | $b = \vert N(s) \vert$ | Number of neighbors of state $s$ |
| **Plateau Count Condition** | $\Delta f(s, s') = 0$ | Flat region indicator |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Notation Note:** In markdown tables, $\vert$ and $\mid$ are used as vertical bar operators in LaTeX (not the table column separator), ensuring the table renders correctly.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

Hill climbing and its derivatives power several real-world systems:

- **🛰️ VLSI Chip Design:** Placing transistors to minimize wire length.
- **📡 Telecommunications Network Design:** Optimizing antenna placement and routing.
- **🎮 Game AI (Chess/Go Engines):** Position evaluation for next-move selection.
- **🚗 Autonomous Vehicle Path Planning:** Local trajectory optimization.
- **🏭 Job-Shop Scheduling:** Minimizing makespan in manufacturing systems.
- **🧬 Bioinformatics:** RNA secondary structure prediction.
- **📊 Machine Learning:** Hyperparameter tuning via *random search* variants.

---

## 2.5 Critical Problems of Hill Climbing (HIGH-YIELD FOR EXAMS)

> [!WARNING]
> **These are the most tested weaknesses** — KTU expects students to identify, explain, AND propose solutions.

### Problem 1: Local Maxima ⛰️
- **Definition:** A state where *no neighbor is better*, but the state is **not the global optimum**.
- **Symptom:** Algorithm halts prematurely with a suboptimal solution.
- **Solution:** Apply **random restart** — run hill climbing multiple times from different initial states and keep the best result.

### Problem 2: Ridges 🏔️
- **Definition:** A sequence of local maxima that prevents direct ascent because the search oscillates between sideways moves.
- **Symptom:** The algorithm keeps moving sideways, never climbing.
- **Solution:** Use **bidirectional search** along the ridge, or apply **multi-direction moves**.

### Problem 3: Plateaus 🏞️
- **Definition:** A flat region where neighboring states have **equal evaluation values**.
- **Symptom:** The algorithm loses its gradient direction and may wander indefinitely.
- **Solution:** Apply a **sideways move limit** (e.g., allow up to 100 sideways moves before declaring failure) or use **stochastic hill climbing**.

### Problem 4: Diagonal Ridges (Antenna Problem)
- **Symptom:** A ridge rises in a direction not aligned with any single move, requiring complex multi-step sequences.
- **Solution:** Use **steeper-than-sideways move limits** combined with **2-step lookahead** evaluation.

---

## 2.6 Comparison with Other Search Strategies

| Property | Hill Climbing | Best-First Search | A* Search |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Uses Heuristic?** | Yes | Yes | Yes ($f = g + h$) |
| **Memory** | $O(1)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ |
| **Optimal?** | ❌ No | ❌ Not always | ✅ Yes (with admissible $h$) |
| **Complete?** | ❌ Not always | ✅ Yes (finite graphs) | ✅ Yes (finite graphs) |
| **Backtracking?** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Best Use Case** | Local optimization | Directed exploration | Shortest path with cost |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Example & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Numerical Example: 8-Queens Heuristic Evaluation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 8-Queens problem is the most heavily tested example in KTU exams** for hill climbing. Master the heuristic calculation method below.

### 3.1.1 Problem Formulation

Place **8 queens** on an **8×8 chessboard** such that no two queens attack each other. Two queens attack each other if they are in the **same row, column, or diagonal**.

### 3.1.2 The Heuristic Function

Let $h(s)$ = the number of **attacking pairs** of queens in state $s$.

$$h(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} \sum_{j=i+1}^{8} \mathbb{1}[\text{queens at } i \text{ and } j \text{ attack each other}]$$

where $\mathbb{1}$ is the indicator function (1 if true, 0 if false).

The **goal** is $h(s) = 0$.

The **maximum possible value** is the number of pairs in 8 queens:

$$h_{max} = \binom{8}{2} = 28$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

Consider the following initial state $s_0$ (queen positions given by row index for each column, read left to right):

$$s_0 = [3, 5, 0, 4, 1, 6, 2, 7]$$

(Column 1 has a queen in Row 3, Column 2 in Row 5, etc.)

**Step 1: Compute $h(s_0)$ — count all attacking pairs.**

We need to check **every pair** of columns $(i, j)$ where $i < j$ for attacks:

| Column Pair $(i,j)$ | Row Difference $\vert r_i - r_j \vert$ | Column Difference $\vert i - j \vert$ | Attacking? |
|---|---|---|---|
| (1,2) | $\vert 3-5 \vert = 2$ | 1 | No (not on diagonal) |
| (1,3) | $\vert 3-0 \vert = 3$ | 2 | No |
| (1,4) | $\vert 3-4 \vert = 1$ | 3 | No |
| (1,5) | $\vert 3-1 \vert = 2$ | 4 | No |
| (1,6) | $\vert 3-6 \vert = 3$ | 5 | No |
| (1,7) | $\vert 3-2 \vert = 1$ | 6 | No |
| (1,8) | $\vert 3-7 \vert = 4$ | 7 | No |
| (2,3) | $\vert 5-0 \vert = 5$ | 1 | No |
| (2,4) | $\vert 5-4 \vert = 1$ | 2 | No |
| (2,5) | $\vert 5-1 \vert = 4$ | 3 | No |
| (2,6) | $\vert 5-6 \vert = 1$ | 4 | No |
| (2,7) | $\vert 5-2 \vert = 3$ | 5 | No |
| (2,8) | $\vert 5-7 \vert = 2$ | 6 | No |
| (3,4) | $\vert 0-4 \vert = 4$ | 1 | No |
| (3,5) | $\vert 0-1 \vert = 1$ | 2 | No |
| (3,6) | $\vert 0-6 \vert = 6$ | 1 | No |
| (3,7) | $\vert 0-2 \vert = 2$ | 2 | **YES** (diag) |
| (3,8) | $\vert 0-7 \vert = 7$ | 1 | No |
| (4,5) | $\vert 4-1 \vert = 3$ | 1 | No |
| (4,6) | $\vert 4-6 \vert = 2$ | 2 | **YES** (diag) |
| (4,7) | $\vert 4-2 \vert = 2$ | 3 | No |
| (4,8) | $\vert 4-7 \vert = 3$ | 4 | No |
| (5,6) | $\vert 1-6 \vert = 5$ | 1 | No |
| (5,7) | $\vert 1-2 \vert = 1$ | 2 | No |
| (5,8) | $\vert 1-7 \vert = 6$ | 3 | No |
| (6,7) | $\vert 6-2 \vert = 4$ | 1 | No |
| (6,8) | $\vert 6-7 \vert = 1$ | 2 | No |
| (7,8) | $\vert 2-7 \vert = 5$ | 1 | No |

> [!NOTE]
> **Attack Rule:** Queens at columns $i$ and $j$ attack diagonally when $\vert r_i - r_j \vert = \vert i - j \vert$.

**Total attacking pairs in $s_0$:** $h(s_0) = 2$

### 3.1.4 Generate Successors (Move Queen Vertically)

For each column, we can move the queen to **7 other rows** (keeping the column fixed). This generates $8 \times 7 = 56$ successor states. The **best** successor is the one with the **minimum $h$ value**.

Suppose after evaluating all successors, we find the best move is moving the **queen in Column 7 from Row 2 to Row 4**:

$$s_1 = [3, 5, 0, 4, 1, 6, \mathbf{4}, 7]$$

The new heuristic value is $h(s_1) = 1$ (a single attacking pair, e.g., Columns 4 and 7).

### 3.1.5 Convergence Check

$$h(s_0) = 2, \quad h(s_1) = 1$$

Since $h(s_1) < h(s_0)$, we have **strict improvement**, and we move to $s_1$. The process continues until $h(s_k) = 0$ (success) or no successor has $h < h(s_k)$ (local minima — failure).

### 3.1.6 Probability Analysis (For Steepest-Ascent Variant)

The probability that steepest-ascent hill climbing solves a randomly-generated 8-Queens instance is approximately **14%** without random restarts. With random restarts, the probability of success approaches:

$$P(\text{success after } N \text{ restarts}) = 1 - (1 - p)^N$$

where $p \approx 0.14$. So with $N = 10$ restarts:

$$P(\text{success}) = 1 - (0.86)^{10} \approx 1 - 0.221 = 0.779 \text{ or } 77.9\%$$

This is why **random restarts are critical** in practice.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Performance Matrix

| Metric | Simple Hill Climbing | Steepest-Ascent | Stochastic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Time per iteration** | $O(b)$ (early exit) | $O(b)$ (full scan) | $O(b)$ (random sample) |
| **Solution Quality** | Poor | Moderate | Moderate to Good |
| **Local Maxima Trapping** | Frequent | Frequent | Reduced |
| **Memory Footprint** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic | Deterministic | Non-deterministic |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Low | Medium | Medium |

---

## 3.3 Mathematical Proof of Termination

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does hill climbing always terminate?** This is a classic KTU theoretical question.

For a **finite state space** of size $n$, the algorithm can make **at most $n$ state transitions** (it never revisits a state with a strictly better evaluation, and there are only $n$ possible states). Since the evaluation function $f$ produces a strictly monotonic improvement sequence:

$$f(s_0) < f(s_1) < f(s_2) < \cdots < f(s_k)$$

And $f$ maps to a finite set of values (the state space evaluations), the sequence must terminate. Therefore:

$$T_{max} = O(n) \text{ where } n = \vert \text{State Space} \vert$$

However, **this does not guarantee a correct (global) solution** — only termination.

---

## 3.4 Comparison: 8-Queens vs. Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)

| Property | 8-Queens | TSP |
|---|---|---|
| **State Space Size** | $\approx 4.4 \times 10^9$ | $n!$ |
| **Heuristic** | $h$ = attacking pairs | $h$ = total tour length |
| **Goal $h$ value** | $0$ | Theoretical minimum |
| **Steepest-Ascent Success** | $\sim 14\%$ | $< 1\%$ for $n=20$ |
| **Preferred Algorithm** | HC + Random Restarts | Simulated Annealing / GA |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hill Climbing Control Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> **This diagram precisely mirrors the algorithm evaluated by KTU examiners** — memorize the control flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([START]) --> init[Initialize current state s0]
    init --> eval[Compute f_s0 using heuristic]
    eval --> goaltest{Is s0 the goal?}
    goaltest -->|Yes| success([SUCCESS: Return s0])
    goaltest -->|No| gen[Generate all successor states Sprime]
    gen --> evalall[Evaluate f for every successor]
    evalall --> select[Select s_best = argmax f_sprime]
    select --> check{f_s_best greater than f_s0?}
    check -->|Yes| update[Set s0 = s_best]
    update --> eval
    check -->|No| localmax[Local Maxima Detected]
    localmax --> restart{Apply Random Restart?}
    restart -->|Yes| init
    restart -->|No| failure([FAILURE: Return s0 as local optimum])
    success --> stopend([END])
    failure --> stopend
```
