Initial bounds: $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$.

**Step 2: Descend into A (MIN).**
Pass current bounds: $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$.

**Step 3: Descend into A1 (MIN).**
Pass current bounds: $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$.

**Step 4: Evaluate leaf = 3.**
At MIN-node A1: $v = \min(+\infty, 3) = 3$.
Update $\beta = \min(+\infty, 3) = 3$.
Check cutoff: $v \leq \alpha$? i.e. $3 \leq -\infty$? **No.** Continue.

**Step 5: Evaluate leaf = 12.**
At A1: $v = \min(3, 12) = 3$.
Update $\beta = \min(3, 3) = 3$.
Return $v = 3$ to A.

**Step 6: Back at A (MIN) — first child returned 3.**
Update $v_A = 3$.
At MAX-level logic for A's parent: $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 3) = 3$.

**Step 7: Descend into A2 (MIN) with $(\alpha, \beta) = (3, +\infty)$.**

**Step 8: Evaluate leaf = 2.**
At A2: $v = \min(+\infty, 2) = 2$.
Update $\beta = \min(+\infty, 2) = 2$.
Check cutoff: $v \leq \alpha$? i.e. $2 \leq 3$? **YES — $\alpha$-cutoff triggered.**

**Step 9: PRUNE — leaf "8" under A2 is skipped.**
Return $v = 2$ to A.

**Step 10: Back at A (MIN).**
$v_A = \min(3, 2) = 2$.
Return 2 to M. Update $\alpha$ at M: $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 2) = 2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pruning #1 Achieved:** The subtree rooted at the second child of A2 (leaf = 8) is pruned because MAX already has a guaranteed move worth 3 from A1, and MIN at A2 cannot drag the value below 2 — which is strictly worse for MAX.

**Step 11: Descend into B (MIN) with $(\alpha, \beta) = (2, +\infty)$.**

**Step 12: Descend into B1 (MIN) with $(\alpha, \beta) = (2, +\infty)$.**

**Step 13: Evaluate leaf = 14.**
At B1: $v = \min(+\infty, 14) = 14$.
Update $\beta = \min(+\infty, 14) = 14$.
Check cutoff: $v \leq \alpha$? i.e. $14 \leq 2$? **No.** Continue.

**Step 14: Evaluate leaf = 5.**
At B1: $v = \min(14, 5) = 5$.
Update $\beta = \min(14, 5) = 5$.
Return 5 to B.

**Step 15: Back at B (MIN).**
At B's parent M (MAX): $\alpha = \max(2, 5) = 5$.

**Step 16: Descend into B2 (MIN) with $(\alpha, \beta) = (5, +\infty)$.**

**Step 17: Evaluate leaf = 2.**
At B2: $v = \min(+\infty, 2) = 2$.
Update $\beta = \min(+\infty, 2) = 2$.
Check cutoff: $v \leq \alpha$? i.e. $2 \leq 5$? **YES — $\alpha$-cutoff triggered.**

**Step 18: PRUNE — leaf "8" under B2 is skipped.**
Return 2 to B.

**Step 19: Back at B (MIN).**
$v_B = \min(5, 2) = 2$.

**Step 20: Back at Root M (MAX).**
$$V(M) = \max(V(A), V(B)) = \max(2, 2) = 2$$

Wait — recheck: $V(A) = 2$, $V(B) = 2$. The root's optimal value is **2**, with **A** being chosen as the first move.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pruning Log

| Step | Node Visited | Node Type | $\alpha$ | $\beta$ | Leaf Value | Action | Pruned? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | M | MAX | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | — | Descend A | — |
| 2 | A | MIN | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | — | Descend A1 | — |
| 3 | A1 | MIN | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | 3 | $\beta \to 3$ | No |
| 4 | A1 | MIN | $-\infty$ | 3 | 12 | $v=3$ | No |
| 5 | A | MIN | 3 | $+\infty$ | — | Descend A2 | — |
| 6 | A2 | MIN | 3 | $+\infty$ | 2 | $v=2 \leq \alpha=3$ | **YES — PRUNE leaf 8** |
| 7 | B | MIN | 2 | $+\infty$ | — | Descend B1 | — |
| 8 | B1 | MIN | 2 | $+\infty$ | 14 | $\beta \to 14$ | No |
| 9 | B1 | MIN | 2 | 14 | 5 | $v=5$ | No |
| 10 | B | MIN | 5 | $+\infty$ | — | Descend B2 | — |
| 11 | B2 | MIN | 5 | $+\infty$ | 2 | $v=2 \leq \alpha=5$ | **YES — PRUNE leaf 8** |
| 12 | M | MAX | 5 | $+\infty$ | — | Return 2 | — |

**Result:** **2 leaves pruned** out of 8 → effective reduction = **25%** with imperfect ordering; with perfect ordering, we would prune up to **6 leaves (75%)** in this 8-leaf tree.

### 3.4 Final Decision

$$\boxed{V_{\text{root}} = 2, \quad \text{Best first move} = A}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Game-Tree Architecture with Alpha-Beta Pruning

The following Mermaid diagram visualises the same 4-ply tree with pruning annotations. Node identifiers use alphanumeric prefixes to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Mnode["M MAX  alpha=-inf  beta=+inf"]
    Anode["A MIN  alpha=-inf  beta=+inf"]
    Bnode["B MIN  alpha=2  beta=+inf"]
    A1node["A1 MIN  alpha=-inf  beta=+inf"]
    A2node["A2 MIN  alpha=3  beta=+inf"]
    B1node["B1 MIN  alpha=2  beta=+inf"]
    B2node["B2 MIN  alpha=5  beta=+inf"]
    L1["Leaf 3"]
    L2["Leaf 12"]
    L3["Leaf 2"]
    L4["Leaf 8  PRUNED"]
    L5["Leaf 14"]
    L6["Leaf 5"]
    L7["Leaf 2"]
    L8["Leaf 8  PRUNED"]

    Mnode --> Anode
    Mnode --> Bnode
    Anode --> A1node
    Anode --> A2node
    Bnode --> B1node
    Bnode --> B2node
    A1node --> L1
    A1node --> L2
    A2node --> L3
    A2node -.-> L4
    B1node --> L5
    B1node --> L6
    B2node --> L7
    B2node -.-> L8

    style L4 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-dasharray:5 5
    style L8 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-dasharray:5 5
    style Mnode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style Anode fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style Bnode fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#cc6600
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Solid arrows** = edges traversed during alpha-beta search.
- **Dotted arrows** = edges skipped due to pruning.
- **Blue node** = MAX layer; **Orange nodes** = MIN layers.
- **Red dashed leaves** = pruned subtrees (no evaluation cost incurred).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Flow (Alpha-Beta Control Logic)

The following block schematic captures the *flow of control* between the recursive Max-Value and Min-Value functions, illustrating how the $(\alpha, \beta)$ window is passed, tightened, and used to short-circuit further recursion.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Begin: alpha=-inf, beta=+inf])
    CallMax["Invoke MAX-VALUE state, alpha, beta"]
    CheckTerm{"Is state Terminal?"}
    EvalUtil["Return Utility state"]
    InitVmax["v := -inf"]
    LoopMax{"For each child c"}
    CallMin["Invoke MIN-VALUE c, alpha, beta"]
    UpdateVmax["v := max v, returned"]
    BetaCutoff{"v >= beta?"}
    ReturnVmax["Return v"]
    CheckBoundMax["alpha := max alpha, v"]
    NextChildMax["Continue loop"]

    Start --> CallMax --> CheckTerm
    CheckTerm -- Yes --> EvalUtil
    CheckTerm -- No --> InitVmax --> LoopMax
    LoopMax -- Each child --> CallMin --> UpdateVmax
    UpdateVmax --> BetaCutoff
    BetaCutoff -- Yes --> ReturnVmax
    BetaCutoff -- No --> CheckBoundMax --> NextChildMax
    NextChildMax --> LoopMax
    LoopMax -- All done --> ReturnVmax
```

**Key functional insights from the flowchart:**

1. The **Beta Cutoff** at MAX is the only path that returns early without exploring all children.
2. The recursive descent alternates MAX ↔ MIN with the alpha/beta window carried unchanged until a value is returned.
3. After *each* child returns, the parent's bound is updated **before** checking the next child.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define alpha-beta pruning. State the role of the α and β parameters with their initial values.**

**Model Answer:**

Alpha-beta pruning is an **optimisation of the Minimax algorithm** that eliminates branches of the game tree which cannot influence the final decision at the root, thereby reducing the number of nodes evaluated.

- **α (alpha):** The value of the **best (maximum)** move that the **MAX player** has found so far along the current path. Initial value: $\alpha = -\infty$.
- **β (beta):** The value of the **best (minimum)** move that the **MIN player** has found so far along the current path. Initial value: $\beta = +\infty$.

A subtree is pruned as soon as $\alpha \geq \beta$ at any node, because the opponent has already secured a better alternative higher up the tree.

*(3 marks — full credit requires both definitions and initial values.)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Compare the time complexity of plain Minimax and alpha-beta pruning in the best case. What is meant by "perfect move ordering"?**

**Model Answer:**

| Algorithm | Best-Case Time Complexity | Effective Branching Factor |
|---|---|---|
| Plain Minimax | $O(b^d)$ | $b$ |
| Alpha-Beta (best ordering) | $O(b^{d/2})$ | $\sqrt{b}$ |

**Perfect move ordering** means exploring the **best move first** at every node — the move most likely to cause a cutoff. With perfect ordering, alpha-beta evaluates only the **leftmost (best) branch fully** and prunes everything else, achieving the square-root reduction.

*(3 marks — table + definition both required.)*

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> Each Part B question carries an internal choice. You are required to attempt **ONE** of Q1A / Q1B and **ONE** of Q2A / Q2B. Sub-parts carry **7 + 7 = 14** marks.

---

### **Q1A.** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a) Explain the Minimax algorithm with a suitable state-space formulation. How does it work for two-player zero-sum games? (7 marks)**

**(b) Apply alpha-beta pruning to the following 4-ply game tree. Show all $(\alpha, \beta)$ values, the pruned branches, and the final minimax value. Identify the best first move. (7 marks)**

```
                      [M  MAX]
                     /        \
                [A MIN]      [B MIN]
                /     \       /     \
             [A1 MAX][A2 MAX][B1 MAX][B2 MAX]
             /  \    /  \    /  \    /  \
            3   12  2   8  14   5   2   8
```

*(Note: This variant uses MAX at A1/A2/B1/B2. You must adapt the cutoff logic accordingly: at MAX children, check $v \geq \beta$; at MIN children, check $v \leq \alpha$.)*

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a) Minimax Algorithm

**Definition:** Minimax is a recursive depth-first algorithm for two-player, **zero-sum**, **perfect-information** games. Player MAX aims to maximise the utility; player MIN aims to minimise it.

**Recursive formulation:**

$$V(n) = \begin{cases} \text{Utility}(n) & \text{terminal} \\ \max\limits_{s} V(s) & \text{MAX node} \\ \min\limits_{s} V(s) & \text{MIN node} \end{cases}$$

**Working principle:**

- The algorithm builds the **full game tree** up to terminal states (or to a fixed depth, with an evaluation function $f(n)$ at the cutoff).
- MAX chooses the move leading to the **maximum** minimax value.
- MIN chooses the move leading to the **minimum** minimax value.
- Optimal play assumes **both players play perfectly**.

**Key properties:**

- **Completeness:** Yes (if tree is finite).
- **Optimality:** Yes (against an optimal opponent).
- **Time complexity:** $O(b^d)$.
- **Space complexity:** $O(b \cdot d)$ (DFS).

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b) Alpha-Beta Pruning

**Trace table** (note: in this variant, A1/A2/B1/B2 are MAX, so beta cutoffs are checked there):

| Step | Node | Type | $\alpha$ | $\beta$ | Leaf / Action | Pruned? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | M | MAX | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | Descend A | — |
| 2 | A | MIN | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | Descend A1 | — |
| 3 | A1 | MAX | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | Leaf 3 → v=3, β→3 | No |
| 4 | A1 | MAX | $-\infty$ | 3 | Leaf 12 → v=12 ≥ β=3 | **YES — β-cutoff, prune A12** |
| 5 | A | MIN | $-\infty$ | 3 | v=3, α→3 | — |
| 6 | A | MIN | 3 | $+\infty$ | Descend A2 | — |
| 7 | A2 | MAX | 3 | $+\infty$ | Leaf 2 → v=2, β→2 | No |
| 8 | A2 | MAX | 3 | 2 | $v=2 \leq \alpha=3$ triggers | — (continues) |
| 9 | A2 | MAX | 3 | 2 | Leaf 8 → v=max(2,8)=8 ≥ β=2 | **YES — β-cutoff, prune** |
| 10 | A | MIN | 3 | 2 | v=min(3,8)=3 | — |
| 11 | M | MAX | 3 | 2 | v=3, descend B | — |
| 12 | B | MIN | 3 | 2 | Leaf 14 → v=2, β→2 | No (since 14>2) |
| 13 | B | MIN | 3 | 2 | $v=2 \leq \alpha=3$ triggers | — |
| 14 | B | MIN | 3 | 2 | Leaf 5 → v=min(2,5)=2 | No (already pruned by α) |

**Best first move = A** (since A=3 vs B=2). The root value is **V(M) = 3**, with A and B yielding 3 and 2 respectively after pruning.

---

### **Q1B (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a) Differentiate between Minimax and Alpha-Beta pruning algorithms in terms of optimality, time complexity, and the use of cutoff conditions. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain with a diagram the working of alpha-beta pruning on a binary game tree of depth 3 with leaf values {5, 1, 3, 8, 2, 9, 4, 7}. Show pruned leaves. (7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a) Comparison Table

| Parameter | Minimax | Alpha-Beta Pruning |
|---|---|---|
| **Optimality** | Optimal | Equally optimal (same root move) |
| **Time (worst)** | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ |
| **Time (best)** | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^{d/2})$ |
| **Time (avg)** | $O(b^d)$ | $\approx O(b^{3d/4})$ |
| **Space** | $O(bd)$ | $O(bd)$ |
| **Cutoff used?** | No | Yes ($\alpha \geq \beta$) |
| **Search order** | Any | Order matters (best-first is critical) |

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b) Pruning Trace

```
                            [M MAX] α=-∞, β=+∞
                           /        \
                      [A MIN]      [B MIN]
                      α=-∞,β=+∞   α=5, β=+∞
                      /     \      /     \
                  [A1 MAX] [A2 MAX][B1 MAX][B2 MAX]
                  α=-∞,β=+∞ α=5,β=+∞ α=5,β=+∞ α=5,β=+∞
                  /  \      /  \    /  \    /  \
                 5    1    3    8   2    9   4    7
```

| Step | Node | Type | $\alpha$ | $\beta$ | Action | Prune? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A1 | MAX | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | 5 → v=5, β=5 | No |
| 2 | A1 | MAX | $-\infty$ | 5 | 1 → v=5, β=5 | No |
| 3 | A | MIN | 5 | $+\infty$ | A=5, α→5 | — |
| 4 | A2 | MAX | 5 | $+\infty$ | 3 → v=3, β=3 | No |
| 5 | A2 | MAX | 5 | 3 | $v=3 \leq \alpha=5$ triggers | — |
| 6 | A2 | MAX | 5 | 3 | 8 → v=max(3,8)=8 ≥ β=3 | **YES — β-cutoff** |
| 7 | B | MIN | 5 | $+\infty$ | 2 → v=2, β=2 | No |
| 8 | B | MIN | 5 | 2 | $v=2 \leq \alpha=5$ triggers | — |
| 9 | B | MIN | 5 | 2 | 9 → v=min(2,9)=2 | **YES — α-cutoff** |
| 10 | B2 | MAX | 5 | 2 | Not reached | **YES** |

**Pruned leaves: 8, 9, and both leaves under B2** (i.e., 8, 9, 4, 7). Total pruned = **4 of 8 (50%)**.

---

### **Q2A.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) What is a game tree? Explain the concepts of MAX nodes, MIN nodes, terminal states, and utility function with a chess example. (7 marks)**

**(b) Prove that alpha-beta pruning returns the same minimax value as plain Minimax at the root node. (7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a) Game Tree Components

- **Game Tree:** A tree where each node is a **game state**, and each branch is a legal **move** leading to a successor state.
- **MAX nodes:** Layers where the player to move (the AI) wants to **maximise** the utility.
- **MIN nodes:** Layers where the **opponent** wants to **minimise** the utility.
- **Terminal states:** End-of-game positions (checkmate, stalemate, draw) with no successors.
- **Utility function** $U(n)$: A numeric score at terminal states. In chess: win=+∞, loss=−∞, draw=0.

**Chess example:** The root is the current board; depth-1 children are the legal moves (e.g., e4, d4, Nf3); depth-d leaves are the result of a search-depth-d simulation; minimax then propagates the optimal score up.

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b) Correctness Proof Sketch

**Claim:** Alpha-beta returns the same value as Minimax for the root.

**Proof by induction on depth $d$:**

- **Base case ($d=0$):** Both algorithms return $\text{Utility}(n)$ at terminal nodes. Identical.
- **Inductive hypothesis:** Suppose alpha-beta and Minimax return the same value for all nodes of depth $\leq k-1$.
- **Inductive step (MAX node at depth $k$):**
  - Let $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_m$ be the children's values computed by alpha-beta.
  - Suppose alpha-beta cuts off after child $j$. By the cutoff rule, $v_j \geq \beta$, and $\alpha$ is the value of the best previously seen move $v_i$ where $i<j$.
  - For Minimax: $V(n) = \max(v_1, \ldots, v_m)$. Since $v_j \geq \beta \geq \alpha = v_i$, the pruned children cannot change the maximum — they would need to exceed $v_j$, but MIN (their grandparent context) is already unwilling to allow that.
  - Therefore the max remains unchanged. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Q2B (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) Discuss the effect of move ordering on the efficiency of alpha-beta pruning. What is iterative deepening? (7 marks)**

**(b) For a chess-like game with $b = 35$ and depth $d = 6$, compute: (i) nodes in plain minimax, (ii) best-case alpha-beta nodes, (iii) effective depth gained. (7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a) Move Ordering

- **Best move first** at every node maximises cutoffs. Random ordering gives $\approx b^{3d/4}$; perfect ordering gives $b^{d/2}$.
- **Heuristics for ordering:** killer-move heuristic, history heuristic, transposition tables, MVV-LVA (Most Valuable Victim — Least Valuable Attacker).
- **Iterative Deepening (ID):** A loop runs depth-1 search, then depth-2, ..., up to time budget. Each iteration provides a **best-first ordering** for the next via the move chosen at the previous shallower depth. ID + alpha-beta = gold standard in game engines.

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b) Numerical Computation

Given: $b = 35$, $d = 6$.

**(i) Plain Minimax nodes:**

$$N_{\text{minimax}} = b^d = 35^6$$

$$35^2 = 1225, \quad 35^3 = 42875, \quad 35^6 = (35^3)^2 = 42875^2 \approx 1.838 \times 10^9$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{minimax}} \approx 1.84 \times 10^9 \text{ nodes}}$$

**(ii) Best-case Alpha-Beta nodes:**

$$N_{\alpha\beta}^{\text{best}} = b^{d/2} = 35^{6/2} = 35^3 = 42{,}875$$

$$\boxed{N_{\alpha\beta}^{\text{best}} \approx 4.29 \times 10^4 \text{ nodes}}$$

**(iii) Effective Depth Gained:**

Speedup factor:

$$\frac{N_{\text{minimax}}}{N_{\alpha\beta}^{\text{best}}} = \frac{35^6}{35^3} = 35^3 \approx 42{,}875$$

Effective depth at the **same** cost as plain minimax at depth $d$:

$$b^{d_{\text{eff}}} = b^d \implies d_{\text{eff}} = d = 6 \text{ (same), but with same cost we can go to:}$$

$$b^{d'} = b^d \implies d' = 2d = 12$$

$$\boxed{d_{\text{eff}} = 12 \text{ (doubled depth)}}$$

This proves that alpha-beta with perfect ordering can search **twice as deep** for the same computational cost — a literal "free lunch" in game-tree search.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in alpha-beta questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing which node type triggers which cutoff.** A beta-cutoff ($\alpha$-cutoff) happens at a **MAX** (**MIN**) node, NOT both. The student who writes "beta-cutoff at MIN" loses 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting to update $\alpha$ or $\beta$ after each child return.** The bound check is meaningless if the bound itself wasn't updated.
> 3. **Confusing the strict vs. non-strict inequality.** Using $\alpha > \beta$ instead of $\alpha \geq \beta$ is acceptable (they're equivalent in this context), but writing $\alpha = \beta$ as a "no prune" condition is **wrong** — it does cause a cutoff.
> 4. **Returning the wrong root value.** The root's value is the maximum (or minimum, depending on player) of the **pruned** child values, not of all leaves.
> 5. **Not labelling pruned subtrees clearly in the diagram.** The examiner expects a visual cue (dashed line, X mark, or red colour). Without it, partial credit is lost on the "show pruned branches" sub-question.
> 6. **Mixing up alpha and beta initial values.** Both must be stated; examiners often award a separate 1 mark for these.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Alpha-Beta Pruning** is an *optimisation* of Minimax — it returns the **same optimal move**, not a different one.
- **Initial bounds:** $\alpha = -\infty$ and $\beta = +\infty$.
- **Pruning condition:** $\alpha \geq \beta$ at any node → prune remaining siblings.
- **Beta-cutoff** at MAX: $v \geq \beta$ → MAX cannot be forced to choose worse.
- **Alpha-cutoff** at MIN: $v \leq \alpha$ → MIN cannot be forced to choose worse.
- **Best-case complexity:** $O(b^{d/2})$ — **doubles the searchable depth** for the same cost.
- **Worst-case complexity:** $O(b^d)$ — same as plain Minimax.
- **Perfect move ordering** is the key to achieving best-case complexity.
- **Depth-first traversal** is mandatory (bounds tighten only along a path).
- **Space complexity** is unchanged: $O(bd)$.
- **Real-world enablers:** Iterative Deepening + Transposition Tables + Move Ordering + Alpha-Beta = modern chess engine core.
- **Common in KTU exams:** Expect a 4-ply or 6-ply binary tree, leaf values given, and the ask: "apply alpha-beta pruning and find the root value + pruned branches."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation functions and heuristics in game playing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCAT502 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Evaluation functions and heuristics in game playing # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition — Evaluation Function > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Evaluation Function, $f(n)$):** > An *evaluation function* (also called a **static evaluator**) is a heuristic function that assigns a numerical score to a non-terminal game state, estimating its expected utility from the perspective of the player whose turn it is to move. It enables an agent to rank successor positions without expanding the search tree all the way to terminal (win/loss/draw) states. Mathematically, for a game position $n$: $$f(n) = \text{estimated utility of } n \text{ from the current player's perspective}$$ The sign convention is: - $f(n) > 0$ → position is favorable to the player to move (**MAX**) - $f(n) < 0$ → position is favorable to the opponent (**MIN**) - $f(n) = 0$ → position is balanced - $f(n) = +\infty$ → immediate winning state for MAX - $f(n) = -\infty$ → immediate losing state for MAX ## 1.2 Formal Definition — Heuristic in Game Playing > [!NOTE] > **Definition (Game-Playing Heuristic):** > A *heuristic* in adversarial search is a domain-specific, computationally cheap rule of thumb that guides the search toward promising branches, prunes hopeless ones, or estimates the value of unexpanded nodes. Heuristics trade off *guaranteed optimality* for *feasible computation under time limits*. ## 1.3 Intuitive Overview — The Chess Master's "Glance" > [!TIP] > **Conceptual Analogy — The Café Owner:** > Imagine a café owner who must decide whether a particular day will be profitable. Instead of simulating every customer interaction, every coffee brewed, every electricity unit consumed (an intractable **search to terminal state**), the owner uses a few quick rules of thumb: *"Is it raining? Is there a festival? Is it a Monday?"* Combining these simple signals into a single number — say, "expected profit score" — gives a fast, **good-enough** estimate. > > In game playing, the **evaluation function** is exactly this rule-of-thumb estimator. The chess grandmaster does not mentally play out all 40 moves to checkmate; they glance at the position, count pieces, check king safety, scan for weak pawns, and form a near-instant judgment. That judgment is the evaluation function in action. ## 1.4 Why Are Evaluation Functions Necessary? A complete game tree for chess has approximately $b \approx 35$ legal moves per position and $d \approx 80$ plies (half-moves) to a typical conclusion. The total number of nodes is on the order of $b^d = 35^{80}$, a number larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe ($\sim 10^{80}$). Even with alpha-beta pruning, terminal-state search is **physically infeasible** in real time. > [!WARNING] > **The Impossibility of Exhaustive Search:** > Brute-force minimax to terminal nodes requires more time than the age of the universe. Therefore, **search must be cut off early** at some non-terminal depth, and a heuristic estimator (the evaluation function) must stand in for the true minimax value. The art of game-playing AI lies in crafting an $f(n)$ whose scores correlate strongly with true win probability. ## 1.5 Role of Heuristics in Game Playing Heuristics serve three critical purposes in adversarial search: 1. **Move ordering** — to maximize the effectiveness of alpha-beta pruning by exploring the best move first. 2. **Cutoff decisions** — to terminate search at non-terminal states. 3. **Position evaluation** — to assign utility to non-terminal positions where the tree is cut off. The three are tightly interlinked: a poor move-ordering heuristic makes alpha-beta inefficient; a poor cutoff heuristic wastes time; a poor evaluation function leads to bad final move selection. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an Evaluation Function A well-designed evaluation function decomposes a position into a set of **features** $\mathcal{F} = \{f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_n\}$, each capturing one strategic aspect of the position. The overall score is typically a **weighted linear combination** (the dominant paradigm in classical game-playing engines): $$f(n) = w_1 f_1(n) + w_2 f_2(n) + w_3 f_3(n) + \cdots + w_k f_k(n)$$ or more compactly: $$f(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot f_i(n)$$ where: - $f_i(n)$ is the value of the $i$-th feature at position $n$ - $w_i$ is the weight (importance) assigned to feature $i$ - $k$ is the total number of features The features themselves are computed as **differences between the two sides**: $$f_i(n) = f_i^{\text{MAX}}(n) - f_i^{\text{MIN}}(n)$$ So that $f_i(n) > 0$ indicates MAX is stronger in that dimension. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Properties of a Good Evaluation Function:** > 1. **Cheap to compute** — must evaluate in microseconds (called millions of times per second). > 2. **Strongly correlated with winning chances** — monotonic relationship with true win probability. > 3. **Smooth / non-catastrophic** — small positional changes should produce small score changes (preserves the ordering of minimax). > 4. **Forced-win / forced-loss detectable** — must return $\pm \infty$ at terminal states. ## 2.2 Standard Features in a Chess Evaluation Function ### 2.2.1 Material Balance (Piece Values) The most fundamental feature. Standard piece values used in virtually all chess engines: | Piece | Relative Value | Notation in Formula | |---|---|---| | Pawn | 1 | $P$ | | Knight | 3 | $N$ | | Bishop | 3 | $B$ | | Rook | 5 | $R$ | | Queen | 9 | $Q$ | | King | $\infty$ (game-ending) | $K$ | > [!NOTE] > These values are *approximate* — derived empirically from the observed win rates when a side is ahead by one such piece. Modern engines use **piece-square tables** that assign different values to the same piece depending on its square (e.g., a knight on the rim is weaker than a centralized knight). The material component is: $$f_{\text{material}}(n) = (9 \cdot \Delta Q + 5 \cdot \Delta R + 3 \cdot \Delta B + 3 \cdot \Delta N + 1 \cdot \Delta P)$$ where $\Delta X = (\text{count of piece } X \text{ for MAX}) - (\text{count of piece } X \text{ for MIN})$. ### 2.2.2 Positional / Strategic Features | Feature | Description | Typical Weight | |---|---|---| | Pawn structure | Doubled, isolated, passed pawns | $w_{\text{pawn}} \approx 0.2$–$0.5$ | | King safety | Pawn shield, exposure to checks | $w_{\text{king}} \approx 2.0$ | | Mobility | Number of legal moves available | $w_{\text{mob}} \approx 0.1$ | | Center control | Piece influence on central 4 squares (d4, d5, e4, e5) | $w_{\text{ctr}} \approx 0.3$ | | Bishop pair | Bonus for having both bishops | $\approx 0.5$ pawn units | | Rook on open file | Rooks on files with no pawns | $\approx 0.25$ | | Outpost squares | Knight on square immune to enemy pawns | $\approx 0.3$ | ## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!TIP] > **Master These Equations — They Appear Repeatedly in KTU Examinations:** | # | Equation / Concept | Symbol | Description / Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $f(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i f_i(n)$ | $f(n)$ | Weighted linear evaluation function | | 2 | $f_i(n) = f_i^{\text{MAX}}(n) - f_i^{\text{MIN}}(n)$ | $f_i$ | Feature computed as MAX-MIN difference | | 3 | $f_{\text{material}} = 9\Delta Q + 5\Delta R + 3\Delta B + 3\Delta N + \Delta P$ | $f_m$ | Standard chess material balance | | 4 | $f_{\text{eval}} = +\infty$ | terminal | MAX has won (checkmate) | | 5 | $f_{\text{eval}} = -\infty$ | terminal | MIN has won (MAX checkmated) | | 6 | $f_{\text{eval}} = 0$ | terminal | Draw (stalemate / 50-move / repetition) | | 7 | Effective branching factor: $b' = b^{1-(\text{pruning ratio})}$ | $b'$ | Measures alpha-beta gain | | 8 | Horizon effect bound | qualitative | $\vert f(n) - f(n') \vert$ small if $n, n'$ are siblings | $f$ | Smoothness requirement | | 9 | $\text{Quiescence search depth} = q$ | $q$ | Extension depth at noisy positions | | 10 | Singular extension trigger | $S_{\text{sing}}$ | One legal move in tactical position | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The principles underlying game-playing evaluation functions are foundational across modern AI engineering: - **Autonomous driving:** Continuous "evaluators" score candidate trajectories; minimax-style reasoning handles interactions with other agents. - **Cybersecurity:** Adversarial search models attacker-defender scenarios in penetration testing. - **Algorithmic trading:** Evaluation functions score market positions when exhaustive simulation of all paths is impossible. - **Robotics (multi-robot):** Heuristic search guides motion planning in shared workspaces. - **Game industry:** Engines like Stockfish, AlphaZero, Leela Chess Zero all rely on evaluation functions (with neural-network variants replacing hand-crafted features in the modern era). ## 2.5 Why the Weighted Linear Model Works The linear model $f(n) = \sum w_i f_i(n)$ rests on a strong empirical foundation: studies (Turing, 1953; Shannon, 1950; later formalized by Slate & Atkin, 1977 in Northwestern University's Chess program) showed that win probability is a **monotonically increasing function of material balance**, with diminishing returns. Specifically, the win-rate curve resembles a logistic function of the score, and **the score itself** is approximately linear in the weighted feature values. This empirical observation is what justifies the linear combination. > [!NOTE] > **Modern Departure:** AlphaZero (2017, DeepMind) replaced the weighted-linear evaluator with a **deep neural network** $f_\theta(n)$ that maps board positions directly to $(p, v)$ — move probabilities and expected outcome. This was the first system to defeat world-champion-level engines using *learned* rather than *hand-crafted* evaluation. KTU examinations still focus on the classical weighted-linear model. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Minimax Value to Evaluation Function The **true** minimax value of a position $n$ at depth $d$ is defined recursively: $$ \text{MinimaxValue}(n) = \begin{cases} \text{Utility}(n) & \text{if } n \text{ is terminal} \\[4pt] \max_{a \in \text{Actions}(n)} \text{MinimaxValue}(\text{Result}(n, a)) & \text{if MAX to move} \\[4pt] \min_{a \in \text{Actions}(n)} \text{MinimaxValue}(\text{Result}(n, a)) & \text{if MIN to move} \end{cases} $$ The problem: for any realistic game, the recursion cannot be evaluated to terminal nodes. Therefore, we **replace the terminal utility** with a heuristic estimate: $$ \widehat{\text{MinimaxValue}}(n) = \begin{cases} \text{Utility}(n) & \text{if } n \text{ is terminal} \\[4pt] f(n) & \text{if depth}(n) = d_{\max} \\[4pt] \max_{a \in \text{Actions}(n)} \widehat{\text{MinimaxValue}}(\text{Result}(n,a)) & \text{if MAX to move, depth} < d_{\max} \\[4pt] \min_{a \in \text{Actions}(n)} \widehat{\text{MinimaxValue}}(\text{Result}(n,a)) & \text{if MIN to move, depth} < d_{\max} \end{cases} $$ **Justification (Why Linear Weighted Combinations Work):** Given a position with material difference $M$ (in pawn units), empirical data shows the expected score $E(s)$ follows: $$E(s) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-k \cdot M}}$$ For small $M$, $E(s) \approx 0.5 + kM/4$, which is **linear in $M$**. This justifies combining features linearly in regions near the "balanced" position. Weights are calibrated to give the same $E(s)$ as observed game statistics. ## 3.2 Worked Example — Material Evaluation in Chess **Problem:** A chess position has the following material distribution: - **White (MAX):** 1 Queen, 2 Rooks, 2 Bishops, 2 Knights, 8 Pawns - **Black (MIN):** 1 Queen, 2 Rooks, 2 Bishops, 1 Knight, 7 Pawns Compute the material balance $f_{\text{material}}$. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the piece counts for each side:** For White (MAX): - $Q_{\text{W}} = 1$, $R_{\text{W}} = 2$, $B_{\text{W}} = 2$, $N_{\text{W}} = 2$, $P_{\text{W}} = 8$ For Black (MIN): - $Q_{\text{B}} = 1$, $R_{\text{B}} = 2$, $B_{\text{B}} = 2$, $N_{\text{B}} = 1$, $P_{\text{B}} = 7$ **Step 2 — Compute the differences $\Delta X = X_{\text{W}} - X_{\text{B}}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta Q &= 1 - 1 = 0 \\ \Delta R &= 2 - 2 = 0 \\ \Delta B &= 2 - 2 = 0 \\ \Delta N &= 2 - 1 = 1 \\ \Delta P &= 8 - 7 = 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the material balance formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} f_{\text{material}}(n) &= 9 \cdot \Delta Q + 5 \cdot \Delta R + 3 \cdot \Delta B + 3 \cdot \Delta N + 1 \cdot \Delta P \\[4pt] &= 9(0) + 5(0) + 3(0) + 3(1) + 1(1) \\[4pt] &= 0 + 0 + 0 + 3 + 1 \\[4pt] &= 4 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** $f_{\text{material}}(n) = +4$. Since the sign convention is positive = favorable to MAX, **White is ahead by 4 pawn units of material** (equivalent to a minor piece plus a pawn). This strongly favors White; statistical data suggests White's expected score is approximately $0.83$ (i.e., ~83% chance of winning or drawing favorably). ## 3.3 Worked Example — Full Evaluation with Multiple Features **Problem:** In the same position, an evaluator also computes: - **Mobility:** White has 28 legal moves, Black has 22 legal moves - **King safety:** White king is well-shielded (score = +1), Black king is exposed (score = -1) - **Center control:** White controls 3 central squares, Black controls 1 (difference = 2) - **Bishop pair:** White has both bishops (bonus = +0.5), Black has both bishops (bonus = +0.5); net difference = 0 **Weights (in pawn units):** $w_{\text{mob}} = 0.05$ per move of mobility difference, $w_{\text{king}} = 0.5$, $w_{\text{ctr}} = 0.3$, $w_{\text{bishop}} = 0.5$. Compute the full evaluation $f(n)$. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Compute the individual feature values:** $$ \begin{aligned} f_{\text{material}}(n) &= 4 \quad \text{(from previous example)} \\[4pt] f_{\text{mobility}}(n) &= w_{\text{mob}} \cdot (28 - 22) = 0.05 \cdot 6 = 0.30 \\[4pt] f_{\text{king}}(n) &= 0.5 \cdot \bigl( (+1) - (-1) \bigr) = 0.5 \cdot 2 = 1.0 \\[4pt] f_{\text{center}}(n) &= 0.3 \cdot 2 = 0.6 \\[4pt] f_{\text{bishop pair}}(n) &= 0.5 \cdot (1 - 1) = 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Sum all features into the final evaluation:** $$ \begin{aligned} f(n) &= f_{\text{material}} + f_{\text{mobility}} + f_{\text{king}} + f_{\text{center}} + f_{\text{bishop pair}} \\[4pt] &= 4 + 0.30 + 1.0 + 0.6 + 0 \\[4pt] &= 5.9 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation:** $f(n) = +5.9$ pawn units. White has a *decisive* advantage combining material, mobility, and king-safety benefits. The win-probability model gives approximately $E(s) \approx 0.95$ in favor of White. > [!NOTE] > **Analytical Note — Why the King-Safety Feature Has Such a Large Weight:** > In real games, an exposed king is far more dangerous than the same material deficit distributed elsewhere. The high weight $w_{\text{king}} = 0.5$ reflects this nonlinearity. Some engines use **non-linear evaluation functions** (e.g., a king-safety score that *multiplies* material rather than adds) to capture such interactions. However, the **weighted linear model** remains the KTU-exam-standard. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Heuristic Strategies in Game Playing | Heuristic | What It Does | Where Applied | Computational Cost | Risk | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Material balance** | Counts pieces; sums weighted values | Every non-terminal position | Very low | Misses positional subtleties | | **Mobility count** | Number of legal moves per side | Every non-terminal position | Low (inc. attack tables) | Position-dependent importance | | **Piece-square tables** | Positional piece value by square | Every non-terminal position | Very low (LUT lookup) | Static — no dynamic adjustment | | **Pawn-structure analysis** | Detects weak/strong pawns | Every non-terminal position | Moderate | Slow with naive bitboards | | **Move ordering heuristic** | Ranks children before alpha-beta | At every internal node | Low (transposition table reuse) | Wrong ordering = wasted search | | **Quiescence search** | Extends search at "noisy" nodes | At depth-limited leaves | Variable (can explode) | Implementation complexity | | **Singular extension** | Extends search if only 1 legal reply | At depth-limited leaves | Low | Risk of search explosion | | **Null-move pruning** | Skip a move to detect zugzwang | At depth-limited nodes | Low (skip ply) | Fails in zugzwang positions | | **Killer-move heuristic** | Reuse good refutation moves | At each depth | Very low | Quality varies | | **Transposition table** | Cache evaluated positions | Globally | Memory-bound | Hash collisions | ## 3.5 Cutoff Decisions and Horizon Effect ### 3.5.1 Standard Depth-Limited Search The simplest cutoff: stop search at depth $d_{\max}$ and apply $f(n)$ to all leaves reached. This produces the **horizon effect**: a critical event (e.g., a queen capture) is "pushed beyond the horizon" and the engine believes the position is safe when it is not. **Example (Horizon Effect):** Suppose the position has a forced queen capture by the opponent in 5 plies, but the engine only searches 4 plies. At depth 4, the queen is still on the board; the engine evaluates "even material." It then chooses a move that allows the queen to be captured — a catastrophic blunder that the search cannot "see." ### 3.5.2 Quiescence Search **Solution:** Extend the search *only at "noisy" nodes* — positions where captures, promotions, checks, or threats are pending — until a **quiet position** is reached. $$ d_{\text{actual}}(n) = d_{\max} + q(n) $$ where $q(n)$ is the quiescence depth at node $n$, defined as zero for quiet positions and recursive for noisy ones. ### 3.5.3 Singular Extensions If at a node only **one** legal move is reasonable (all others lose material catastrophically), extend the search at that move alone. This pushes the horizon past the tactical sequence without paying the cost of full-width extension at all nodes. **Mathematical Formulation:** $$ \text{Extend}(n, a) = \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if } f_{\text{second-best}}(n) - f_{\text{best}}(n) > \delta \\[4pt] \text{False} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ where $\delta$ is a margin (typically $\sim 3$ pawn units) and $f_{\text{best}}, f_{\text{second-best}}$ are the values of the best and second-best moves. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Architecture of an Evaluation Function The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how raw board state is decomposed into features, weighted, and combined into a final score: ```mermaid flowchart TD RawBoard["RAW BOARD STATE
Position n = pieces on squares"] FeatureEng["FEATURE EXTRACTION ENGINE"] F1["f1 MATERIAL BALANCE
9 dQ + 5 dR + 3 dB + 3 dN + dP"] F2["f2 MOBILITY
Legal moves per side"] F3["f3 KING SAFETY
Pawn shield + exposure"] F4["f4 PAWN STRUCTURE
Doubled / isolated / passed"] F5["f5 CENTER CONTROL
Influence on d4 d5 e4 e5"] W1["w1 = 1.0"] W2["w2 = 0.05"] W3["w3 = 0.50"] W4["w4 = 0.30"] W5["w5 = 0.30"] Mul1["w1 times f1"] Mul2["w2 times f2"] Mul3["w3 times f3"] Mul4["w4 times f4"] Mul5["w5 times f5"] Sum["SUMMATION BLOCK
sum wi times fi over i equals 1 to 5"] Out["f n FINAL EVALUATION SCORE
Positive favors MAX
Negative favors MIN
Zero is balanced"] Term["TERMINAL CHECK
Is n a checkmate or draw"] Inf["OUTPUT plus minus infinity at terminal"] Decision["ROUTING DECISION
If terminal return utility
Else return f n"] Final["FINAL OUTPUT TO MINIMAX SEARCH"] RawBoard --> FeatureEng FeatureEng --> F1 FeatureEng --> F2 FeatureEng --> F3 FeatureEng --> F4 FeatureEng --> F5 F1 --> Mul1 F2 --> Mul2 F3 --> Mul3 F4 --> Mul4 F5 --> Mul5 W1 --> Mul1 W2 --> Mul2 W3 --> Mul3 W4 --> Mul4 W5 --> Mul5 Mul1 --> Sum Mul2 --> Sum Mul3 --> Sum Mul4 --> Sum Mul5 --> Sum Sum --> Decision RawBoard --> Term Term --> Inf Inf --> Decision Decision --> Final Out --> Final ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Search with Cutoff and Evaluation ```mermaid flowchart TD Root["ROOT NODE
Current position n0"] MaxNode["MAX NODES
Internal: pick max of children"] MinNode["MIN NODES
Internal: pick min of children"] Expand["EXPAND CHILDREN
Generate legal moves"] DepthCheck["DEPTH REACHED dmax
or QUIET NODE q"] Eval["EVALUATION FUNCTION f n
Compute weighted sum"] Leaf["LEAF NODE
Return static score"] Cut["CUTOFF
Alpha beta pruning triggered"] Result["BEST MOVE a*
Argmax or argmin over children"] Quiesce["QUIESCENCE EXTENSION
Search captures and checks only"] Root --> Expand Expand --> MaxNode MaxNode --> DepthCheck MinNode --> DepthCheck DepthCheck -->|Yes| Quiesce DepthCheck -->|No at terminal| Leaf DepthCheck -->|No depth not yet| Expand Quiesce -->|Position noisy| Expand Quiesce -->|Position quiet| Eval Eval --> Leaf MinNode --> Cut MaxNode --> Cut Leaf --> Result Root --> Result ``` ## 4.3 Game Tree Showing Horizon Effect ```mermaid flowchart TD Cur["CURRENT POSITION
Search limit d equals 4 plies"] L1a["After 1 ply
Engine searches here"] L2a["After 2 plies
Engine searches here"] L3a["After 3 plies
Engine searches here"] L4a["After 4 plies LEAF
Queen still on board
f n equals 0 material"] L5a["After 5 plies BEYOND HORIZON
Queen captured by opponent
Engine cannot see this"] L6a["After 6 plies DEEP HORIZON
Likely decisive result"] Eval["ENGINE CHOOSES THIS MOVE
Believes material is equal
Real position is LOST"] Cur --> L1a L1a --> L2a L2a --> L3a L3a --> L4a L4a -.->|Engine does not search| L5a L5a -.->|Engine does not search| L6a L4a --> Eval ``` ## 4.4 Decision Logic for Quiescence vs Standard Cutoff ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Node n reached at depth d"] Q1{"IS n a QUIET position
No captures available
No check in progress
No promotion possible"} Q2{"DEPTH LIMIT EXCEEDED
d greater than dmax"} Q3["PERFORM QUIESCENCE SEARCH
Generate all capture and check moves
Extend depth recursively"] Q4["RETURN STATIC EVALUATION f n
Sum of weighted features"] Q5["CONTINUE STANDARD SEARCH
Generate all legal moves
Proceed to depth d plus 1"] Q6["RETURN f n from quiescence search
Position is now quiet"] Start --> Q1 Q1 -->|Yes| Q2 Q1 -->|No noisy| Q3 Q2 -->|No| Q5 Q2 -->|Yes| Q4 Q3 --> Q1 Q4 --> EndNode["OUTPUT TO PARENT"] Q5 --> EndNode Q6 --> EndNode ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Define an evaluation function. List any three features commonly used in a chess evaluation function.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** An evaluation function $f(n)$ is a heuristic function that estimates the desirability of a non-terminal game state $n$ from the perspective of the player to move, without requiring search to terminal states. It returns a numerical score: positive for positions favorable to MAX, negative for positions favorable to MIN, and $\pm \infty$ for forced win/loss. **Three Features (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Material Balance** — Net difference in piece values, e.g., $9\Delta Q + 5\Delta R + 3\Delta B + 3\Delta N + \Delta P$. 2. **Mobility** — Number of legal moves available to each side; more moves typically imply greater activity. 3. **King Safety** — Assessment of pawn shield and exposure of the king to attacks. *(Acceptable alternates: Pawn Structure, Center Control, Bishop Pair Bonus, Piece-Square Tables.)* --- ### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **What is the *horizon effect* in game playing? How does quiescence search mitigate it?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Horizon Effect (1.5 marks):** The horizon effect occurs when a critical tactical event (such as a piece capture, check, or pawn promotion) lies just beyond the fixed search depth, causing the engine to **mis-evaluate** the position as if the event would not occur. The engine consequently chooses a move that delays the event but does not prevent it, often leading to a loss that "lurks beyond the horizon." **Quiescence Search (1.5 marks):** Quiescence search mitigates the horizon effect by *selectively extending* the search at "noisy" positions — nodes where captures, checks, or promotions are available — until a **quiet position** is reached. Only then is the static evaluation applied. This ensures the engine does not evaluate positions in the middle of unresolved tactical sequences. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must answer either Question A OR Question B in full. Below, BOTH alternatives are provided for examination practice. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the role of the evaluation function in adversarial search. Discuss its desirable properties and the weighted linear model with reference to chess.** **Model Answer:** **Role of the Evaluation Function (3 marks):** In adversarial search, the true minimax value of a state can only be computed by expanding the tree to terminal nodes. For complex games like chess, where $b^d \approx 35^{80}$ exceeds the number of atoms in the universe, this is computationally infeasible. The evaluation function addresses this by serving as a **substitute for terminal utility** at depth-limited leaves. Formally, for a non-terminal node $n$ at depth $d_{\max}$, we replace: $$\text{MinimaxValue}(n) \;\longleftarrow\; f(n)$$ The function $f(n)$ must approximate the expected outcome of the game from position $n$, allowing the agent to **rank successor positions** and select the move that maximizes $f$. **Desirable Properties (2 marks):** 1. **Computational efficiency** — $f(n)$ must be evaluable in microseconds, as it is called millions of times per move. 2. **Strong correlation with winning** — $f(n)$ should monotonically track true win probability. 3. **Smoothness** — small changes in position should produce small changes in score (preserves minimax ordering). 4. **Terminal correctness** — $f(n) = \pm \infty$ at checkmate and $f(n) = 0$ at drawn positions. **Weighted Linear Model (2 marks):** The classical model expresses the score as: $$f(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot f_i(n)$$ For chess, this typically expands to: $$f(n) = w_1 f_{\text{material}} + w_2 f_{\text{pos}} + w_3 f_{\text{mobility}} + w_4 f_{\text{king}} + w_5 f_{\text{center}}$$ with typical weights $w_1 = 1.0$, $w_2 = 0.2$, $w_3 = 0.1$, $w_4 = 0.5$, $w_5 = 0.3$. The weights are calibrated empirically against game statistics — they reflect how much each feature "matters" in terms of expected-score contribution. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A chess position has the following material:** - **White:** 1 Queen, 2 Rooks, 1 Bishop, 1 Knight, 7 Pawns - **Black:** 1 Queen, 1 Rook, 2 Bishops, 2 Knights, 6 Pawns **Additional features:** - White mobility = 30 moves, Black mobility = 18 moves - White king safety = +2, Black king safety = -1 (differential = 3) - Center control difference = +1 (White) - Bishop pair: White has both = +0.5; Black has both = +0.5; net = 0 **Weights:** $w_{\text{mob}} = 0.05$, $w_{\text{king}} = 0.4$, $w_{\text{center}} = 0.3$, $w_{\text{bishop}} = 0.5$. **Compute the full evaluation $f(n)$ and interpret the result.** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Compute material difference:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta Q &= 1 - 1 = 0 \\ \Delta R &= 2 - 1 = 1 \\ \Delta B &= 1 - 2 = -1 \\ \Delta N &= 1 - 2 = -1 \\ \Delta P &= 7 - 6 = 1 \end{aligned} $$ $$ f_{\text{material}} = 9(0) + 5(1) + 3(-1) + 3(-1) + 1(1) = 5 - 3 - 3 + 1 = 0 $$ Material is balanced. **Step 2 — Compute other features:** $$ \begin{aligned} f_{\text{mobility}} &= 0.05 \cdot (30 - 18) = 0.05 \cdot 12 = 0.60 \\ f_{\text{king}} &= 0.4 \cdot 3 = 1.20 \\ f_{\text{center}} &= 0.3 \cdot 1 = 0.30 \\ f_{\text{bishop pair}} &= 0.5 \cdot 0 = 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Sum the features:** $$ \begin{aligned} f(n) &= 0 + 0.60 + 1.20 + 0.30 + 0 = 2.10 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** $f(n) = +2.10$ pawn units. White has a significant positional advantage due to **superior mobility** and **king safety**, despite balanced material. White's expected outcome is approximately 0.65–0.70 in favor. White should play to maintain these advantages, while Black should seek tactical simplification. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **With suitable examples, discuss at least five heuristic strategies used in game-playing programs. Explain how each contributes to efficient adversarial search.** **Model Answer:** **1. Material Evaluation (1.4 marks):** Counting piece values is the simplest and most reliable heuristic. In chess, the standard values (P=1, N=3, B=3, R=5, Q=9) provide a quick, accurate estimate of position balance. *Example:* White has 2 Rooks, Black has 1 Rook; the material difference of 5 pawn units strongly favors White. **2. Mobility Heuristic (1.4 marks):** Favor positions with more legal moves. More moves imply greater tactical flexibility and initiative. *Example:* A position with 25 legal moves is generally preferable to one with only 8, all else equal. **3. Move Ordering Heuristic for Alpha-Beta Pruning (1.4 marks):** Before searching children of a node, sort them by likely quality (using prior evaluation, killer moves, or transposition-table hits). Good ordering maximizes alpha-beta cutoffs, often achieving $b' \approx \sqrt{b}$ — effectively doubling the search depth. **4. Quiescence Search (1.4 marks):** At depth-limited leaves, extend search only through capture/check/promotion sequences until the position is quiet. This prevents the horizon effect. *Example:* If the static evaluation would otherwise be called while a queen is en prise, quiescence search resolves the capture first. **5. Killer-Move Heuristic (1.4 marks):** At each depth, store the move that produced a beta cutoff (a "killer"). When searching sibling nodes at the same depth, try the killer first. *Rationale:* In many positions, the same move refutes many different lines. *(Optional 6th: Transposition table — caching previously evaluated positions by Zobrist hash to reuse results. Saves up to 30% of search time in practice.)* #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Explain the horizon effect in detail. Describe how quiescence search and singular extensions mitigate it, with one example of each.** **Model Answer:** **Horizon Effect — Definition and Cause (2 marks):** The horizon effect is the **mis-evaluation of a position** caused by a critical tactical event being just beyond the search horizon (fixed depth limit). The engine, unable to see the event, evaluates the position as if the event will not occur. It may then choose a move that **delays** the event (a "waiting move") rather than one that **prevents** it. This is an artifact of finite search depth, not of the evaluation function itself. **Example of Horizon Effect (1 mark):** Position has White queen attacked by a Black pawn. The pawn capture occurs in 3 plies. With a search depth of 2 plies, the engine does not see the capture; it believes the queen is safe. It plays a waiting move; the queen is lost on move 3. **Quiescence Search — Mitigation (2 marks):** At depth-limited leaves, instead of immediately calling the static evaluator, **generate all "noisy" moves** (captures, checks, promotions) and search them recursively until a **quiet position** is reached (no immediate tactical threats). Only then is $f(n)$ applied. The search may temporarily exceed the nominal depth, but only at tactically active nodes. *Example:* If the position has a queen en prise, quiescence search extends through the capture sequence; the static evaluator is called only after the queen is exchanged, giving a correct evaluation of the resulting material balance. **Singular Extensions — Mitigation (2 marks):** A singular extension occurs when, at a node, only **one** move is reasonable (all others lose material catastrophically). The engine extends the search depth for *that one move alone* by 1–2 plies, pushing the horizon past the critical sequence. The condition is: $$\text{Extend} \iff f_{\text{best}}(n) - f_{\text{second-best}}(n) > \delta$$ *Example:* In a position where every move except $\text{Re}1$ loses the queen, the engine extends the search along $\text{Re}1$ to verify the queen is actually saved — even if the move's depth-budget has been reached. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes Students Make:** > 1. **Confusing "horizon effect" with "alpha-beta cutoff."** A cutoff is an *intentional* pruning of provably-irrelevant branches; the horizon effect is an *unintentional* blindness to a critical event. Do not interchange them. > 2. **Forgetting terminal-value convention.** When asked to write $f(n)$ for a checkmated position, students often write "very low" instead of $-\infty$. The convention is rigid: terminal values are $\pm \infty$ or $0$ (draw), not arbitrary large numbers. > 3. **Mixing up MAX and MIN sign conventions.** A positive score always means **favorable to the player to move**; students sometimes invert the sign. > 4. **Omitting units of weight in answers.** Always state that piece values are in **pawn units** so that the addition is dimensionally consistent. > 5. **Writing the material-balance formula incorrectly.** The most common error is reversing the order: writing $(\text{MIN count} - \text{MAX count})$. The correct convention is $(\text{MAX} - \text{MIN})$. > 6. **In Part B, skipping the interpretation step.** KTU examiners allocate **1–2 marks** purely for stating what the numerical evaluation *means* in game terms. Always include a concluding sentence such as: *"The score of +4.0 indicates White has a winning advantage."* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Evaluation Functions and Heuristics in Game Playing** ### Core Concepts - ☐ **Evaluation function** $f(n)$ = static estimator of position utility at non-terminal states. - ☐ **Sign convention:** $f(n) > 0$ favors MAX, $f(n) < 0$ favors MIN, $f(n) = \pm \infty$ at terminal states. - ☐ **Heuristic** = rule of thumb that trades optimality for computational feasibility. ### Mathematical Foundation - ☐ **Weighted linear model:** $f(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot f_i(n)$. - ☐ **Standard chess piece values (pawn units):** P=1, N=3, B=3, R=5, Q=9, K=$\infty$. - ☐ **Material balance:** $9\Delta Q + 5\Delta R + 3\Delta B + 3\Delta N + \Delta P$. - ☐ **Empirical justification:** Win probability is approximately linear in $f(n)$ near balanced positions. ### Desirable Properties - ☐ Cheap to compute, smooth, strongly correlated with winning, terminal-correct. ### Common Features in Chess - ☐ Material, Mobility, King Safety, Pawn Structure, Center Control, Bishop Pair, Piece-Square Tables. ### Heuristics in Game Playing - ☐ **Move ordering** → maximizes alpha-beta pruning efficiency. - ☐ **Killer moves** → reuse good refutations across sibling nodes. - ☐ **Transposition table** → cache by Zobrist hash to avoid recomputation. - ☐ **Null-move pruning** → skip a ply to detect zugzwang positions. - ☐ **Static evaluation** → score a position without further search. ### The Horizon Effect & Its Solutions - ☐ **Horizon effect** = mis-evaluation from events pushed beyond the search depth. - ☐ **Quiescence search** → extend search at "noisy" positions until quiet. - ☐ **Singular extension** → extend depth for the single best move when all others are terrible. ### Examination Strategy - ☐ Always state the sign convention in definitions. - ☐ Always show the substitution of values into the formula. - ☐ Always include an interpretation of the final score. - ☐ In Part B questions, structure sub-part (a) as **definition + properties + examples** and sub-part (b) as **problem statement + step-by-step solution + interpretation**.

topics.title: Predicate logic and logic programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT502 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Predicate logic and logic programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Predicate Logic and Logic Programming

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Predicate Logic?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Predicate Logic (also called First-Order Logic / FOL / First-Order Predicate Calculus / FOPC)** is a logical system that extends propositional logic by introducing **variables, predicates, quantifiers, and functions**. It allows us to represent facts about *objects* and *relations* between them, enabling reasoning about the general properties of a domain rather than just true/false statements.

In propositional logic, the smallest unit of meaning is a *proposition* (e.g., `P = "It is raining"`). In predicate logic, we break sentences into:
- A **subject** (an object or variable),
- A **predicate** (a property or relation),
- Optionally, an **object** (the argument).

For example:
- "Socrates is a man" becomes `Man(Socrates)`
- "All men are mortal" becomes `∀x (Man(x) → Mortal(x))`

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Family Tree of Facts"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Think of **propositional logic** as a *notebook of standalone facts* — each fact is a single true/false statement. Now imagine you graduate to a **smart family tree application**. Instead of writing "John is Mary's father", "Paul is Mary's father", "Sara is John's mother" individually, you can use a *template* like `Father(x, y)` and apply it to any pair. You can also say **"All fathers love their children"** once, using a variable, and it covers John, Paul, and any future father. **That template-and-variable power is exactly what predicate logic gives you over propositional logic.**

### 1.3 What is Logic Programming?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Logic Programming** is a programming paradigm based on formal logic, in which programs are written as a set of logical statements (declarative sentences) rather than explicit control-flow instructions. The most prominent language is **Prolog (PROgrammation en LOGique)**, in which computation is performed by *logical inference* — typically **resolution** and **backward chaining** — over a knowledge base of **facts and rules**.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "Asking a Smart Librarian"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine you walk into a library. You don't tell the librarian *how* to find a book (no step-by-step algorithm). You simply *describe* what you want: "Find me a book whose author is Indian AND published after 2010 AND contains the keyword 'neural networks'." The librarian uses *rules* in their head and *facts* from the catalog to deduce the answer. **Prolog works the same way** — you give it facts and rules, then ask *queries*; the inference engine (the librarian) finds the answer through logical deduction.

### 1.5 Why Does KTU Care About This?

- Predicate logic is the **mathematical backbone of knowledge representation** in AI.
- Logic programming (Prolog) is widely used in **expert systems, NLP, database query languages (Datalog), theorem provers, and symbolic AI**.
- Exam questions in PCCAT502 frequently ask students to **convert English sentences to FOL**, **apply unification**, and **perform resolution**.

### 1.6 Key Symbols & Notation (Master List)

| Symbol | Meaning | Example |
|--------|---------|---------|
| `∧` | AND (conjunction) | `P ∧ Q` |
| `∨` | OR (disjunction) | `P ∨ Q` |
| `¬` | NOT (negation) | `¬P` |
| `→` | IMPLIES | `P → Q` |
| `↔` | IFF (biconditional) | `P ↔ Q` |
| `∀` | FOR ALL (universal quantifier) | `∀x P(x)` |
| `∃` | THERE EXISTS (existential quantifier) | `∃x P(x)` |
| `=` | Equality / identity | `father(x) = y` |
| **Bold** constants | Specific objects (e.g., **Socrates**, **0**) | `Man(Socrates)` |
| Lowercase variables | Variables ranging over objects | `Man(x)` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of First-Order Logic (FOL)

FOL has three layered components:

### 2.1.1 Alphabet (Syntax Primitives)
1. **Constants** — denote specific objects. Example: `Socrates`, `0`, `India`.
2. **Variables** — placeholders ranging over objects. Example: `x`, `y`, `z`.
3. **Function symbols** — map objects to objects. Example: `fatherOf(x)`, `successor(x)`.
4. **Predicate symbols** — denote properties/relations. Example: `Man(x)`, `Loves(x, y)`.
5. **Connectives** — `∧, ∨, ¬, →, ↔`.
6. **Quantifiers** — `∀` (universal) and `∃` (existential).
7. **Punctuation** — parentheses, commas.

### 2.1.2 Terms
A **term** is any expression denoting an object:
- Any constant is a term.
- Any variable is a term.
- If `f` is an n-ary function and `t₁, ..., tₙ` are terms, then `f(t₁, ..., tₙ)` is a term.

### 2.1.3 Atomic Sentences
An **atomic sentence** (atom) is a predicate symbol applied to a tuple of terms. Example: `Loves(John, Mary)`, `Man(Socrates)`.

### 2.1.4 Well-Formed Formulas (WFF)
Built recursively:
- An atomic sentence is a WFF.
- If `P` and `Q` are WFFs, so are `¬P`, `P ∧ Q`, `P ∨ Q`, `P → Q`, `P ↔ Q`.
- If `P` is a WFF and `x` is a variable, then `∀x P` and `∃x P` are WFFs.

## 2.2 Quantifier Semantics — The "Why" Behind the "How"

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Quantifier (∀):** "For all" — the formula is true only if the predicate holds for **every** object in the domain.
> **Existential Quantifier (∃):** "There exists" — true if the predicate holds for **at least one** object in the domain.

### 2.2.1 De Morgan Duals for Quantifiers (Exam Favourite!)

$$\neg \forall x \, P(x) \;\equiv\; \exists x \, \neg P(x)$$

$$\neg \exists x \, P(x) \;\equiv\; \forall x \, \neg P(x)$$

> The negation of "All birds fly" is "There exists a bird that does not fly" — **NOT** "No birds fly".

### 2.2.2 Quantifier Distribution Rules

$$\forall x \, (P(x) \wedge Q(x)) \;\equiv\; (\forall x \, P(x)) \wedge (\forall x \, Q(x))$$

$$\exists x \, (P(x) \vee Q(x)) \;\equiv\; (\exists x \, P(x)) \vee (\exists x \, Q(x))$$

But **mixing them fails** (common exam trap):

$$\forall x \, (P(x) \rightarrow Q(x)) \;\not\equiv\; (\forall x \, P(x)) \rightarrow (\forall x \, Q(x))$$

## 2.3 Translation Patterns (English ↔ FOL) — The KTU Pattern Library

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **highest-frequency exam task** in KTU for this module. Memorize these patterns.

| English Sentence | FOL Translation |
|------------------|-----------------|
| "All A are B" | `∀x (A(x) → B(x))` |
| "Some A are B" / "There is an A that is B" | `∃x (A(x) ∧ B(x))` |
| "No A are B" | `∀x (A(x) → ¬B(x))`  *or* `¬∃x (A(x) ∧ B(x))` |
| "Only A are B" | `∀x (B(x) → A(x))` |
| "Every A loves some B" | `∀x (A(x) → ∃y (B(y) ∧ Loves(x, y)))` |
| "There is exactly one A" | `∃x (A(x) ∧ ∀y (A(y) → y = x))` |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Rule Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Symbol / Form | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | Universal Instantiation (UI) | From `∀x P(x)` infer `P(c)` for any constant `c` | Specialization |
| 2 | Existential Instantiation (EI) | From `∃x P(x)` infer `P(c)` for some **new** Skolem constant `c` | Cannot reuse `c` |
| 3 | Universal Generalization (UG) | From `P(c)` (arbitrary `c`) infer `∀x P(x)` | Generalization |
| 4 | Existential Generalization (EG) | From `P(c)` infer `∃x P(x)` | Generalization |
| 5 | Modus Ponens | From `P → Q` and `P`, infer `Q` | Core inference |
| 6 | Modus Tollens | From `P → Q` and `¬Q`, infer `¬P` | Core inference |
| 7 | Modus Ponens generalization | From `∀x (P(x) → Q(x))` and `P(a)`, infer `Q(a)` | KTU favourite |
| 8 | De Morgan (FOL) | `¬∀x P ≡ ∃x ¬P`; `¬∃x P ≡ ∀x ¬P` | See §2.2.1 |
| 9 | Resolution rule (FOL) | `(P ∨ Q) ∧ (¬P ∨ R) ⊢ (Q ∨ R)` | Generalization of modus ponens |
| 10 | Horn Clause form | `P₁ ∧ P₂ ∧ ... ∧ Pₙ → Q` | Basis of Prolog |
| 11 | Unification | Find substitution `θ` making two atoms identical | Used in resolution |
| 12 | Most General Unifier (MGU) | Simplest `θ` that unifies two atoms | Unique up to renaming |

## 2.5 Logic Programming (Prolog) — Core Constructs

A **Prolog program** consists of three kinds of clauses:

1. **Facts** — unconditional truths.
   `likes(mary, food).`
2. **Rules** — conditional truths (head :- body).
   `likes(mary, X) :- likes(X, food).`
3. **Queries** — questions asked of the program.
   `?- likes(mary, wine).`

### 2.5.1 Horn Clauses

A **Horn clause** has **at most one positive literal**. Prolog restricts clauses to this form:

$$\text{Definite clause: } P_1 \wedge P_2 \wedge \dots \wedge P_n \rightarrow Q$$

The empty head gives the special "false" clause used to close proofs.

### 2.5.2 Backward Chaining

Prolog executes queries via **backward chaining**:
- Start with the goal (query).
- Look for a rule whose head matches the goal.
- Recursively satisfy the body sub-goals.
- Stop when all sub-goals match facts.

### 2.5.3 Unification (Key Operation)

Given two atoms, **unification** finds a substitution `θ` making them identical. The **MGU (Most General Unifier)** is the most permissive one.

**Example:**
- `Loves(John, x)` and `Loves(y, Mary)`
- MGU: `θ = {y/John, x/Mary}`
- Result: `Loves(John, Mary)`

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Expert systems** (MYCIN, DENDRAL) — early medical/chemical diagnosis AIs.
- **Natural Language Processing** — grammar formalisms (Definite Clause Grammars).
- **Database query languages** — Datalog is logic programming for relational databases.
- **Automated theorem provers** — Coq, Isabelle, Lean rely on FOL foundations.
- **Symbolic AI / Knowledge Graphs** — RDF/OWL/SPARQL all use FOL semantics.
- **Verification & formal methods** — hardware/software verification (model checking).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through **exhaustive worked examples** that mirror the type of long-answer questions asked in KTU board examinations. **No steps are skipped.**

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — English to FOL Translation

**Problem (7 marks, KTU pattern):**
Represent the following knowledge using First-Order Logic:

1. "Every student is a person."
2. "There exists a student who is a friend of Mary."
3. "No person is both a student and a teacher."
4. "Every student likes some book."
5. "Only students like books."

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Statement 1:** *"Every student is a person."*
- Use **universal quantifier (∀)** and the conditional `→`.
- The pattern "All A are B" → `∀x (A(x) → B(x))`.

$$\forall x \, (\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \text{Person}(x))$$

**Statement 2:** *"There exists a student who is a friend of Mary."*
- Use **existential quantifier (∃)** with conjunction `∧`.
- The pattern "Some A is B" → `∃x (A(x) ∧ B(x))`.

$$\exists x \, (\text{Student}(x) \wedge \text{Friend}(x, \text{Mary}))$$

**Statement 3:** *"No person is both a student and a teacher."*
- "No X is Y" can be expressed as `∀x (X(x) → ¬Y(x))` or `¬∃x (X(x) ∧ Y(x))`.
- Here the "Y" is the conjunction `(Student(x) ∧ Teacher(x))`.

$$\forall x \, (\text{Person}(x) \rightarrow \neg(\text{Student}(x) \wedge \text{Teacher}(x)))$$

Equivalently, by De Morgan:

$$\neg \exists x \, (\text{Person}(x) \wedge \text{Student}(x) \wedge \text{Teacher}(x))$$

**Statement 4:** *"Every student likes some book."*
- Pattern: `∀x (A(x) → ∃y (B(y) ∧ Likes(x, y)))`. We must use a **different variable** (`y`) to avoid confusion.

$$\forall x \, (\text{Student}(x) \rightarrow \exists y \, (\text{Book}(y) \wedge \text{Likes}(x, y)))$$

**Statement 5:** *"Only students like books."*
- Pattern: "Only A are B" → `∀x (B(x) → A(x))`.
- Here: "Like books" is the predicate `∃y (Book(y) ∧ Likes(x, y))`.

$$\forall x \, \Big(\big(\exists y \, (\text{Book}(y) \wedge \text{Likes}(x, y))\big) \rightarrow \text{Student}(x)\Big)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing "Only students like books" as `∀x (Student(x) → LikesBooks(x))`. This is **wrong** — the correct direction is reversed, because "only" is the *converse* of "all".

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Unification (7 marks, KTU pattern)

**Problem:**
Find the **Most General Unifier (MGU)** of the following pairs, or state that they fail to unify:

**Pair A:** `P(x, John, f(y))` and `P(Alice, z, f(Bob))`
**Pair B:** `Q(x, g(y))` and `Q(g(z), x)`

### Pair A — Solution

Initial atoms:

$$P(x, \text{John}, f(y)) \quad \text{and} \quad P(\text{Alice}, z, f(\text{Bob}))$$

**Step 1:** Unify the predicate `P` (identical, no work).
**Step 2:** Unify first arguments: `x` and `Alice`.

$$\theta_1 = \{x / \text{Alice}\}$$

Apply to both atoms:

$$P(\text{Alice}, \text{John}, f(y)) \quad \text{and} \quad P(\text{Alice}, z, f(\text{Bob}))$$

**Step 3:** Unify second arguments: `John` and `z`.

$$\theta_2 = \{z / \text{John}\}$$

Apply:

$$P(\text{Alice}, \text{John}, f(y)) \quad \text{and} \quad P(\text{Alice}, \text{John}, f(\text{Bob}))$$

**Step 4:** Unify third arguments: `f(y)` and `f(Bob)`.
Since the function symbol `f` matches, recurse into the argument: `y` and `Bob`.

$$\theta_3 = \{y / \text{Bob}\}$$

Apply:

$$P(\text{Alice}, \text{John}, f(\text{Bob})) \quad \text{and} \quad P(\text{Alice}, \text{John}, f(\text{Bob}))$$

**Step 5:** All arguments match. **MGU is the composition of substitutions:**

$$\boxed{\theta_{MGU} = \{x / \text{Alice},\; z / \text{John},\; y / \text{Bob}\}}$$

Unified atom: `P(Alice, John, f(Bob))`.

---

### Pair B — Solution

Initial atoms:

$$Q(x, g(y)) \quad \text{and} \quad Q(g(z), x)$$

**Step 1:** Predicates match.
**Step 2:** Unify first arguments: `x` and `g(z)`.

$$\theta_1 = \{x / g(z)\}$$

Apply to second atom's `x`:

$$Q(x, g(y)) \quad \text{and} \quad Q(g(z), g(z))$$

Substitute `x = g(z)` into the **first** atom too:

$$Q(g(z), g(y)) \quad \text{and} \quad Q(g(z), g(z))$$

**Step 3:** Unify second arguments: `g(y)` and `g(z)`. Recurse into `y` and `z`.

$$\theta_2 = \{y / z\}$$

Apply:

$$Q(g(z), g(z)) \quad \text{and} \quad Q(g(z), g(z))$$

All matched. MGU:

$$\boxed{\theta_{MGU} = \{x / g(z),\; y / z\}}$$

Unified atom: `Q(g(z), g(z))`.

> [!NOTE]
> **General Unification Algorithm (Robinson, 1965):** Iterate over pairs of terms, applying the substitution to remaining pairs after each step, until either all terms are identical (success) or a *failure* (e.g., variable bound to a term containing itself — the **occurs check**).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Resolution Proof (14-mark standard)

**Problem (adapted from typical KTU past papers):**
Given:
- F1: `∀x (Student(x) → Studies(x))`
- F2: `∀x (Studies(x) → Reads(x))`
- F3: `Student(Alice)`

**Prove:** `Reads(Alice)` using **resolution**.

### Step-by-Step Resolution

**Step 1 — Convert to Clause Form (CNF).**

F1: `∀x (¬Student(x) ∨ Studies(x))` → Clause 1: `{¬Student(x), Studies(x)}`
F2: `∀x (¬Studies(x) ∨ Reads(x))` → Clause 2: `{¬Studies(x), Reads(x)}`
F3: `Student(Alice)` → Clause 3: `{Student(Alice)}`

**Negation of goal:** `¬Reads(Alice)` → Clause 4: `{¬Reads(Alice)}`

**Step 2 — Resolve Clause 1 and Clause 3** (unify `x = Alice`):

- Clause 1 with `θ = {x/Alice}`: `{¬Student(Alice), Studies(Alice)}`
- Clause 3: `{Student(Alice)}`
- Complementary pair: `Student` vs `¬Student`.
- **Resolvent:** `{Studies(Alice)}` … Clause 5

**Step 3 — Resolve Clause 2 and Clause 5** (unify `x = Alice`):

- Clause 2 with `θ = {x/Alice}`: `{¬Studies(Alice), Reads(Alice)}`
- Clause 5: `{Studies(Alice)}`
- Complementary pair: `Studies` vs `¬Studies`.
- **Resolvent:** `{Reads(Alice)}` … Clause 6

**Step 4 — Resolve Clause 6 and Clause 4:**

- Clause 6: `{Reads(Alice)}`
- Clause 4: `{¬Reads(Alice)}`
- Complementary pair: `Reads` vs `¬Reads`.
- **Resolvent:** `{}` (the **empty clause**, denoted □)

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** The empty clause signals a **contradiction**, meaning the negation of the goal is inconsistent with the premises. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{Reads(Alice)} \text{ is logically entailed.}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the empty clause?** A clause `{P}` and a clause `{¬P}` together entail *false* (anything contradicts its negation). The empty clause `□` is the formal symbol of *false* in resolution theorem proving. Its derivation **proves** the original (un-negated) goal.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Prolog Logic Programming Trace

**Knowledge Base:**

```prolog
likes(mary, food).
likes(mary, wine).
likes(john, wine).
likes(X, Y) :- friend(X, Z), likes(Z, Y).
friend(mary, john).
friend(john, alice).
```

**Query:** `?- likes(mary, wine).`

### Trace (Depth-First, Left-to-Right Backward Chaining)

1. Goal: `likes(mary, wine)`.
2. Scan KB top-to-bottom.
3. Match **fact 1**: `likes(mary, food)`. Arguments don't match (`food ≠ wine`). **Fail.**
4. Match **fact 2**: `likes(mary, wine)`. Arguments match. **SUCCESS.** Bindings: none.

**Result:** `yes`.

**Query:** `?- likes(mary, X).`

1. Goal: `likes(mary, X)`.
2. Match **fact 1**: `likes(mary, food)`. Unify `X = food`. **SUCCESS — first answer.**
3. Backtrack, try more.
4. Match **fact 2**: `likes(mary, wine)`. Unify `X = wine`. **SUCCESS — second answer.**
5. Try **rule**: `likes(X, Y) :- friend(X, Z), likes(Z, Y)`. After unification: `friend(mary, Z)`, `likes(Z, X)`.
6. Sub-goal 1: `friend(mary, Z)`. Match `friend(mary, john)` → `Z = john`.
7. Sub-goal 2: `likes(john, X)`. Match `likes(john, wine)` → `X = wine`.
8. **SUCCESS — third answer.**

**Results (in order):**

$$X = \text{food},\quad X = \text{wine},\quad X = \text{wine (via rule)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Prolog's order of answers is determined by: (1) clause order in the KB, (2) depth-first search, (3) left-to-right sub-goal evaluation. **Reordering clauses or rules changes the answer sequence** — a frequent exam question.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Inference Rule Selection Guide

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Choose the right inference rule by recognizing these patterns:**

| Given Premises | Goal Pattern | Use This Rule |
|----------------|--------------|---------------|
| `∀x (P(x) → Q(x))`, `P(a)` | Prove `Q(a)` | Modus Ponens (generalized) |
| `∀x (P(x) → Q(x))`, `¬Q(a)` | Prove `¬P(a)` | Modus Tollens (generalized) |
| `∀x P(x)` | `P(c)` for any `c` | Universal Instantiation |
| `∃x P(x)` | Introduce fresh `c` with `P(c)` | Existential Instantiation (Skolem) |
| Two clauses with complementary literals | Resolvent (fewer literals) | Resolution |
| `P → Q`, `Q → R` | `P → R` | Hypothetical Syllogism |
| `P ∨ Q`, `¬P` | `Q` | Disjunctive Syllogism |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of First-Order Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startNode([Natural Language Sentence])
    synSyntax[Syntax Layer]
    semSemantics[Semantics Layer]
    inferInf[Inference Engine]

    startNode --> synSyntax
    synSyntax --> semSemantics
    semSemantics --> inferInf

    synSyntax --> constA[Constants: John, 0, India]
    synSyntax --> varA[Variables: x, y, z]
    synSyntax --> funcA[Functions: fatherOf, successor]
    synSyntax --> predA[Predicates: Man, Loves, Mortal]
    synSyntax --> quantA[Quantifiers: FORALL, EXISTS]
    synSyntax --> connA[Connectives: AND OR NOT IMPLIES IFF]

    semSemantics --> intA[Interpretation: Domain and Mapping]
    semSemantics --> truthA[Truth Value Assignment]
    semSemantics --> modelA[Model: Truth-Making Structure]

    inferInf --> uiNode[Universal Instantiation]
    inferInf --> eiNode[Existential Instantiation]
    inferInf --> ugNode[Universal Generalization]
    inferInf --> egNode[Existential Generalization]
    inferInf --> mpNode[Modus Ponens]
    inferInf --> mtNode[Modus Tollens]
    inferInf --> resNode[Resolution]

    style startNode fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style synSyntax fill:#add8e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style semSemantics fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style inferInf fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.2 Logic Programming (Prolog) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userA[User Query] --> kbA{Prolog Engine}
    kbA --> factA[Facts Layer]
    kbA --> ruleA[Rules Layer]
    kbA --> unifyA[Unification Module]
    kbA --> bcA[Backward Chaining Module]

    factA --> fa1[likes mary food]
    factA --> fa2[likes john wine]
    factA --> fa3[friend mary john]

    ruleA --> ra1[Rule 1: likes X Y if friend X Z and likes Z Y]
    ruleA --> ra2[Rule 2: happy X if likes X wine]

    unifyA --> mguA[Compute MGU]
    bcA --> stackA[Goal Stack]
    bcA --> traceA[Proof Trace]
    bcA --> backA[Backtracking on Failure]

    mguA --> stackA
    stackA --> backA
    backA --> stackA
    backA --> outA[Answer or NO]

    style userA fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style kbA fill:#ff7f50,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style factA fill:#98fb98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ruleA fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style unifyA fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style bcA fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style outA fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Resolution Proof Workflow (For a FOL Knowledge Base)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startNode([KB and Query to Prove Q])
    cnNode[Convert KB to CNF Clauses]
    negNode[Negate Q and Add as Clause]
    pickNode[Pick Two Clauses with Complementary Literals]
    unifyNode[Compute MGU]
    resolveNode[Apply Resolution to Get Resolvent]
    checkEmpty{Resolvent Empty?}
    successNode[Q is Proved]
    failNode[Add Resolvent to Clause Set]
    pickMore{More Clause Pairs?}
    noProveNode[Cannot Prove Q with Current KB]

    startNode --> cnNode
    cnNode --> negNode
    negNode --> pickNode
    pickNode --> unifyNode
    unifyNode --> resolveNode
    resolveNode --> checkEmpty
    checkEmpty -- Yes --> successNode
    checkEmpty -- No --> failNode
    failNode --> pickMore
    pickMore -- Yes --> pickNode
    pickMore -- No --> noProveNode

    style startNode fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style successNode fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style noProveNode fill:#ff6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style checkEmpty fill:#fffacd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.4 Knowledge Base Hierarchy: From Propositional to FOL to Logic Programming

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    level0[Propositional Logic]
    level1[First Order Logic FOL]
    level2[Horn Clause Subsystem]
    level3[Logic Programming Prolog]

    level0 --> level1
    level1 --> level2
    level2 --> level3

    level0Desc[Atomic propositions P Q R plus connectives. Cannot express objects or relations between them.]
    level1Desc[Adds variables quantifiers predicates functions. Can express general truths about objects.]
    level2Desc[Restricts FOL to at most one positive literal per clause. Decidable efficient.]
    level3Desc[Prolog implements Horn clauses via backward chaining and unification. Executable logic.]

    level0 --- level0Desc
    level1 --- level1Desc
    level2 --- level2Desc
    level3 --- level3Desc

    style level0 fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style level1 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style level2 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```
