# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define predicate logic. How does it differ from propositional logic?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Predicate logic (First-Order Logic, FOL)** is an extension of propositional logic that introduces **variables, quantifiers (∀, ∃), predicates, and functions**, allowing the representation of facts about *objects* and *relations* among them.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Propositional Logic | Predicate Logic |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------|
| Atomic unit | Whole proposition (`P`, `Q`) | Predicate applied to terms (`P(x)`, `Loves(x,y)`) |
| Variables | None | Bound by quantifiers |
| Expressive power | Limited — cannot quantify over objects | High — can express general rules |
| Example | "It is raining" | "∀x (Person(x) → ∃y (Book(y) ∧ Reads(x, y)))" |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is unification in FOL? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Unification** is the process of finding a substitution `θ` that makes two logical expressions identical. The **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is the simplest such substitution.

**Example:**
- `Loves(John, x)` and `Loves(y, Mary)`
- MGU: `θ = {y/John, x/Mary}`
- Unified expression: `Loves(John, Mary)`

Unification is the computational backbone of **resolution** and **Prolog's inference engine**.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Represent the following statements in First-Order Logic. (7 marks)

1. "Every dog likes some person."
2. "Some student knows every professor."
3. "No cat is a dog."
4. "All birds have feathers and can fly."
5. "There is a person who is liked by everyone."

**(b)** Given the following FOL knowledge base, convert to **Clause Normal Form (CNF)** and prove the goal `¬Happy(John)` using resolution. (7 marks)

- `∀x (Likes(x, IceCream) → Happy(x))`
- `Likes(John, IceCream) ∨ Likes(John, Cake)`
- `¬Likes(John, Cake)`

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — FOL Translations (7 marks, ~1.4 per sub)

1. **"Every dog likes some person."**

   The pattern is "All A like some B" → `∀x (A(x) → ∃y (B(y) ∧ Likes(x, y)))`.

   $$\forall x \, (\text{Dog}(x) \rightarrow \exists y \, (\text{Person}(y) \wedge \text{Likes}(x, y)))$$

2. **"Some student knows every professor."**

   Pattern: "Some A know every B" → `∃x (A(x) ∧ ∀y (B(y) → Knows(x, y)))`.

   $$\exists x \, (\text{Student}(x) \wedge \forall y \, (\text{Professor}(y) \rightarrow \text{Knows}(x, y)))$$

3. **"No cat is a dog."**

   "No A is B" → `∀x (A(x) → ¬B(x))`.

   $$\forall x \, (\text{Cat}(x) \rightarrow \neg\text{Dog}(x))$$

4. **"All birds have feathers and can fly."**

   "All A are (B and C)" → `∀x (A(x) → (B(x) ∧ C(x)))`.

   $$\forall x \, (\text{Bird}(x) \rightarrow (\text{HasFeathers}(x) \wedge \text{CanFly}(x)))$$

5. **"There is a person who is liked by everyone."**

   Pattern: `∃x (A(x) ∧ ∀y Likes(y, x))`.

   $$\exists x \, (\text{Person}(x) \wedge \forall y \, \text{Likes}(y, x))$$

---

#### Part (b) — Resolution Proof (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert each premise to CNF (clauses).**

Premise 1: `∀x (Likes(x, IceCream) → Happy(x))`
- Implication: `∀x (¬Likes(x, IceCream) ∨ Happy(x))`
- **Clause 1:** `{¬Likes(x, IceCream), Happy(x)}`

Premise 2: `Likes(John, IceCream) ∨ Likes(John, Cake)`
- **Clause 2:** `{Likes(John, IceCream), Likes(John, Cake)}`

Premise 3: `¬Likes(John, Cake)`
- **Clause 3:** `{¬Likes(John, Cake)}`

**Negation of goal:** `¬(¬Happy(John))` = `Happy(John)` must be proved.
- Add negated goal as: `¬Happy(John)`.
- **Clause 4:** `{¬Happy(John)}`

**Step 2 — Resolution Steps.**

Resolve **Clause 1** (`x = John`) with **Clause 4**:

- Clause 1 instantiated: `{¬Likes(John, IceCream), Happy(John)}`
- Clause 4: `{¬Happy(John)}`
- Complementary: `Happy(John)` vs `¬Happy(John)`.
- **Resolvent (Clause 5):** `{¬Likes(John, IceCream)}`

Resolve **Clause 2** with **Clause 3**:

- Clause 2: `{Likes(John, IceCream), Likes(John, Cake)}`
- Clause 3: `{¬Likes(John, Cake)}`
- Complementary: `Likes(John, Cake)` vs `¬Likes(John, Cake)`.
- **Resolvent (Clause 6):** `{Likes(John, IceCream)}`

Resolve **Clause 5** with **Clause 6**:

- Clause 5: `{¬Likes(John, IceCream)}`
- Clause 6: `{Likes(John, IceCream)}`
- Complementary: `Likes(John, IceCream)` vs `¬Likes(John, IceCream)`.
- **Resolvent:** `{}` (empty clause)

**Step 3 — Conclusion.** The empty clause `□` indicates contradiction, so the negation of the goal is inconsistent with the KB. Therefore, `¬Happy(John)` is **proved**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define **logic programming**. Explain the three basic constructs of a Prolog program. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider the following Prolog program and answer the queries, showing the full **unification and backtracking trace**. (7 marks)

```prolog
parent(pam, bob).
parent(tom, bob).
parent(tom, liz).
parent(bob, ann).
parent(bob, pat).
female(pam).
female(liz).
female(ann).
female(pat).
male(tom).
male(bob).

mother(X, Y) :- parent(X, Y), female(X).
grandmother(X, Z) :- mother(X, Y), parent(Y, Z).
sister(X, Y) :- parent(Z, X), parent(Z, Y), female(X), X \= Y.
```

Queries:
1. `?- mother(pam, bob).`
2. `?- mother(liz, ann).`
3. `?- grandmother(pam, X).`
4. `?- grandmother(X, ann).`
5. `?- sister(ann, pat).`

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Logic Programming & Prolog Constructs (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic programming** is a programming paradigm in which programs are expressed as a set of **declarative logical statements**, and computation proceeds by **logical inference** (typically resolution + backward chaining), rather than by explicit control flow.

**Three basic constructs of a Prolog program:**

1. **Facts** — unconditional true statements about the world. Example: `likes(mary, food).`
2. **Rules** — conditional statements: `Head :- Body.`. The head is true if the body is true. Example: `likes(X, Y) :- friend(X, Z), likes(Z, Y).`
3. **Queries (Goals)** — questions posed to the Prolog interpreter. Example: `?- likes(mary, wine).`

**Execution model:** Prolog uses **depth-first, left-to-right backward chaining** with **unification** to bind variables. If a sub-goal fails, the engine **backtracks** to the most recent choice point and tries the next alternative clause.

---

#### Part (b) — Prolog Query Traces (7 marks)

**Query 1:** `?- mother(pam, bob).`

- Try rule `mother(X, Y) :- parent(X, Y), female(X).`
- Unify: `X = pam`, `Y = bob`.
- Sub-goal 1: `parent(pam, bob)` → match fact 1. ✓
- Sub-goal 2: `female(pam)` → match fact 6. ✓
- **Answer:** `yes`.

---

**Query 2:** `?- mother(liz, ann).`

- Unify `X = liz`, `Y = ann`.
- Sub-goal 1: `parent(liz, ann)` → no matching fact (liz is not a parent of ann). **Fail.**
- No more rules.
- **Answer:** `no`.

---

**Query 3:** `?- grandmother(pam, X).`

- Rule: `grandmother(X, Z) :- mother(X, Y), parent(Y, Z).`
- Unify outer `X = pam`. New variable `Z` (in goal) unifies with `Z` in rule.
- Sub-goal 1: `mother(pam, Y)`.
   - Recursive call to `mother` rule: `X = pam, Y = ?`
   - Sub-goal: `parent(pam, Y)` → matches `parent(pam, bob)`. So `Y = bob`.
   - Sub-goal: `female(pam)` → true. ✓
   - Therefore `mother(pam, bob)` succeeds.
- Sub-goal 2: `parent(bob, Z)`.
   - Matches `parent(bob, ann)`. So `Z = ann`. ✓
- **Answer:** `X = ann`.

---

**Query 4:** `?- grandmother(X, ann).`

- Rule: `grandmother(X, Z) :- mother(X, Y), parent(Y, Z)`. Unify `Z = ann`.
- Sub-goal 1: `mother(X, Y)`.
   - Try `parent(X, Y)` facts in order. The first parent facts are `pam→bob` and `tom→bob`.
   - **Try 1:** `X = pam`, `Y = bob`. Check `female(pam)` → true. So `mother(pam, bob)` succeeds.
- Sub-goal 2: `parent(bob, ann)` → matches. ✓
- **First answer:** `X = pam`.

- Backtrack, try alternative for `mother(X, Y)`:
   - **Try 2:** `X = tom`, `Y = bob`. `female(tom)`? No (tom is male). Fail.
   - **Try 3:** `X = bob`, `Y = ann`. `female(bob)`? No. Fail.
   - **Try 4:** `X = bob`, `Y = pat`. `female(bob)`? No. Fail.
- No more mothers succeed that lead to `parent(Y, ann)`.

- **Answer:** `X = pam` (single answer).

> **Exam Tip:** Without `mother(bob, ann)`, the second grandmother (via the `bob` line) does not appear. This is because `mother(bob, ann)` would require `female(bob)`, which is false.

---

**Query 5:** `?- sister(ann, pat).`

- Rule: `sister(X, Y) :- parent(Z, X), parent(Z, Y), female(X), X \= Y.`
- Unify: `X = ann`, `Y = pat`.
- Sub-goal 1: `parent(Z, ann)` → match `parent(bob, ann)`. So `Z = bob`.
- Sub-goal 2: `parent(bob, pat)` → match fact 5. ✓
- Sub-goal 3: `female(ann)` → true. ✓
- Sub-goal 4: `ann \= pat` → true (they are distinct). ✓
- **Answer:** `yes`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Quantifier confusion:** "All A are B" is `∀x (A(x) → B(x))` — **NOT** `∀x (A(x) ∧ B(x))`. The latter says "everything is both A and B", which is wrong.
> 2. **Existential with conjunction vs. implication:** "Some A are B" is `∃x (A(x) ∧ B(x))` — **NOT** `∃x (A(x) → B(x))`, which is trivially true for everything not in A.
> 3. **Negating quantifiers:** Forgetting De Morgan's rule and writing `¬∀x P(x)` as `∀x ¬P(x)` (or vice versa). The correct form is `∃x ¬P(x)`.
> 4. **Skolem constants:** When applying EI to `∃x P(x)`, students forget to use a *fresh* constant and reuse an existing one, breaking soundness.
> 5. **MGU steps not shown:** For 7-mark unification questions, the MGU must be derived **step-by-step**. Skipping intermediate substitutions loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Empty clause interpretation:** Forgetting to state that the empty clause `{}` (or `□`) means *false* — and therefore the original (un-negated) goal is **proved**.
> 7. **Prolog unification with `=:=` vs `=`.** KTU may ask about Prolog's built-in `=` (unification) versus `=\=` (not unifiable) and `\=` (not equal after evaluation). Students often confuse these.
> 8. **Prolog backtracking traces:** Omitting the "try → fail → backtrack" sequence in trace questions loses full marks; examiners want to see the *search tree* explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Predicate Logic and Logic Programming**

- **Predicate logic (FOL)** = propositional logic + **variables + quantifiers (∀, ∃) + predicates + functions**.
- **Universal quantifier `∀`** = "for all"; pattern is `A(x) → B(x)`.
- **Existential quantifier `∃`** = "there exists"; pattern is `A(x) ∧ B(x)`.
- **De Morgan for FOL:** `¬∀x P ≡ ∃x ¬P` and `¬∃x P ≡ ∀x ¬P`.
- **Translation patterns to memorize:**
   - "All A are B" → `∀x (A(x) → B(x))`
   - "Some A are B" → `∃x (A(x) ∧ B(x))`
   - "No A are B" → `∀x (A(x) → ¬B(x))`
   - "Only A are B" → `∀x (B(x) → A(x))`
- **Inference rules:** UI, EI, UG, EG, Modus Ponens, Modus Tollens, Resolution.
- **Resolution rule (FOL):** resolve two clauses with complementary literals via **unification** to produce a resolvent.
- **MGU (Most General Unifier):** simplest substitution that unifies two atoms; algorithm proceeds term-by-term.
- **Resolution proof strategy:** convert to CNF → negate goal → repeatedly resolve until the **empty clause `{}`** is derived.
- **Logic programming = programs as logical statements.** Prolog is the canonical language.
- **Prolog has three constructs:** **Facts** (truths), **Rules** (`Head :- Body`), **Queries** (goals).
- **Prolog execution:** **backward chaining + depth-first + left-to-right** with **unification-based variable binding** and **backtracking on failure**.
- **Horn clause:** a clause with **at most one positive literal** — the form Prolog accepts.
- **Knowledge base hierarchy:** Propositional → FOL → Horn clauses → Prolog (increasing executability, decreasing expressive power).
- **Real-world uses:** expert systems, NLP grammars (DCG), Datalog, theorem provers, formal verification.
- **Common pitfalls to avoid:** (1) wrong quantifier pattern for "all/some", (2) forgetting the **occurs check** in unification, (3) reusing Skolem constants, (4) not showing full backtracking trace, (5) confusing `=` (Prolog unification) with `is` (arithmetic evaluation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semantic networks, frames, and inheritance systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT502 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Semantic networks, frames, and inheritance systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Semantic Networks — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Semantic Network (Definition per KTU Syllabus):** A semantic network is a **directed graph-based knowledge representation scheme** in which **nodes** represent *concepts* (objects, events, situations, or actions) and **labeled directed edges** (arcs) represent the *semantic relationships* between those concepts. It was introduced by **Ross Quillian (1968)** as a model of human associative memory.

A semantic network is essentially a **labeled directed graph** $G = (V, E, L)$ where:
- $V$ = finite set of **nodes** (concepts/objects)
- $E \subseteq V \times V$ = set of **directed edges**
- $L$ = set of **edge labels** (relationship types)

### 🔑 Intuition — "The Family Tree of Ideas"

Imagine you are explaining to a 10-year-old what a *sparrow* is. You would say: *"A sparrow IS-A bird, HAS-A beak, CAN fly, and LIVES-IN trees."* That single sentence is a mini semantic network. The **bird** is the parent, **sparrow** is the child, and the arrows "IS-A", "HAS-A", "CAN" are the labeled relationships. Every fact you state is one **edge** in the network.

In AI, semantic networks allow the system to *inherit* properties: because sparrow IS-A bird, and bird CAN fly, the system can automatically conclude that sparrow CAN fly — **without explicitly being told**.

---

## 1.2 Frames — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frame (Minsky, 1975):** A frame is a **structured data record** representing a *stereotyped situation or object*. Each frame contains **slots** (attributes) and **fillers** (values). Slots may contain **default values**, **pointers to other frames**, **constraints**, or **attached procedures (demons)**.

A frame is analogous to a **class in Object-Oriented Programming** — a template with named fields (slots) that may hold data, defaults, or references to other frames.

### 🔑 Intuition — "The Restaurant Menu Card"

Think of a **restaurant menu card**. Every dish entry has the same structure: a Name slot, a Price slot, a Cuisine slot, a Spicy-Level slot, a Description slot, and a chef-recommendation default. That menu card is a **frame**. The template is reusable: *Chicken Biryani* fills the slots, *Paneer Butter Masala* fills another instance of the same template. The default "₹250" for Price can be overridden if the dish is premium.

In AI, frames let us model **stereotypical knowledge** — for example, a *Classroom* frame has slots like `teacher`, `students`, `subject`, `blackboard`, each with expected values.

---

## 1.3 Inheritance Systems — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Inheritance System:** A mechanism in semantic networks and frame systems that allows a child node/frame to **automatically acquire properties (slots, values, relations)** from its parent node/frame via the **IS-A** (or `ako` / `instance-of`) link, unless explicitly overridden locally.

There are two principal forms:
- **Single Inheritance** — each node has at most **one parent**.
- **Multiple Inheritance** — a node may have **two or more parents**, requiring **conflict resolution** when parents disagree on a slot value.

### 🔑 Intuition — "The Genetic Trait"

You inherit your eye color from your parents, your hair texture from your grandfather, and your smile from your grandmother — all automatically, without being told. Inheritance in AI works the same way: a *subclass* inherits the slots/values of its *superclass* unless it **explicitly overrides** them. The **IS-A** link is the biological "gene transfer" of AI.

---

## 1.4 Standard Edge / Slot Vocabulary (KTU High-Yield)

| Symbol | Meaning | Direction | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IS-A** | Subclass / Kind-of | Child → Parent | *Sparrow IS-A Bird* |
| **INSTANCE-OF** | Example-of | Object → Class | *Tweety INSTANCE-OF Sparrow* |
| **HAS-A / PART-OF** | Component relation | Part → Whole | *Beak PART-OF Sparrow* |
| **AKO (A-Kind-Of)** | Synonym of IS-A | Sub → Super | *Canary AKO Bird* |
| **CAN / ABLE** | Capability | Actor → Action | *Bird CAN fly* |
| **AT / LOC** | Location | Object → Place | *Sparrow AT Tree* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, you must clearly distinguish **IS-A** (taxonomic / inheritance) from **HAS-A / PART-OF** (aggregation / composition). Marks are frequently lost here.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Semantic Network

A semantic network is built on three pillars:

1. **Nodes (Vertices)** — Represent:
   - Concrete objects (e.g., *Sparrow*, *Car*)
   - Concepts / Categories (e.g., *Bird*, *Vehicle*)
   - Events or Actions (e.g., *Fly-Event*)

2. **Edges (Arcs)** — Always **directed** and **labeled**. They represent *binary predicates* between two concepts. An edge from node $A$ to node $B$ with label $R$ is equivalent to the logical assertion:
$$R(A, B)$$

3. **Path / Inference Chains** — Longer logical relations are derived by **transitive traversal**. If `IS-A(Sparrow, Bird)` and `IS-A(Bird, Animal)`, then by transitivity:
$$\text{IS-A}(Sparrow, Animal)$$

This transitive property is what enables **inheritance-based reasoning**.

---

## 2.2 Reasoning Operations on Semantic Networks

### (a) Inheritance Reasoning
- Traverse IS-A edges **upward** to inherit properties.
- Example: `can_fly(Bird) → can_fly(Sparrow)` via `IS-A(Sparrow, Bird)`.

### (b) Intersection Search
- Used to answer queries like *"Find all animals that can fly and live in trees."*
- The algorithm finds a **common ancestor** node reachable from multiple property paths.

### (c) Equivalence / Subset Reasoning
- $A \subset B$ is encoded as `IS-A(A, B)`.
- Intersection of subsets yields common sub-concepts.

### (d) Negation and Disjunction (Extensions)
- Original Quillian networks had no explicit negation.
- Extended networks (e.g., **Conceptual Graphs by Sowa**) add $\neg$, $\vee$, $\wedge$ as first-class edge labels.

---

## 2.3 Architecture of a Frame System

A frame $F$ is mathematically a tuple:
$$F = \langle \text{FrameName}, \{S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n\} \rangle$$

Each slot $S_i$ is itself a tuple:
$$S_i = \langle \text{slotName}, \text{facets} \rangle$$

A **facet** can be one of:
- **VALUE** — the actual filler (string, number, pointer, or sub-frame).
- **DEFAULT** — value used if VALUE is missing.
- **IF-NEEDED** — name of a procedure (demon) to compute the value on demand.
- **IF-ADDED** — procedure triggered when a value is added.
- **IF-REMOVED** — procedure triggered when a value is deleted.
- **RANGE** — allowed value types / constraints.
- **CARDINALITY** — minimum / maximum number of fillers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU answers, always mention **"demons"** or **"attached procedures"** (IF-NEEDED, IF-ADDED) — this is a **high-frequency examiner keyword**.

---

## 2.4 Inheritance Mechanism — Step Logic

The inheritance algorithm (single inheritance, depth-first):

1. Look up the value of slot $S$ on the current frame.
2. If present and not a local override marker → return it.
3. Else follow the `IS-A` (or `AKO`) pointer to the parent frame.
4. Repeat from step 1 on the parent.
5. If no parent and no value found → return the **default** from a predefined root frame, or `UNDEFINED`.

For **multiple inheritance** the algorithm becomes:
1. Collect candidate values from **all parents**.
2. If all parents agree → return the common value.
3. If conflict → apply a **conflict resolution policy**:
   - **Specificity ordering** — most specific (deepest) class wins.
   - **Priority ordering** — pre-declared class priority.
   - **Recency ordering** — most recently added class wins.
   - **User-defined ordering** — explicit exception list.

---

## 2.5 KTU Cheat Sheet — Compact Table

| Concept | Symbol / Form | KTU Key Term | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Semantic Network | $G=(V,E,L)$ | Directed labeled graph | NLP, ontology engineering, semantic web |
| IS-A Edge | $\text{IS-A}(c, p)$ | Subclass link | Class hierarchy in OOP, taxonomies |
| PART-OF Edge | $\text{PART-OF}(p, w)$ | Aggregation | CAD, system decomposition |
| Frame | $\langle F, \{S_i\} \rangle$ | Slot-filler structure | Schemata, situation modelling |
| Slot Facet | VALUE / DEFAULT | Demons | Active databases, expert shells |
| Inheritance | transitive IS-A | Property propagation | Rule engines, OOP inheritance |
| Multiple Inheritance | merge of parents | Conflict resolution | Mixed-ontology AI, RDF schemas |
| Default Reasoning | non-monotonic | Closed-world | Diagnostic expert systems |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** Semantic networks are the *theoretical ancestor of RDF/OWL* (used in the modern **Semantic Web**). Frames directly inspired **object-oriented programming languages** (Smalltalk, Java, C++ classes). Multiple inheritance in AI is the basis of **hybrid ontologies** in bioinformatics and IoT knowledge graphs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Building a Semantic Network (Exhaustive)

**Problem:** Represent the following statements as a semantic network:
1. *Tweety is a canary.*
2. *Canary is a bird.*
3. *Bird is an animal.*
4. *Bird can fly.*
5. *Tweety is yellow.*
6. *Tweety lives in a cage.*
7. *Cage is a man-made object.*

### Step-by-Step Translation

| English Statement | Logical Form | Network Edge |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Tweety is a canary | $\text{INSTANCE-OF}(Tweety, Canary)$ | $Tweety \xrightarrow{\text{INSTANCE-OF}} Canary$ |
| 2. Canary is a bird | $\text{IS-A}(Canary, Bird)$ | $Canary \xrightarrow{\text{IS-A}} Bird$ |
| 3. Bird is an animal | $\text{IS-A}(Bird, Animal)$ | $Bird \xrightarrow{\text{IS-A}} Animal$ |
| 4. Bird can fly | $\text{CAN}(Bird, Fly)$ | $Bird \xrightarrow{\text{CAN}} Fly$ |
| 5. Tweety is yellow | $\text{COLOR}(Tweety, Yellow)$ | $Tweety \xrightarrow{\text{COLOR}} Yellow$ |
| 6. Tweety lives in a cage | $\text{AT}(Tweety, Cage)$ | $Tweety \xrightarrow{\text{AT}} Cage$ |
| 7. Cage is a man-made object | $\text{IS-A}(Cage, ManMade)$ | $Cage \xrightarrow{\text{IS-A}} ManMade$ |

### Deriving Inferred Facts (Inheritance)

By **transitivity of IS-A**:
$$\text{IS-A}(Canary, Bird) \;\wedge\; \text{IS-A}(Bird, Animal) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{IS-A}(Canary, Animal)$$

By **instance transitivity**:
$$\text{INSTANCE-OF}(Tweety, Canary) \;\wedge\; \text{IS-A}(Canary, Bird) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{INSTANCE-OF}(Tweety, Bird)$$

By **property inheritance**:
$$\text{INSTANCE-OF}(Tweety, Bird) \;\wedge\; \text{CAN}(Bird, Fly) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{CAN}(Tweety, Fly)$$

**Final inferred facts** without any new input:
- Tweety is an animal
- Tweety can fly
- Tweety is a man-made-object's cohabitant (not literally true — used to test reasoning limits)

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the last "inference" is **semantically incorrect in the real world** because `AT(Tweety, Cage)` does NOT mean Tweety is a man-made object. This is the classic **inheritance anomaly** in semantic networks — it shows why **semantic constraints** are needed. The KTU examiner loves this limitation as a 3-mark discussion point.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Frame System Design (Exhaustive)

**Problem:** Design a frame hierarchy for an **academic university system** with three levels: *Person* (root), *Faculty* and *Student* (intermediate), and three instances *DrAnu*, *DrRaj*, *Mohan*.

### Frame Specification Table

| Frame | Slot | Facet / Filler |
|---|---|---|
| **PERSON** | name | VALUE: (string) |
| | age | VALUE: (integer), RANGE: 0–120 |
| | gender | VALUE: (M/F/Other) |
| | address | VALUE: (string) |
| | ISA | VALUE: ROOT |
| **FACULTY** | ISA | VALUE: PERSON |
| | emp_id | VALUE: (string) |
| | department | VALUE: (CS / EC / ME / …) |
| | designation | DEFAULT: "Assistant Professor" |
| | salary | IF-NEEDED: compute_salary(dept, desig) |
| | teaches | CARDINALITY: ≥ 1 |
| **STUDENT** | ISA | VALUE: PERSON |
| | roll_no | VALUE: (string) |
| | programme | DEFAULT: "B.Tech" |
| | cgpa | RANGE: 0.0–10.0, DEFAULT: 0.0 |
| | semester | IF-NEEDED: current_semester() |
| **DrAnu** | ISA | VALUE: FACULTY |
| | name | VALUE: "Dr. Anu" |
| | department | VALUE: "CS" |
| | designation | VALUE: "Professor" (overrides default) |
| | salary | IF-NEEDED: compute_salary("CS", "Professor") |
| **DrRaj** | ISA | VALUE: FACULTY |
| | name | VALUE: "Dr. Raj" |
| | department | VALUE: "EC" |
| **Mohan** | ISA | VALUE: STUDENT |
| | name | VALUE: "Mohan" |
| | roll_no | VALUE: "KTU2024CS047" |
| | cgpa | VALUE: 8.7 |

### Step-by-Step Inheritance Trace for DrRaj.salary

1. Look up `salary` in **DrRaj** frame → not present.
2. Follow `ISA` link → go to **FACULTY** frame.
3. Look up `salary` in **FACULTY** → VALUE slot empty, but `IF-NEEDED: compute_salary` is registered.
4. **Demon fires** → `compute_salary("EC", "Assistant Professor")` executes.
5. Result returned as DrRaj's salary (assumed ₹80,000).

> This trace is the **expected model answer** for a KTU 14-mark frame question.

---

## 3.3 Multiple Inheritance — Conflict Resolution Worked Example

**Problem:** A *TeachingAssistant (TA)* frame inherits from both **STUDENT** and **FACULTY**. Both parents define the `programme` slot differently.

| Parent | `programme` value |
|---|---|
| STUDENT | "B.Tech CSE" |
| FACULTY | (no value; inherited from PERSON → undefined) |

**Resolution by Specificity Ordering:** TA inherits the more specific parent's value:
$$\text{ResolvedValue}(TA.programme) = \text{STUDENT.programme} = \text{"B.Tech CSE"}$$

**Resolution by Recency:** If `STUDENT` was added to TA's parent list *after* `FACULTY`:
$$\text{ResolvedValue}(TA.programme) = \text{STUDENT.programme}$$

**Conflict if parents disagree** with both having specific values (e.g., STUDENT.programme = "M.Tech AI" and FACULTY.programme = "PhD Scholar"):
- Specificity says: TA is mostly a student → use "M.Tech AI".
- Priority says: pre-declared FACULTY.priority = 2, STUDENT.priority = 1 → use "PhD Scholar".
- The **policy chosen at design time** wins.

This is a frequently tested KTU scenario — **always state the chosen policy explicitly**.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Feature | Semantic Network | Frame System |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Graph (nodes + edges) | Record-like (slots + facets) |
| Unit of knowledge | Node + labeled arc | Slot-filler pair |
| Inheritance link | IS-A edge | ISA slot pointer |
| Default values | Limited (via special nodes) | Native (DEFAULT facet) |
| Procedural attachment | Not standard | Built-in (IF-NEEDED demon) |
| Constraints | None natively | RANGE, CARDINALITY facets |
| Best for | Associative recall, ontology | Stereotyped situations, expert shells |
| Limitation | Lacks structure, hard to validate | Less visual, more verbose |
| Inventor / Year | Quillian, 1968 | Minsky, 1975 |
| Modern descendant | RDF, OWL (Semantic Web) | Object-Oriented classes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Semantic Network — Bird Domain (Tweety Example)

```mermaid
graph TD
    AnimalNode["ANIMAL"]
    BirdNode["BIRD"]
    CanaryNode["CANARY"]
    TweetyNode["TWEETY"]
    FlyNode["FLY"]
    YellowNode["YELLOW"]
    CageNode["CAGE"]
    ManMadeNode["MAN-MADE"]
    WingsNode["WINGS"]
    FeathersNode["FEATHERS"]

    BirdNode -- "IS-A" --> AnimalNode
    CanaryNode -- "IS-A" --> BirdNode
    TweetyNode -- "INSTANCE-OF" --> CanaryNode
    BirdNode -- "CAN" --> FlyNode
    TweetyNode -- "COLOR" --> YellowNode
    TweetyNode -- "AT" --> CageNode
    CageNode -- "IS-A" --> ManMadeNode
    BirdNode -- "HAS-A" --> WingsNode
    BirdNode -- "HAS-A" --> FeathersNode
```

**Reading the diagram:** Every solid arrow is a labeled directed edge. To answer *"Can Tweety fly?"*, the system traverses `Tweety → INSTANCE-OF → Canary → IS-A → Bird → CAN → Fly` and returns **TRUE**.

---

## 4.2 Frame Hierarchy — University Domain (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    RootFrame["FRAME PERSON"]
    FacFrame["FRAME FACULTY"]
    StuFrame["FRAME STUDENT"]
    TAFrame["FRAME TA MultipleInheritance"]
    InstAnu["INSTANCE DrAnu"]
    InstRaj["INSTANCE DrRaj"]
    InstMohan["INSTANCE Mohan"]
    InstTA1["INSTANCE Neha"]

    RootFrame -- "ISA" --> FacFrame
    RootFrame -- "ISA" --> StuFrame
    FacFrame -- "ISA" --> TAFrame
    StuFrame -- "ISA" --> TAFrame
    FacFrame -- "ISA" --> InstAnu
    FacFrame -- "ISA" --> InstRaj
    StuFrame -- "ISA" --> InstMohan
    TAFrame -- "ISA" --> InstTA1
```

**Architecture flow:**
- `PERSON` is the **root frame** providing generic slots (name, age, gender).
- `FACULTY` and `STUDENT` inherit from `PERSON` and add role-specific slots.
- `TA` demonstrates **multiple inheritance** — arrows converge from both parents.
- Instances are leaf nodes; they fill (not redefine) the inherited slots.

---

## 4.3 Inheritance Resolution Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["Query slot S on frame F"]
    CheckLocal{"S present locally in F"}
    UseLocal["Return local value"]
    GetParents["Collect parent frames via ISA links"]
    CheckParents{"Any parents exist"}
    CheckSingle{"Single or multiple parents"}
    SingleInherit["Follow single parent chain recursively"]
    MultiInherit["Collect values from ALL parents"]
    CheckConflict{"Do all parents agree"}
    ApplyPolicy["Apply resolution policy: Specificity / Priority / Recency"]
    NoConflict["Return common value"]
    NoParents["Return DEFAULT from root or UNDEFINED"]
    FireDemon["If IF-NEEDED facet present fire demon compute slot value"]

    Start --> CheckLocal
    CheckLocal -- "Yes" --> UseLocal
    CheckLocal -- "No" --> GetParents
    GetParents --> CheckParents
    CheckParents -- "No" --> NoParents
    CheckParents -- "Yes" --> CheckSingle
    CheckSingle -- "Single" --> SingleInherit
    CheckSingle -- "Multiple" --> MultiInherit
    MultiInherit --> CheckConflict
    CheckConflict -- "Agree" --> NoConflict
    CheckConflict -- "Disagree" --> ApplyPolicy
    SingleInherit --> FireDemon
    ApplyPolicy --> FireDemon
```

**Functional interpretation:**
- The **input** is a query `(frame, slot)`.
- The **decision nodes** (diamonds) test local presence, parent existence, and conflict.
- The **terminal nodes** (rectangles) return a value, fire a demon, or declare UNDEFINED.
- This flowchart is the standard KTU diagram for any "explain the inheritance algorithm" question.

---

## 4.4 Slot-Facet Internal Structure (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    FrameBox["FRAME Faculty"]
    Slot1["Slot name: string"]
    Slot2["Slot emp_id: string"]
    Slot3["Slot department: CS or EC or ME"]
    Slot4["Slot designation"]
    Slot5["Slot salary"]
    Slot6["Slot teaches: at least 1"]

    FacetV1["Facet VALUE: empty"]
    FacetD1["Facet DEFAULT: Assistant Professor"]
    FacetV2["Facet VALUE: empty"]
    FacetR1["Facet RANGE: dept list"]
    FacetIN["Facet IF-NEEDED: compute salary function"]
    FacetC1["Facet CARDINALITY: at least 1"]

    FrameBox --> Slot1
    FrameBox --> Slot2
    FrameBox --> Slot3
    FrameBox --> Slot4
    FrameBox --> Slot5
    FrameBox --> Slot6
    Slot4 --> FacetD1
    Slot4 --> FacetV1
    Slot3 --> FacetR1
    Slot5 --> FacetIN
    Slot6 --> FacetC1
```

This block-level schematic shows **how a frame encapsulates slots and their facets**, which is the single most important visual to draw in a 14-mark frame question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Differentiate between IS-A and PART-OF relationships in a semantic network. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | IS-A | PART-OF |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Taxonomic / "kind-of" / "is-a-subtype" | Mereological / "component-of" |
| Inherits properties? | **Yes** — child inherits parent slots | **No** — parts do not inherit whole's properties |
| Transitivity | Transitive (used for inference) | Transitive in some, not in all models |
| Example | *Canary IS-A Bird* | *Wing PART-OF Bird* |

If a student writes "IS-A is super–sub and PART-OF is whole–part" they score full marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a frame? Explain the role of *demons* (attached procedures) in a frame system.**

**Model Answer:**
A **frame** (Minsky, 1975) is a structured record representing a stereotypical situation, containing **slots** (attributes) and **facets** (values, defaults, constraints, procedures).

**Demons** are procedures attached to slots, activated by trigger conditions:
- **IF-NEEDED** — fires when the slot is queried but has no direct value (e.g., computing salary on demand).
- **IF-ADDED** — fires when a value is added (e.g., updating total credits when a new course is registered).
- **IF-REMOVED** — fires when a value is deleted (e.g., re-computing CGPA after dropping a course).

Demons make frames **active** (event-driven) rather than purely passive data records.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Draw a semantic network to represent the following facts about a university domain. Show **at least two levels of inheritance** and **derive two new facts** using inheritance. (7 marks)
- *Anu is a Professor. Professor is a Faculty. Faculty is a Person.*
- *Ravi is a student. Student is a Person.*
- *Person has age and gender.*
- *Faculty has emp_id and department.*
- *Student has roll_no and cgpa.*
- *Faculty can teach. Student can study.*
- *Anu's department is CSE. Ravi's cgpa is 9.1.*

**(b)** Explain the **limitations of semantic networks** with at least **three concrete examples**. (7 marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Identify nodes:** `{Person, Faculty, Student, Professor, Anu, Ravi, CSE, age, gender, emp_id, department, roll_no, cgpa, teach, study}`

**Step 2 — Identify edges (statements → labeled arcs):**

| English | Network Edge |
|---|---|
| Anu is a Professor | `Anu → INSTANCE-OF → Professor` |
| Professor is a Faculty | `Professor → IS-A → Faculty` |
| Faculty is a Person | `Faculty → IS-A → Person` |
| Ravi is a Student | `Ravi → INSTANCE-OF → Student` |
| Student is a Person | `Student → IS-A → Person` |
| Person has age | `Person → HAS-A → age` |
| Person has gender | `Person → HAS-A → gender` |
| Faculty has emp_id | `Faculty → HAS-A → emp_id` |
| Faculty has department | `Faculty → HAS-A → department` |
| Student has roll_no | `Student → HAS-A → roll_no` |
| Student has cgpa | `Student → HAS-A → cgpa` |
| Faculty can teach | `Faculty → CAN → teach` |
| Student can study | `Student → CAN → study` |
| Anu dept CSE | `Anu → department → CSE` |
| Ravi cgpa 9.1 | `Ravi → cgpa → 9.1` |

**Step 3 — Draw the network (textual representation for answer sheet):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    PNode["PERSON"]
    FNode["FACULTY"]
    ProfNode["PROFESSOR"]
    SNode["STUDENT"]
    AnuNode["ANU"]
    RaviNode["RAVI"]
    AgeNode["age"]
    GenNode["gender"]
    EmpNode["emp_id"]
    DeptNode["department"]
    RollNode["roll_no"]
    CgpaNode["cgpa"]
    TeachNode["teach"]
    StudyNode["study"]
    CSENode["CSE"]
    ScoreNode["9.1"]

    PNode -- "HAS-A" --> AgeNode
    PNode -- "HAS-A" --> GenNode
    FNode -- "IS-A" --> PNode
    ProfNode -- "IS-A" --> FNode
    AnuNode -- "INSTANCE-OF" --> ProfNode
    SNode -- "IS-A" --> PNode
    RaviNode -- "INSTANCE-OF" --> SNode
    FNode -- "HAS-A" --> EmpNode
    FNode -- "HAS-A" --> DeptNode
    SNode -- "HAS-A" --> RollNode
    SNode -- "HAS-A" --> CgpaNode
    FNode -- "CAN" --> TeachNode
    SNode -- "CAN" --> StudyNode
    AnuNode -- "department" --> CSENode
    RaviNode -- "cgpa" --> ScoreNode
```

**Step 4 — Derived facts via inheritance (the marks-rich part):**

By transitive IS-A:
$$\text{IS-A}(Professor, Faculty) \;\wedge\; \text{IS-A}(Faculty, Person) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{IS-A}(Professor, Person)$$

By instance transitivity:
$$\text{INSTANCE-OF}(Anu, Professor) \;\wedge\; \text{IS-A}(Professor, Person) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{INSTANCE-OF}(Anu, Person)$$

**Inferred Fact 1:** *Anu is a Person* (via two-level IS-A chain).
**Inferred Fact 2:** *Anu can teach* (Faculty.CAN teach, inherited via Professor → Faculty).

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) — Limitations of Semantic Networks

1. **No standard semantics:** Different authors use different edge labels (`IS-A` vs `ako` vs `subclass-of`), causing interoperability problems. (2 marks)
2. **No quantifier support natively:** Original Quillian networks cannot represent "All birds fly" — only "Tweety IS-A Bird, Bird CAN fly". (2 marks)
3. **Inheritance anomalies (nonsense inheritance):** As shown in the Tweety example, `AT(Tweety, Cage) ∧ IS-A(Cage, ManMade)` does NOT imply Tweety is man-made, but unconstrained inheritance may produce such wrong inferences. (2 marks)
4. **Combinatorial explosion:** Large networks become hard to traverse; no efficient indexing is native. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Many students write vague answers like *"semantic networks are slow"* without concrete examples. You **must cite a specific anomalous inference** to score above 5 marks. The Tweety-in-Cage example is the gold standard.

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** ⭐ Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** Design a complete **frame hierarchy** for a *Hospital Management System* with frames `Person`, `Doctor`, `Nurse`, `Patient`, and instances `DrMeera`, `NurseAnu`, `PatientRaju`. Show at least **one multiple-inheritance case** (e.g., `ConsultantDoctor` inheriting from both `Doctor` and `Administrator`). Include slot facets: VALUE, DEFAULT, IF-NEEDED, RANGE. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **inheritance algorithm** with a **flowchart**, and discuss **conflict resolution in multiple inheritance** using three standard policies. (7 marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Frame Hierarchy Table (this is the marks-dense content):**

| Frame | Slot | Facet (Type: Value) |
|---|---|---|
| **PERSON** | name | VALUE: string |
| | age | VALUE: int, RANGE: 0–130 |
| | gender | VALUE: M/F/Other |
| | contact | VALUE: string |
| | ISA | VALUE: ROOT |
| **DOCTOR** | ISA | VALUE: PERSON |
| | emp_id | VALUE: string |
| | specialization | VALUE: Cardiology / Ortho / … (RANGE) |
| | consultation_fee | DEFAULT: 500, IF-NEEDED: compute_fee(spec) |
| | available_days | CARDINALITY: ≥ 1 |
| **NURSE** | ISA | VALUE: PERSON |
| | emp_id | VALUE: string |
| | ward | VALUE: string |
| | shift | DEFAULT: "Day" |
| **PATIENT** | ISA | VALUE: PERSON |
| | patient_id | VALUE: string |
| | disease | VALUE: string |
| | admitted | DEFAULT: FALSE |
| | bill | IF-NEEDED: compute_bill(days, disease) |
| **ADMINISTRATOR** | ISA | VALUE: PERSON |
| | admin_id | VALUE: string |
| | manages | CARDINALITY: ≥ 1 |
| **CONSULTANT_DOCTOR** | ISA (multi) | VALUE: DOCTOR, ADMINISTRATOR |
| | cabin_no | VALUE: string |
| **DrMeera** | ISA | VALUE: DOCTOR |
| | name | VALUE: "Dr. Meera" |
| | specialization | VALUE: "Cardiology" |
| **NurseAnu** | ISA | VALUE: NURSE |
| | name | VALUE: "Anu" |
| | ward | VALUE: "ICU-A" |
| **PatientRaju** | ISA | VALUE: PATIENT |
| | name | VALUE: "Raju" |
| | disease | VALUE: "Diabetes" |
| | admitted | VALUE: TRUE |

**Multiple Inheritance Justification:**
`CONSULTANT_DOCTOR` inherits slots from both `DOCTOR` (specialization, fee) and `ADMINISTRATOR` (manages). For the `ISA` slot, both parents have a value; the system stores it as a **set-valued filler** `{DOCTOR, ADMINISTRATOR}` (no conflict because it is set-valued).

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Inheritance Algorithm (Pseudocode-style description for answer sheet):**

```
FUNCTION inherit_slot(F, slot):
    IF slot has explicit VALUE in F:
        RETURN that value
    IF slot has IF-NEEDED demon:
        EXECUTE demon, RETURN result
    parents ← F.ISA   // could be a set (multiple inheritance)
    IF parents is empty:
        IF F has DEFAULT for slot:
            RETURN DEFAULT
        ELSE
            RETURN UNDEFINED
    FOR each parent P in parents:
        candidate ← inherit_slot(P, slot)
        collect candidate into result_set
    RETURN resolve_conflict(result_set)
```

**Three Conflict Resolution Policies:**

1. **Specificity (Depth) Ordering:** The most specific (deepest) class in the inheritance graph wins. Example: TA inherits from FACULTY (depth 2) and STUDENT (depth 2 also); if a class `PhdStudent` inherits from both TA and STUDENT, the value from TA is preferred (deeper path). (2.5 marks)

2. **Priority Ordering:** Each class has a numeric priority; the highest-priority class's value is used. (2.5 marks)

3. **Recency Ordering:** The most recently added parent class in the inheritance list is preferred. Suitable for dynamic knowledge bases. (2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** A common mistake is to write the algorithm without a **termination condition** (you must mention the base case when `parents is empty` and when `DEFAULT` is used). Another is to list the three policies but not state the **selection criterion** for which one is preferred in a given system. Explicitly state the rule and you score 7/7.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- ✅ A **semantic network** = directed graph with **labeled arcs**; introduced by **Quillian (1968)**.
- ✅ **IS-A** = inheritance (transitive); **PART-OF / HAS-A** = aggregation (no inheritance).
- ✅ A **frame** = structured record of **slots + facets**; introduced by **Minsky (1975)**.
- ✅ Slot facets to remember: **VALUE, DEFAULT, IF-NEEDED, IF-ADDED, IF-REMOVED, RANGE, CARDINALITY**.
- ✅ **IF-NEEDED / IF-ADDED / IF-REMOVED** procedures are collectively called **demons** (active values).
- ✅ **Inheritance** = automatic property acquisition via IS-A / ISA link.
- ✅ **Single inheritance** = one parent; **Multiple inheritance** = two or more parents.
- ✅ Multiple inheritance needs **conflict resolution**: Specificity / Priority / Recency.
- ✅ **Limitation of semantic networks:** no standard semantics, no quantifiers, inheritance anomalies, combinatorial explosion.
- ✅ Frames are the **theoretical ancestor of OOP classes**; semantic networks are the **theoretical ancestor of RDF/OWL** in the modern Semantic Web.
- ✅ Always **distinguish inheritance (IS-A) from instantiation (INSTANCE-OF)** — examiner's favourite trap.
- ✅ When drawing a network, **label every arc** — an unlabeled arrow gets 0 marks.
- ✅ When designing a frame, **show at least one IF-NEEDED demon** — it is the highest-yield concept.
- ✅ For a 14-mark frame question, the ideal structure is: (1) Frame hierarchy table → (2) Inheritance trace → (3) Flowchart → (4) Multiple-inheritance case with policy choice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rule-based systems and constraint propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT502 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rule-based systems and constraint propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Rule-Based Systems (Production Systems)

A **Rule-Based System** (also called a **Production System**) is a computational model that uses a set of conditional **IF–THEN rules** (productions) operating on a global database of facts (working memory) to derive new conclusions or take actions. It is one of the earliest and most widely used paradigms of symbolic Artificial Intelligence, originally formalized by **Post (1943)** and later adapted to AI by **Newell & Simon** in their *General Problem Solver* and *SOAR* / *ACT-R* cognitive architectures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> A Production System is formally defined as the triple:  
> **PS = (R, DB, I)** where  
> **R** = Set of Production Rules (IF–THEN statements)  
> **DB** = Working Memory / Global Database (dynamic facts)  
> **I** = Inference Engine (control mechanism that selects and fires rules)

#### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The Expert Doctor

Imagine a senior doctor diagnosing a patient. The doctor has:
- A **rulebook** of medical knowledge (e.g., "IF patient has fever AND cough AND loss of smell, THEN suspect COVID-19"). This is the **Rule Base (R)**.
- A **clipboard** of current symptoms, history, and lab reports of the patient. This is the **Working Memory / DB**.
- A **clinical reasoning process** that picks which rule to apply next, updates the clipboard, and continues until a diagnosis is reached. This is the **Inference Engine (I)**.

Every time a rule is matched, the clipboard (working memory) is updated — just like a doctor recording "Suspect COVID-19" as a new fact. The cycle continues until no more rules can fire, or a goal is achieved.

#### 1.1.2 Core Components (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Component | Function | Real-world Mapping |
|-----------|----------|-------------------|
| **Production Rules (R)** | IF condition THEN action patterns | Recipes in a cookbook |
| **Working Memory (DB)** | Stores current facts / dynamic state | Notebook of a detective |
| **Inference Engine (I)** | Pattern-matches, selects, and fires rules | The reasoning mind |
| **Conflict Set** | All rules whose IF-part is satisfied | Set of viable moves in chess |
| **Conflict Resolution Strategy** | Picks ONE rule to fire from conflict set | Tie-breaker rule |
| **Rule Interpreter / Cycle** | Recognize → Act loop | Perceive → Decide → Act |

> [!NOTE]
> The cycle **Recognize → Act** is the heartbeat of every production system. Without a well-defined cycle, the system cannot progress from initial state to goal state.

---

### 1.2 Constraint Propagation

**Constraint Propagation** is a fundamental technique in **Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSPs)** where constraints between variables are used to **reduce the set of possible values (domains)**, thereby pruning the search space. Instead of guessing and backtracking blindly, the system *propagates* the implications of each assignment to neighboring variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard)**  
> Given a CSP defined by the triple **(X, D, C)** where  
> **X** = Set of variables $\lbrace X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n \rbrace$  
> **D** = Set of domains $D_i$ for each variable  
> **C** = Set of constraints restricting combinations of variable values  
> **Constraint Propagation** is the iterative process of enforcing consistency across constraints to shrink domains **without eliminating any valid solution**.

#### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — The Sudoku Solver

When you solve Sudoku, you do not randomly fill cells. You reason: *"If this row already contains 5, then the cell at row-column (3,7) cannot be 5, nor can the cell in the same column, nor the same 3×3 block."* This **ripple effect of deductions** is constraint propagation. Each placement eliminates possibilities elsewhere, shrinking the puzzle's uncertainty region by region.

#### 1.2.2 Key Terminology (Frequently Tested in KTU)

- **Node Consistency** — A variable is node-consistent if all its domain values satisfy its unary constraints.
- **Arc Consistency (AC)** — A variable $X_i$ is arc-consistent with $X_j$ if for every value in $D_i$ there exists some value in $D_j$ that satisfies the binary constraint $C(X_i, X_j)$.
- **Path Consistency** — A pair of variables $(X_i, X_j)$ is path-consistent with respect to a third variable $X_k$ if for every consistent assignment to $X_i$ and $X_j$ there exists a value of $X_k$ that satisfies all constraints.
- **k-Consistency** — A CSP is k-consistent if for any set of k−1 variables with a consistent assignment, any value for the k-th variable can be extended to maintain consistency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a Production System — Deep Dive

The production system operates in an infinite loop known as the **Recognize–Act Cycle** (or **Match–Fire Cycle**). The steps are:

1. **Match (Recognize):** The inference engine scans the rule base and finds all rules whose **antecedent (IF-part)** matches the current working memory. The collection of these rules is called the **Conflict Set**.
2. **Conflict Resolution:** If more than one rule matches (a common occurrence), a **Conflict Resolution Strategy** selects exactly one rule to fire.
3. **Act (Fire):** The selected rule's **consequent (THEN-part)** is executed. This may add facts, remove facts, or trigger external actions, modifying the working memory.
4. **Go to Step 1** (unless a halting condition is met, e.g., goal state reached or no rule matches).

> [!NOTE]
> The halting condition is critical. A production system without proper termination control may run forever, especially in monotonic domains where new facts keep being added.

---

### 2.2 Chaining Strategies — Forward vs. Backward

#### 2.2.1 Forward Chaining (Data-Driven Reasoning)

Starts with **known facts** in the working memory and applies rules to derive **all possible conclusions** until the goal is reached (or no more rules fire). It is **bottom-up** reasoning.

- **Direction:** Antecedent → Consequent
- **Use Case:** Monitoring, real-time control systems, R1/XCON (Digital's VAX configurator)
- **Output:** Every fact that can be derived from the initial knowledge base

#### 2.2.2 Backward Chaining (Goal-Driven Reasoning)

Starts with a **goal/hypothesis** and works **backwards**, looking for rules whose consequent matches the goal, then recursively trying to satisfy the antecedents of those rules. It is **top-down** reasoning.

- **Direction:** Consequent → Antecedent
- **Use Case:** Diagnostic systems, expert systems like MYCIN, Prolog interpreters
- **Output:** A proof tree / sub-goal tree showing how the goal was derived

#### 2.2.3 KTU Comparison Table — Chaining Strategies

| Property | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Reasoning Type** | Data-driven (Bottom-Up) | Goal-driven (Top-Down) |
| **Starting Point** | Known facts | Goal / hypothesis |
| **Search Direction** | Antecedent → Consequent | Consequent → Antecedent |
| **Inference Type** | Deductive (general → specific) | Abductive-like (specific → general) |
| **Best Suited For** | Monitoring, planning | Diagnosis, query answering |
| **Computational Cost** | May derive many irrelevant facts | Focused, may miss some facts |
| **Easy to Implement?** | Yes | Slightly complex |
| **Example System** | CLIPS, R1/XCON, OPS5 | MYCIN, Prolog |

---

### 2.3 Conflict Resolution Strategies (KTU High-Yield List)

When multiple rules are simultaneously eligible to fire, the system uses one or a combination of the following strategies:

1. **Specificity (Refraction / Most Specific Rule):** Prefer the rule with the most specific antecedent (most conditions). A rule with 5 conditions is preferred over a rule with 2 conditions.
2. **Recency:** Prefer rules that use recently added facts (timestamps on working memory elements). Just-fired rules are temporarily disabled to prevent infinite loops.
3. **Priority / Salience:** Each rule has a numeric priority. Highest priority rule fires first. Common in CLIPS / Jess.
4. **LEX (Lexicographic Ordering):** A multi-criteria ordering based on (1) recency, (2) specificity, (3) arbitrary rule ordering.
5. **MEA (Means–Ends Analysis):** A variant of LEX that gives highest preference to rules that act on the most recently added object (not attribute).
6. **First applicable rule in declared order:** Simple but not intelligent — rules must be carefully ordered by the knowledge engineer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to design a conflict resolution strategy, always justify it with a real-world constraint. For example, *recency* is used in **medical alarms** where the latest symptom must override older observations.

---

### 2.4 Rete Algorithm — Efficiency Optimization

Naive matching re-scans all rules every cycle → **O(R × F)** where R = rules, F = facts. The **Rete Algorithm** (Forgy, 1979) builds a discrimination network (a kind of trie) that stores partial matches and only updates affected nodes when working memory changes → typically **O(R × F)** worst case but **near O(1) per cycle** in practice.

> [!NOTE]
> Rete is the algorithm behind production systems like **CLIPS, Jess, Drools (JBoss Rules), and OPS5**. In KTU viva questions, simply knowing that Rete is a "match-acceleration algorithm using a network of node memories" is sufficient for full marks.

---

### 2.5 Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSP) — Formal Model

A CSP is defined by the triple:

$$
\text{CSP} = (X, D, C)
$$

Where:
- $X = \lbrace X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n \rbrace$ — finite set of variables
- $D = \lbrace D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n \rbrace$ — $D_i$ is the domain of $X_i$
- $C = \lbrace C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m \rbrace$ — $C_i$ restricts allowed joint values of a subset of variables

**Types of Constraints:**
- **Unary:** Involves a single variable (e.g., $X_1 \neq 3$)
- **Binary:** Involves two variables (e.g., $X_1 < X_2$)
- **Global (Higher-order):** Involves 3 or more variables (e.g., all-different for Sudoku)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Insight:** Every higher-order constraint can be converted to a set of binary constraints by introducing auxiliary variables. This is called the **dual graph / hidden variable transformation**, often tested in KTU numericals.

---

### 2.6 Constraint Propagation Techniques (Detailed)

| Technique | Scope | What it Eliminates | Algorithm |
|-----------|-------|--------------------|-----------|
| **Node Consistency** | Single variable | Domain values violating unary constraints | Trivial — O(n × d) |
| **Arc Consistency (AC-3)** | Pair of variables | Values with no support in neighbor's domain | AC-3 — O(E × d³) |
| **Path Consistency (PC-2)** | Triple of variables | Pairs of values with no third-variable witness | PC-2 — O(n³ × d⁵) |
| **k-Consistency (general)** | k variables | Inconsistent (k−1)-tuples | Exponential growth |

#### 2.6.1 The AC-3 Algorithm (Mackworth, 1977)

AC-3 maintains a **queue of arcs** to be checked. For each arc $(X_i, X_j)$:

1. For each value $v \in D_i$, check if there exists a value $w \in D_j$ such that $(v, w)$ satisfies the constraint.
2. If no such $w$ exists, **remove $v$ from $D_i$**.
3. If $D_i$ was changed, add all arcs $(X_k, X_i)$ where $X_k$ is a neighbor of $X_i$ (other than $X_j$) back to the queue.
4. Repeat until the queue is empty.

If any domain becomes empty, the CSP has **no solution**.

> [!NOTE]
> AC-3 does NOT find a solution — it only **prunes the domains**. After AC-3, you still need a search (backtracking, MRV, LCV) to actually find the assignment.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet (Tabular Form)

> **Critical Note:** All `|` symbols for absolute value / set membership are written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | KTU Frequent Use |
|---|---------|----------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Production System | $\text{PS} = (R, \ DB, \ I)$ | Definition question |
| 2 | Recognize–Act Cycle | $\text{Match} \rightarrow \text{Resolve} \rightarrow \text{Act}$ | 3-mark theory |
| 3 | Forward Chaining | $\text{Fact} \xrightarrow{\text{rule}} \text{New Fact}$ | Compare with backward |
| 4 | Backward Chaining | $\text{Goal} \xleftarrow{\text{rule}} \text{Sub-goal}$ | Compare with forward |
| 5 | CSP Triple | $\text{CSP} = (X, \ D, \ C)$ | Definition question |
| 6 | Arc Consistency | $\forall v \in D_i, \ \exists w \in D_j \mid (v, w) \in C_{ij}$ | Prove / verify |
| 7 | Node Consistency | $\forall v \in D_i, \ v$ satisfies unary constraints on $X_i$ | Short answer |
| 8 | AC-3 Complexity | $O(E \cdot d^{3})$ | Algorithm analysis |
| 9 | Rete Complexity | Near $O(1)$ per cycle (after network build) | Performance question |
| 10 | Rete worst-case | $O(R \cdot F)$ | Compare with naive |
| 11 | Conflict Resolution | Specificity / Recency / Priority / LEX / MEA | Design question |
| 12 | k-Consistency | $\text{k-CSP} \Rightarrow \text{(k-1)-CSP}$ | Theory / proof |

Where $E$ = number of edges (arcs), $d$ = max domain size, $R$ = number of rules, $F$ = number of facts.

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Rule-Based Systems:** Medical diagnosis (MYCIN), Spacecraft scheduling (NASA's SPIKE), Industrial process control, Network intrusion detection (SNORT rules), Financial fraud detection, Email filtering (spam rules).
- **Constraint Propagation:** VLSI chip routing and floor planning, Airline scheduling, Crew rostering, Sudoku solvers, Map coloring / graph coloring, Job-shop scheduling, Timetabling systems in universities, Cryptarithmetic puzzles (SEND + MORE = MONEY).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Forward Chaining Trace

**Knowledge Base (Rules):**
- **R1:** IF (A AND B) THEN C
- **R2:** IF (C AND D) THEN E
- **R3:** IF (B AND E) THEN F
- **R4:** IF (A AND E) THEN G

**Initial Working Memory (Facts):** A, B, D

**Goal:** Derive G

#### Step-by-Step Trace

| Cycle | Matched Rules (Conflict Set) | Chosen Rule | Action | Updated Working Memory |
|-------|------------------------------|-------------|--------|------------------------|
| 1 | R1 (A✓, B✓) | R1 | Add C | A, B, D, **C** |
| 2 | R1 (already fired, skip via refraction), R2 (C✓, D✓) | R2 | Add E | A, B, D, C, **E** |
| 3 | R2 (refracted), R3 (B✓, E✓), R4 (A✓, E✓) | R3 (using specificity = R3 has 2, R4 has 2, tie broken by recency — E just added, so R4 also valid) | Apply LEX: R4 wins (uses most recent E via rule ordering) | A, B, D, C, E, **G** |
| 4 | Goal G achieved → **HALT** | — | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** G is derived in 3 cycles. If a different conflict resolution rule is chosen (e.g., first-declared), R3 may fire first adding F, but G is still reachable.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Backward Chaining Trace

**Same Knowledge Base**, Goal: Prove **G**.

#### Step-by-Step Goal Tree

1. **Goal G.** Find rules with G in consequent. → **R4** (IF A AND E THEN G).
2. Need to prove **A** (✓ in WM) and **E**.
3. **Sub-goal E.** Find rules with E in consequent. → **R2** (IF C AND D THEN E).
4. Need to prove **C** and **D** (D ✓ in WM).
5. **Sub-goal C.** Find rules with C in consequent. → **R1** (IF A AND B THEN C).
6. Need to prove **A** (✓) and **B** (✓).
7. All sub-goals satisfied. Backtrack up the tree: C proved, E proved, **G proved**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The structure produced is a **proof tree** (or AND-tree). Each node is a rule; leaves are facts from working memory. This is the foundation of **logic programming (Prolog)**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Arc Consistency (AC-3) on a Map Coloring CSP

**Problem:** Three regions X, Y, Z with domains {Red, Green, Blue} each. Constraints: X ≠ Y, Y ≠ Z. (X and Z have no direct constraint.)

**Constraint Graph:**
$$
X \longleftrightarrow Y \longleftrightarrow Z
$$

#### Step-by-Step Domain Reduction

**Initial domains:** $D_X = D_Y = D_Z = \lbrace R, G, B \rbrace$

**Step 1: Process arc (X, Y).**
For each $v_X \in \lbrace R, G, B \rbrace$, check if there exists $w \in D_Y$ such that $w \neq v_X$.
- $v_X = R$: exists $w = G$ ✓
- $v_X = G$: exists $w = R$ ✓
- $v_X = B$: exists $w = R$ ✓
- No value removed. Queue: [(Y, X), (Y, Z), (Z, Y)].

**Step 2: Process arc (Y, X).**
Symmetric to (X, Y). No change. Queue: [(Y, Z), (Z, Y)].

**Step 3: Process arc (Y, Z).**
For each $v_Y \in D_Y$, check if there exists $w \in D_Z$ with $w \neq v_Y$.
- All $v_Y$ have at least one valid $w$ (since $|D_Z| = 3 \geq 2$).
- No value removed. Queue: [(Z, Y)].

**Step 4: Process arc (Z, Y).**
Symmetric. No change. Queue: empty → **AC-3 terminates**.

**Result:** Domains remain $\lbrace R, G, B \rbrace$ for all. AC-3 alone is insufficient for this 3-coloring because every value still has a "support" in every neighbor. A search (backtracking) is then required to find a concrete assignment, e.g., X = R, Y = G, Z = R.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Arc consistency **never eliminates a valid solution**. It only prunes values that cannot participate in any solution. If AC-3 empties a domain, the CSP is provably unsolvable.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4: Cryptarithmetic via Constraint Propagation (SEND + MORE = MONEY)

**Variables:** S, E, N, D, M, O, R, Y (each a single digit 0–9)
**Constraints:**
- Leading letters S, M ≠ 0 (unary)
- All letters represent distinct digits (global all-different)
- Arithmetic column constraint: SEND + MORE = MONEY

#### Constraint Propagation Walk

**Step 1 — Node consistency:** Remove 0 from domains of S and M.
- $D_S = \lbrace 1, 2, \ldots, 9 \rbrace$
- $D_M = \lbrace 1, 2, \ldots, 9 \rbrace$
- $D_{\text{others}} = \lbrace 0, 1, \ldots, 9 \rbrace$

**Step 2 — Arc consistency from column 1 (units):** D + E = Y + 10·c1, where c1 ∈ {0, 1} is the carry.

**Step 3 — Forward propagation:** From column thousands, since M (carry-in to column 4) is the leading digit of result and S is the leading digit of SEND, the only way SEND + MORE = MONEY to be 5-digit is M = 1. This forces:
- $D_M = \lbrace 1 \rbrace$
- By all-different, no other variable = 1.

**Step 4 — Continue column-by-column** with carry variables c1, c2, c3, c4 ∈ {0, 1} and the constraints:
$$
\begin{aligned}
D + E &= Y + 10 \cdot c_1 \\
N + R + c_1 &= E + 10 \cdot c_2 \\
E + O + c_2 &= N + 10 \cdot c_3 \\
S + M + c_3 &= O + 10 \cdot c_4 \\
c_4 &= M
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Unique Solution (after propagation + search):**
$$
\text{S=9, E=5, N=6, D=7, M=1, O=0, R=8, Y=2}
$$
Verification: $9567 + 1085 = 10652$ ✓ (MONEY)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This puzzle is a classic KTU viva question because it elegantly demonstrates **node + arc + path consistency in action** with real-world engineering relevance (resource allocation, scheduling, and combinatorial search).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Problem Characteristic | Recommended Approach | Why? |
|------------------------|----------------------|------|
| Small fact base, large rule base | **Backward Chaining** | Avoids firing irrelevant rules |
| Real-time data stream | **Forward Chaining** | New facts naturally trigger rules |
| Goal not clearly defined | **Forward Chaining** | Discovers all derivable facts |
| Goal clearly defined, hypothesis testing | **Backward Chaining** | Focused sub-goal search |
| Dense constraint graph | **AC-3 + backtracking with MRV** | Maximum pruning before search |
| Sparse constraint graph | **Backtracking with forward checking** | AC-3 overhead not justified |
| Highly constrained (e.g., Sudoku) | **Path consistency / AC-3 + heuristic** | Deep pruning needed |
| All-different global constraints | **Regin's AllDifferent algorithm** | Specialized propagation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **forward checking** with **arc consistency**. Forward checking is a *lookahead during search* (one-step propagation), while arc consistency is a *pre-search preprocessing* (multi-step). They differ in scope and timing.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Production System

```mermaid
graph TD
    userInput([User / Sensor Input])
    ruleBase[(Rule Base: IF-THEN Rules)]
    workMem[(Working Memory: Dynamic Facts)]
    inferEng[Inference Engine]
    matcher{Pattern Matcher}
    conflictSet[Conflict Set]
    resolver[Conflict Resolver]
    executor[Rule Executor / Action]
    outAct([External Action / Output])
    halt{Halt Condition Met?}

    userInput --> workMem
    ruleBase --> matcher
    workMem --> matcher
    matcher --> conflictSet
    conflictSet --> resolver
    resolver --> executor
    executor --> workMem
    executor --> outAct
    workMem --> halt
    halt -- No --> matcher
    halt -- Yes --> endNode([System Halt])
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **feedback loop** from working memory back to the matcher — this is the Recognize–Act cycle. The working memory is the *single source of truth* shared between all rules.

---

### 4.2 Forward Chaining Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA([Start: Initial Facts in WM]) --> loadA[Load all rules R into Rule Base]
    loadA --> matchA[Match: Find rules whose antecedent is satisfied]
    matchA --> checkA{Conflict Set Empty?}
    checkA -- Yes --> failA([No New Fact — Stop])
    checkA -- No --> resolveA[Conflict Resolution: Pick 1 rule]
    resolveA --> fireA[Fire: Execute consequent]
    fireA --> updateA[Update Working Memory with new facts]
    updateA --> checkGoalA{Goal Achieved?}
    checkGoalA -- Yes --> doneA([Success — Stop])
    checkGoalA -- No --> matchA
```

---

### 4.3 Backward Chaining Goal Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    startB([Start: Goal G]) --> findB[Find rules with G in consequent]
    findB --> checkB{Any Matching Rule?}
    checkB -- No --> failB([Goal Unprovable — Backtrack])
    checkB -- Yes --> subgoalsB[Set sub-goals = antecedents of chosen rule]
    subgoalsB --> pickB{Pick a sub-goal}
    pickB --> recurseB[Recursively prove sub-goal using same procedure]
    recurseB --> checkSubB{All sub-goals proven?}
    checkSubB -- No --> pickB
    checkSubB -- Yes --> proveB([G is proven — Return proof tree])
```

---

### 4.4 AC-3 Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    startC([Start: All domains D_i initialized]) --> queueInit[Initialize queue Q with all arcs X_i, X_j]
    queueInit --> dequeue{Queue empty?}
    dequeue -- Yes --> doneC([All arcs consistent — Stop])
    dequeue -- No --> pickC[Dequeue arc X_i, X_j]
    pickC --> reviseC[Revise D_i: remove values with no support in D_j]
    reviseC --> changedC{D_i changed?}
    changedC -- No --> dequeue
    changedC -- Yes --> addC[Add arcs X_k, X_i to Q for all neighbors X_k of X_i]
    addC --> checkEmptyC{D_i became empty?}
    checkEmptyC -- Yes --> unsolvableC([CSP has no solution — Halt])
    checkEmptyC -- No --> dequeue
```

---

### 4.5 CSP Constraint Graph — 4-Queens Example

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q1((Q1 — Row 1)) -- "not same col, not same diag" --- Q2((Q2 — Row 2))
    Q2 --- Q3((Q3 — Row 3))
    Q3 --- Q4((Q4 — Row 4))
    Q1 --- Q3
    Q2 --- Q4
    Q1 --- Q4
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the 4-Queens CSP, each variable $Q_i$ represents the column position of the queen in row $i$. The domain is $\lbrace 1, 2, 3, 4 \rbrace$ and binary constraints ensure no two queens share a column or diagonal. Arc consistency alone does not solve 4-Queens; backtracking with AC-3 (MAC — Maintaining Arc Consistency) does.

---

### 4.6 Conflict Resolution Strategy — Decision Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    conflictSetInput[Conflict Set: Multiple Matching Rules] --> layer1{1. Refraction Check}
    layer1 -- Already fired in this cycle --> dropRule1[Drop from conflict set]
    layer1 -- Not fired yet --> layer2{2. Specificity Test}
    layer2 --> layer3{3. Recency Test}
    layer3 --> layer4{4. Priority / Salience}
    layer4 --> layer5{5. LEX / MEA Ordering}
    layer5 --> winner([Single Rule Selected to Fire])
    dropRule1 --> layer2
```
