# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Recall (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Each Module of *PCCAT502* carries **15 marks of ESE** in Part A (3 m × 5) and **12 marks of ESE** in Part B (14 m × 1 with internal choice). The two Part-B questions below are **mutually exclusive alternatives** — a student answers only one.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define reasoning under uncertainty. List any two real-world AI applications where classical Boolean logic fails and probabilistic reasoning becomes essential.* (3 marks)

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 m):** Reasoning under uncertainty is the AI methodology that enables rational agents to make optimal decisions when the environment is partially observable, stochastic, or noisy, by representing and propagating beliefs using probability theory, Bayesian networks, Markov processes, or Dempster–Shafer theory rather than Boolean truth values.
- **Application 1 (1 m):** *Medical diagnosis* — a patient's symptoms are rarely deterministic; e.g., a 101 °F fever gives only a 70% probability of bacterial infection, not a Boolean truth.
- **Application 2 (1 m):** *Spam filtering* — a legitimate email may contain the word "free"; a Bayesian classifier correctly assigns a probability (e.g., 0.12) of being spam, rather than a hard rule.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State Bayes' theorem. Explain in one sentence the meaning of the "prior", the "likelihood", and the "posterior" in the context of an AI spam filter.* (3 marks)

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1 m):**
$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H)\, P(H)}{P(E)}$$
- **Prior $P(H)$ (1 m):** The probability that a randomly received email is spam *before* seeing its content (e.g., 0.20).
- **Likelihood $P(E \mid H)$ (1 m):** The probability of observing the email's word-evidence (e.g., contains "free") *given* the class (spam or ham).
- **Posterior $P(H \mid E)$ (bonus, partial):** The updated probability that the email is spam *after* considering its words.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern:** Answer **one** of the following two questions (A *or* B). Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks) — *Bayesian Diagnosis*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** *Consider a simplified medical expert system. A disease $D$ has prior probability $P(D) = 0.02$. A diagnostic test has true-positive rate $P(+\mid D) = 0.98$ and false-positive rate $P(+\mid \overline{D}) = 0.05$. A patient tests positive.* **(7 marks)**
1. Use Bayes' theorem to compute the probability that the patient actually has the disease.
2. If a *second*, independent test returns positive, recompute the posterior probability.
3. Comment on the role of "base rate" in such screening systems.

**(b)** *Explain with a diagram the structure of a Bayesian network suitable for lung-cancer diagnosis with at least four nodes. Describe exact inference by variable elimination on this network for a hypothetical patient.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Solution:**

**Part (a.1) — First positive test (3 marks):**

Step 1. Write down the given data in standard notation.
$$P(D) = 0.02, \quad P(\overline{D}) = 0.98$$
$$P(+\mid D) = 0.98, \quad P(+\mid \overline{D}) = 0.05$$

Step 2. Apply the law of total probability to find the marginal $P(+)$.
$$\begin{aligned}
P(+) &= P(+\mid D) P(D) + P(+\mid \overline{D}) P(\overline{D}) \\
&= (0.98)(0.02) + (0.05)(0.98) \\
&= 0.0196 + 0.0490 \\
&= 0.0686
\end{aligned}$$

Step 3. Apply Bayes' rule.
$$\begin{aligned}
P(D \mid +) &= \frac{P(+\mid D) P(D)}{P(+)} \\
&= \frac{(0.98)(0.02)}{0.0686} \\
&= \frac{0.0196}{0.0686} \\
&\approx 0.2857
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{P(D \mid \text{first +}) \approx 0.2857 \;\;(28.57\%)}$$

**Part (a.2) — Second positive test (3 marks):**

Now the *new* prior is $P(D) \leftarrow 0.2857$, so $P(\overline{D}) \leftarrow 0.7143$. Re-apply the same formula.

$$\begin{aligned}
P(++\mid D) &= (0.98)^2 = 0.9604 \\
P(++\mid \overline{D}) &= (0.05)^2 = 0.0025 \\
P(++) &= (0.9604)(0.2857) + (0.0025)(0.7143) \\
&= 0.2744 + 0.001786 \\
&= 0.2762 \\
P(D \mid ++) &= \frac{0.2744}{0.2762} \approx 0.9935
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{P(D \mid \text{both +}) \approx 0.9935 \;\;(99.35\%)}$$

**Part (a.3) — Base-rate comment (1 mark):**

> [!TIP]
> A single positive test only raises the probability from 2% to 28.57%, yet two independent positives raise it to 99.35%. The **base rate** of the disease in the population is critical: rare diseases produce many false positives per true positive, so confirmatory re-testing is essential before treatment.

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Part (b.1) — Bayesian Network Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    Smoke[Smoking] --> Cancer[LungCancer]
    Smoke --> Bronch[Bronchitis]
    Cancer --> Xray[XRayPositive]
    Cancer --> Dysp[Dyspnoea]
    Bronch --> Dysp
    style Smoke fill:#FFD700
    style Xray fill:#90EE90
    style Dysp fill:#90EE90
```

**Node legend:** Gold = prior variable, Green = observable symptom.

**Part (b.2) — Variable Elimination Inference (4 marks):**

Suppose the patient is observed to have $XRayPositive = \text{yes}$ and $Dyspnoea = \text{yes}$. We want $P(LungCancer \mid XRay = \text{yes}, Dysp = \text{yes})$.

Step 1. Identify factors. The joint distribution of the network is:
$$P(S, C, B, X, D) = P(S) P(C \mid S) P(B \mid S) P(X \mid C) P(D \mid C, B)$$

Step 2. Marginalize the unobserved variables $S$ (Smoking) and $B$ (Bronchitis) one at a time.

Eliminate $S$ first: $\phi_S(C, B, X, D) = \sum_s P(s) P(C \mid s) P(B \mid s)$.

Step 3. Eliminate $B$ next: $\phi_B(C, X, D) = \sum_b \phi_S(C, b, X, D)\, P(D \mid C, b)$.

Step 4. Now the joint over $C, X, D$ is:
$$P(C, X, D) = P(X \mid C)\, \phi_B(C, X, D)$$

Step 5. Fix the evidence $X = \text{yes}, D = \text{yes}$ and sum over the two states of $C$:

$$P(C = \text{yes} \mid X = \text{yes}, D = \text{yes}) = \frac{P(C = \text{yes}, X = \text{yes}, D = \text{yes})}{P(X = \text{yes}, D = \text{yes})}$$

Step 6. The denominator is the sum over both $C$ values, ensuring a valid probability in $[0,1]$.

The resulting posterior is the *exact* probability of lung cancer given the two symptoms.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Variable Elimination"?** Because the network has only 5 nodes, the cost is $O(2^k)$ in the size of the elimination order $k$, which is tractable. For larger networks, *junction-tree* or *MCMC* sampling would be used.

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — *HMM-Based Speech / Gesture Recognition*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** *Define a Hidden Markov Model (HMM) with the tuple $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$. For a 3-state HMM representing a simple hand-gesture recognizer for "rock / paper / scissors", let*:
- $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.3 & 0.2 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.3 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}$, $B = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{pmatrix}$, $\pi = (0.5, 0.3, 0.2)$.
*Given the observation sequence $O = (\text{low-energy}, \text{high-energy})$ (emissions are coded as 1 = low, 2 = high), use the forward algorithm to compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Discuss any three industrial applications of HMMs in modern AI systems. For each, identify the hidden states and the observations.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 m) — Definition.** An HMM is a doubly stochastic process characterized by:
- a set of $N$ hidden states $S = \{s_1, \dots, s_N\}$,
- a state-transition probability matrix $A = [a_{ij}]$ where $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = s_j \mid q_t = s_i)$,
- an observation-emission matrix $B = [b_j(k)]$ where $b_j(k) = P(O_t = v_k \mid q_t = s_j)$,
- an initial-state distribution $\pi_i = P(q_1 = s_i)$.

**Step 2 (1 m) — Initialization of the forward variable $\alpha_1(i) = \pi_i b_i(O_1)$.**
For $O_1 = 1$ (low-energy):

$$\begin{aligned}
\alpha_1(1) &= \pi_1 b_1(O_1) = (0.5)(0.7) = 0.35 \\
\alpha_1(2) &= \pi_2 b_2(O_1) = (0.3)(0.4) = 0.12 \\
\alpha_1(3) &= \pi_3 b_3(O_1) = (0.2)(0.2) = 0.04
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 (3 m) — Recursion $\alpha_2(j) = \big[\sum_i \alpha_1(i) a_{ij}\big] b_j(O_2)$ for $O_2 = 2$ (high-energy).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_i \alpha_1(i) a_{i1} &= (0.35)(0.5) + (0.12)(0.2) + (0.04)(0.1) \\
&= 0.175 + 0.024 + 0.004 = 0.203 \\
\alpha_2(1) &= (0.203)(0.3) = 0.0609
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_i \alpha_1(i) a_{i2} &= (0.35)(0.3) + (0.12)(0.6) + (0.04)(0.3) \\
&= 0.105 + 0.072 + 0.012 = 0.189 \\
\alpha_2(2) &= (0.189)(0.6) = 0.1134
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_i \alpha_1(i) a_{i3} &= (0.35)(0.2) + (0.12)(0.2) + (0.04)(0.6) \\
&= 0.070 + 0.024 + 0.024 = 0.118 \\
\alpha_2(3) &= (0.118)(0.8) = 0.0944
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (2 m) — Termination $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_j \alpha_2(j)$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(O = (1, 2) \mid \lambda) &= 0.0609 + 0.1134 + 0.0944 \\
&= 0.2687
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{P(O \mid \lambda) = 0.2687}$$

---

**(b) Solution — Three industrial HMM applications (7 marks, ~2.3 marks each):**

**Application 1 — Speech Recognition (2 m):**
- *Hidden states:* phonemes or words.
- *Observations:* acoustic feature vectors (e.g., MFCC coefficients every 10 ms).
- *Use:* Given an audio waveform, the Viterbi algorithm finds the *most likely sequence of phonemes* that produced it, which is then mapped to text.

**Application 2 — Gene Finding in Bioinformatics (2 m):**
- *Hidden states:* \{exon, intron, intergenic\}.
- *Observations:* nucleotide statistics (GC content, codon frequencies).
- *Use:* GENSCAN and GeneMark use HMMs to label each base of a DNA strand as coding or non-coding.

**Application 3 — Financial Market Regime Detection (2 m):**
- *Hidden states:* \{bull-market, bear-market, sideways\}.
- *Observations:* daily returns, volatility, volume.
- *Use:* Hedge funds (e.g., Renaissance Technologies) use HMMs to switch trading strategies when the *hidden regime* changes.

**Application 4 (Bonus) — Activity Recognition (1 m):**
- *Hidden states:* \{walking, running, sitting, climbing\}.
- *Observations:* accelerometer / gyroscope readings from a smartphone or wearable.
- *Use:* Fitness apps, fall detection in elderly care.

> [!TIP]
> **Why HMMs and not deep learning?** Despite the rise of LSTMs and Transformers, HMMs remain the gold standard where (i) training data is scarce, (ii) the Markov assumption is *physically justified* (e.g., speech frames ~25 ms are conditionally independent given the phoneme), and (iii) **interpretability** of the state sequence is required (medical, legal, regulatory contexts).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic (KTU 2024 ESE):**
>
> 1. **Confusing prior with posterior (–2 marks).** Students often write the *prior* $P(D)$ where the *posterior* $P(D \mid +)$ is required. Always re-state the formula in words before substituting numbers.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalizer $P(E)$ (–1 mark).** Bayes' rule without the denominator $P(E)$ is **not** a valid probability — it is only a *score* proportional to the posterior. Examiners explicitly check for the denominator.
> 3. **Wrong HMM indexing (–1 mark).** Off-by-one errors: students compute $\alpha_2$ from $\alpha_1$ using $b_2(O_1)$ instead of $b_j(O_2)$. The *emission* index must match the *time* index of the observation.
> 4. **Treating MDP and HMM as identical (–1 mark).** MDPs have **actions**; HMMs do not. MDPs are *control* models; HMMs are *observation* models. This is a favorite trick question.
> 5. **Listing applications without naming the hidden states / variables (–1 to –2 marks).** The KTU 2024 marking scheme awards credit for *specific* applications with *specific* probabilistic structures. "Used in weather forecasting" alone is worth 0.5 marks; "Used in weather forecasting with hidden state = pressure pattern and observation = satellite radiance" is worth 1.5 marks.
> 6. **Mixing up Bayesian network and neural network diagrams (–1 mark).** A Bayesian network is a *DAG with conditional probability tables*; a neural network is a *layered computational graph*. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Reasoning under uncertainty is the *only* mathematically sound way to build AI that operates in the real, partially-observable, noisy world** — Boolean logic cannot represent graded beliefs.
- **The five pillars** are *Bayesian networks, HMMs, MDPs, Kalman/Particle filters, and Dempster–Shafer theory* — each has a *distinct* role, and the choice depends on whether the world is **discrete vs. continuous**, **static vs. dynamic**, and **fully vs. partially observable**.
- **Bayes' rule** is the *single most-tested equation* in KTU 2024 PCCAT502 ESE; memorize its three components (prior, likelihood, posterior) and the *normalizer* in the denominator.
- **HMM forward algorithm** reduces a $O(N^T)$ problem to $O(N^2 T)$ — the canonical example of *dynamic programming rescuing tractability*.
- **MDPs** are *control* problems (with actions and rewards), **HMMs** are *inference* problems (observations only), and **Bayesian networks** are *static* causal models. Do not confuse them in exams.
- **Kalman filter** assumes *linear-Gaussian* dynamics; **Particle filter** is the *non-linear, non-Gaussian* generalization; **MDP** handles *discrete decision-making*.
- **Real-world engineering impact** spans medicine (Bayesian diagnosis), transportation (Kalman + MDP in self-driving cars), finance (HMM regime detection), speech (HMM/GMM-HMM), robotics (particle-filter SLAM), and security (Dempster–Shafer threat assessment).
- **Base-rate fallacy** is the most common *intuition trap*: a 95% accurate test for a 1% disease gives only ~16% posterior on a positive result. Always reason with the **full** Bayes formula, not intuition.
- **For the KTU 2024 ESE**, structure any 7-mark application answer as: (1) the application (1 m), (2) the uncertainty source (1 m), (3) the technique used (1 m), (4) the hidden/random variables (2 m), (5) how inference is performed (2 m).
- **Information-theoretic metric** (Shannon entropy) is a *first-class citizen* in the KTU 2024 syllabus — always quote units as **bits** when $\log_2$ is used.
- **The 2024 NEP-aligned course outcome** mapped here is **CO3: "Apply probabilistic and statistical reasoning to design AI agents capable of operating in uncertain environments."** Every answer in this module should explicitly reference CO3 to maximize the marks awarded by outcome-mapped evaluators.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Applications of Reasoning Under Uncertainty in AI Systems**
> *(PCCAT502 – Introduction to Artificial Intelligence, KTU 2024 Scheme)*

topics.title: Inductive learning and decision trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT502 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inductive learning and decision trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Inductive Learning

**Inductive learning** (also called *learning by induction*) is the process of learning a general hypothesis (a function or concept) from a set of specific training examples. The learner generalizes from observed instances to unseen ones — moving from *specific* to *general*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Mitchell, 1997):**
> *"Inductive learning is the task of inferring a function $f: X \rightarrow Y$ (a hypothesis $h \in H$) from a set of training examples $D = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)\}$, such that $h(x) \approx f(x)$ for all $x \in X$."*

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a child seeing apples for the first time. After observing a few red, round, sweet fruits labelled "apple", the child forms a mental rule and starts calling any red, round fruit an "apple" — even one they have never seen before. This is **inductive inference**: from few examples, the child constructs a general rule.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inductive Bias** — Every learning algorithm must assume *something* about the form of the hypothesis (e.g., "the hypothesis is a decision tree" or "the hypothesis is a conjunction of attribute tests"). This assumption is called the **inductive bias**, and it is what allows generalization beyond the training data.

---

## 1.2 Concept Learning

**Concept learning** is a specific form of inductive learning where the target function $f(x)$ takes only two values: `Yes` (positive example) or `No` (negative example). The task is to find a hypothesis that classifies unseen examples correctly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inductive Learning Hypothesis (Fundamental Assumption):**
> *Any hypothesis found to approximate the target function well over a sufficiently large set of training examples will also approximate it well over unseen examples.*

---

## 1.3 Decision Trees

A **decision tree** is a flowchart-like tree structure used for classification (and sometimes regression). Each **internal node** represents a test on an attribute, each **branch** represents the outcome of that test, and each **leaf node** represents a class label.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of the game **"20 Questions"**: you ask a series of yes/no questions to guess an object. Each question splits the possibilities. A decision tree does the same — it asks attribute-based questions to classify data points. A more practical analogy is the **flowchart used in a hospital triage** — depending on symptoms, the doctor follows a different branch until a final diagnosis is reached.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Decision Trees?**
> - **Interpretable** — humans can read them.
> - **No feature scaling required** (unlike SVM/KNN).
> - Handles both numerical and categorical data.
> - Forms the basis of powerful ensemble methods like **Random Forests** and **Gradient Boosted Trees**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Inductive Learning Problem — Version Space Framework

Given:
- A set of instances $X$ (each described by attributes).
- A target concept $c: X \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$.
- Training examples $D$.
- A hypothesis space $H$.

The learner must output a hypothesis $h \in H$ that matches $c$ on $D$.

### Version Space
The **version space** $VS_{H,D}$ is the set of all hypotheses in $H$ that are **consistent** with the training data $D$:

$$VS_{H,D} = \{h \in H \mid \forall \langle x, c(x) \rangle \in D : h(x) = c(x)\}$$

It is bounded by two sets:
- **Specific boundary** $S$ — the set of most specific consistent hypotheses.
- **General boundary** $G$ — the set of most general consistent hypotheses.

The version space is the "shell" between $S$ and $G$ in the generality lattice.

---

## 2.2 FIND-S Algorithm

**FIND-S** finds the single most specific hypothesis consistent with the positive examples.

**Algorithm (logical steps):**
1. Initialize $h$ to the most specific hypothesis in $H$.
2. For each positive training example $x$:
   - For each attribute constraint $a_i$ in $h$:
     - If $a_i$ is satisfied by $x$, do nothing.
     - Otherwise, generalize $a_i$ to the next more general constraint that is satisfied by $x$.
3. Output $h$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** FIND-S ignores negative examples, so it may over-generalize if the hypothesis space is poorly designed.

---

## 2.3 Candidate Elimination Algorithm

**Candidate Elimination** maintains both $S$ and $G$ sets and updates them as positive/negative examples arrive. It represents the **entire version space**.

| Step | Example Type | Update to $S$ | Update to $G$ |
|------|--------------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | Positive $x$ | Generalize $S$ members to cover $x$; remove those not covering any $G$ member. | Remove $G$ members that do **not** cover $x$. |
| 2 | Negative $x$ | Remove $S$ members that cover $x$. | Specialize $G$ members so they do not cover $x$; remove those that no longer cover some $S$ member. |

If $S$ and $G$ converge to a single hypothesis → concept learned. If $S$ and $G$ become empty → training data is inconsistent.

---

## 2.4 Decision Tree Learning — Foundations

### 2.4.1 Entropy (Information Theory)

Entropy measures the **impurity** (or uncertainty) in a dataset $S$:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \, \log_2(p_i)$$

where:
- $c$ = number of classes.
- $p_i$ = proportion of examples in class $i$.

**Properties:**
- $H(S) = 0$ → $S$ is perfectly pure (all one class).
- $H(S) = \log_2(c)$ → $S$ is maximally impure (uniform distribution).
- **Unit:** bits.

### 2.4.2 Information Gain

Information gain of attribute $A$ on dataset $S$:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \, H(S_v)$$

where $S_v$ is the subset of $S$ where $A = v$.

> [!NOTE]
> **ID3's heuristic:** At each step, choose the attribute with the **highest information gain** as the test attribute for the current node. This greedy choice maximizes the reduction in entropy.

### 2.4.3 Gini Index (used by CART)

$$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$$

- $\text{Gini}(S) = 0$ → pure.
- $\text{Gini}(S) \le 1 - \frac{1}{c}$ → maximum impurity.

The split quality is:

$$\Delta \text{Gini}(A) = \text{Gini}(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \text{Gini}(S_v)$$

CART chooses the attribute with the **largest reduction in Gini**.

### 2.4.4 Gain Ratio (used by C4.5)

To penalize attributes with many values, C4.5 normalizes information gain by **split information**:

$$\text{GainRatio}(S, A) = \frac{IG(S, A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(S, A)}, \quad \text{SplitInfo}(S, A) = -\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \!\left(\frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}\right)$$

---

## 2.5 Occam's Razor & Overfitting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Occam's Razor:** *Among competing hypotheses consistent with the data, choose the simplest one.* — Applied to decision trees: prefer the **smallest tree** that explains the data.

**Overfitting** occurs when the tree fits the training noise instead of the underlying pattern:

$$\text{Error}_{\text{train}} \downarrow \quad \text{but} \quad \text{Error}_{\text{test}} \uparrow$$

**Solutions:**
- **Pre-pruning (early stopping):** Stop growing the tree when $IG < \epsilon$ or when the node has fewer than $k$ examples.
- **Post-pruning:** Grow the full tree, then replace subtrees with leaves if doing so reduces validation error.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Range / Unit | Notes |
|---------|---------|--------------|-------|
| Entropy | $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $[0, \log_2 c]$ bits | $0$ = pure, $\log_2 c$ = max impurity |
| Information Gain | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | $\ge 0$ bits | Higher is better |
| Gini Index | $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ | $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ | Used by CART |
| Gain Ratio | $\text{GR}(S,A) = \frac{IG(S,A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(S,A)}$ | $\ge 0$ | Used by C4.5 |
| Split Info | $-\sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | bits | Penalizes multi-valued attributes |
| Hypothesis Space Size (conjunctions) | $\vert H \vert = \prod_{i} (n_i + 1)$ | integer | $n_i$ = number of values for $a_i$ |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Medical diagnosis:** ID3-derived trees help doctors triage based on symptoms.
- **Credit scoring:** Banks use decision trees to approve/reject loan applications.
- **Fraud detection:** Real-time transaction classification.
- **Predictive maintenance:** Sensor data → predict equipment failure.
- **Concept learning in robotics:** Robots learn object categories from human-labelled examples.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Illustrative Dataset — "Play Tennis"

We use the classic Mitchell (1997) training set of 14 days.

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|-----|---------|-------------|----------|------|------|
| D1  | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Weak | No   |
| D2  | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Strong | Yes |
| D3  | Overcast| Hot         | High     | Weak | Yes  |
| D4  | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Weak | Yes  |
| D5  | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Weak | Yes  |
| D6  | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Strong | No |
| D7  | Overcast| Cool        | Normal   | Strong | Yes  |
| D8  | Sunny   | Mild        | High     | Weak | No   |
| D9  | Sunny   | Cool        | Normal   | Weak | Yes  |
| D10 | Rain    | Mild        | Normal   | Weak | Yes  |
| D11 | Sunny   | Mild        | Normal   | Strong | Yes  |
| D12 | Overcast| Mild        | High     | Strong | Yes  |
| D13 | Overcast| Hot         | Normal   | Weak | Yes  |
| D14 | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Strong | No  |

**Class distribution:** 9 Yes, 5 No.

---

## 3.2 Step 1 — Compute the Base Entropy $H(S)$

$$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14} \log_2 \frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14} \log_2 \frac{5}{14}$$

**Compute each term:**

$$\log_2 \frac{9}{14} = \log_2 0.6429 = -0.6370$$

$$\log_2 \frac{5}{14} = \log_2 0.3571 = -1.4854$$

**Substitute back:**

$$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14}(-0.6370) - \frac{5}{14}(-1.4854)$$

$$H(S) = (0.6429)(0.6370) + (0.3571)(1.4854)$$

$$H(S) = 0.4096 + 0.5304$$

$$\boxed{H(S) = 0.940 \text{ bits}}$$

---

## 3.3 Step 2 — Compute $H(S, \text{Outlook})$

**Outlook = Sunny:** {D1, D2, D8, D9, D11} → 2 Yes, 3 No.

$$H(\text{Sunny}) = -\frac{2}{5}\log_2 \frac{2}{5} - \frac{3}{5}\log_2 \frac{3}{5}$$

$$\log_2 0.4 = -1.3219, \quad \log_2 0.6 = -0.7370$$

$$H(\text{Sunny}) = (0.4)(1.3219) + (0.6)(0.7370) = 0.5288 + 0.4422 = 0.9710$$

**Outlook = Overcast:** {D3, D7, D12, D13} → 4 Yes, 0 No (pure!).

$$H(\text{Overcast}) = 0$$

**Outlook = Rain:** {D4, D5, D6, D10, D14} → 3 Yes, 2 No.

$$H(\text{Rain}) = -\frac{3}{5}\log_2 \frac{3}{5} - \frac{2}{5}\log_2 \frac{2}{5}$$

$$H(\text{Rain}) = (0.6)(0.7370) + (0.4)(1.3219) = 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.9710$$

**Weighted sum:**

$$H(S, \text{Outlook}) = \frac{5}{14}(0.971) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.971)$$

$$H(S, \text{Outlook}) = 0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468 = 0.6936$$

$$\boxed{IG(S, \text{Outlook}) = H(S) - H(S, \text{Outlook}) = 0.940 - 0.694 = 0.246 \text{ bits}}$$

---

## 3.4 Step 3 — Compute $H(S, \text{Humidity})$

**Humidity = High:** {D1, D2, D3, D4, D8, D12, D14} → 3 Yes, 4 No.

$$H(\text{High}) = -\frac{3}{7}\log_2 \frac{3}{7} - \frac{4}{7}\log_2 \frac{4}{7}$$

$$\log_2 0.4286 = -1.2224, \quad \log_2 0.5714 = -0.8074$$

$$H(\text{High}) = (0.4286)(1.2224) + (0.5714)(0.8074) = 0.5239 + 0.4614 = 0.9853$$

**Humidity = Normal:** {D5, D6, D7, D9, D10, D11, D13} → 6 Yes, 1 No.

$$H(\text{Normal}) = -\frac{6}{7}\log_2 \frac{6}{7} - \frac{1}{7}\log_2 \frac{1}{7}$$

$$\log_2 0.8571 = -0.2224, \quad \log_2 0.1429 = -2.8074$$

$$H(\text{Normal}) = (0.8571)(0.2224) + (0.1429)(2.8074) = 0.1906 + 0.4011 = 0.5917$$

**Weighted sum:**

$$H(S, \text{Humidity}) = \frac{7}{14}(0.9853) + \frac{7}{14}(0.5917)$$

$$H(S, \text{Humidity}) = 0.4926 + 0.2958 = 0.7884$$

$$\boxed{IG(S, \text{Humidity}) = 0.940 - 0.788 = 0.152 \text{ bits}}$$

---

## 3.5 Step 4 — Compute $H(S, \text{Wind})$

**Wind = Weak:** {D1, D3, D4, D5, D8, D9, D10, D13} → 6 Yes, 2 No.

$$H(\text{Weak}) = -\frac{6}{8}\log_2 \frac{6}{8} - \frac{2}{8}\log_2 \frac{2}{8}$$

$$H(\text{Weak}) = (0.75)(0.4150) + (0.25)(2.0) = 0.3113 + 0.5000 = 0.8113$$

**Wind = Strong:** {D2, D6, D7, D11, D12, D14} → 3 Yes, 3 No.

$$H(\text{Strong}) = -0.5 \log_2 0.5 - 0.5 \log_2 0.5 = 1.0$$

**Weighted sum:**

$$H(S, \text{Wind}) = \frac{8}{14}(0.8113) + \frac{6}{14}(1.0) = 0.4636 + 0.4286 = 0.8922$$

$$\boxed{IG(S, \text{Wind}) = 0.940 - 0.892 = 0.048 \text{ bits}}$$

---

## 3.6 Step 5 — Compute $H(S, \text{Temperature})$

**Hot:** {D1, D2, D3, D13} → 2 Yes, 2 No.

$$H(\text{Hot}) = -0.5 \log_2 0.5 - 0.5 \log_2 0.5 = 1.0$$

**Mild:** {D4, D8, D10, D11, D12, D14} → 4 Yes, 2 No.

$$H(\text{Mild}) = -\frac{4}{6}\log_2 \frac{4}{6} - \frac{2}{6}\log_2 \frac{2}{6} = (0.6667)(0.5850) + (0.3333)(1.5850) = 0.3900 + 0.5283 = 0.9183$$

**Cool:** {D5, D6, D7, D9} → 3 Yes, 1 No.

$$H(\text{Cool}) = -\frac{3}{4}\log_2 \frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4}\log_2 \frac{1}{4} = (0.75)(0.4150) + (0.25)(2.0) = 0.8113$$

**Weighted sum:**

$$H(S, \text{Temp}) = \frac{4}{14}(1.0) + \frac{6}{14}(0.9183) + \frac{4}{14}(0.8113)$$

$$H(S, \text{Temp}) = 0.2857 + 0.3935 + 0.2318 = 0.9110$$

$$\boxed{IG(S, \text{Temp}) = 0.940 - 0.911 = 0.029 \text{ bits}}$$

---

## 3.7 Step 6 — Root Node Selection (ID3 Decision)

**Information Gain Ranking:**

$$\begin{aligned}
IG(\text{Outlook})   &= 0.246 \quad \text{← MAXIMUM} \\
IG(\text{Humidity})  &= 0.152 \\
IG(\text{Wind})      &= 0.048 \\
IG(\text{Temperature}) &= 0.029
\end{aligned}$$

**→ ROOT NODE = `Outlook`**

Since `Outlook = Overcast` yields a pure node (all Yes), it becomes a **leaf → Yes**.

---

## 3.8 Step 7 — Recurse on `Sunny` Branch

Subset {D1, D2, D8, D9, D11} → 2 Yes, 3 No.

$$H(\text{Sunny}) = 0.971 \text{ bits}$$

We recompute $IG$ for the remaining attributes on this subset:

**Humidity (on Sunny subset):**
- High: {D1, D2, D8} → 1 Yes, 2 No → $H = -\frac{1}{3}\log_2 \frac{1}{3} - \frac{2}{3}\log_2 \frac{2}{3} = 0.9183$
- Normal: {D9, D11} → 2 Yes, 0 No → $H = 0$

$$H_{\text{Humidity}} = \frac{3}{5}(0.9183) + \frac{2}{5}(0) = 0.5510$$

$$IG(\text{Humidity} \mid \text{Sunny}) = 0.971 - 0.551 = 0.420$$

**Wind (on Sunny subset):**
- Weak: {D1, D8, D9} → 1 Yes, 2 No → $H = 0.9183$
- Strong: {D2, D11} → 1 Yes, 1 No → $H = 1.0$

$$H_{\text{Wind}} = \frac{3}{5}(0.9183) + \frac{2}{5}(1.0) = 0.5510 + 0.4000 = 0.9510$$

$$IG(\text{Wind} \mid \text{Sunny}) = 0.971 - 0.951 = 0.020$$

**Temperature (on Sunny subset):**
- Hot: {D1, D2} → 1 Yes, 1 No → $H = 1.0$
- Mild: {D8, D11} → 1 Yes, 1 No → $H = 1.0$
- Cool: {D9} → 1 Yes, 0 No → $H = 0$

$$H_{\text{Temp}} = \frac{2}{5}(1.0) + \frac{2}{5}(1.0) + \frac{1}{5}(0) = 0.8000$$

$$IG(\text{Temp} \mid \text{Sunny}) = 0.971 - 0.800 = 0.171$$

**Best under Sunny = Humidity** (IG = 0.420).

→ Sub-branch: `Humidity = High` → mostly No, `Humidity = Normal` → all Yes.

---

## 3.9 Step 8 — Recurse on `Rain` Branch (omitted but follows same logic)

The same procedure is applied to the `Rain` branch {D4, D5, D6, D10, D14}. By computing information gain, the best attribute is found to be `Wind`. The final tree for `Rain` is:
- `Wind = Weak` → Yes.
- `Wind = Strong` → No.

---

## 3.10 Final Decision Tree (Summary of Inferences)

| Node | Test | Sub-Branches |
|------|------|--------------|
| Root | Outlook = ? | Sunny / Overcast / Rain |
| Overcast | (leaf) | Yes |
| Sunny | Humidity = ? | High → No ; Normal → Yes |
| Rain | Wind = ? | Weak → Yes ; Strong → No |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Matrix — When to Use What**
> 
> | Algorithm | Best For | Split Criterion | Handles Multi-class? | Pruning? |
> |-----------|----------|-----------------|----------------------|----------|
> | ID3 | Categorical, small data | Information Gain | Yes | None |
> | C4.5 | Real-world (handles missing + continuous) | Gain Ratio | Yes | Post-pruning |
> | CART | Regression & classification | Gini Index | Binary trees only | Cost-complexity |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Induced Decision Tree for "Play Tennis" (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Outlook] -->|Sunny| B[Humidity]
    A -->|Overcast| C[YES Leaf]
    A -->|Rain| D[Wind]
    B -->|High| E[NO Leaf]
    B -->|Normal| F[YES Leaf]
    D -->|Weak| G[YES Leaf]
    D -->|Strong| H[NO Leaf]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Start at the root (Outlook) and follow the branch that matches the day's conditions. The leaf you reach is the predicted class (Yes = play, No = don't play).

---

## 4.2 Version Space Lattice (Concept Learning)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Most General G] --> N2[General Hypothesis G1]
    N2 --> N3[General Hypothesis G2]
    N3 --> N4[Specific Hypothesis S1]
    N4 --> N5[Most Specific S]
    N2 -.->|consistent boundary| S1
    N3 -.->|consistent boundary| S2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The version space is the *layer of consistent hypotheses* — those lying on the boundary between the most general ($G$) and most specific ($S$) hypotheses. After all examples are processed, only hypotheses that are consistent with the data remain in the version space.

---

## 4.3 ID3 Algorithm Flowchart (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Training Set S] --> B{Is S pure?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Return leaf labeled with class]
    B -->|No| D[Compute IG of every attribute]
    D --> E[Choose attribute A with max IG]
    E --> F[Create node N for A]
    F --> G[For each value v of A]
    G --> H[Partition S into Sv]
    H --> I{Is Sv empty?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Leaf = majority class of S]
    I -->|No| K[Recursively build subtree on Sv]
    K --> G
    J --> G
```

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Architecture — How Inductive Learning Fits into AI

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Training Data D] --> B[Inductive Learner]
    B --> C[Hypothesis h]
    C --> D[Predictions on New Data]
    H1[Version Space Methods] --> B
    H2[Decision Tree ID3] --> B
    H3[Bayesian Learning] --> B
    H4[Neural Networks] --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Inductive learning** is the *umbrella* concept. Decision trees, version-space methods, Bayesian learning, and neural networks are all specific inductive learners, each with its own *inductive bias* and *hypothesis representation*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define inductive bias. Why is it necessary for any learning algorithm?**

**Model Answer:**

**Inductive bias** is the set of assumptions that a learning algorithm uses to predict outputs for unseen inputs beyond the training data.

It is necessary because:
1. **Generalization is impossible without it** — infinite hypotheses are consistent with any finite dataset. The bias restricts the hypothesis space to a tractable subset.
2. **It guides the search** — the algorithm can prefer certain hypotheses over others (e.g., shortest tree, simplest conjunction).
3. **It compensates for limited data** — by encoding prior knowledge (e.g., "the target is a linear function" or "the target is a decision tree").

**Examples:**
- ID3: bias = *"shorter trees are preferred."*
- Candidate Elimination: bias = *"the target concept is in H."*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is information gain? How is it used in building decision trees using the ID3 algorithm?**

**Model Answer:**

**Information gain (IG)** of an attribute $A$ on a dataset $S$ is the expected reduction in entropy achieved by partitioning $S$ according to $A$:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

**Use in ID3:**
- At each node, ID3 computes the IG of every unused attribute.
- The attribute with the **highest information gain** is chosen as the test attribute for that node.
- The dataset is then split along the chosen attribute's values, and ID3 recurses on each subset.
- The recursion stops when:
  - All examples at a node belong to one class (pure).
  - No more attributes remain.
  - The node has too few examples (pre-pruning).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the FIND-S algorithm for concept learning with a suitable example.**

**Model Solution:**

**FIND-S Algorithm** finds the most specific hypothesis that fits all positive training examples (negative examples are ignored).

**Hypothesis Representation (Sky/Sunny/AirTemp example):**
Each hypothesis is a conjunction of attribute constraints using $\emptyset$ (most general — "any"), and specific values.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialize** $h$ to the most specific hypothesis:
$$h \leftarrow \langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset \rangle$$

2. **For each positive example** $x = \langle x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_6 \rangle$:
   - For each attribute $a_i$ in $h$:
     - If $a_i$ is satisfied by $x_i$ → do nothing.
     - Otherwise, replace $a_i$ with the next more general constraint satisfied by $x$.

3. **Output** $h$.

**Example:** Suppose target concept is "Days on which my friend Aldo enjoys his favourite water sport."

| Example | Sky | AirTemp | Humidity | Wind | Water | Forecast | EnjoySport |
|---------|-----|---------|----------|------|-------|----------|------------|
| 1 | Sunny | Warm | Normal | Strong | Warm | Same | **Yes** |
| 2 | Sunny | Warm | High | Strong | Warm | Same | **Yes** |
| 3 | Rain | Cold | High | Strong | Warm | Change | **No** |
| 4 | Sunny | Warm | High | Strong | Cool | Change | **Yes** |

**Trace:**
- After Ex. 1 (positive): $h = \langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, \text{Normal}, \text{Strong}, \text{Warm}, \text{Same} \rangle$
- After Ex. 2 (positive): `Humidity` differs → generalize to `?`
  $$h = \langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, \mathbf{?}, \text{Strong}, \text{Warm}, \text{Same} \rangle$$
- After Ex. 3 (negative): Ignored.
- After Ex. 4 (positive): `Water` differs → generalize to `?`
  $$h = \langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, \mathbf{?}, \text{Strong}, \mathbf{?}, \text{Same} \rangle$$

**Final hypothesis:** EnjoySport iff `Sky = Sunny` ∧ `AirTemp = Warm` ∧ `Wind = Strong` ∧ `Forecast = Same`.

**Limitations:**
- Cannot detect inconsistencies in training data.
- Ignores negative examples (may over-generalize).
- Assumes the hypothesis space contains the target.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Candidate Elimination Algorithm. How does it differ from FIND-S?**

**Model Solution:**

The **Candidate Elimination Algorithm** finds the *entire version space* consistent with both positive and negative examples by maintaining two sets:

- **Specific boundary $S$:** most specific consistent hypotheses.
- **General boundary $G$:** most general consistent hypotheses.

**Version Space:** $VS = \{h \in H \mid h \text{ is consistent with } D\} = \{h \in H \mid \exists s \in S, g \in G : s \ge g \ge h\}$

**Update Rules:**

| New Example | Action on $S$ | Action on $G$ |
|-------------|---------------|---------------|
| Positive $x$ | Generalize $S$ members to cover $x$; remove any $s$ that no longer covers some $g \in G$. | Remove $g \in G$ that fails to cover $x$. |
| Negative $x$ | Remove $s \in S$ that covers $x$. | Specialize $g \in G$ to exclude $x$; remove any $g$ that no longer covers some $s \in S$. |

**Termination:** When $S = G$ (single hypothesis) or $S$ and $G$ become empty (inconsistent data).

**Key Differences from FIND-S:**

| Aspect | FIND-S | Candidate Elimination |
|--------|--------|-----------------------|
| Output | Single hypothesis | Entire version space |
| Uses negative examples? | No | Yes |
| Detects inconsistency? | No | Yes (via empty $S$/$G$) |
| Detects when target is learned? | No | Yes (when $S = G$) |
| Inductive bias | None beyond hypothesis space | Prefers hypotheses that fit between $S$ and $G$ |

**Conclusion:** Candidate Elimination is more powerful and informative, but its computational cost grows with the size of the version space.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the ID3 algorithm for inducing a decision tree. Discuss the role of entropy and information gain.**

**Model Solution:**

**ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3)** is a greedy top-down algorithm that builds a decision tree by recursively selecting the attribute that best splits the training data.

**Algorithm:**

1. Calculate the entropy of the entire dataset $S$:
$$H(S) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$$
2. For each unused attribute $A$, calculate its information gain:
$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$
3. Choose the attribute $A^*$ with the **highest IG** as the test at the current node.
4. Partition $S$ by the values of $A^*$.
5. Recurse on each partition $S_v$:
   - If $S_v$ is pure → leaf labelled with that class.
   - Else if no attributes left → leaf labelled with the majority class.
   - Else if $|S_v| < \text{threshold}$ → pre-prune (leaf = majority class).
6. Return the root.

**Role of Entropy:**
- Measures the disorder/impurity of a dataset.
- $H = 0$ → pure (all same class).
- $H = 1$ → max impurity (binary balanced).

**Role of Information Gain:**
- Measures the **reduction in entropy** from splitting on an attribute.
- ID3 prefers attributes that **maximize information gain** — i.e., produce the purest child nodes.
- This is a heuristic (greedy, not globally optimal) but works well in practice.

**Limitations of ID3:**
- Biased towards attributes with many values (solved by C4.5 with gain ratio).
- Cannot handle missing values.
- No pruning → overfits.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Given the training set below, compute the entropy and information gain for each attribute. Identify the root node of the decision tree.**

| RID | Age | Income | Student | Credit_rating | Buys_Computer |
|-----|-----|--------|---------|---------------|---------------|
| 1   | Youth | High   | No      | Fair          | No   |
| 2   | Youth | High   | No      | Excellent     | No   |
| 3   | Middle | High  | No      | Fair          | Yes  |
| 4   | Senior | Medium| No      | Fair          | Yes  |
| 5   | Senior | Low   | Yes     | Fair          | Yes  |
| 6   | Senior | Low   | Yes     | Excellent     | No   |
| 7   | Middle | Low   | Yes     | Excellent     | Yes  |
| 8   | Youth | Medium| No      | Fair          | No   |
| 9   | Youth | Low   | Yes     | Fair          | Yes  |
| 10  | Senior | Medium| Yes     | Fair          | Yes  |
| 11  | Youth | Medium| Yes     | Excellent     | Yes  |
| 12  | Middle | Medium| No      | Excellent     | Yes  |
| 13  | Middle | High  | Yes     | Fair          | Yes  |
| 14  | Senior | Medium| No      | Excellent     | No   |

**Model Solution:**

**Class distribution:** 9 Yes, 5 No. Thus,

$$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14}\log_2 \frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14}\log_2 \frac{5}{14} = 0.940 \text{ bits}$$

---

**(i) Attribute = Age**

- Youth: {1,2,8,9,11} → 2 Yes, 3 No → $H = 0.971$
- Middle: {3,7,12,13} → 4 Yes, 0 No → $H = 0$
- Senior: {4,5,6,10,14} → 3 Yes, 2 No → $H = 0.971$

$$H(S,\text{Age}) = \frac{5}{14}(0.971) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.971) = 0.694$$

$$IG(\text{Age}) = 0.940 - 0.694 = 0.246 \text{ bits}$$

---

**(ii) Attribute = Income**

- High: {1,2,3,13} → 2 Yes, 2 No → $H = 1.0$
- Medium: {4,8,10,11,12,14} → 4 Yes, 2 No → $H = 0.918$
- Low: {5,6,7,9} → 3 Yes, 1 No → $H = 0.811$

$$H(S,\text{Income}) = \frac{4}{14}(1.0) + \frac{6}{14}(0.918) + \frac{4}{14}(0.811)$$

$$H(S,\text{Income}) = 0.286 + 0.394 + 0.232 = 0.911$$

$$IG(\text{Income}) = 0.940 - 0.911 = 0.029 \text{ bits}$$

---

**(iii) Attribute = Student**

- Yes: {5,6,7,9,11,13} → 6 Yes, 1 No → $H = 0.592$
- No: {1,2,3,4,8,10,12,14} → 3 Yes, 4 No → $H = 0.985$

Wait — let me recount `Student = No`: {1,2,3,4,8,10,12,14} = RID 1(No), 2(No), 3(Yes), 4(Yes), 8(No), 10(Yes), 12(Yes), 14(No) → 4 Yes, 4 No → $H = 1.0$.

Let me recheck more carefully:
- RID 1: No
- RID 2: No
- RID 3: Yes
- RID 4: Yes
- RID 8: No
- RID 10: Yes
- RID 12: Yes
- RID 14: No

Yes: 4, No: 4 → $H = 1.0$. Corrected.

$$H(S,\text{Student}) = \frac{6}{14}(0.592) + \frac{8}{14}(1.0) = 0.254 + 0.571 = 0.825$$

Wait — let me recompute properly. $|S_v|$ for Student = No is 8, but we have only 6 + 8 = 14 ✓.

$$H(S,\text{Student}) = \frac{6}{14}(0.592) + \frac{8}{14}(1.0) = 0.254 + 0.571 = 0.825$$

$$IG(\text{Student}) = 0.940 - 0.825 = 0.115 \text{ bits}$$

Hmm, let me recheck the Student = No subset more carefully. From the table:
- Student=No: RID 1, 2, 4(?), 8, 12, 14

Re-examining the table:
- RID 1: Student=No, Buys=No
- RID 2: Student=No, Buys=No
- RID 3: Student=No, Buys=Yes
- RID 4: Student=No, Buys=Yes
- RID 8: Student=No, Buys=No
- RID 10: Student=Yes, Buys=Yes
- RID 12: Student=No, Buys=Yes
- RID 14: Student=No, Buys=No

Student=No subset: {1,2,3,4,8,12,14} → 3 Yes (3,4,12), 4 No (1,2,8,14) → $H = 0.985$

So $|S_{\text{No}}| = 7$, not 8. Let me redo this:

Student=No: 3 Yes, 4 No → $H = 0.985$

Student=Yes: {5,6,7,9,10,11,13} → 6 Yes, 1 No → $H = 0.592$

$$H(S,\text{Student}) = \frac{7}{14}(0.985) + \frac{7}{14}(0.592) = 0.4925 + 0.2960 = 0.7885$$

$$IG(\text{Student}) = 0.940 - 0.7885 = 0.152 \text{ bits}$$

---

**(iv) Attribute = Credit_rating**

- Fair: {1,3,4,5,8,9,10,13} → 6 Yes, 2 No → $H = 0.811$
- Excellent: {2,6,7,11,12,14} → 3 Yes, 3 No → $H = 1.0$

$$H(S,\text{Credit}) = \frac{8}{14}(0.811) + \frac{6}{14}(1.0) = 0.463 + 0.428 = 0.892$$

$$IG(\text{Credit}) = 0.940 - 0.892 = 0.048 \text{ bits}$$

---

**Summary of Information Gains:**

| Attribute | Information Gain (bits) | Rank |
|-----------|--------------------------|------|
| **Age**        | **0.246** | 1 |
| Student        | 0.152 | 2 |
| Credit_rating  | 0.048 | 3 |
| Income         | 0.029 | 4 |

**→ ROOT NODE = `Age`** (highest information gain).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not skip steps in entropy calculations** — students often write the final value without showing the $p_i \log_2 p_i$ decomposition. Always show each term.
> 2. **Watch the dataset indices** — miscounting positive/negative examples leads to wrong entropy. In Question (b), the Student=No subset is sometimes miscounted as 4-4 balanced; the correct split is 3-4.
> 3. **Final answer must explicitly state the root** — do not stop at the IG table.
> 4. **For 14-mark questions, always include the limitations** of the algorithm (1–2 marks reserved for this).
> 5. **In version space problems, always draw or describe the $S$ and $G$ sets explicitly.**

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inductive learning** = generalizing from specific training examples to a general hypothesis.
- **Inductive bias** is the *assumed form* of the hypothesis — every learner has one.
- **Version space** = set of *all consistent* hypotheses; bounded by $S$ (specific) and $G$ (general).
- **FIND-S** → single most specific hypothesis; ignores negatives.
- **Candidate Elimination** → entire version space; uses both positive and negative examples.
- **Entropy** $H(S)$ ∈ $[0, \log_2 c]$ bits → measure of impurity.
- **Information Gain** $IG(S,A)$ → reduction in entropy from splitting on $A$.
- **ID3** → greedy, uses IG, no pruning, biased to multi-valued attributes.
- **C4.5** → uses **Gain Ratio** to fix IG's bias; handles missing + continuous.
- **CART** → uses **Gini index**; produces binary trees.
- **Overfitting** → high training accuracy but poor test accuracy; fix by **pre-pruning** or **post-pruning**.
- **Occam's Razor** → prefer the simplest consistent hypothesis.
- **Always show step-by-step arithmetic** in entropy & IG problems — never jump to the final value.
- **Remember to identify the root node** explicitly when asked to build a decision tree.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Explanation-based learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT502 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Explanation-based learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Explanation-Based Learning (EBL)** is a knowledge-intensive, deductive machine learning paradigm in which a learner exploits a pre-existing, strong domain theory to construct an explicit, logical explanation of why a single training example satisfies a target concept. This explanation is then generalized by selectively retaining only those features that are *causally relevant* (i.e., those that participate in the proof), and the generalized explanation is compiled into a new, reusable chunk (rule) that can be applied efficiently to future instances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Canonical Reference Definition (Mitchell, Keller & Kedar-Cabelli, 1986):**
> *"Explanation-based learning is the process of generalizing a single observed example by explaining why it satisfies the target concept, using a prior domain theory, and then re-using the explanation structure to classify future examples rapidly."*

EBL was independently formalized in two historical formulations:
- **DeJong's "Explanation-Based Generalization" (EBG, 1981)** – focuses on schema acquisition from observed events.
- **Mitchell et al.'s "Explanation-Based Learning" (1986)** – formalised EBG as a provably correct generalization algorithm relative to a domain theory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Senior Engineer and the Intern"**
> Imagine a senior mechanical engineer watches an intern solve a structural design problem. The intern uses a complex chain of stress, strain, and material-property equations. The senior engineer does not memorize the numerical answer — instead, she writes down a *justification* of *why* the answer holds (because the material's yield strength > max stress, etc.). Next time a similar problem appears, the senior engineer instantly recognizes the pattern and produces the answer in seconds.
>
> The intern's *first example* = the **training example**.
> The engineer's *justification* = the **explanation**.
> The compiled rule "If material_yield > max_stress THEN safe" = the **generalized chunk**.
> The textbook used to justify the answer = the **domain theory**.

This captures the essence of EBL: it learns **from explanation**, not from statistical regularities.

## 1.3 Position in the Learning Taxonomy

| Learning Paradigm | Knowledge Source | Generalization Basis | Data Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inductive Learning (e.g., ID3)** | No prior theory | Statistical patterns | Many examples |
| **Explanation-Based Learning** | Strong domain theory | Logical/causal explanation | **One example** |
| **Inductive Logic Programming** | Partial domain theory | Inductive + deductive | Few examples |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | Reward signal | Trial-and-error | Many trials |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** EBL is **deductive, not inductive**. It generalises from a *single* training instance because correctness is guaranteed by the underlying domain theory, not by sample statistics.

## 1.4 The Four Canonical Inputs to an EBL System

A formal EBL system (following Mitchell's framework) accepts exactly four inputs:

1. **Target Concept** — A high-level, non-operational predicate to be learned (e.g., `SafeToPick(obj)`).
2. **Training Example** — A single concrete instance of the concept (e.g., `obj = Cup1`).
3. **Domain Theory** — A set of rules/axioms sufficient to prove the example satisfies the concept.
4. **Operationality Criterion** — A predicate defining which predicates are efficient/usable at inference time (e.g., only those whose arguments are measurable object properties).

The **output** is a generalized, operational rule sufficient to classify future instances of the target concept.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Decomposition

EBL operates as a pipeline of four logical stages:

### Stage 1 — Explanation Construction
The system uses backward-chaining reasoning from the target concept and the training example to construct a **proof tree** (an "explanation structure"). Each leaf of the tree is an *operationally verifiable* fact about the training example.
- **Why?** Without an explanation, the system cannot distinguish *correlated* features from *causally relevant* ones.
- **How?** Standard AI inference (resolution, backward chaining, or forward chaining) is applied over the domain theory.

### Stage 2 — Explanation Generalization
The proof tree is generalized by *replacing every training-example-specific constant with a universally quantified variable* while **preserving the structural dependencies** of the proof.
- **Why?** This converts the single instance into a class of instances that share the same causal structure.
- **How?** A process called *goal regression* in EBG.

### Stage 3 — Operationality Check
The generalized proof is examined against the operationality criterion. Predicates that are still non-operational are further expanded using the domain theory until every leaf predicate is operational.
- **Why?** Ensures the learned rule is efficient enough to use at classification time.

### Stage 4 — Compilation (Chunking)
The generalized, operational proof is compiled into a single, new rule (a *chunk*) that maps directly from observable features to the target concept.
- **Why?** Avoids re-deriving the explanation every time.
- **How?** In systems like **SOAR** (Rosenbloom & Newell, 1986), this is implemented via *chunking* of problem-space search results.

## 2.2 The Goal-Regression Mechanism (Central Algorithm of EBG)

Goal regression is the heart of explanation-based generalization. Formally:

> Given an operationality criterion and a proof that $Goal(TrainEx)$ holds, regression re-expresses the proof in terms of generalized variables.

**Definition (Goal Regression, Mitchell 1986):**

Let $P$ be a proof of $Goal(Ex)$ from the domain theory $T$. The **regression** of $P$ with respect to a literal $L$ in the proof is the weakest pre-condition $W$ such that if $W$ holds for any example $Ex'$, then $L$ holds for $Ex'$ implies $Goal(Ex')$ holds.

In first-order logic terms, given the rule:

$$R: \ Q(x) \ \land \ P(x,y) \ \rightarrow \ Goal(y)$$

If the literal $Q(x)$ in the proof of $Goal(TrainEx)$ has its argument regressed through $R$, then $Q$ is replaced by the conjunction of the *other* antecedents of $R$ with appropriate substitutions, yielding:

$$R':\ P(f(z),y) \ \land \ \text{regression through remaining rules} \ \rightarrow \ Goal(y)$$

This recursive substitution propagates regressed preconditions upward until all leaves of the proof are operational literals in the training example.

## 2.3 Properties of the Learned Rule

| Property | Description | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Sufficiency** | The rule's antecedent guarantees the consequent. | Guarantees correctness on unseen data. |
| **Generality** | Rule is no more specific than required by the operationality criterion. | Prevents over-generalisation. |
| **Operationality** | All antecedent predicates are operationally evaluable. | Ensures efficient inference at runtime. |
| **Intelligibility** | The rule's structure mirrors the explanation. | Useful for knowledge acquisition from experts. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Propositional vs First-Order EBL:** Mitchell's original EBG worked in first-order Horn-clause logic. *Propositional EBL* (Boutilier, Dearden & Goldszmidt, 1995) extends the idea to probabilistic/stochastic domains via Bayesian networks.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Symbol / Form** | **Meaning** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Target Concept | $C(x)$ | Predicate to be learned | Non-operational initially |
| Training Example | $e$ | Concrete instance | Single instance suffices |
| Domain Theory | $T$ | Set of Horn clauses | Must be sufficient to prove $C(e)$ |
| Operationality | $\text{Op}(p)$ | Predicate $p$ is efficiently testable | Defined by user/system |
| Explanation | $\Pi(e)$ | Proof tree of $T \vdash C(e)$ | Logical structure of *why* |
| Generalized Rule | $R_{\text{gen}}$ | $\bigwedge \text{leaf}(\Pi') \ \rightarrow \ C(x)$ | Compiled chunk |
| Goal Regression | $\text{Reg}(L, R)$ | Weakest pre-condition of $L$ w.r.t. rule $R$ | Core algorithmic primitive |
| Generalization Operator | $\theta$-subsumption | $G \preceq_{\theta} S$ | Plotkin's criterion for generality |
| Operationality Bias | $\mathcal{O}$ | Set of operationally acceptable predicates | User-defined heuristic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Condition:** The domain theory $T$ must be **sufficient** to prove $C(e)$. If $T \not\vdash C(e)$, then EBL **cannot proceed** — this is the well-known *knowledge-utility problem*. Also, $T$ must be **consistent**; an inconsistent domain theory produces incorrect generalized rules.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of EBL in Engineering

- **Intelligent Tutoring Systems (ITS):** Learns why a student solved a problem a certain way to provide targeted feedback.
- **Computer-Aided Design (CAD):** Generalises why a particular design satisfies engineering constraints (e.g., stress, thermal) into reusable design patterns.
- **Autonomous Robotics:** Learns macro-operators from a single demonstrated task (relevant to Soar-based cognitive architectures).
- **Automated Theorem Proving:** Macros learned via EBL speed up subsequent proofs in the same theory.
- **Network Configuration:** Cisco's knowledge-based assistants use EBL-like techniques to generalise configuration rules.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: The Canonical "Safe-To-Stake" Example (Mitchell, 1997)

This is the **exact worked example** appearing in Mitchell's *Machine Learning* textbook (the de facto reference for KTU EBL questions). I will solve it **exhaustively**, step by step.

### 3.1.1 The Four Inputs

**Input 1 — Target Concept:**

$$\text{SafeToStake}(x) \ \leftarrow \ \text{???}$$

**Input 2 — Training Example:**

$$x = \text{Obj}_1$$

where $\text{Obj}_1$ has properties:

$$\text{Wet}(\text{Obj}_1) = \text{false}, \quad \text{Lighter}(\text{Obj}_1, \text{Couch}) = \text{true}$$

**Input 3 — Domain Theory** (rules $R_1, \dots, R_5$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1: \quad & \neg \text{Wet}(z) \ \rightarrow \ \text{Dry}(z) \\
R_2: \quad & \text{Dry}(z) \ \land \ \text{Lighter}(z, \text{Couch}) \ \rightarrow \ \text{SafeToStake}(z) \\
R_3: \quad & \text{Lighter}(z, w) \ \land \ \text{Lighter}(w, u) \ \rightarrow \ \text{Lighter}(z, u) \\
R_4: \quad & \text{Lighter}(z, w) \ \land \ \text{Weight}(w, w_{\text{light}}) \ \rightarrow \ \text{Lighter}(z, u) \ \text{(transitivity expansion)} \\
R_5: \quad & \text{Weight}(\text{Couch}, 5) \ \land \ \text{Weight}(\text{Obj}_1, 1) \rightarrow \text{Lighter}(\text{Obj}_1, \text{Couch})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Input 4 — Operationality Criterion:**

$$\text{Op}(p) \iff \text{Atom}(p) \ \lor \ (\text{Weight}(p, \_)) \ \lor \ \text{Lighter}(p, \_)$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step EBG Execution

**Step A — Build the Explanation (Backward Chaining)**

Goal: prove $\text{SafeToStake}(\text{Obj}_1)$.

Apply $R_2$ (the only rule whose head matches the goal). We must show:

1. $\text{Dry}(\text{Obj}_1)$ — not yet operational (requires $R_1$).
2. $\text{Lighter}(\text{Obj}_1, \text{Couch})$ — operational, satisfied by training data.

For (1), apply $R_1$. We must show $\neg \text{Wet}(\text{Obj}_1)$.

By training data, $\text{Wet}(\text{Obj}_1) = \text{false}$, hence $\neg \text{Wet}(\text{Obj}_1)$ holds. $\text{Wet}$ is operational.

The complete proof tree is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SafeToStake}(\text{Obj}_1) & \leftarrow R_2: \text{Dry}(\text{Obj}_1) \ \land \ \text{Lighter}(\text{Obj}_1, \text{Couch}) \\
\text{Dry}(\text{Obj}_1) & \leftarrow R_1: \neg \text{Wet}(\text{Obj}_1) \\
\neg \text{Wet}(\text{Obj}_1) & \leftarrow \text{Training Data} \\
\text{Lighter}(\text{Obj}_1, \text{Couch}) & \leftarrow \text{Training Data}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Operationality Filtering**

Check each leaf against $\text{Op}$:
- $\neg \text{Wet}(\text{Obj}_1)$: $\text{Wet}$ is operational ✓
- $\text{Lighter}(\text{Obj}_1, \text{Couch})$: $\text{Lighter}$ is operational ✓
- $\text{Dry}(\text{Obj}_1)$: $\text{Dry}$ is **NOT** operational ✗

**Step C — Generalisation by Variable Replacement**

Replace every constant specific to the training example with a universally quantified variable. $\text{Obj}_1 \rightarrow x$, and $\text{Couch} \rightarrow c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SafeToStake}(x) & \leftarrow \text{Dry}(x) \ \land \ \text{Lighter}(x, c) \\
\text{Dry}(x) & \leftarrow \neg \text{Wet}(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Goal Regression to Eliminate Non-Operational Predicates**

The predicate $\text{Dry}(x)$ is non-operational. We regress it through $R_1$. The regression of $\text{Dry}(x)$ w.r.t. $R_1$ is $\neg \text{Wet}(x)$:

$$\text{SafeToStake}(x) \ \leftarrow \ \neg \text{Wet}(x) \ \land \ \text{Lighter}(x, c)$$

Now all leaves are operational ✓.

**Step E — Compile the Generalised Rule**

$$
\boxed{\,R_{\text{gen}}:\ \text{Lighter}(x, c) \ \land \ \neg \text{Wet}(x) \ \rightarrow \ \text{SafeToStake}(x)\,}
$$

where $c$ is a globally known reference object (the couch).

### 3.1.3 Analytical Matrix — Trace Summary

| Stage | Input State | Transformation | Output State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | $\text{SafeToStake}(\text{Obj}_1)$ | Initial query | Single goal |
| **Decompose (R2)** | Apply $R_2$ backward | Add $\text{Dry} \land \text{Lighter}$ | Two sub-goals |
| **Decompose (R1)** | Apply $R_1$ on $\text{Dry}$ | Add $\neg\text{Wet}$ | Leaf $\neg\text{Wet}$ reached |
| **Operationality Filter** | Check all leaves | $\text{Wet}$ ✓, $\text{Lighter}$ ✓, $\text{Dry}$ ✗ | Filter $\text{Dry}$ |
| **Generalise** | Replace constants | $\text{Obj}_1 \to x$ | Universal rule |
| **Goal Regression** | Eliminate $\text{Dry}$ | Substitute $R_1$ antecedent | All leaves operational |
| **Compile** | Flatten proof tree | Single new rule | $R_{\text{gen}}$ added to $T$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why did EBL succeed with one example?** Because the domain theory *fully explains* why $\text{Obj}_1$ is safe. Any other object $x$ that is lighter than the couch and not wet is *provably* safe by the same argument. This is the deductive power of EBL — and the reason it does not need statistical sampling.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison: EBL vs Pure Inductive Learning

### Worked Comparison on a Boolean Concept

Suppose we want to learn $\text{SafeToStake}(x)$. With inductive learning using **5 examples**, the learner might generalise to:

$$\text{Inductive:}\ \text{Lighter}(x, c) \ \rightarrow \ \text{SafeToStake}(x)$$

(omitting $\neg \text{Wet}(x)$ because no example violated it). This is **over-general**: a wet object lighter than the couch is unsafe but would be wrongly classified as safe.

EBL, by contrast, **retains** $\neg \text{Wet}(x)$ because the explanation requires it:

$$\text{EBL:}\ \text{Lighter}(x, c) \ \land \ \neg \text{Wet}(x) \ \rightarrow \ \text{SafeToStake}(x)$$

**Analytical Trade-off Matrix:**

| Dimension | Inductive Learning | EBL |
|---|---|---|
| Examples needed | Many (10s–1000s) | **One** |
| Knowledge required | None | Strong domain theory |
| Bias source | Hypothesis space | Operationality criterion |
| Handles noise | Yes (statistical averaging) | **Poorly** (errors propagate through proof) |
| Computational cost per example | Low | High (proof construction) |
| Reuse cost | Moderate (re-run learner) | **Very low** (compiled chunk) |
| Suffering from | Underfitting / overfitting | Imperfect domain theory |

## 3.3 Derivation of the EBL Generalisation Bound (Plotkin's $\theta$-Subsumption)

Plotkin's *relative generalisation* framework provides a formal basis for comparing the EBL-learned rule to the inductive rule.

**Definition (Relative Generalisation):**
A clause $C_1$ is *more general than* $C_2$ (denoted $C_1 \preceq C_2$) iff there exists a substitution $\theta$ such that $C_1 \theta \subseteq C_2$.

**Lemma (EBG Generality Guarantee, Mitchell 1986):**
The rule $R_{\text{EBG}}$ produced by explanation-based generalisation is the **least general generalisation (LGG)** of the training example under the constraint that $R_{\text{EBG}}$ be operational and sufficient.

**Proof sketch:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{EBG}} & \preceq R_{\text{any other sufficient operational rule}} \\
\text{(i.e., } R_{\text{EBG}} \text{ is the maximally specific operational rule covering } e \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is because every leaf of the proof tree is a *causal necessity* of the goal, so dropping any leaf would break the proof. Hence EBG cannot be more general than required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequence:** EBG cannot over-generalise *relative to its domain theory*. Inductive learners can. This is the central formal advantage of EBL.

## 3.4 Failure Modes of EBL (Analytical Pitfalls)

| Failure Mode | Cause | Symptom | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Imperfect Domain Theory** | Incomplete rules | Learns incorrect rule | Use probabilistic EBL (PEBL) |
| **Utility Problem** | Too many chunks accumulated | Inference slows down | Forgetting strategies |
| **Non-Operational Leaves** | Bad operationality criterion | Learns rule referencing non-testable predicates | Refine $\text{Op}$ predicate |
| **Plateau Effect** | Proof structure is identical across examples | No new chunks learned | Use multi-example EBG |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EBL System

The following Mermaid diagram captures the data flow of the four canonical inputs through the four EBG stages to produce the learned chunk.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Target Concept C of x] --> E[EBL Engine]
    B[Training Example e] --> E
    C[Domain Theory T] --> E
    D[Operationality Criterion Op] --> E

    E --> F[Stage 1 Explanation Construction]
    F --> G[Stage 2 Explanation Generalization]
    G --> H[Stage 3 Operationality Filtering]
    H --> I{All Leaves Operational?}
    I -- No --> J[Stage 3b Goal Regression]
    J --> H
    I -- Yes --> K[Stage 4 Compilation Chunking]
    K --> L[Generalized Rule R gen stored in KB]
    L --> M[Reused on future instances]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Proof-Tree Construction (Mermaid View)

The following diagram shows the explanation-tree construction step for the SafeToStake example, illustrating how each rule application refines the goal into operational leaves.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[SafeToStake Obj1] --> R2[Apply R2 Dry AND Lighter]
    R2 --> D[Dry Obj1]
    R2 --> L[Lighter Obj1 Couch]
    D --> R1[Apply R1 neg Wet]
    R1 --> W[Wet Obj1 false from training data]
    L --> T[Lighter Obj1 Couch from training data]
    W --> F{Operational?}
    T --> F2{Operational?}
    F -- Yes --> LEAF1[Operational Leaf neg Wet]
    F2 -- Yes --> LEAF2[Operational Leaf Lighter]
    D --> F3{Operational?}
    F3 -- No --> REG[Goal Regression through R1]
    REG --> LEAF1
```

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture: EBL vs Inductive Learning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EBL_Path[EBL Deductive Path]
        E1[Single Example] --> E2[Domain Theory]
        E2 --> E3[Explanation]
        E3 --> E4[Generalized Chunk]
    end
    subgraph IND_Path[Inductive Statistical Path]
        I1[Many Examples] --> I2[Hypothesis Search]
        I2 --> I3[Best-Fit Rule]
    end
    E4 --> OUT[Knowledge Base]
    I3 --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Knowledge-Flow Schematic of the EBG Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start EBG]) --> P1[Step 1 Explain Training Example]
    P1 --> P2[Step 2 Identify Operational Leaves]
    P2 --> P3{All Operational?}
    P3 -- No --> P4[Step 3 Goal Regression on Non-Operational Leaf]
    P4 --> P2
    P3 -- Yes --> P5[Step 4 Generalize Constants to Variables]
    P5 --> P6[Step 5 Compile Single Rule]
    P6 --> P7([Return R gen to Knowledge Base])
```

## 4.5 Memory & Inference Lifecycle (Chunk Reuse)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Inference_Engine
    participant Domain_Theory as Domain Theory T
    participant Chunk_Store as Chunk Store

    User->>Inference_Engine: Query classify Obj2
    Inference_Engine->>Chunk_Store: Check for compiled rule
    Chunk_Store-->>Inference_Engine: Match R gen
    Inference_Engine->>Inference_Engine: Evaluate operational antecedents
    Inference_Engine-->>User: SafeToStake true false
    Note over Chunk_Store: No re-derivation needed<br>Speedup vs from-scratch proof
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Explanation-Based Learning. State its four inputs.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

Explanation-Based Learning (EBL) is a knowledge-intensive, deductive learning method in which a single training example is generalized by constructing an explanation of why it satisfies the target concept using a domain theory, then compiling the generalized explanation into a reusable rule.

The four inputs are:
1. **Target Concept** — the high-level predicate to be learned.
2. **Training Example** — a single positive instance of the concept.
3. **Domain Theory** — a set of rules sufficient to explain why the example satisfies the target.
4. **Operationality Criterion** — a predicate defining which sub-concepts are efficiently testable.

**(3 marks)** — Definition (1.5) + Four inputs (1.5).

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Inductive Learning and Explanation-Based Learning.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Inductive Learning | EBL |
|---|---|---|
| Generalization basis | Statistical patterns | Logical/causal explanation |
| Examples required | Many | One |
| Prior knowledge | None or weak | Strong domain theory required |
| Bias | Hypothesis space | Operationality criterion |
| Over-generalisation risk | High (no causal info) | Low (guided by proof) |

**(3 marks)** — Any three correct distinct differences with proper technical justification.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Evaluation Pattern, Module 4]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Describe the Explanation-Based Generalization (EBG) algorithm in detail. Explain each step with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following domain theory and training example. Apply EBG step by step and derive the generalized rule.

*Target Concept:* $\text{SuitableGift}(x)$
*Training Example:* $\text{SuitableGift}(\text{Watch A})$
*Domain Theory:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1: \quad & \text{Expensive}(y) \ \land \ \text{ForPerson}(y, z) \ \rightarrow \ \text{SuitableGift}(z) \\
R_2: \quad & \text{MadeOf}(y, \text{Gold}) \ \rightarrow \ \text{Expensive}(y) \\
R_3: \quad & \text{MadeOf}(\text{Watch A}, \text{Gold}) \ \text{(ground fact)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Operationality:* Predicates $\text{MadeOf}, \text{ForPerson}$ are operational.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) EBG Algorithm Steps (7 marks):**

1. **Explain the Example** — Use backward chaining from $\text{SuitableGift}(\text{Watch A})$ over the domain theory to construct a proof tree. Apply $R_1$: need $\text{Expensive}(\text{Watch A})$ and $\text{ForPerson}(\text{Watch A}, z)$. Apply $R_2$: need $\text{MadeOf}(\text{Watch A}, \text{Gold})$, which is a ground fact.

2. **Operationalize the Explanation** — Check each leaf. $\text{MadeOf}$ and $\text{ForPerson}$ are operational ✓. $\text{Expensive}$ is not operational ✗.

3. **Generalize the Explanation** — Replace constants: $\text{Watch A} \rightarrow x$. The generalized structure:
$$\text{SuitableGift}(x) \leftarrow \text{Expensive}(x) \ \land \ \text{ForPerson}(x, z)$$

4. **Goal-Regress** the non-operational $\text{Expensive}(x)$ through $R_2$:
$$\text{SuitableGift}(x) \leftarrow \text{MadeOf}(x, \text{Gold}) \ \land \ \text{ForPerson}(x, z)$$

5. **Compile the Rule**:
$$\boxed{\text{SuitableGift}(x) \ \leftarrow \ \text{MadeOf}(x, \text{Gold}) \ \land \ \text{ForPerson}(x, z)}$$

(Each step carries 1.4 marks; diagram for 1 mark.)

**(b) Step-by-Step EBG Trace (7 marks):**

| Step | Operation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Goal | Prove $\text{SuitableGift}(\text{Watch A})$ | Initial query |
| 2. Apply $R_1$ | Need $\text{Expensive}(\text{Watch A})$ and $\text{ForPerson}(\text{Watch A}, z)$ | Two sub-goals |
| 3. Apply $R_2$ on $\text{Expensive}$ | Need $\text{MadeOf}(\text{Watch A}, \text{Gold})$ | Operational ✓ |
| 4. Ground fact | $\text{MadeOf}(\text{Watch A}, \text{Gold})$ | Operational ✓ |
| 5. Generalise | $\text{Watch A} \to x$ | Universal rule |
| 6. Regress $\text{Expensive}(x)$ through $R_2$ | Replace with $\text{MadeOf}(x, \text{Gold})$ | Operational ✓ |
| 7. Compile | Final rule | $\text{MadeOf}(x, \text{Gold}) \ \land \ \text{ForPerson}(x, z) \ \rightarrow \ \text{SuitableGift}(x)$ |

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Goal Regression** in Explanation-Based Learning. Why is it central to EBG? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** "EBL requires a strong, consistent domain theory." Discuss the consequences of an *imperfect* domain theory on the learned rule, and explain how Probabilistic EBL (PEBL) addresses this. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Goal Regression (7 marks):**

Goal regression is the formal primitive used by EBG to transform non-operational predicates in the proof tree into operationally testable ones by recursively substituting the antecedents of relevant domain rules.

- **Definition:** Given a proof of $\text{Goal}(e)$ using rule $R: \ Q(x) \land P(x,y) \rightarrow \text{Goal}(y)$, the regression of a literal $L$ in the proof is the *weakest pre-condition* on the training example that, together with $L$, implies $\text{Goal}(e)$.
- **Why central:** Without regression, the generalised proof would contain non-operational leaves, violating the operationality criterion. Regression *flattens* the proof into a single, efficient, operational rule.
- **Mechanism:** For each non-operational leaf $L$, find a rule $R$ whose head unifies with $L$, replace $L$ by the conjunction of $R$'s other antecedents (after appropriate substitution), and recurse until all leaves are operational.
- **Order of recursion:** Bottom-up — innermost leaves are regressed first.
- **Termination:** Guaranteed because the domain theory is finite and acyclic (a standard assumption).
- **Complexity:** Exponential in proof-tree depth in the worst case, but tractable for typical domain theories.

**(b) Imperfect Domain Theory and PEBL (7 marks):**

A domain theory may be imperfect in three ways:

1. **Incomplete** — Missing rules. EBG fails to construct an explanation; no rule learned.
2. **Inconsistent** — Contradictory rules. EBG may produce a *false* generalised rule.
3. **Probabilistic** — Rules hold with probability < 1. Standard EBG treats them as certain.

**Consequences of Imperfection:**
- An incomplete theory means EBL silently fails on examples it cannot explain.
- An inconsistent theory means the learned rule may misclassify unseen instances — defeating the deductive correctness guarantee.
- A purely-probabilistic domain cannot be used with first-order EBG because the proof assumes certainty.

**Probabilistic EBL (PEBL, Givan & DeJong 1997):**
- Replaces deterministic Horn clauses with conditional probability distributions.
- The explanation tree is still constructed via backward chaining, but each leaf carries a probability.
- The learned rule is a probabilistic summary: $P(\text{Goal}\vert \text{Antedecents}) = \prod P_i$.
- Enables learning in noisy, real-world domains where perfect theories are unavailable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often claim EBL is "a form of inductive learning." This will cost **2 marks**. EBL is **deductive**, not inductive. Use the words "logical," "proof-based," or "deductive" explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **chunking** (Soar) with **induction**. Chunking stores the *result* of a problem-space search and is implementation-level; EBG is the *learning theory* that justifies what gets stored.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to **regress** non-operational predicates. Many students stop after generalisation and lose 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Writing "EBL requires many examples." This is the most common factual error. **EBL requires only ONE example.**
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Failing to mention the **operationality criterion**. Without it, the algorithm has no termination condition.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU Module 4 — Explanation-Based Learning**

- **EBL = deductive + single-example + knowledge-intensive learning.** Not inductive.
- **Four inputs:** Target Concept, Training Example, Domain Theory, Operationality Criterion.
- **Four stages:** Explain → Generalize → Operationalize (via Goal Regression) → Compile (Chunk).
- **Goal Regression** is the *central algorithmic primitive* that replaces non-operational leaves with regressed antecedents.
- **Mitchell et al. (1986)** — formal EBG; **DeJong (1981)** — schema-based EBL; **Rosenbloom & Newell (1986)** — chunking in Soar.
- **EBL rule is the least-general operational rule** sufficient to explain the example (maximally specific → no over-generalisation).
- **Sufficient domain theory** is a precondition. Incomplete theory ⇒ no learning.
- **Operationality** is user-defined; without it, the algorithm has no termination condition.
- **Pros:** Fast inference at reuse time, requires only one example, causally justified rules.
- **Cons:** Needs strong domain theory, brittle under noise, susceptible to the *utility problem* (too many chunks slow retrieval).
- **Variants:** Propositional EBL (Boutilier et al.), Probabilistic EBL / PEBL (Givan & DeJong), Inductive-Analytical Learning (combines induction + EBL).
- **Key distinction from RL:** EBL has no reward signal; from ILP: EBL has a *complete* domain theory.
- **Real systems:** Soar (chunking), PRODIGY (EBL for problem solving), ICARUS cognitive architecture.
- **Worked example to memorise:** SafeToStake / Cup example from Mitchell's *Machine Learning* — frequently asked in KTU exams.
- **Equation to remember:** The compiled EBG rule has the form $\bigwedge_i \text{OpLeaf}_i(x_1, \dots, x_k) \ \rightarrow \ \text{TargetConcept}(x)$.
- **Plotkin's $\theta$-subsumption** provides the formal generality ordering underpinning EBG.
- **Examiner's golden phrase:** *"EBL generalises the explanation, not the example directly."* — Use this in any 7-mark answer for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical learning methods and reinforcement learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT502 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Statistical learning methods and reinforcement learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Statistical Learning Methods

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Statistical Learning Methods are a family of machine learning algorithms that build predictive and inferential models by leveraging probabilistic frameworks, data distributions, and statistical decision theory. They include Bayesian learning, Expectation-Maximization, and generative classifiers (e.g., Naive Bayes), all of which treat learning as a problem of estimating probability distributions from data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Statistical learning methods are grounded in the assumption that data is generated by an unknown probability distribution $P(X, Y)$. The learner's task is to approximate this distribution well enough to make accurate predictions on unseen data.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are a doctor trying to diagnose whether a patient has a flu or a common cold based on symptoms (fever, cough, runny nose).
