```mermaid
    GOV[Governance Layer: Laws, Regulations, Ethics Boards]
    ORG[Organizational Layer: Internal Policies, Model Cards, Audits]
    TECH[Technical Layer: Fairness Algorithms, XAI, Differential Privacy]
    DATA[Data Layer: Quality, Consent, Representation]
    FOUN[Foundation: Human Values and Rights]
    FOUN --> DATA
    DATA --> TECH
    TECH --> ORG
    ORG --> GOV
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Map of AI Technologies to Ethical Risk Domains

```mermaid
graph LR
    FR[Facial Recognition] --> RISK1[Privacy and Surveillance]
    LLM[Generative AI] --> RISK2[Hallucination and Deepfakes]
    POL[Predictive Policing] --> RISK3[Bias Amplification]
    REC[Recommender Systems] --> RISK4[Filter Bubbles]
    AW[Autonomous Weapons] --> RISK5[Loss of Human Control]
    HIR[Hiring AI] --> RISK6[Workplace Discrimination]
    MED[Medical AI] --> RISK7[Liability and Safety]
    EDU[Educational AI] --> RISK8[Student Surveillance]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to use these diagrams in the exam:** In a 14-mark answer, reproducing a clean version of Diagram 1 or Diagram 3 (in Mermaid-style boxes drawn by hand) and **linking it to your textual analysis** can fetch **1–2 extra marks** under "presentation and structure" per KTU valuation norms.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Each Part A question carries **3 marks**. The model answer below is calibrated to the length and depth expected in a 3-mark KTU 2024 answer (roughly 1 page).*

---

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]

**Differentiate between algorithmic bias and societal bias. Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Algorithmic Bias (1 mark):** Bias that arises *within* an AI system — typically due to skewed training data, flawed feature engineering, or optimization choices made by the developer. It is a *technical* defect.
- **Societal Bias (1 mark):** Pre-existing *human* bias reflected in historical data and institutions — e.g., gender pay gaps, racial disparities in arrests. It exists in the real world *before* any algorithm is built.
- **Example of Algorithmic Bias:** Amazon's recruiting AI (2018) downranked resumes containing the word "women's" because it was trained on 10 years of male-dominated hiring data.
- **Example of Societal Bias:** Historical underrepresentation of women in STEM fields — this is the *input* that the algorithm learned from.
- **Linkage (1 mark):** Societal bias is the *source*; algorithmic bias is the *amplifier*. AI does not create bias from nothing — it scales and operationalizes existing human prejudice at machine speed.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]

**List and briefly explain any three principles from the UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of AI (2021).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Proportionality and Do No Harm (1 mark):** AI systems must not be used in ways that cause harm disproportionate to their intended benefits. Risk-benefit analysis is mandatory.

2. **Fairness and Non-Discrimination (1 mark):** AI must be designed to avoid biased outcomes and to promote social justice. Developers should actively test for disparate impact across protected groups.

3. **Transparency and Explainability (1 mark):** AI systems should be understandable to their users and stakeholders. Black-box deployments in high-stakes domains (healthcare, criminal justice) are discouraged.

*(Optional bonus, only if asked: A 4th principle — **Responsibility and Accountability** — assigns human moral responsibility for AI behavior, irrespective of the system's autonomy.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> *Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and features a sub-part split of **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, exactly as per the KTU 2024 End Semester Exam (ESE) pattern. Two alternative choices are provided.*

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, Q A]**

**(a)** *Explain the **"Black Box Problem"** in AI. Why is it ethically problematic? Discuss with reference to the **Right to Explanation** under GDPR. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *With a neat diagram, describe the **ETHICS-A framework** for evaluating the ethical implications of an AI system. Apply it to **AI-based recruitment**. **(7 marks)***

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Q A (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition of the Black Box Problem (2 marks):**
A *black box* AI system is one whose internal decision-making process is **opaque** — even to its developers. Deep neural networks, large language models, and ensemble methods are classic examples. The user receives an *output* (e.g., "Loan denied") but cannot trace *why*.

**2. Why It Is Ethically Problematic (3 marks):**

- **No meaningful consent:** A person denied a loan cannot meaningfully contest the decision if no one can explain it.
- **No error correction:** If the model is biased, the bias is invisible and self-perpetuating.
- **Erosion of trust:** Institutions lose legitimacy when their decisions are unfathomable.
- **Moral crumple zone:** Frontline workers (e.g., loan officers) are unfairly blamed for algorithmic decisions they cannot interpret.

**3. Right to Explanation under GDPR (2 marks):**
Article 22 of the EU's **General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)** grants individuals the right **"not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing"** that produces legal or similarly significant effects. Recital 71 further interprets this as a **right to obtain an explanation** of the decision. This directly conflicts with the deployment of fully opaque black-box models in high-stakes domains like credit scoring, employment, and criminal sentencing.

**Conclusion (½ mark):** Hence, ethically and legally, **Explainable AI (XAI)** is no longer optional in regulated industries.

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Q A (b) — 7 Marks

**1. ETHICS-A Framework Diagram (3 marks):**

```
        ┌───────────────────────────┐
        │  ESTABLISH the use case    │
        └─────────────┬─────────────┘
                      ▼
        ┌───────────────────────────┐
        │   TAG all stakeholders     │
        └─────────────┬─────────────┘
                      ▼
        ┌───────────────────────────┐
        │  HARM inventory + matrix   │
        └─────────────┬─────────────┘
                      ▼
        ┌───────────────────────────┐
        │  IDENTIFY FAT+R principles │
        └─────────────┬─────────────┘
                      ▼
        ┌───────────────────────────┐
        │   COUNTERFACTUAL analysis  │
        └─────────────┬─────────────┘
                      ▼
        ┌───────────────────────────┐
        │    SUGGEST mitigations     │
        └─────────────┬─────────────┘
                      ▼
        ┌───────────────────────────┐
        │  AUDIT & monitor (loop)    │
        └───────────────────────────┘
```

*(1 mark for the diagram structure, 1 mark for the 7 steps being correctly named, 1 mark for arrows and visual clarity.)*

**2. Application to AI-Based Recruitment (4 marks):**

- **E — Use Case:** Company "TechNova" deploys an AI to filter 50,000 resumes per quarter.
- **T — Stakeholders:** Applicants, HR team, hiring managers, the company, marginalized groups.
- **H — Harms:** Gender bias (S=5, P=4, Risk=20), privacy leakage (S=4, P=2, Risk=8), skill atrophy in HR (S=2, P=5, Risk=10).
- **I — Principles:** Fairness (parity across gender/caste), Accountability (final say remains human), Transparency (rejected candidates get reasons), Responsibility (robust to adversarial resumes).
- **C — Counterfactual:** Without AI, human recruiters also have bias — but AI may *amplify* it through scale.
- **S — Mitigations:** Use counterfactually fair models, blind resume screening for protected attributes, third-party bias audits.
- **A — Audit:** Quarterly demographic parity reports; opt-in candidate feedback.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, Q B]**

**(a)** *Discuss the **societal impact of AI on employment**. Explain the concepts of **task-level** vs. **job-level automation** and **job polarization**. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *Explain the **EU AI Act's risk-based classification**. List the four risk categories and give one example application for each. **(7 marks)***

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Q B (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Societal Impact on Employment — Overview (2 marks):**
AI is a **general-purpose technology** that automates not only manual labor (as machines did during the Industrial Revolution) but also **cognitive labor** — pattern recognition, document review, basic coding, translation, and even medical diagnosis. This has three major effects:

- **Job displacement** in automatable occupations.
- **Job creation** in new AI-related roles (prompt engineers, AI auditors, data curators).
- **Job transformation** in most existing roles (radiologists using AI as a second reader).

**2. Task-Level vs. Job-Level Automation (3 marks):**

- **Task-level automation:** A *task* is a discrete unit of work within a job. A radiologist's job includes many tasks — image acquisition, image analysis, report writing, patient communication. AI currently automates only the *image analysis* task. The radiologist's job persists, but its nature changes.
- **Job-level automation:** The *entire* job is performed by AI. This is rarer — e.g., a fully autonomous taxi replacing a human driver.

The **critical insight** is that even *partial* task-level automation can reduce wages and headcount, because if 70% of a paralegal's tasks are automated, the firm may need only 30% of the previous headcount.

**3. Job Polarization (2 marks):**
Job polarization is the phenomenon where:

- **High-skill jobs** (AI researchers, lawyers, creative directors) grow in number and pay.
- **Low-skill jobs** (cleaning, personal care) remain stable — they are *hard to automate* because they require physical dexterity or human empathy.
- **Middle-skill jobs** (clerical, repetitive factory work, basic accounting) shrink because they are *highly automatable*.

This hollowing out of the middle is called the **"barbell" or "hourglass" shape** of the labor market, and it is correlated with rising inequality in many economies.

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Q B (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Overview of the EU AI Act (1 mark):**
The **EU AI Act (2024)** is the world's first comprehensive horizontal regulation of AI. It adopts a **risk-based pyramid approach** — the higher the potential harm, the stricter the obligations.

**2. The Four Risk Categories (6 marks — 1.5 each):**

| Risk Level | Definition | Example Application | Obligations |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Unacceptable Risk** | Banned outright. | Social scoring by governments, real-time biometric ID in public spaces (with narrow exceptions). | Prohibited. |
| **High Risk** | Strict compliance required. | AI in hiring, credit scoring, medical devices, critical infrastructure. | Risk assessments, high-quality datasets, logging, human oversight, registration. |
| **Limited Risk** | Transparency obligations. | Chatbots, deepfake generators. | Must disclose AI interaction; label AI-generated content. |
| **Minimal / No Risk** | Voluntary best practices. | AI-powered video games, spam filters. | No mandatory obligations; voluntary codes of conduct encouraged. |

**Conclusion (½ mark):** The risk-based approach is significant because it **scales regulatory burden with potential harm**, avoiding the trap of over-regulating low-stakes AI while under-regulating high-stakes AI.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students lose marks in Module 4 questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "AI Bias" with "human bias" without distinguishing the amplifier role.** Always state: *AI does not invent bias — it scales and operationalizes pre-existing human bias.* (Lose 1 mark if you skip this nuance.)
>
> 2. **Writing opinions instead of structured analysis in 14-mark questions.** Always use a **framework** (ETHICS-A, FAT+R, or the EU AI Act pyramid). Unstructured answers score under 50% even if content is correct.
>
> 3. **Forgetting real-world examples.** KTU 2024 valuation explicitly awards **½ to 1 mark** for a relevant real-world case study (Amazon hiring, Cambridge Analytica, Clearview AI, etc.).
>
> 4. **Mixing up "Transparency" and "Explainability."** Transparency = open about *what the system is and what data it uses.* Explainability = can describe *why a specific decision was made.*
>
> 5. **Ignoring the Indian context.** Where appropriate, mention **Aadhaar, the DPDP Act 2023 (Digital Personal Data Protection), or NITI Aayog's "Responsible AI for All" (2021)** — this shows depth and is rewarded.
>
> 6. **Writing too generically.** "AI is good" or "AI is bad" earns zero. Always **qualify**: *"AI in X context, deployed under Y safeguards, can be Z, but…"*

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> *Use this section as a **last-night revision checklist** before the KTU 2024 ESE.*

### ✅ Must-Memorize Definitions (3-mark questions love these)

- **AI Ethics** — normative principles for responsible AI design.
- **Algorithmic Bias** — systematic unfair error in AI decisions.
- **Black Box** — opaque AI whose reasoning cannot be inspected.
- **Deepfake** — AI-generated synthetic media.
- **Differential Privacy** — mathematical noise injection to protect individuals.
- **Existential Risk** — catastrophic long-term risk from advanced AI.
- **Filter Bubble** — AI personalization narrowing a user's information diet.
- **Hallucination (LLM)** — confidently fabricated output.
- **Job Polarization** — hollowing of middle-skill jobs.
- **Right to Explanation** — GDPR Article 22 right to contest automated decisions.
- **Value Alignment** — ensuring AI goals match human values.

### ✅ Must-Memorize Frameworks

- **FAT+R** (Fairness, Accountability, Transparency, Responsibility/Safety).
- **ETHICS-A** 7-step evaluation flow.
- **EU AI Act's 4-tier risk pyramid** (Unacceptable → High → Limited → Minimal).
- **Three-Layer Model** of Societal Impact (Individual → Societal → Global).

### ✅ Must-Memorize Real-World Cases

- Amazon's hiring AI (gender bias).
- Cambridge Analytica (data exploitation).
- COMPAS (recidivism prediction bias).
- Clearview AI (facial recognition controversy).
- Aadhaar in India (digital identity ethics).
- Tesla Autopilot fatal crashes (safety & accountability).
- The **NIST Face Recognition Vendor Test** (FRVT) — bias benchmarks.

### ✅ Must-Memorize Indian Context

- **DPDP Act 2023** (Digital Personal Data Protection Act).
- **NITI Aayog's "Responsible AI for All"** (2021) — India's national AI ethics strategy.
- **AI in Aadhaar** — biometric authentication at 1.4 billion scale.
- **MeitY's IndiaAI Mission** (2024) — sovereign AI compute and dataset.

### ✅ High-Yield Comparative Concepts (favourite of KTU examiners)

- **Task-level** vs. **Job-level automation.**
- **Group fairness** vs. **Individual fairness.**
- **Interpretability** vs. **Explainability (XAI).**
- **Data minimization** vs. **Data maximization.**
- **Hard law** (EU AI Act) vs. **Soft law** (UNESCO Recommendation).

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"In every AI ethics question, the KTU 2024 examiner is testing three things — (1) Do you know the framework? (2) Can you apply it to a real case? (3) Can you weigh the trade-offs fairly? Master these three, and Module 4 is yours."* 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reasoning techniques: rule-based, frame-based, model-based, and case-based
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCAT502 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Reasoning techniques: rule-based, frame-based, model-based, and case-based ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Reasoning in AI? In the context of KTU 2024 syllabus for **PCCAT502 – Introduction to Artificial Intelligence**, **Reasoning** is formally defined as the cognitive process by which an intelligent agent draws conclusions, makes predictions, or derives new facts from existing knowledge using logical or heuristic methods. Reasoning is the *inference engine* that transforms static knowledge into dynamic, actionable intelligence. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):** > *"Reasoning is the mechanism by which an AI system manipulates its knowledge base to produce new knowledge or decisions, typically classified by the structure of the knowledge representation scheme employed."* ### 1.2 The Four Major Reasoning Techniques The KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus explicitly highlights **four canonical reasoning paradigms** that every B.Tech student must master: | # | Reasoning Type | Knowledge Representation | Core Mechanism | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Rule-Based Reasoning (RBR)** | IF-THEN Production Rules | Logical Inference (Forward/Backward Chaining) | | 2 | **Frame-Based Reasoning (FBR)** | Structured Frames/Slots | Inheritance & Defaults | | 3 | **Model-Based Reasoning (MBR)** | Causal/Structural Models | Simulation & Prediction | | 4 | **Case-Based Reasoning (CBR)** | Past Experience Cases | Retrieve-Reuse-Revise-Retain | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogies (Real-World Intuition) **1. Rule-Based Reasoning → The Traffic Police Manual** > Imagine a traffic policeman who has been given a thick rulebook: *"IF signal is RED, THEN stop the vehicles."* He doesn't think creatively — he just looks at the situation and applies the matching rule. This is exactly how rule-based systems work. They follow pre-defined logical rules mechanically. **2. Frame-Based Reasoning → The Family Photo Album** > Think of a "frame" as a structured photo album page. The page for *"Elephant"* has slots like *size = large*, *trunk = yes*, *legs = 4*. If you encounter a baby elephant, the system fills the "age" slot as "young" while inheriting everything else from the parent "Mammal" frame. Inheritance is the key idea. **3. Model-Based Reasoning → The Car Mechanic's Mental Simulation** > When your car won't start, a skilled mechanic doesn't just guess. He runs a mental *model*: *"If the battery is dead, the headlights would also be dim. Since the headlights are bright, the battery is fine — so the problem must be fuel-related."* This is model-based reasoning — reasoning over a causal/structural model of the system. **4. Case-Based Reasoning → The Experienced Doctor** > A senior doctor, upon seeing a new patient, recalls: *"I saw a patient with very similar symptoms last year. We diagnosed it as dengue and the treatment worked."* He then *adapts* that previous case to the current patient. This is case-based reasoning — solving new problems by remembering similar past experiences. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** > - *Rule-Based* uses **general rules** (abstract knowledge). > - *Case-Based* uses **specific past experiences** (concrete episodes). > - *Frame-Based* uses **structured object descriptions with inheritance**. > - *Model-Based* uses a **simulatable model of how the world works**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### 2.1 Rule-Based Reasoning (RBR) — Deep Analysis A **Rule-Based System (Production System)** is built on three classical components introduced by Newell & Simon: 1. **Production Rules (Knowledge Base):** A set of IF-THEN statements of the form `IF THEN ` 2. **Working Memory (Fact Base):** A dynamic database of currently known facts. 3. **Inference Engine:** The reasoning mechanism that matches rules against facts and fires applicable rules. **Two Fundamental Inference Strategies:** - **Forward Chaining (Data-Driven):** Starts from known facts and derives new facts until the goal is reached or no more rules can fire. *Analogy: Bottom-up reasoning.* - **Backward Chaining (Goal-Driven):** Starts from a hypothesis (goal) and works backward to find supporting facts. *Analogy: Top-down reasoning.* **Conflict Resolution Strategies** (when multiple rules match simultaneously): - Recency (prefer recently added facts) - Specificity (prefer more specific rules) - Priority (rule ordering) - Refractoriness (don't fire the same rule twice on the same data) ### 2.2 Frame-Based Reasoning (FBR) — Deep Analysis Introduced by **Marvin Minsky (1975)** as a representation scheme for *stereotyped situations*. A frame is a data structure with: - **Slots:** Named attributes (like object properties). - **Slot Values:** The content of each slot (can be data, procedures, or pointers to other frames). - **Defaults:** Values assumed when no specific information is available. - **Attached Procedures (Daemons):** IF-NEEDED, IF-ADDED, IF-REMOVED — triggered by slot access/modification. **Core Mechanism: Inheritance Hierarchy** - Frames are organized in a taxonomic tree (subclass / superclass relations). - A child frame inherits all slots and defaults from its parent unless explicitly overridden. - *Exception* is handled naturally because a child frame's local value overrides the inherited one. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Frames are Powerful:** They combine declarative knowledge (slot values) with procedural knowledge (daemons) in a single structure, supporting default reasoning and exception handling elegantly. ### 2.3 Model-Based Reasoning (MBR) — Deep Analysis A **Model-Based Reasoning system** contains an explicit, simulatable model of the physical or conceptual system being reasoned about. The model captures: - **Structure:** Components and their connections. - **Behavior:** How components function and interact (often via differential equations, logic, or qualitative rules). - **Functional Causal Relations:** How a fault in one component propagates to observable symptoms. **Two Principal Sub-Modalities:** 1. **Model-Based Diagnosis (MBD):** Given observed symptoms, identify the faulty component(s). Classical algorithm: *Reiter's Theory of Diagnosis* — compute minimal *conflict sets* and from them *hitting sets* (candidate diagnoses). 2. **Qualitative Reasoning (QR):** Reason about continuous systems using qualitative values {+, 0, −} instead of exact numbers (e.g., Forbus, de Kleer, Kuipers). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Model-Based Reasoning is widely used in **industrial fault diagnosis, aerospace, medical diagnosis, and digital twins** — a hot industry 4.0 topic. ### 2.4 Case-Based Reasoning (CBR) — Deep Analysis A **Case** is a contextualized piece of past experience representing a problem and its solution. The classical **Aamodt & Plaza (1994) CBR Cycle** has four Rs: 1. **Retrieve:** Given a new problem, find the most similar case(s) from the case library. Similarity is computed using k-NN, similarity metrics, or indexing schemes. 2. **Reuse:** Adapt the retrieved solution to fit the new problem. 3. **Revise:** Evaluate the proposed solution (often by testing in the real world or via simulation) and repair if needed. 4. **Retain:** If the revised solution is useful, integrate the new case into the case library for future use (learning). **CBR vs. RBR Comparison:** | Aspect | Rule-Based | Case-Based | |---|---|---| | Knowledge Type | General abstractions | Specific past episodes | | Learning | Manual rule updates | Automatic via Retain step | | Explanation | Logical deduction trace | "I solved a similar problem..." | | Novelty Handling | Poor (no rule = no answer) | Good (can adapt old cases) | ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet | # | Concept | Key Property / Formula / Term | Application Domain | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Production Rule | $\text{IF } P_1 \land P_2 \land \dots \land P_n \text{ THEN } Q$ | Expert Systems (MYCIN, CLIPS) | | 2 | Forward Chaining | $\text{Facts} \rightarrow \text{Derived Facts} \rightarrow \text{Goal}$ | Monitoring, Control | | 3 | Backward Chaining | $\text{Goal} \rightarrow \text{Sub-goals} \rightarrow \text{Facts}$ | Diagnosis, Query answering | | 4 | Frame | $F = \langle \text{Name}, \text{Slots}, \text{Defaults}, \text{Daemons} \rangle$ | Object-oriented AI, vision | | 5 | Inheritance | $\text{value}(c) = \text{local}(c) \lor \text{value}(\text{parent}(c))$ | Knowledge sharing | | 6 | Model | $M = \langle \text{Components}, \text{Connections}, \text{Behavior} \rangle$ | Fault diagnosis | | 7 | Reiter's Diagnosis | $\Delta = \text{Hitting-Sets}(\text{Conflict-Sets}(SD, \text{OBS}))$ | Industrial diagnostics | | 8 | CBR Cycle | $\text{Retrieve} \rightarrow \text{Reuse} \rightarrow \text{Revise} \rightarrow \text{Retain}$ | Help-desk, law, medicine | | 9 | Similarity Metric | $\text{sim}(T, S) = \frac{\sum_{i} w_i \cdot \text{sim}_i(T_i, S_i)}{\sum_{i} w_i}$ | Case retrieval | | 10 | Default Logic | $\frac{: A \mid B}{C}$ (pragmatic rule) | Frame defaults | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** RBR powers expert diagnostic systems in healthcare and finance; FBR underlies semantic web ontologies; MBR drives modern digital-twin platforms in Industry 4.0; CBR is the foundation of modern recommendation systems and conversational AI memory modules. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example: Rule-Based Reasoning with Forward Chaining **Problem Setup:** Consider the following knowledge base of rules and facts: - **Rule R1:** IF `(has_feathers)` AND `(lays_eggs)` THEN `is_bird` - **Rule R2:** IF `(is_bird)` AND `(cannot_fly)` AND `(swims)` THEN `is_penguin` - **Rule R3:** IF `(is_bird)` AND `(runs_fast)` AND `(has_long_neck)` THEN `is_ostrich` **Initial Working Memory Facts:** `has_feathers`, `lays_eggs`, `cannot_fly`, `swims` **Goal:** Prove `is_penguin` **Step-by-Step Forward Chaining Execution:** Step 1: Examine the working memory. The current known facts are: $$\text{WM}_0 = \{ \text{has\_feathers},\ \text{lays\_eggs},\ \text{cannot\_fly},\ \text{swims} \}$$ Step 2: Test Rule R1's antecedent against $\text{WM}_0$. The condition `has_feathers AND lays_eggs` is satisfied. Therefore, R1 fires and we add `is_bird` to the working memory. $$\text{WM}_1 = \text{WM}_0 \cup \{ \text{is\_bird} \}$$ Step 3: Test Rule R2's antecedent against $\text{WM}_1$. The condition requires `is_bird AND cannot_fly AND swims`. All three are present in $\text{WM}_1$. Step 4: Fire R2 and add `is_penguin` to working memory. $$\text{WM}_2 = \text{WM}_1 \cup \{ \text{is\_penguin} \}$$ Step 5: Test Rule R3 — condition `runs_fast AND has_long_neck` is not satisfied, so R3 is ignored. Step 6: Re-test R1 and R2 — they have already fired (refractoriness), so no new facts are produced. The system halts. **Final Conclusion:** Goal `is_penguin` is derived in 2 firing cycles. ### 3.2 Worked Example: Frame-Based Inheritance Resolution **Frame Hierarchy:** ``` Frame: Animal Slots: { has_spine = YES, limbs = 4, habitat = land } Frame: Mammal (subclass: Animal) Slots: { has_fur = YES, gives_milk = YES } Inheritance: inherits from Animal Frame: Dog (subclass: Mammal) Slots: { breed = Labrador, legs = 4 } ``` **Query:** What is `has_spine` for a Dog? Step 1: Look in Dog's local slots. The slot `has_spine` is **not** defined locally. Step 2: Traverse the inheritance chain upward. Move from Dog → Mammal. Step 3: Mammal's local slots do not contain `has_spine`. Continue upward to Animal. Step 4: Animal's local slots define `has_spine = YES`. Inherit this value through the chain. $$\text{value}_{\text{Dog}}(\text{has\_spine}) = \text{value}_{\text{Animal}}(\text{has\_spine}) = \text{YES}$$ **Result via Inherited Resolution:** $$\text{value}(\text{slot}, c) = \begin{cases} \text{local}(c) & \text{if } c.\text{slot} \text{ is locally defined} \\ \text{value}(\text{slot}, \text{parent}(c)) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Example: Model-Based Diagnosis (Reiter's Algorithm) **System Description (SD):** A simple circuit with three inverters in series. - Output of $I_1$ goes to $I_2$, output of $I_2$ goes to $I_3$. - $I_3$'s output is the system output $O$. **Components:** $C = \{I_1, I_2, I_3\}$. Each is either `OK` (⊤) or `AB` (abnormal). **Observed Output (OBS):** $O = 1$ (HIGH), given input = 0. Step 1: Compute the **System Behavior** under each possible assumption. For the input 0, if all inverters are OK, then $O$ should be 0. $$\text{predicted}(O \mid \text{all OK}) = 1 \quad \text{(after three inversions: } 0 \to 1 \to 0 \to 1\text{)}$$ Wait — three inversions of 0 yields 1, which **matches** the observation. So the *single-fault* assumption produces no conflict yet. Let us adjust the example to a system where inconsistency is introduced. **Revised System:** Two inverters in series. Input = 0. Observed $O = 0$. If both are OK, predicted $O = 1$. Step 2: Compute the **Conflict Set** (CS) — the minimal set of components whose *abnormality* explains the discrepancy. $$\text{CS}_1 = \{I_1, I_2\}$$ Step 3: Compute the **Hitting Set** — the minimal set of components that intersects every conflict set. This is the **candidate diagnosis**. $$\Delta = \{I_1\} \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta = \{I_2\}$$ Step 4: Both $\{I_1\}$ and $\{I_2\}$ are minimal single-fault diagnoses. The system reports: *"Either $I_1$ or $I_2$ is faulty, but not necessarily both."* ### 3.4 Worked Example: CBR Similarity Computation (k-NN style) **New Problem (Target T):** - Symptom 1 (Fever): present - Symptom 2 (Cough): present - Symptom 3 (Body ache): absent - Symptom 4 (Rash): present **Case Library:** - **Case $S_1$ (Dengue):** Fever=yes, Cough=no, Body-ache=yes, Rash=yes → Dengue - **Case $S_2$ (Flu):** Fever=yes, Cough=yes, Body-ache=yes, Rash=no → Flu - **Case $S_3$ (Measles):** Fever=yes, Cough=yes, Body-ache=no, Rash=yes → Measles **Weights:** $w_1 = 0.4,\ w_2 = 0.2,\ w_3 = 0.1,\ w_4 = 0.3$. Each $\text{sim}_i$ is 1 if both present, 0 otherwise. **Compute similarity to $S_1$:** Fever:1, Cough:0, Body-ache:0, Rash:1. $$\text{sim}(T, S_1) = \frac{(0.4)(1) + (0.2)(0) + (0.1)(0) + (0.3)(1)}{0.4 + 0.2 + 0.1 + 0.3} = \frac{0.7}{1.0} = 0.70$$ **Compute similarity to $S_2$:** Fever:1, Cough:1, Body-ache:0, Rash:0. $$\text{sim}(T, S_2) = \frac{(0.4)(1) + (0.2)(1) + (0.1)(0) + (0.3)(0)}{1.0} = \frac{0.6}{1.0} = 0.60$$ **Compute similarity to $S_3$:** Fever:1, Cough:1, Body-ache:1, Rash:1. $$\text{sim}(T, S_3) = \frac{(0.4)(1) + (0.2)(1) + (0.1)(1) + (0.3)(1)}{1.0 = 1.00}$$ **Decision:** $S_3$ is most similar (similarity 1.00) → Diagnosis: **Measles**. Reuse its treatment plan, possibly revise, and retain the new case for future learning. ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Criterion | Rule-Based | Frame-Based | Model-Based | Case-Based | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Knowledge Form** | Production rules | Hierarchical frames | Causal/structural model | Past episodes | | **Reasoning Direction** | Forward / Backward | Top-down inheritance | Simulation / abduction | Similarity-based retrieval | | **Handling Novelty** | Weak | Moderate (defaults) | Strong | Strong (analogies) | | **Learning** | Manual | Manual | Manual | Automatic (Retain) | | **Uncertainty Handling** | Requires CF (MYCIN) | Limited | Strong (via model) | Inherent in similarity | | **Explanation** | Deduction trace | Inheritance path | Model simulation | "Similar case was..." | | **Knowledge Acquisition** | Bottleneck (expert) | Moderate | Hard (build model) | Easy (record cases) | | **KTU Example System** | MYCIN, CLIPS | KRL, FrameNet | DART, Livingstone | CASEY, ANAPRON | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Architecture of a Rule-Based Production System ```mermaid flowchart TB user([User Query / New Fact]) kb[(Knowledge Base
IF-THEN Rules)] wm[(Working Memory
Current Facts)] ie{Inference Engine} fc[Forward Chaining
Module] bc[Backward Chaining
Module] cr[Conflict Resolver] out([Inferred Conclusion]) user --> ie kb --> ie wm --> ie ie --> fc ie --> bc fc --> cr bc --> cr cr --> wm wm --> out ``` ### 4.2 Frame Inheritance Hierarchy (Sample) ```mermaid flowchart TB a1[Animal
has_spine YES
limbs 4] a2[Mammal
has_fur YES
gives_milk YES] a3[Bird
has_feathers YES
lays_eggs YES] a4[Dog
breed Labrador
legs 4] a5[Penguin
cannot_fly YES
swims YES] a1 --> a2 a1 --> a3 a2 --> a4 a3 --> a5 ``` ### 4.3 Model-Based Reasoning Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR obs[Observed Symptoms
from Sensors] model[(Structural and
Behavioral Model)] sim[Simulation Engine] pred[Predicted Behavior] cmp{Comparator} cs[Conflict Set
Computation] hs[Hitting Set
Algorithm] diag[Final Diagnosis
Candidate] obs --> cmp model --> sim sim --> pred pred --> cmp cmp -->|Mismatch Detected| cs cs --> hs hs --> diag ``` ### 4.4 The Aamodt-Plaza CBR Cycle (4 R's) ```mermaid flowchart LR newp[New Problem] ret[Retrieve
Most Similar Case] reu[Reuse
Adapt Solution] rev[Revise
Test and Repair] retn[Retain
Store New Case] lib[(Case Library
Past Experiences)] newp --> ret lib --> ret ret --> reu reu --> rev rev --> retn retn --> lib retn --> newp ``` ### 4.5 Comparative Reasoning Architecture Map ```mermaid flowchart TB kb[Knowledge Source] kb --> r1[Rules: IF-THEN] kb --> r2[Frames: Slots and Defaults] kb --> r3[Model: Structure and Behavior] kb --> r4[Cases: Past Experiences] r1 --> e1[Inference Engine
Forward / Backward] r2 --> e2[Inheritance Engine
Slot Resolution] r3 --> e3[Simulation Engine
Causal Propagation] r4 --> e4[Retrieval Engine
Similarity and Adaptation] e1 --> out1[Logical Conclusion] e2 --> out2[Inherited and Filled Slots] e3 --> out3[Predicted Behavior or Diagnosis] e4 --> out4[Adapted Past Solution] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PCCAT502]** **Differentiate between forward chaining and backward chaining in rule-based reasoning with a suitable example.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** *Forward chaining* is a **data-driven** inference strategy that starts from known facts in the working memory and applies rules repeatedly to derive new facts until the goal is reached or no rule is applicable. It is best suited for monitoring and control tasks. *Backward chaining* is a **goal-driven** strategy that starts from a hypothesis (goal) and recursively tries to prove it by finding supporting sub-goals, eventually reducing them to known facts. It is best suited for diagnostic and query-answering tasks. **Example:** For the rule `IF fever AND cough THEN flu`: - *Forward chaining:* Given facts `fever` and `cough`, derive `flu`. - *Backward chaining:* To prove `flu`, verify `fever` and `cough` are true in the knowledge base. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, PCCAT502]** **What is a frame in AI? List its components and explain the concept of inheritance with an example.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** A **frame** (Minsky, 1975) is a structured knowledge representation scheme used to represent stereotypical situations, objects, or concepts. Its key components are: 1. **Frame Name** — unique identifier. 2. **Slots** — named attributes of the entity. 3. **Slot Values** — content stored in slots (data, defaults, pointers, or procedures). 4. **Attached Procedures (Daemons)** — IF-NEEDED, IF-ADDED, IF-REMOVED triggers. 5. **Inheritance Links** — connections to parent frames in a hierarchy. **Inheritance** allows a child frame to automatically acquire the slot values of its parent frame unless the child explicitly defines its own value. For example, a frame `Dog` (child of `Mammal`) inherits `gives_milk = YES` from its parent unless overridden, enabling compact and reusable knowledge representation. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCCAT502 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the architecture of a **Rule-Based Production System** in detail. Describe its three main components. Compare **forward chaining** and **backward chaining** with a clear example for each. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe **Frame-Based Reasoning** in detail. Explain the structure of a frame, the role of **slots and daemons**, and the mechanism of **inheritance with exceptions**. Provide a small example frame hierarchy. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Rule-Based Production System Architecture:** A rule-based production system (as proposed by Newell & Simon) consists of three primary components: 1. **Production Memory (Knowledge Base):** Contains a set of IF-THEN rules of the form `IF THEN `. For example: `IF (fever) AND (cough) THEN (flu)`. 2. **Working Memory (Fact Base):** A dynamic store of facts known to the system at any instant during inference. 3. **Inference Engine:** The reasoning module that controls the reasoning cycle — pattern matching, conflict resolution, and rule execution. **Comparison of Forward and Backward Chaining:** | Aspect | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining | |---|---|---| | Direction | Data → Goal | Goal → Data | | Strategy | Data-driven | Goal-driven | | Search | Antecedent matching | Consequent matching | | Suitable for | Monitoring, control | Diagnosis, queries | | Termination | No more fireable rules | Goal proven or disproven | **Example for Forward Chaining:** Given rules `R1: IF A AND B THEN C` and `R2: IF C THEN D`, and facts `A`, `B`, the system fires R1 to derive `C`, then R2 to derive `D`. **Example for Backward Chaining:** To prove `D`, the system looks for rules with `D` in consequent → finds `R2` → needs `C` → finds `R1` → needs `A` and `B` → both present → goal `D` proven. --- **(b) Frame-Based Reasoning:** A **frame** is a structured data structure representing a stereotypical concept. Its elements include: - **Frame Name:** Identifies the frame (e.g., `Mammal`). - **Slots:** Named attributes (e.g., `habitat`, `diet`, `legs`). - **Facet / Value:** The content of each slot. - **Defaults:** Pre-filled values used when no specific information exists. - **Daemons (Attached Procedures):** `IF-ADDED`, `IF-NEEDED`, `IF-REMOVED` — small procedures triggered by slot events. - **Inheritance Links:** Connections to parent frames in a hierarchy. **Inheritance with Exception Mechanism:** Inheritance propagates slot values down the hierarchy. When a child frame has its own local value, it **overrides** the inherited value. This is called *exception handling*. **Example Hierarchy:** - `Frame: Vehicle → has_wheels = YES` - `Frame: Car (subclass: Vehicle) → no_of_wheels = 4` (inherits has_wheels) - `Frame: Boat (subclass: Vehicle) → has_wheels = NO` (overrides parent — exception) Thus, `Boat.has_wheels = NO` because the local value overrides the inherited default — this is **default reasoning with exceptions**, a key strength of frame-based systems. --- **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCCAT502 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain **Model-Based Reasoning (MBR)** in detail. What is its architecture? Discuss **Reiter's theory of diagnosis** with an example circuit. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe the **Case-Based Reasoning (CBR)** methodology. Explain the **Aamodt-Plaza 4-R cycle** with a real-world example. Compare CBR with Rule-Based Reasoning. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Model-Based Reasoning:** Model-Based Reasoning is an inference technique in which the system reasons over an explicit **simulatable model** of the physical or conceptual system under study, rather than over pre-stored rules or cases. **Architecture of an MBR System:** 1. **Model Component:** Encodes the structure (components and connections) and behavior (operational laws) of the system. 2. **Observation Module:** Receives symptoms from the user or sensors. 3. **Simulation Engine:** Computes predicted behavior of the model under different component states. 4. **Discrepancy Detector:** Compares predictions with observations to identify conflicts. 5. **Diagnosis Generator:** Computes candidate fault sets via Reiter's hitting-set algorithm. **Reiter's Theory of Diagnosis:** Given a system description (SD), a set of components (C), and observations (OBS): - A **conflict set** is a minimal set of components $\{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_k\}$ such that the assumption "all components are OK" leads to a contradiction with OBS. - A **diagnosis** is a minimal set $\Delta \subseteq C$ that intersects every conflict set (a *hitting set*). **Example Circuit:** A circuit with two NOT gates in series, input = 0, expected output = 1, but observed output = 0. - $\text{CS}_1 = \{G_1, G_2\}$ — both gates cannot be OK. - Hitting sets: $\{G_1\}$, $\{G_2\}$ — minimal single-fault diagnoses. **Conclusion:** Either gate $G_1$ or gate $G_2$ is faulty. --- **(b) Case-Based Reasoning:** CBR solves new problems by retrieving and adapting solutions from a library of past similar cases rather than from general rules or abstract models. **The Aamodt-Plaza 4-R Cycle:** 1. **Retrieve:** Given a new problem $P_{\text{new}}$, search the case library for cases $C_i$ with maximum similarity $\text{sim}(P_{\text{new}}, C_i)$. Common algorithms include k-NN, nearest-neighbor indexing, and inductive retrieval. 2. **Reuse:** Take the retrieved solution and adapt it to fit $P_{\text{new}}$. Adaptation may involve substitution, transformation, or special-case re-use. 3. **Revise:** Test the proposed solution in the real world (or via simulation). If it fails, repair the solution. 4. **Retain:** If the new solved case contains reusable knowledge, store it in the case library for future use — this is the **learning step** of CBR. **Real-World Example — Medical Diagnosis:** A patient presents with fever, rash, and joint pain. The system retrieves a prior case of Chikungunya with similar symptoms (similarity 0.85). It reuses the treatment plan, revises the dosage based on the patient's age, and retains the new case for future patients. **CBR vs. RBR Comparison:** | Feature | CBR | RBR | |---|---|---| | Knowledge source | Specific past cases | General rules | | Learning | Automatic (Retain) | Manual updates | | Novelty handling | Strong (analogies) | Weak | | Explanation | "I solved a similar case" | Logical deduction | | Knowledge acquisition | Easy (record cases) | Hard (expert elicitation) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > 1. Students often confuse **frame-based inheritance** with simple copying — remember, inheritance is *dynamic* and *default-overridable*. > 2. In **CBR**, students forget the *Revise* step and jump directly from *Reuse* to *Retain* — this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss. > 3. In **Reiter's diagnosis**, students compute conflict sets but forget the *hitting set* step — diagnosis ≠ conflict set, it is the *minimal hitting set* of all conflict sets. > 4. Don't write *pseudo-code* for non-CS subjects — focus on clear prose, diagrams, and worked examples in the exam. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam — Module 4: Reasoning Techniques** - ✅ **Rule-Based Reasoning** uses **IF-THEN rules**; inference engine performs **forward** (data-driven) or **backward** (goal-driven) chaining. - ✅ **Production System** = Knowledge Base + Working Memory + Inference Engine + Conflict Resolver. - ✅ **Frame-Based Reasoning** uses **hierarchical frames with slots, defaults, and daemons**; key mechanism is **inheritance with exception overriding**. - ✅ **Frames** were introduced by **Marvin Minsky (1975)** to represent stereotypical situations. - ✅ **Model-Based Reasoning** uses an **explicit simulatable model** of the system (structure + behavior) — strongest technique for diagnosis. - ✅ **Reiter's Diagnosis** = minimal *hitting set* of all *conflict sets*. - ✅ **Case-Based Reasoning (CBR)** = 4-R cycle: **Retrieve → Reuse → Revise → Retain** (Aamodt & Plaza, 1994). - ✅ CBR is the only reasoning technique with **built-in learning** (via the Retain step). - ✅ **Similarity metric** in CBR is typically a **weighted nearest-neighbor** formula. - ✅ **MYCIN** is a classic rule-based medical expert system; **CASEY** is a classic CBR system; **Livingstone** is a famous model-based NASA system. - ✅ **Forward vs Backward Chaining:** Forward → monitoring/control; Backward → diagnosis/query. - ✅ **Conflict Resolution** in RBR: recency, specificity, priority, refractoriness. - ✅ **Daemons** in frames: IF-NEEDED, IF-ADDED, IF-REMOVED. - ✅ **Default Reasoning** in frames: parent value is used *unless* child explicitly overrides it. - ✅ Always draw a **mermaid/block diagram** in exam answers — it earns easy 1–2 marks. --- **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — REASONING TECHNIQUES** *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — PCCAT502*

topics.title: Handling uncertainty in expert systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAT502 - INTRODUCTION TO ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Handling uncertainty in expert systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Uncertainty in Expert Systems?

**Uncertainty in expert systems** is the lack of exact knowledge or complete confidence about a fact, rule, or conclusion during the reasoning process. It arises because real-world knowledge is often imprecise, incomplete, ambiguous, or probabilistic. An expert system must be capable of representing, propagating, and reasoning with this uncertainty to produce reliable conclusions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim style):** Uncertainty refers to the lack of exact knowledge that may be caused by unreliable, incomplete, or imprecise information. Expert systems employ several numerical and logical frameworks to manage this.

## 1.2 Why Does Uncertainty Arise?

Uncertainty creeps into expert knowledge due to the following causes:

1. **Weak Implications:** Rules of the form "If $A$ then $B$" may not hold in all cases. The actual relationship is more like "If $A$ then $B$ with probability $p$."
2. **Imprecise Language:** Words like *"often", "sometimes", "rarely"* cannot be mapped cleanly to Boolean truth.
3. **Missing Data:** Sensors or user inputs may be unavailable or noisy.
4. **Conflicting Evidence:** Multiple rules may give mutually exclusive conclusions with varying confidence.
5. **Aggregation Problem:** Combining conclusions from many uncertain rules accumulates error.
6. **Default (Non-Monotonic) Knowledge:** Assumptions may need to be retracted when new facts arrive.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Foggy Road Trip

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are driving from Kerala to Delhi. You *know* the route, but you are *uncertain* about traffic (probabilistic evidence), whether it will rain in Coimbatore (fuzzy belief), and whether a road is closed (default reasoning). A GPS navigation system is essentially an *expert system* — it handles these uncertainties by combining multiple weak signals into one strong recommendation. The methods discussed in this module (Bayesian, Certainty Factor, Dempster–Shafer, Fuzzy) are the four different "weather sensors" the AI uses to give you a final, trustworthy answer.

## 1.4 Categories of Uncertainty

| Category | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Statistical (Aleatoric)** | Arises from inherent randomness in the world | "70% chance of fever given flu" |
| **Subjective (Epistemic)** | Arises from the observer's ignorance | A doctor's belief that a patient has dengue |
| **Vagueness (Fuzziness)** | Boundaries of categories are not sharp | "High temperature", "tall person" |
| **Inconsistency** | Evidence contradicts itself | Two rules giving opposite conclusions |

## 1.5 Major Approaches to Handle Uncertainty (KTU Syllabus Map)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly expects knowledge of the following four classical approaches:
> 1. **Bayesian Reasoning** (Probabilistic)
> 2. **Certainty Factors (CF) Model** — used in MYCIN
> 3. **Dempster–Shafer Theory** (Evidential Reasoning)
> 4. **Fuzzy Logic** (Possibility-based Reasoning)

Each method is a mathematical tool that attaches a *number* to the system's confidence in a hypothesis, and provides *rules* for combining such numbers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Approach 1 — Bayesian Reasoning

### 2.1.1 The Core Idea
Bayesian reasoning treats hypotheses as **random variables** and updates belief in a hypothesis as new evidence arrives. The posterior probability is computed from the prior probability and the likelihood of the evidence.

### 2.1.2 Bayes' Theorem

For a hypothesis $H$ and evidence $E$:

$$
P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}
$$

where:

- $P(H)$ = **Prior probability** — initial belief in $H$ before evidence
- $P(E \mid H)$ = **Likelihood** — probability of observing $E$ if $H$ is true
- $P(H \mid E)$ = **Posterior probability** — updated belief after evidence
- $P(E)$ = **Marginal probability of evidence** (normalizing constant)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** It is a direct application of the definition of conditional probability, rewritten algebraically. Conceptually, it asks: *"Given what I just observed, how much should I revise my belief in $H$?"*

### 2.1.3 Bayesian Updating for Multiple Evidence

For independent pieces of evidence $E_1, E_2, \dots, E_n$:

$$
P(H \mid E_1, \dots, E_n) = \frac{P(E_n \mid H) \cdot P(H \mid E_1, \dots, E_{n-1})}{P(E_n \mid E_1, \dots, E_{n-1})}
$$

This is applied **iteratively**, which is the foundation of Bayesian Networks.

### 2.1.4 Engineering Utility
- Medical diagnosis systems
- Spam filtering (naive Bayes)
- Speech and image recognition
- Autonomous vehicle perception

---

## 2.2 Approach 2 — Certainty Factors (CF) Model

### 2.2.1 Origin
Developed for the **MYCIN** medical expert system (Stanford, 1970s) because pure Bayesian methods required probabilities that were hard to obtain from human experts.

### 2.2.2 Two Components

A certainty factor is defined as the **difference between two measures**:

$$
CF(H, E) = MB(H, E) - MD(H, E)
$$

where:

- $MB(H, E)$ = **Measure of Belief** in hypothesis $H$ given evidence $E$
- $MD(H, E)$ = **Measure of Disbelief** in hypothesis $H$ given evidence $E$
- $CF \in [-1, +1]$

$$
MB(H, E) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{\max\bigl[P(H \mid E), P(H)\bigr] - P(H)}{1 - P(H)}, & \text{if } P(H \mid E) > P(H) \\[6pt] 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

$$
MD(H, E) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{P(H) - \min\bigl[P(H \mid E), P(H)\bigr]}{P(H)}, & \text{if } P(H \mid E) < P(H) \\[6pt] 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### 2.2.3 Combining Certainty Factors (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked CF question** in KTU exams.

**(a) Conjunction of evidence (AND):**
$$
CF(H, E_1 \cap E_2) = \min\bigl[CF(H, E_1),\; CF(H, E_2)\bigr]
$$

**(b) Disjunction of evidence (OR):**
$$
CF(H, E_1 \cup E_2) = \max\bigl[CF(H, E_1),\; CF(H, E_2)\bigr]
$$

**(c) Consequence of a rule (chaining):**
$$
CF(\text{conclusion}) = CF(\text{rule}) \times CF(\text{premise})
$$

**(d) Two parallel rules supporting the same hypothesis:**
$$
CF_{combined}(CF_1, CF_2) = \begin{cases} CF_1 + CF_2 (1 - CF_1), & \text{if both } CF_1, CF_2 \geq 0 \\[4pt] \dfrac{CF_1 + CF_2}{1 - \min(\vert CF_1 \vert, \vert CF_2 \vert)}, & \text{if both } \leq 0 \\[6pt] \dfrac{CF_1 + CF_2}{1 - \min(\vert CF_1 \vert, \vert CF_2 \vert)}, & \text{if they have opposite signs} \end{cases}
$$

### 2.2.4 Engineering Utility
MYCIN-style clinical decision support, agricultural advisory, troubleshooting systems.

---

## 2.3 Approach 3 — Dempster–Shafer Theory (Evidential Reasoning)

### 2.3.1 Core Idea
Unlike Bayes, it allows assigning belief to **sets of hypotheses** rather than to individual ones, expressing **ignorance** explicitly.

### 2.3.2 Key Functions

Let $\Theta$ be the **frame of discernment** (set of all mutually exclusive hypotheses).

**Basic Probability Assignment (BPA) / Mass Function** $m$:
$$
m: 2^{\Theta} \rightarrow [0, 1], \quad \sum_{A \subseteq \Theta} m(A) = 1
$$

**Belief Function** $\text{Bel}(A)$:
$$
\text{Bel}(A) = \sum_{B \subseteq A} m(B)
$$

**Plausibility Function** $\text{Pl}(A)$:
$$
\text{Pl}(A) = 1 - \text{Bel}(\bar{A}) = \sum_{B \cap A \neq \emptyset} m(B)
$$

**Uncertainty Interval:**
$$
[\text{Bel}(A),\; \text{Pl}(A)]
$$

### 2.3.3 Dempster's Rule of Combination (KTU High-Yield)

For two independent sources of evidence with BPAs $m_1$ and $m_2$:

$$
(m_1 \oplus m_2)(C) = \frac{1}{1 - K} \sum_{A \cap B = C} m_1(A) \cdot m_2(B)
$$

where the **conflict** $K$ is:
$$
K = \sum_{A \cap B = \emptyset} m_1(A) \cdot m_2(B)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $K = 1$, the two sources totally conflict and the rule is undefined.

### 2.3.4 Engineering Utility
Sensor fusion, military target identification, multi-agent decision systems, diagnostic reasoning with incomplete information.

---

## 2.4 Approach 4 — Fuzzy Logic

### 2.4.1 Core Idea
Classical logic is **bivalent** ($0$ or $1$). Fuzzy logic, introduced by **Lotfi Zadeh (1965)**, allows **degrees of truth** between 0 and 1.

### 2.4.2 Membership Function

For a fuzzy set $A$ in universe $X$:
$$
\mu_A : X \rightarrow [0, 1]
$$

A value of $\mu_A(x) = 0.8$ means "$x$ is a member of $A$ to degree 0.8" (e.g., "temperature 38°C is *high* with degree 0.8").

### 2.4.3 Standard Operations

For fuzzy sets $A, B$ with memberships $\mu_A, \mu_B$:

$$
\mu_{A \cap B}(x) = \min[\mu_A(x),\, \mu_B(x)]
$$

$$
\mu_{A \cup B}(x) = \max[\mu_A(x),\, \mu_B(x)]
$$

$$
\mu_{\bar{A}}(x) = 1 - \mu_A(x)
$$

### 2.4.4 Fuzzy Inference Process (Mamdani)

1. **Fuzzification** — convert crisp inputs to membership degrees.
2. **Rule Evaluation** — apply IF–THEN rules using fuzzy operators.
3. **Aggregation** — combine rule outputs.
4. **Defuzzification** — produce a crisp output (e.g., centroid method):
$$
z^{*} = \frac{\int z \cdot \mu(z)\, dz}{\int \mu(z)\, dz}
$$

### 2.4.5 Engineering Utility
Washing machines, air conditioners, ABS braking, stock market prediction, control systems.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Method | Symbol / Formula | Range | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bayesian posterior | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) P(H)}{P(E)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Update belief from evidence |
| Certainty Factor | $CF(H, E) = MB(H, E) - MD(H, E)$ | $[-1, +1]$ | MYCIN-style rule confidence |
| CF Conjunction | $CF(H, E_1 \cap E_2) = \min(CF_1, CF_2)$ | $[-1, +1]$ | AND combination |
| CF Disjunction | $CF(H, E_1 \cup E_2) = \max(CF_1, CF_2)$ | $[-1, +1]$ | OR combination |
| CF Chaining | $CF(\text{conclusion}) = CF(\text{rule}) \times CF(\text{premise})$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Forward inference |
| BPA sum | $\sum_A m(A) = 1$ | $[0, 1]$ | Dempster–Shafer mass |
| Belief function | $\text{Bel}(A) = \sum_{B \subseteq A} m(B)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Lower bound on belief |
| Plausibility | $\text{Pl}(A) = 1 - \text{Bel}(\bar{A})$ | $[0, 1]$ | Upper bound on belief |
| Dempster combine | $(m_1 \oplus m_2)(C) = \dfrac{1}{1-K} \sum_{A \cap B = C} m_1(A) m_2(B)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fuse two sources |
| Fuzzy AND | $\min(\mu_A, \mu_B)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fuzzy intersection |
| Fuzzy OR | $\max(\mu_A, \mu_B)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fuzzy union |
| Defuzzification (centroid) | $z^* = \dfrac{\int z \mu(z) dz}{\int \mu(z) dz}$ | real | Crisp output |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Bayesian Reasoning (Cancer Test)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU 14-mark type problem**. The student is expected to show all five steps of Bayes' theorem application.

**Problem Statement:**
A screening test for a disease has the following properties:
- $P(D) = 0.01$ — prevalence of the disease in the population
- $P(\bar{D}) = 0.99$
- $P(+\mid D) = 0.95$ — sensitivity (true positive rate)
- $P(+\mid \bar{D}) = 0.02$ — false positive rate

A person tests positive. What is the probability that they actually have the disease?

### Step 1 — Identify the Goal
We need $P(D \mid +)$, the posterior probability of disease given a positive test.

### Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Theorem
$$
P(D \mid +) = \frac{P(+\mid D) \cdot P(D)}{P(+)}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Marginal $P(+)$ Using the Law of Total Probability
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(+) &= P(+\mid D) \cdot P(D) + P(+\mid \bar{D}) \cdot P(\bar{D}) \\
&= (0.95)(0.01) + (0.02)(0.99) \\
&= 0.0095 + 0.0198 \\
&= 0.0293
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into Bayes' Theorem
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(D \mid +) &= \frac{(0.95)(0.01)}{0.0293} \\
&= \frac{0.0095}{0.0293} \\
&\approx 0.3242
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Even with a 95% accurate test, a positive result only implies about a **32.4% chance** of having the disease, because the disease is rare (low prior). This is the **base-rate fallacy** — a key insight in AI-based medical diagnosis.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Certainty Factor Combination

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
The system has three rules supporting the same hypothesis $H$:

| Rule | Premise CF | Rule CF (CFi) |
|---|---|---|
| $R_1$ | $CF_1 = 0.7$ | $\text{CF of premise} = 0.8$ |
| $R_2$ | $CF_2 = 0.6$ | $\text{CF of premise} = 0.9$ |
| $R_3$ | $CF_3 = -0.4$ | $\text{CF of premise} = 0.7$ |

Find the final certainty factor of $H$.

### Step 1 — Compute the effective CF contributed by each rule (chaining formula)

For each rule: $CF(\text{effective}) = CF(\text{rule}) \times CF(\text{premise})$

$$
\begin{aligned}
CF(R_1) &= 0.7 \times 0.8 = 0.56 \\
CF(R_2) &= 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.54 \\
CF(R_3) &= -0.4 \times 0.7 = -0.28
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Combine the two positive CFs first ($R_1$ and $R_2$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CF_{12} &= CF_{R_1} + CF_{R_2} \cdot (1 - CF_{R_1}) \\
&= 0.56 + 0.54 \cdot (1 - 0.56) \\
&= 0.56 + 0.54 \cdot 0.44 \\
&= 0.56 + 0.2376 \\
&= 0.7976
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Combine $CF_{12}$ (positive) with the negative $CF_{R_3}$

Opposite signs ⇒ use:
$$
CF_{final} = \frac{CF_{12} + CF_{R_3}}{1 - \min(\vert CF_{12} \vert, \vert CF_{R_3} \vert)}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
CF_{final} &= \frac{0.7976 + (-0.28)}{1 - \min(0.7976, 0.28)} \\
&= \frac{0.5176}{1 - 0.28} \\
&= \frac{0.5176}{0.72} \\
&\approx 0.7189
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Answer
> The combined certainty factor for hypothesis $H$ is approximately **$+0.719$**, indicating moderately strong belief.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Dempster–Shafer Combination

**Problem Statement:**
Two sensors $S_1$ and $S_2$ observe whether an object is type $A$, type $B$, or type $C$. Frame $\Theta = \{A, B\}$.

- Sensor $S_1$: $m_1(\{A\}) = 0.6$, $m_1(\{B\}) = 0.1$, $m_1(\{A, B\}) = 0.3$
- Sensor $S_2$: $m_2(\{A\}) = 0.5$, $m_2(\{B\}) = 0.2$, $m_2(\{A, B\}) = 0.3$

Compute the combined mass for $\{A\}$, $\{B\}$, and $\{A, B\}$.

### Step 1 — Build the Combination Table

| $m_1 \downarrow$ \ $m_2 \rightarrow$ | $\{A\}=0.5$ | $\{B\}=0.2$ | $\{A,B\}=0.3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\{A\}=0.6$ | $\{A\}=0.30$ | $\emptyset=0.12$ | $\{A\}=0.18$ |
| $\{B\}=0.1$ | $\emptyset=0.05$ | $\{B\}=0.02$ | $\{B\}=0.03$ |
| $\{A,B\}=0.3$ | $\{A\}=0.15$ | $\{B\}=0.06$ | $\{A,B\}=0.09$ |

### Step 2 — Compute the Conflict $K$
$$
K = 0.12 + 0.05 = 0.17
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Combined Masses (after normalization)

Combined mass for $\{A\}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
m(\{A\}) &= \frac{0.30 + 0.18 + 0.15}{1 - 0.17} \\
&= \frac{0.63}{0.83} \\
&\approx 0.759
\end{aligned}
$$

Combined mass for $\{B\}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
m(\{B\}) &= \frac{0.02 + 0.03 + 0.06}{0.83} \\
&= \frac{0.11}{0.83} \\
&\approx 0.133
\end{aligned}
$$

Combined mass for $\{A, B\}$ (ignorance):
$$
\begin{aligned}
m(\{A, B\}) &= \frac{0.09}{0.83} \\
&\approx 0.108
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Verification
$$
0.759 + 0.133 + 0.108 = 1.000 \;\checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that $m(\{A\})$ *increased* (0.6 → 0.759) because both sensors support $A$. The mass on $\{A, B\}$ dropped, meaning **ignorance decreased** after fusing the two sources — a key benefit of Dempster–Shafer fusion.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — When to Use What?

| Criterion | Bayesian | Certainty Factor | Dempster–Shafer | Fuzzy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data needed | $P(H), P(E \mid H)$ | Expert judgments | BPAs from sources | Membership functions |
| Handles ignorance | No | Partially | **Yes (explicitly)** | Yes |
| Mathematical rigor | Very high | Medium | High | Medium |
| Computational cost | Medium | **Low** | High | Low |
| Domain suitability | Frequency-based data | Rule-based expert systems | Sensor fusion | Linguistic variables |
| Famous example | Pathfinder | MYCIN | HUGIN / Smaragd | Washing machine control |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Diagram — Taxonomy of Uncertainty Handling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Uncertainty in Expert Systems] --> B[Probabilistic Approach]
    A --> C[Rule-Based Approach]
    A --> D[Evidential Approach]
    A --> E[Possibilistic Approach]

    B --> B1[Bayesian Reasoning]
    B1 --> B1a[Prior Probability P of H]
    B1 --> B1b[Likelihood P of E given H]
    B1 --> B1c[Posterior P of H given E]
    B1 --> B1d[Bayesian Networks DAG]

    C --> C1[Certainty Factor MYCIN]
    C1 --> C1a[Measure of Belief MB]
    C1 --> C1b[Measure of Disbelief MD]
    C1 --> C1c[CF equals MB minus MD]
    C1 --> C1d[Combine rules by AND OR CHAIN]

    D --> D1[Dempster Shafer Theory]
    D1 --> D1a[Frame of Discernment Theta]
    D1 --> D1b[Basic Probability Assignment m]
    D1 --> D1c[Belief Bel of A]
    D1 --> D1d[Plausibility Pl of A]
    D1 --> D1e[Dempster Rule of Combination]

    E --> E1[Fuzzy Logic Zadeh 1965]
    E1 --> E1a[Membership Function mu of x]
    E1 --> E1b[Min for AND]
    E1 --> E1c[Max for OR]
    E1 --> E1d[Defuzzification Centroid]
```

## 4.2 Bayesian Inference Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Prior P of H] --> upd[Bayesian Update]
    ev1[Evidence E] --> upd
    lh1[Likelihood P of E given H] --> upd
    upd --> out1[Posterior P of H given E]
    out1 --> next1{Need more evidence?}
    next1 -- Yes --> upd
    next1 -- No --> dec1[Final Decision]
```

## 4.3 Certainty Factor Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    r1[Rule 1 with CF1] --> ch1[Apply Chaining Formula]
    p1[Premise CFp1] --> ch1
    r2[Rule 2 with CF2] --> ch2[Apply Chaining Formula]
    p2[Premise CFp2] --> ch2
    ch1 --> cmb[Combine Effective CFs]
    ch2 --> cmb
    cmb --> out1[Final CF for Hypothesis H]
    out1 --> intp{Interpretation}
    intp --> i1[CF greater than 0.8 Strong Belief]
    intp --> i2[CF between 0.4 and 0.8 Moderate Belief]
    intp --> i3[CF between 0 and 0.4 Weak Belief]
    intp --> i4[CF less than 0 Evidence Against]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    bp1 --> cbn[Dempster Rule]
    bp2 --> cbn
    cbn --> kc[Compute Conflict K]
    kc --> nrm[Normalize by 1 minus K]
    nrm --> f1[Combined Mass m of A]
    nrm --> f2[Combined Mass m of B]
    nrm --> f3[Combined Mass m of Theta]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define uncertainty. List any four sources of uncertainty in expert systems. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Uncertainty is the lack of exact knowledge due to incomplete, imprecise, or ambiguous information available to an expert system. It refers to the degree of confidence (or lack of it) that the system has in its facts, rules, and conclusions.

Four sources of uncertainty are:

1. **Weak implications** — Rules of the form "If $A$ then $B$" do not hold with absolute certainty; they hold only with a certain probability.
2. **Imprecise language** — Expert knowledge is often expressed using vague terms such as *"frequently", "rarely"*, which cannot be mapped to Boolean truth.
3. **Missing or noisy data** — Sensor or user-provided input may be unavailable, partial, or corrupted.
4. **Conflicting evidence** — Multiple rules may suggest mutually exclusive conclusions with varying levels of confidence, requiring the system to resolve the conflict.

---

### Question 2: What is a Certainty Factor? Write the formula relating CF to MB and MD. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Certainty Factor (CF)** is a numerical measure, used in expert systems such as MYCIN, that represents the degree of confidence (or disbelief) an expert has in a hypothesis given some evidence. It ranges from **$-1$ (complete disbelief)** to **$+1$ (complete belief)**, with $0$ meaning no evidence either way.

It is defined as the difference between the **Measure of Belief (MB)** and the **Measure of Disbelief (MD)**:

$$
CF(H, E) = MB(H, E) - MD(H, E)
$$

where $MB$ and $MD$ are computed from the prior and posterior probabilities of the hypothesis $H$ given evidence $E$.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, every Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice. Two completely independent alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Bayesian Reasoning

**(a)** Derive Bayes' theorem from the definition of conditional probability. State clearly what each term represents. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A diagnostic expert system must decide between two diseases $D_1$ and $D_2$. The system uses three symptoms $S_1, S_2, S_3$. Given:

$$
P(D_1) = 0.4, \quad P(D_2) = 0.6
$$

$$
P(S_1 \mid D_1) = 0.8, \quad P(S_1 \mid D_2) = 0.2
$$

A patient shows symptom $S_1$. Using Bayes' theorem, compute $P(D_1 \mid S_1)$ and $P(D_2 \mid S_1)$. Decide which disease is more likely. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Bayes' Theorem **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Definition of conditional probability.**
For any two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:
$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to express the joint probability.**
$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)
$$

**Step 3 — Symmetrically, write the joint probability the other way.**
$$
P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions for the joint probability.**
$$
P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $P(A \mid B)$ (the desired posterior).**
$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

This is **Bayes' Theorem**.

**Term Meanings:**

- $P(A)$ = prior probability of hypothesis $A$.
- $P(B \mid A)$ = likelihood of observing evidence $B$ given $A$ is true.
- $P(B)$ = marginal probability of evidence $B$ (acts as a normalizer).
- $P(A \mid B)$ = posterior probability of $A$ after observing $B$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Application **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute $P(S_1)$ using the Law of Total Probability.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(S_1) &= P(S_1 \mid D_1) \cdot P(D_1) + P(S_1 \mid D_2) \cdot P(D_2) \\
&= (0.8)(0.4) + (0.2)(0.6) \\
&= 0.32 + 0.12 \\
&= 0.44
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' theorem for $D_1$.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(D_1 \mid S_1) &= \frac{P(S_1 \mid D_1) \cdot P(D_1)}{P(S_1)} \\
&= \frac{(0.8)(0.4)}{0.44} \\
&= \frac{0.32}{0.44} \\
&\approx 0.7273
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' theorem for $D_2$.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(D_2 \mid S_1) &= \frac{P(S_1 \mid D_2) \cdot P(D_2)}{P(S_1)} \\
&= \frac{(0.2)(0.6)}{0.44} \\
&= \frac{0.12}{0.44} \\
&\approx 0.2727
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification.**
$$
0.7273 + 0.2727 = 1.000 \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Decision.**
> [!NOTE]
> Since $P(D_1 \mid S_1) \approx 0.7273 > P(D_2 \mid S_1) \approx 0.2727$, the expert system concludes that **disease $D_1$ is more likely**. The symptom $S_1$ strongly increases the system's confidence in $D_1$ (from prior 0.4 to posterior 0.73).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Certainty Factors

**(a)** Explain the Certainty Factor (CF) model used in MYCIN. Define MB, MD, and CF, and derive the combination formulas for AND, OR, and chained rules. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A medical expert system uses the following rules to conclude that the patient has disease $H$:

| Rule | Premise CF | Rule CF (CF of implication) |
|---|---|---|
| $R_1$ | 0.9 | 0.6 |
| $R_2$ | 0.8 | 0.5 |
| $R_3$ | 0.7 | $-0.3$ |

Compute the final CF for hypothesis $H$ using chaining and combination. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution B

#### Part (a) — CF Model Explanation and Formulas **[7 marks]**

The **Certainty Factor (CF) model** was developed by Shortliffe and Buchanan for the MYCIN expert system (1970s) to handle uncertainty in medical diagnosis using approximate rules rather than full Bayesian statistics.

**Key Definitions:**

- **Measure of Belief $MB(H, E)$** — quantifies the extent to which evidence $E$ *increases* belief in hypothesis $H$.
- **Measure of Disbelief $MD(H, E)$** — quantifies the extent to which evidence $E$ *decreases* belief in $H$.
- **Certainty Factor** — net change in belief:

$$
CF(H, E) = MB(H, E) - MD(H, E), \quad CF \in [-1, +1]
$$

**Combination Formulas:**

- **Conjunction (AND) of evidence:** Take the minimum of the individual CFs, because the weakest link dominates.
$$
CF(H, E_1 \cap E_2) = \min\bigl[CF(H, E_1),\, CF(H, E_2)\bigr]
$$

- **Disjunction (OR) of evidence:** Take the maximum, because the strongest evidence dominates.
$$
CF(H, E_1 \cup E_2) = \max\bigl[CF(H, E_1),\, CF(H, E_2)\bigr]
$$

- **Rule chaining:** When a rule's premise itself has a CF, multiply it with the rule's CF to get the effective CF.
$$
CF(\text{conclusion}) = CF(\text{rule}) \times CF(\text{premise})
$$

- **Parallel rules supporting same hypothesis (same sign):**
$$
CF_{combined} = CF_1 + CF_2 \cdot (1 - CF_1) \quad \text{(for positive CFs)}
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Combination **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute effective CF for each rule (chaining).**
$$
\begin{aligned}
CF(R_1) &= 0.9 \times 0.6 = 0.54 \\
CF(R_2) &= 0.8 \times 0.5 = 0.40 \\
CF(R_3) &= 0.7 \times (-0.3) = -0.21
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Combine the two positive CFs ($R_1$ and $R_2$) using the parallel-rule formula.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
CF_{12} &= 0.54 + 0.40 \cdot (1 - 0.54) \\
&= 0.54 + 0.40 \cdot 0.46 \\
&= 0.54 + 0.184 \\
&= 0.724
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine $CF_{12}$ (positive) with $CF(R_3) = -0.21$ (negative).**

Opposite signs → use the formula:
$$
CF_{final} = \frac{CF_{12} + CF_{R_3}}{1 - \min(\vert CF_{12} \vert, \vert CF_{R_3} \vert)}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
CF_{final} &= \frac{0.724 + (-0.21)}{1 - \min(0.724, 0.21)} \\
&= \frac{0.514}{1 - 0.21} \\
&= \frac{0.514}{0.79} \\
&\approx 0.6506
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
> The final certainty factor of hypothesis $H$ is approximately **$+0.65$**, indicating **moderately strong belief** in the hypothesis, despite one rule providing opposing (negative) evidence. The positive rules dominate, but the negative rule reduces the overall confidence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up $\min$ and $\max$ in CF combination.** KTU answer scripts routinely lose 2 marks by writing "AND = max" or "OR = min". Memorize: **AND = min**, **OR = max**.
> 2. **Forgetting the chaining step.** If a rule's premise itself is uncertain, students often forget to multiply $CF(\text{rule}) \times CF(\text{premise})$ — this is worth 2 marks.
> 3. **Missing the normalization $1-K$ in Dempster–Shafer.** Sum of joint masses including $\emptyset$ is *not* the final mass; divide by $(1 - K)$. Losing this loses 3 marks.
> 4. **Using probability when CF is asked (or vice versa).** Bayes' $P \in [0, 1]$ only; CF $\in [-1, 1]$ because it models disbelief.
> 5. **Confusing fuzzy AND/OR with crisp AND/OR.** Fuzzy AND is $\min$, fuzzy OR is $\max$, fuzzy NOT is $1 - \mu$. Students sometimes incorrectly write $\mu_A \cdot \mu_B$.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Handling Uncertainty in Expert Systems**

- **Why uncertainty?** Real-world knowledge is incomplete, vague, probabilistic, or conflicting.
- **Bayesian Reasoning** uses $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) P(H)}{P(E)}$; well-suited when prior and likelihood probabilities are available.
- **Base-rate fallacy:** Even with a highly accurate test, low prior $P(D)$ leads to surprisingly low $P(D \mid +)$.
- **Certainty Factor (CF):** $CF = MB - MD$, range $[-1, +1]$. Used in MYCIN. Doesn't need full probabilities.
- **CF combination rules:** AND = min, OR = max, chaining = multiply, parallel positive = $CF_1 + CF_2(1 - CF_1)$.
- **Dempster–Shafer:** Uses mass $m$ on subsets of $\Theta$; $\text{Bel}(A) \le P(A) \le \text{Pl}(A)$; combines with Dempster's rule, normalized by $(1 - K)$.
- **Fuzzy Logic:** Truth is a degree in $[0, 1]$; AND = min, OR = max, NOT = $1 - \mu$; Mamdani inference pipeline (fuzzify → evaluate → aggregate → defuzzify).
- **Centroid defuzzification:** $z^* = \dfrac{\int z\, \mu(z)\, dz}{\int \mu(z)\, dz}$.
- **When to use which?** Probabilistic data → Bayes; rule-based expert system → CF; multi-sensor fusion → Dempster–Shafer; linguistic / continuous control → Fuzzy.
- **Verifying combination results:** Sum of all final masses/probabilities/CFs over the hypothesis space should equal **$1$** (or for CF in $[-1, +1]$, results should be within range).
- **Most testable KTU concepts:** Bayes' numerical application, CF combination with chaining, Dempster's rule with conflict $K$, and the Mamdani fuzzy inference pipeline.
